name: Albanian war dances
tagline: Martial sword-and-fire dances re-enacting battle before Albanian warriors fought.
profile: Albanian war dances (Albanian: valle luftarake) were historically performed before departure for battle and now appear at feasts and festivals, differing by region. Early-19th-century Western travellers described a circle dance around a ritual purifying fire, beginning with a battle cry and war drums; the northern-highland sword-dance type spread to the Lezha lowlands via mountain shepherds. The Rugova variant is a mock duel between two men competing for a maiden (English Wikipedia, 'Albanian war dances').
insight: A living relic of pantomimic combat rites linking modern Albanian identity to ancient Illyrian/Pyrrhic martial traditions; the folktale 'Skanderbeg and Ballaban' narrates the sword dance performed by Skanderbeg's soldiers before battle (Robert Elsie, Albanian Folktales and Legends, via English Wikipedia).

sources.title: Introduction to Albanian Dance Forms in Kosovo

notes: Genre/category article, not one discrete dance. QID Q31667951 could not be fetched live; article was renamed from 'Rugova (sword dance)', so its stored Wikidata English label may still read the old title - verify the live enwiki sitelink before publishing.

name: K'cimi i Tropojes
era: Traditional highland dance of great antiquity; UNESCO-inscribed 2024
tagline: Tropoja's festive highland couples' dance of eagles, handkerchiefs and springtime.
profile: The k'cimi dance is a popular, festive dance performed at spontaneous gatherings or ceremonies such as weddings, birthdays, initiations and village celebrations (UNESCO ICH). Accompanied by drums, it is danced by men, women and children, usually in couples who change partners, facing one another without touching, making swooning movements and raising and dropping their arms as they move around one another; women often wave a red handkerchief. It is associated with springtime, mountains and the flight of eagles (UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage, 'K'cimi dancing of Tropojë').
insight: In December 2024 it became Albania's fourth element on UNESCO's Representative List, following Albanian Iso-Polyphony, the Xhubleta, and Transhumance, inscribed at the 19th session of the Intergovernmental Committee in Asunción, Paraguay (UNESCO).
notes: Same dance as Vallja e Tropojës; UNESCO title is 'K'cimi dancing of Tropojë' (file 01881, decision 19.COM 7.b.19). Shares Wikipedia/Wikidata records with that slug.

name: Vallja e Tropojës
tagline: Dynamic circle-and-line folk dance of the northern Albanian Alps.
profile: Vallja e Tropojës is a traditional Albanian folk dance from the Tropojë region of northern Albania (Gjakova Highlands), known for its dynamic and rhythmic movements reflecting the region's history and social values (English Wikipedia). Traditionally performed in a circle or line, it is accompanied by drums (tupan/daulle), the two-string çifteli, and the fyell (flute), with dancers in traditional attire waving red or yellow scarves. Under the name 'K'cimi dancing of Tropojë' it was inscribed on the UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage List in December 2024 (English Wikipedia; UNESCO).
insight: A cornerstone of identity for the people of Tropojë and the Gjakova Highlands spanning northern Albania and Kosovo; its 2024 UNESCO listing elevated it into a globally recognized symbol of Albanian heritage (UNESCO).
notes: Same dance as K'cimi i Tropojes slug; maintain one canonical record.

name: Shota
era: Traditional; stage form codified mid-20th c. (Pristina Shota ensemble founded 1948)
tagline: Flirtatious Kosovar couple's dance with handkerchiefs and quick footwork.
profile: Shota (also Vallja e Shotës) is an Albanian dance from northern Albania and Kosovo: an idyllic, flirtatious duet for a girl and a boy who move around each other without touching, with intricate arm gestures, synchronized steps and a strong 2/4 rhythm, ending with the exchange of a scarf. Commonly performed at weddings and folk festivals, its modern stage choreography originated with the professional Shota ensemble of Pristina and was adopted by the Yugoslav ensembles Kolo and Tanec (English Wikipedia; albanian-folklore.com).
insight: Widely recognized as the symbol of Kosovo folklore and an emblem of Albanian patriotism; its name is popularly but disputedly linked to the heroine Shote Galica, while folklorists trace 'Vallja e shotës' to a folk term for the wild duck whose mating display the movements evoke (English Wikipedia; albanian-folklore.com).
notes: Disambiguate from 'Shota (ensemble)' and unrelated Shota items. The Shote-Galica naming link is contested folkloric attribution, not established fact.

name: Argentine Tango
tagline: Close-embrace improvised partner dance of Río de la Plata passion.
profile: Argentine tango is a partner dance and musical genre that developed in the working-class immigrant neighborhoods (arrabales) and academias of Buenos Aires and Montevideo in the late 19th century, evolving from milonga, candombe, and habanera. It is danced in a close or open embrace built on improvised walking, with the bandoneón giving the orquesta típica its distinctive voice (English Wikipedia). Lyrics are marked by nostalgia and lost love; leading figures include Carlos Gardel and Ástor Piazzolla.
insight: Tango was recognized by Argentina's Ley 24.684 (1996) as integral to the nation's cultural patrimony, covering its music, lyrics, and dance; it embodies and encourages cultural dialogue across the Río de la Plata (Argentina InfoLeg; UNESCO).
notes: Discrete dance but a subset of the broad 'tango' family (Q338450). Covered by the joint Argentina-Uruguay UNESCO Tango inscription (2009, Ref. 00258).

name: Bachatango
tagline: Fusion of Dominican bachata with Argentine tango moves.
profile: Bachatango is a fusion style combining the four-step Dominican bachata with Argentine tango elements - kicks, dips, turns, ganchos, leg wraps, and long pauses - characterized by sensual hip movements; partners hold a closed position with inner-thigh contact (English Wikipedia). It originated among dance instructors in Turin, Italy, was once popular in foreign ballrooms, and is now rare on social floors.
insight: Bachatango exemplifies the early-2000s 'Bachata Fusion' trend in which dancers blended bachata with tango, salsa, and zouk; it is essentially unknown in the Dominican Republic, bachata's homeland (English Wikipedia).
notes: FUSION dance, NOT Argentine folk. Geographic origin is Turin, Italy (~45.07, 7.69), not Argentina - the 'country: Argentina' field is a catalog convention and needs review.

name: Candombe
era: Colonial era; Afro-Argentine presence in Buenos Aires from c. 1580; documented 18th-19th c.
tagline: Afro-Argentine drum-and-dance heritage of Buenos Aires barrios.
profile: Candombe is an Afro-Río de la Plata music-and-dance tradition. The Argentine (porteño) form, centered in Buenos Aires neighborhoods such as Monserrat, San Telmo, and La Boca, is played on two main hand drums - the 'llamador' (bass) and 'repicador' (sharp) - and danced by comparsas in street parades (llamadas) (English Wikipedia). The Argentine variety became largely private due to demographic decline and carnival bans, but is reviving in public street parades.
insight: Argentine candombe is a key root of milonga and tango and a living marker of Afro-Argentine identity; the Kikongo-derived word and the 'tangós' gathering places gave rise to the term tango (English Wikipedia; Buenos Aires City Government).

sources.title: Discover the rhythm of the city's roots

notes: This slug = Argentine/Río de la Plata candombe. The UNESCO 2009 inscription 'Candombe and its socio-cultural space' (Ref. 00182) is the URUGUAYAN form, not this one. Shared Wikidata item Q1033073 covers candombe broadly (predominantly Uruguayan).

name: Chacarera
era: Documented from early 20th century (Chazarreta scores, 1910s-1920s); claimed older rural roots
tagline: Lively Santiago del Estero couple dance with stomping and clapping.
profile: The chacarera is a fast, festive couples folk dance from Santiago del Estero, the rural counterpart to cosmopolitan tango. Dancers face each other without touching; men circle the women with foot-stomping (zapateo) and finger-snapping (castañetas) while women perform the swirling zarandeo, ending with the 'coronación' embrace. It is accompanied by guitar, violin, bombo legüero, and voice, sung in coplas; chacarera simple and doble are the main variants (English Wikipedia; Carnegie Hall).
insight: Standardized by Andrés Chazarreta's early-20th-century scores and the 1950s recordings of Los Hermanos Ábalos, the chacarera became a nationwide emblem of Argentine nativism and rural identity (English Wikipedia).

name: Gato
era: Emerged c. 1820s-1830s; peak gaucho popularity mid-19th century
tagline: Playful, picaresque gaucho couple dance with humorous verses.
profile: The gato ('cat') is a lively, picaresque couples folk dance and music style, hugely popular across rural Argentina and a favorite of gaucho culture. Its rhythm resembles the chacarera but its structure differs; couples perform stomping, spinning, finger-snapping and 'relaciones' (humorous recited couplets when the music stops). It is played on guitar with sung verses; variants include the gato cuyano, patriótico, valseado, and polqueado (English Wikipedia).
insight: The gato was among the most popular dances of independence-era Argentina and strongly influenced the chacarera; Britannica lists it among the core gaucho couple dances based on the fandango (Encyclopædia Britannica).
notes: Coordinates are an approximate Pampas centroid.

name: Malambo
tagline: Competitive solo gaucho footwork duel of the Pampas.
profile: Malambo is a solo, traditionally all-male competitive gaucho dance from the Pampas, with no formal choreography. Dancers face off in counterpoint, displaying elaborate percussive footwork - zapateados (stomping) and cepillados (brushing) - often with spinning boleadoras and accompanied by guitar and bombo (English Wikipedia). Two main styles exist: estilo sureño (southern) and estilo norteño (northern, faster). The first musical version was published by Ventura Lynch in 1883.
insight: Malambo is the central solo expression of gaucho identity; the National Malambo Festival has been held in Laborde, Córdoba, since 1966, and the troupe Malevo brought it to a global audience on America's Got Talent in 2016 (English Wikipedia).
notes: Do NOT confuse with Q568597 (Malambo, a municipality in Colombia). Coordinates are an approximate Pampas centroid.

name: Milonga
tagline: Fast, syncopated precursor and cousin of tango.
profile: Milonga is a music genre and partner dance that originated in the Río de la Plata around the 1870s, derived from the payada de contrapunto and an 'excited habanera,' with strong Afro-Argentine candombe influence. The dance uses the same elements as tango but is faster, more relaxed and humorous, with rhythmic walking; styles include milonga lisa (step on every beat) and milonga con traspié (syncopated double steps) (English Wikipedia). It is considered a precursor of tango.
insight: Milonga is a foundational root of tango and a living part of the tango tradition; the word derives from an African (Kimbundu/Ki-Kongo) term, reflecting tango's Afro-Argentine origins (English Wikipedia; Robert Farris Thompson).
notes: This slug = milonga the dance/music genre (Q942955). Distinguish from 'milonga' meaning a tango dance EVENT/venue (Q1338948). The dance specifically is Q6860415.

name: Pericon
era: Evolved late 18th-early 19th c. from cielito/contradanza; Pericón Nacional registered 1880s
tagline: Stately group square-dance of eight couples waving handkerchiefs.
profile: The pericón is a group folk dance of Argentina and Uruguay performed by a set of independent couples, generally eight, who execute coordinated contradance/quadrille figures at the shout of '¡Aura!' - including the famous 'pabellón,' where couples raise handkerchiefs to form a flag. Dancers wear colonial-era dress and carry pañuelos (Spanish Wikipedia; Plan Ceibal). It evolved from the cielito (a descendant of the British country dance) alongside the media caña.
insight: The pericón was declared the 'Danza Nacional Argentina' by Ley 26.297 (2007); the popular 'Pericón Nacional' score was registered by Gerardo Grasso in the late 1880s (Argentina InfoLeg; Spanish Wikipedia).

sources.title: El Pericón | Danzas de la Patria Vieja

notes: No English Wikipedia article exists - Spanish article used and wikipedia_url left blank per rules. Origin shared/disputed between Argentina and Uruguay.

name: Tango
tagline: Iconic Río de la Plata partner dance and global musical genre.
profile: Tango is a partner and social dance and musical genre that originated in the 1880s in the impoverished port neighborhoods along the Río de la Plata (Buenos Aires and Montevideo), born from Argentine milonga, Spanish-Cuban habanera, and Uruguayan candombe. It encompasses a family of related dances (tango, vals, milonga) and styles ranging from close-embrace milonguero to open salon and stage tango (English Wikipedia). The bandoneón is its signature instrument.
insight: Tango is the most recognizable embodiment of Río de la Plata identity and the only fully UNESCO-inscribed dance in this region's catalog (2009); it both embodies and encourages diversity and cultural dialogue (UNESCO; English Wikipedia).
notes: BROAD genre/family. Parent of argentine-tango. QID Q338450 ('Argentinian-Uruguayan partner dance') - NOT Q34095 (= bronze metal). UNESCO inscription is binational (Argentina & Uruguay), Ref. 00258.

name: Latin dance
era: Term formalized 20th century; roots Indigenous/European/African colonial era
tagline: Umbrella term for many Latin American social and ballroom dances.
profile: 'Latin dance' is a broad umbrella category, not a single dance. It has two meanings: (1) social/street Latin dances (salsa, mambo, merengue, rumba, bachata, bomba, plena) and (2) the competitive ballroom 'International Latin' category (cha-cha-cha, samba, rumba, paso doble, jive). It draws on Indigenous American, European, and African influences (English Wikipedia). Argentine folk dances (chacarera, gato, zamba) are generally NOT classed as 'Latin dance.'
insight: As a category, 'Latin dance' reflects the fusion of Native, European, and African traditions across Latin America and the codification of competitive ballroom Dancesport (English Wikipedia).
notes: BROAD CATEGORY, not a single dance and not specifically Argentine - the 'country: Argentina' field is a catalog convention and needs review.

name: Zamba
era: 19th century (derived from Peruvian zamacueca via Chilean cueca)
tagline: Elegant northwestern courtship dance of waving white handkerchiefs.
profile: Zamba is a slow, elegant couples folk dance and music genre of northwestern Argentina (Salta, Tucumán). In 6/8, couples circle each other without touching, waving white handkerchiefs (pañuelos) to enact a poetic courtship of invitation, acceptance, and gentle rejection; women also work their wide skirts. It is played on guitar and bombo legüero, sometimes with violin (English Wikipedia; Dale Folklore). It derives from the Peruvian zamacueca and shares elements with the cueca.
insight: Often called the 'queen' of Argentine folk dances and once considered the national dance, zamba is a centerpiece of festivals like Cosquín; its name comes from the colonial term 'zambo' (mixed ancestry), distinct from Brazilian samba (English Wikipedia).
notes: Distinguish from Brazilian samba and from the Peruvian zamacueca (its ancestor). Not the song 'Zamba de Vargas' (Q6170836).

name: Ballroom dance
era: Roots traced to late 16th century (Arbeau's Orchésographie, 1588); modern competitive form early 20th century
tagline: Elegant partnered social and competitive dances, from waltz to quickstep.
profile: Ballroom dance is a set of European partner dances enjoyed socially and competitively worldwide. Per Encyclopædia Britannica, it is a type of social dancing originally practiced in Europe and the United States, performed by couples following prescribed steps, historically distinguished from folk dance by its association with elite social classes and invitational dance events. With the rise of DanceSport the term narrowed to the dances recognized by two principal schools - the International School (Standard and Latin) and the American School (Smooth and Rhythm) (Wikipedia).
insight: Ballroom dance democratized over the 20th century from an aristocratic pastime into a global social and competitive activity, popularized by figures such as Vernon and Irene Castle, Fred Astaire, and Arthur Murray (Encyclopædia Britannica).
notes: BROAD UMBRELLA CATEGORY, not a single dance, and NOT Austrian. The 'country: Austria' label is almost certainly a catalog artifact - likely a spillover from the Austrian-associated Viennese Waltz (added to Austria's national ICH inventory in 2017). Recommend blanking country/country_code or marking 'multinational/Europe'.

name: DC Mara
era: Club founded 1 September 1965; formation team active internationally from the 1990s
tagline: Belarusian ballroom dance club and international DanceSport formation team.
profile: DC Mara (Dance Club Mara; Belarusian: Мара, 'dream') is a ballroom dance club in Belarus that fields the official formation team representing Belarus in IDSF/WDSF world-ranking competitions, in both Standard and Latin categories (Wikipedia). Founded on 1 September 1965 at the Palace of Culture of the Minsk Tractor Works by choreographer Max Kats and directed by Lidia Kats-Lazareva (DK MTZ official site; Professional Dance League of Belarus), it is the oldest such club in Belarus, opening its 60th season in 2025.
insight: Under Lidia Kats-Lazareva, Mara became a flagship of Belarusian DanceSport, reaching multiple World and European championship finals and organizing the 2004 World Latin Formation Championship in Minsk (Professional Dance League of Belarus).

sources.title: Kats-Lazareva Lidia Petrovna and Dance Formation 'Mara'

notes: NOT A DANCE - this is a dance organization (ballroom club/formation team), and it is BELARUSIAN, not Austrian. The 'Austria' label likely derives from the team's 2nd-place finish at the 2008 Austrian Open Championship. Should be reclassified as an organization or removed from a dance-styles catalog.

name: Čoček
era: Arose early 19th century from Ottoman military bands; modern Romani form across 19th-20th c.
tagline: Ottoman-rooted Romani brass-band dance of the Balkans - line and solo.
profile: Čoček is a musical genre and dance that emerged in the Balkans in the early 19th century and features prominently in the repertoire of Romani brass bands (Wikipedia). It originated from Ottoman military bands scattered across Serbia, Bulgaria, North Macedonia and Romania, preserved mostly by Roma communities and performed at weddings and banquets. It exists in two forms: a communal line/circle dance (in Macedonia often called 'oro'), and an improvised solo women's dance using hand gestures, pelvic and abdominal isolations and shoulder shakes (Carol Silverman, Romani Routes, 2012). The music is played by brass bands, clarinet, zurna and tapan in syncopated 2/4, 7/8 and 9/8 meters.
insight: Čoček is central to Romani identity and Balkan brass-band culture; ethnomusicologist Carol Silverman calls it the most characteristic Romani solo dance (Romani Routes, Oxford University Press, 2012). It anchors Serbia's Guča Trumpet Festival and the film music of Goran Bregović.

sources.title: Romani Routes: Cultural Politics and Balkan Music in Diaspora
Italy and Serbia: The Ivana Nikolic Collection

notes: Transnational Romani/Balkan genre; MK (North Macedonia) is the primary anchor, with Serbia, Bulgaria, Kosovo, Albania, Greece, Romania as context. Distinct from Ottoman/Turkish 'Köçek' (separate Wikipedia/Wikidata entity). Maps to Q2748579 which is unambiguously the Balkan dance.

name: Hunguhungu
era: Post-1797 Garifuna diaspora in Central America; rooted in St. Vincent culture (1635 ethnogenesis)
tagline: Women's swaying circular dance in triple time, secular echo of the sacred dügü.
profile: Hunguhungu (also 'hüngühüngü' or 'fedu') is a circular swaying dance performed chiefly by Garifuna women, distinctive for being in 3/4 (triple) meter rather than the usual duple-time Garifuna rhythms. Dancers form circles or lines, swaying their hips side to side while singing call-and-response, accompanied by the primero (treble) and segunda (bass) drums plus gourd shakers (sísira). It is described as the secular version of the adugurahani, the sacred drum/dance form of the dügü ancestor ceremony (English Wikipedia; Smithsonian Folkways).
insight: Hunguhungu is a vehicle for women's communal expression and social commentary, with lyrics addressing loss, current events and community concerns; it falls under the UNESCO 2001/2008 inscription of Garifuna language, dance and music (English Wikipedia; UNESCO).

sources.title: Traditional Music of the Garifuna (Black Carib) of Belize
Language, dance and music of the Garifuna

notes: Shared across the Garifuna diaspora (Honduras, Guatemala, Nicaragua). UNESCO coverage is via the broad 2001/2008 Garifuna inscription (ICH no. 00001), not a separate per-dance listing.

name: Sambai
era: Rooted in 18th-c. Afro-Belizean Maroon/Creole culture (Gales Point Manatee); UNESCO-inscribed Dec 2025
tagline: Afro-Creole full-moon fertility and courtship dance around a bonfire.
profile: The sambai (also 'sambay') is a Creole/Kriol fertility dance from the village of Gales Point Manatee, performed during the full moon and at weddings or birthdays. Per Wikipedia (citing O'Donnell, Adventure Guide to Belize, 2002), it is a solitary dance originally performed by men but now by either gender: the dancer enters a ring of drummers to dance, and when finished points to another person who dances next. The National Kriol Council describes a circle gathered around a pinewood fire; music is led by the goombay (gumbeh) drum with call-and-response chants.
insight: The National Kriol Council notes the sambai's link to the full-moon cycle parallels lunar crop cycles, reinforcing its identity as a fertility ritual. It was inscribed by UNESCO in December 2025 (20.COM) as part of 'Christmas Bram and Sambai' (ICH no. 02298) - Belize's second inscription after the 2008 Garifuna listing (UNESCO; National Kriol Council).
notes: ATTRIBUTION CONFLICT: catalog/task framed this as Garifuna, but authoritative sources (Wikipedia, National Kriol Council, the 2025 UNESCO inscription) identify it as an Afro-Belizean KRIOL/CREOLE tradition of Gales Point Manatee (a Maroon community). The Garifuna drum repertoire includes a 'sambai' rhythm, but the dance's home tradition is Creole. UNESCO status is via the SEPARATE Dec 2025 'Christmas Bram and Sambai' inscription (no. 02298), NOT the Garifuna inscription.

name: Wanaragua
era: Emerged 18th c. during slavery; costume/disguise origins tied to St. Vincent resistance (pre-1797)
tagline: Garifuna masked warrior dance of resistance, where the dancer leads the drum.
profile: Wanaragua (Garifuna for 'mask'; also 'Jankunu' or 'John Canoe') is a masked dance performed by men during the Christmas-New Year season, traveling house to house. Dancers wear pink/white wire masks with European features (satirizing colonial slave masters), tall crepe-paper crowns, mirrors and feathers, ribboned women's-style costumes, and cowrie/shell knee rattles, dancing to the primero and segunda drums while women sing as 'gayusas' (chorus). It is one of the few dances where the drummers follow the dancer's movements rather than the reverse (MyBelize.Net; UNICEF Belize).
insight: Wanaragua is the most culturally significant of Garifuna masked performances - an embodied performance of resistance and resilience commemorating ancestors (Constellations, F. Arzu Carmichael; Oliver N. Greene Jr.). It falls under the UNESCO 2001/2008 Garifuna inscription.

sources.title: Preserving Garifuna culture and engaging youth through a dance known as Wanaragua in Belize
Wanaragua: Resistance, Recognition and Resilience Among The Garinagu
Language, dance and music of the Garifuna

notes: NO dedicated English Wikipedia article or Wikidata item exists - the topic is covered under 'Junkanoo' (Q1612069, en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Junkanoo). wikipedia_url/wikidata_qid left blank per rules. The Garifuna Wanaragua is RELATED TO but DISTINCT FROM the pan-Caribbean Jonkonnu complex. Do NOT attach the UNESCO 'Junkanoo' inscription (RL/01988, 2023) - that is the BAHAMAS' element. Wanaragua's UNESCO coverage is via the broad 2001/2008 Garifuna inscription only.

name: Bossa Nova
tagline: Rio's samba-jazz fusion - a music genre, not a dance.
profile: Bossa nova is a Brazilian music genre that evolved in Rio de Janeiro in the late 1950s from a union of samba and cool jazz, associated with Joao Gilberto and Antonio Carlos (Tom) Jobim. Per English Wikipedia, unlike samba it has no dance steps to accompany it and did not become dance music; it is primarily a listening/song form (e.g. 'Garota de Ipanema'). Britannica defines it as music evolving from samba and cool jazz.
insight: Despite frequent listing among 'Brazilian dances,' bossa nova has no established choreography; Britannica defines it as a music genre evolving from samba and cool jazz.
notes: Primarily a MUSIC genre, not a partner dance - flagged.

name: Brazil
notes: NOT A DANCE - this is the COUNTRY of Brazil. Mislabel. Do not build a dance profile.

name: Brazilian Zouk
tagline: Sensual partner dance evolved from lambada, danced to zouk.
profile: Brazilian Zouk is a partner dance that began in Brazil in the early 1990s when lambada dancers, losing access to lambada music, adapted their movements to slower Caribbean zouk music (e.g. Kassav'). Structured for teaching at Jaime Aroxa's Rio school by Renata Pecanha and Adilio Porto, it features undulating upper-body movement, body isolations, tilted turns, dips and whip-like head movements. Main substyles: Traditional/Rio-style, Lambazouk (Porto Seguro), and Neo/Modern zouk (English Wikipedia).
insight: The name distinguishes the Brazilian dance from Caribbean zouk music; today 'zouk' in dance contexts usually means Brazilian Zouk, danced worldwide (English Wikipedia).

sources.title: What is Samba de Gafieira / Brazilian Zouk

name: Candomble
tagline: Afro-Brazilian religion whose orixa worship centers on ritual dance.
profile: Candomble is an African diasporic religion developed in Brazil in the 19th century, syncretizing Yoruba, Bantu and Gbe traditions with Roman Catholicism. It venerates orixas (spirits) under a creator god, Oludumare. A central ritual involves drumming on atabaque drums, singing and dancing in a circle to invite an orixa to possess an initiate; dances embody each orixa's characteristics. Practiced in autonomous terreiros, often led by priestesses (maes de santo), concentrated in Bahia (English Wikipedia).
insight: Candomble is primarily a RELIGION, not a dance; its dances are sacred trance-inducing rituals, not performance. Per IBGE's 2022 Census, Umbanda and Candomble together had nearly 1.9 million adherents (English Wikipedia; IBGE).

sources.title: 2022 Census religion data
sources.publisher: IBGE (Brazilian Institute of Geography and Statistics)

notes: Primarily a RELIGION; the ritual-dance dimension is described respectfully. Flagged.

name: Capoeira
era: Developed by enslaved Africans; first references early 19th century
tagline: Afro-Brazilian martial art-dance-game; UNESCO-inscribed 2014.
profile: Capoeira is an Afro-Brazilian cultural practice that is simultaneously a fight and a dance, played by two people inside a circle (roda) while others sing, clap and play instruments such as the berimbau and atabaque. Movements require great bodily dexterity. Born of resistance among enslaved Africans, it spread worldwide. The Capoeira circle (Roda de Capoeira) was inscribed on the UNESCO Representative List in 2014 (file 00892, decision 9.COM 10.8); IPHAN registered it nationally in 2008 (UNESCO; Agencia Brasil).
insight: UNESCO inscribed the capoeira circle on 25 November 2014; the nomination was Brazil's only submission that year (UNESCO).

sources.title: Capoeira to be recognized as cultural heritage of humanity

notes: UNESCO file 00892 (Capoeira circle), 2014, decision 9.COM 10.8.

name: Carimbo
tagline: Afro-Indigenous circle dance and drum music of Para, Amazon.
profile: Carimbo is a dance, drum and music genre originating in the 17th century in northern Brazil, centered on Para around Marajo Island and Belem. Named after the curimbo drum (Tupi for 'hollow stick that produces sound'), it blends African syncopation, Indigenous Amazonian circle dancing/maracas and Portuguese elements. The dance is loose and flirtatious: women in long rounded skirts spin while a man tries to retrieve a dropped handkerchief with his mouth. Registered as Intangible Cultural Heritage of Brazil by IPHAN on 9 November 2014 (English Wikipedia; IPHAN).
insight: IPHAN registered carimbo as national heritage on 9 November 2014; the 'King of Carimbo' Pinduca is among its most popular performers (English Wikipedia).
notes: National IPHAN heritage (2014), not UNESCO.

name: Charme
tagline: Rio's soul/R&B 'charme' dance scene of Madureira.
profile: Charme is a soul, funk and R&B dance scene that emerged in Rio de Janeiro, centered on the Baile Charme do Viaduto de Madureira (under the Viaduto Negrao de Lima), which began on 1 May 1990 out of the carnival bloco Pagodao de Madureira. Dancers perform choreographed steps to African-American soul, funk and R&B and Afro-Brazilian black music. The space was recognized as a Cultural Asset of Immaterial Nature of the City of Rio de Janeiro in 2013 (Agencia Brasil; Sesc RJ).
insight: DJ Corello popularized the term 'charme' in Rio's black-music bailes between the 1980s and 1990s; the Madureira baile marked its 35th anniversary in May 2025 (Agencia Brasil, 2025).
notes: Real dance scene; no dedicated English Wikipedia/Wikidata located - those fields left blank rather than guessed.

name: Coco
tagline: Afro-Brazilian Northeastern stomping song-dance (coco de roda).
profile: Coco is an African-influenced musical rhythm and dance from northeastern Brazil, especially Alagoas and Paraiba, performed in a circle (coco de roda) with call-and-response singing and stomping (often in wooden clogs). Instruments include ganza, surdo, pandeiro and triangle. Earliest cocos were work songs of Afro-Brazilians, including at Palmares, sung while breaking coconuts, and tied to communal house-building on clay floors (English Wikipedia; Encyclopedia.com).
insight: Coco is also called embolada and appears under many regional names (coco de ganza, coco de zambe); Encyclopedia.com traces its earliest forms to Afro-Brazilian work songs of Palmares.
notes: Wikidata Q502448 confirmed as the music/dance, NOT the coconut fruit.

name: D-Efeitos
profile: D-Efeitos is reported to be a Brazilian street-dance/hip-hop group from Sao Paulo (associated with 'Discipulos do Ritmo') that won the first season of SBT's talent show 'Qual e o Seu Talento?' (final aired December 2009), known for a glitch-hop style. It is a performing GROUP, not a documented Brazilian dance genre.
notes: NOT A DANCE STYLE - a street-dance performing group. Wikipedia URL/QID reported by a research subagent but not independently verified; left blank. Flagged.

name: Dancing Brasil
profile: Brazilian reality TV series in the Dancing with the Stars franchise, produced by Endemol Shine with BBC Worldwide, hosted by Xuxa Meneghel; premiered 3 April 2017 on RecordTV.
notes: NOT A DANCE - a television program (celebrity ballroom competition). Flagged.

name: Dancing Brasil Junior
profile: A year-end children's special of Dancing Brasil on RecordTV, hosted by Xuxa Meneghel; documented in Brazilian press but lacks its own Wikipedia article or Wikidata item.
notes: NOT A DANCE - TV program segment; no standalone encyclopedia entry. Flagged.

name: Danca dos Famosos
profile: Brazilian celebrity dance competition that premiered 20 November 2005 on TV Globo as a segment of Domingao do Faustao (host Fausto Silva); based on Strictly Come Dancing / the Dancing with the Stars franchise.
notes: NOT A DANCE - a television competition segment. Flagged.

name: Forro
tagline: Northeastern Brazilian couples dance and music super-genre.
profile: Forro is a Northeastern Brazilian couples dance and musical super-genre. Traditional 'pe-de-serra' forro is marked by zabumba, triangle and accordion (sanfona); couples dance face-to-face, dragging their feet (hence 'arrasta-pe'). Created in Pernambuco and popularized nationwide from the 1950s by Luiz Gonzaga, the 'Rei do Baiao.' It encompasses xote, xaxado, baiao and others (English Wikipedia; Agencia Brasil).
insight: NOT a UNESCO element. On 9 December 2021 IPHAN's heritage council registered the 'traditional matrices of forro' as Patrimonio Cultural do Brasil and classified it a musical super-genre (Agencia Brasil).

sources.title: Iphan declares forro Brazilian cultural heritage

notes: Forro is NOT UNESCO-inscribed - only national IPHAN heritage (2021). Verified carefully.

name: Frevo
tagline: Recife's frenetic carnival dance-music; UNESCO 2012.
profile: Frevo is a Brazilian music-and-dance form performed mainly during the Carnival of Recife, Pernambuco. Its fast, vigorous rhythm fuses marching music, Brazilian tango, polka, quadrille and classical pieces played by martial bands; the accompanying dance 'passo' draws on capoeira agility with over a hundred structured steps, high jumps and acrobatics, often with small colorful umbrellas. Inscribed on the UNESCO Representative List in 2012 (file 00603, 7.COM); IPHAN-registered 2007 (UNESCO; Paco do Frevo).
insight: UNESCO inscribed 'Frevo, performing arts of the Carnival of Recife' in 2012; National Frevo Day is 9 February (UNESCO).

sources.title: Frevo, performing arts of the Carnival of Recife

notes: UNESCO file 00603, 2012.

name: Lambada
tagline: Para-origin couples dance, a 1989 global sensation.
profile: Lambada is a partner dance (and music genre) originating in Para, northern Brazil, fusing carimbo with Caribbean and other rhythms. Danced in close embrace with pronounced hip movement and arched turns, often with short skirts and circular motion, it became a worldwide craze in 1989 via Kaoma's single 'Lambada' (English Wikipedia).
insight: Kaoma's 'Lambada' (1989) sold millions worldwide and topped charts in over 15 countries; per English Wikipedia it was the most successful European single in the history of CBS Records. Lambada is the direct parent of Brazilian Zouk.
notes: Dance QID Q533946; the 1989 Kaoma song is a separate item (Q701766), not used here.

name: Lambadao cuiabano
tagline: Fast peripheral couples lambadao of Cuiaba, Mato Grosso.
profile: Lambadao cuiabano (or simply lambadao) is a music-and-dance style characteristic of the Baixada Cuiabana in Mato Grosso, especially Cuiaba and Varzea Grande. A fast, swinging rhythm of the urban periphery with romantic or playful lyrics, it arrived via Para prospectors who brought lambada and synthesizes Para lambada with the local rasqueado heritage. Its first boom came in the 1990s (English Wikipedia; Prefeitura de Cuiaba).
insight: Often marginalized outside the periphery for its more sexualized versions, lambadao is nonetheless protected by municipal (Cuiaba) and Mato Grosso state cultural laws (Prefeitura de Cuiaba).
notes: Wikidata item not verified; left blank per rules.

name: Maculele
era: Documented in Santo Amaro by 1757; revived 1943
tagline: Afro-Brazilian stick/machete warrior dance of Bahia.
profile: Maculele is an Afro-Indigenous Brazilian folkloric dance from Santo Amaro, Bahia, simulating combat with pairs of sticks (grimas, ~50-60 cm of biriba wood) or, in daring versions, machetes that spark on impact. Performed in a roda to atabaque rhythms with agogo and call-and-response songs, often in Yoruba. Revived by Mestre Popo (Paulino Aluisio de Andrade) in 1943 and spread internationally by Emilia Biancardi's Viva Bahia group (English Wikipedia; Encyclopedia.com).
insight: Mestre Popo revived maculele in 1943 in Santo Amaro; it is now widely performed within capoeira schools worldwide (English Wikipedia).

name: Maculele
era: Documented in Santo Amaro by 1757; revived 1943
tagline: Afro-Brazilian stick/machete warrior dance of Bahia.
profile: Maculele is an Afro-Indigenous Brazilian folkloric dance from Santo Amaro, Bahia, simulating combat with pairs of sticks or machetes that spark on impact, performed in a roda to atabaque rhythms with call-and-response songs. Revived by Mestre Popo in 1943 (English Wikipedia; Encyclopedia.com). This is the same dance as the 'maculele' slug.
insight: Mestre Popo revived maculele in 1943 in Santo Amaro; it is now performed within capoeira schools worldwide (English Wikipedia).
notes: SAME dance as the 'maculele' slug (accented spelling). Both retained per instruction.

name: Malhao
tagline: Lively Portuguese folk couples dance from the Minho.
profile: Malhao is a lively Portuguese folk dance and song from northwestern Portugal, especially the Minho region, danced by couples in lines or circular formation with quick rhythmic footwork, accompanied by guitarra portuguesa and tambourine. Its name likely derives from an agricultural implement; the song tells of the 'malhao' (winnower), reflecting rural harvest-celebration origins. Performed at festivals such as Festa das Cruzes in Barcelos (Portugal.com; Society of Folk Dance Historians).
insight: Malhao has many local variants ('Malhao de Cinfaes,' 'Malhao de Agueda') recorded by fado singer Amalia Rodrigues; per folk-dance historians it has changed little in over a century.
notes: COUNTRY CORRECTED from Brazil to Portugal - Malhao is a Portuguese (Minho) folk dance. No dedicated English Wikipedia/Wikidata verified; left blank.

name: Maracatu
tagline: Pernambuco Afro-Brazilian procession; nacao and rural forms.
profile: Maracatu denotes several performance genres of Pernambuco. Maracatu nacao (de baque virado, 'turned-around beat') is an Afro-Brazilian processional genre of Recife/Olinda rooted in the coronations of the Reis do Congo, featuring a royal court (king, queen, dama do paco carrying the calunga doll), a percussion group of 80-100 (alfaias, gongue, tarol, ganzas) and Candomble/Jurema ties. Maracatu rural (de baque solto), from the sugarcane Zona da Mata, is Afro-Indigenous, centered on the caboclo de lanca (English Wikipedia; IPHAN).
insight: IPHAN registered Maracatu Nacao as Patrimonio Cultural do Brasil in 2014; the oldest continuously active rural group dates to the late 19th/early 20th century (English Wikipedia; IPHAN).
notes: Subtypes have separate QIDs (nacao Q108167943; rural Q10324896); Q1753071 used for the general entry. National IPHAN heritage, not UNESCO.

name: Puxada de Rede
tagline: Bahian fishermen's dramatic net-pulling dance honoring Iemanja.
profile: Puxada de rede ('pulling of the net') is a Brazilian folkloric theatrical dance from Bahia/the Northeast, often performed within capoeira shows. Based on a legend of a fisherman who drowns at sea and whose body is found in the net, it dramatizes the labor and grief of traditional xareu net-fishing and invokes Iemanja, goddess of the sea. Danced to a slow atabaque beat with songs and synchronized net-pulling movements; families sing and clap on the beach (English Wikipedia; Mandingueira).
insight: The tradition is rooted in the post-slavery period when freed people turned to artisanal fishing in Santo Amaro, Bahia; the artisanal practice has declined since the 1970s (English Wikipedia).

name: Rebita
tagline: Traditional Angolan couples ballroom dance, ancestor of semba.
profile: Rebita (also Massemba) is a traditional Angolan music and dance from Luanda in which a circle of couples, led by a 'commander' (Commandante) who calls directions, dance the umbigada-based Massemba movement, often to bands with concertina/accordion, horns and guitars. It contributed to the development of semba. Massemba was carried by enslaved Africans to Brazil, influencing umbigada, lundu and ultimately samba (English Wikipedia).
insight: Angola included Massemba/Rebita within a UNESCO ICH nomination process ('Semba: music and dance in Angola'); the umbigada belly-touch is the shared root linking Rebita to Brazilian samba (English Wikipedia; UNESCO).

sources.title: Semba: music and dance in Angola

notes: COUNTRY CORRECTED from Brazil to Angola. Connected to Brazil only via the diasporic umbigada lineage.

name: Roda
tagline: Ambiguous 'circle'; best-supported referent is Samba de Roda.
profile: 'Roda' means circle/wheel and is ambiguous: it can denote the capoeira roda or, most defensibly as a dance, Samba de Roda of the Reconcavo of Bahia - a music-dance-poetry tradition from 17th-century Bahia drawing on enslaved Africans' traditions plus Portuguese elements. Performers form a circle (roda); a soloist dances in the center using the umbigada (belly-bump) to invite a successor, with footwork, leg and hip movements, accompanied by atabaque, berimbau, pandeiro, chocalho, singing and clapping (UNESCO; English Wikipedia).
insight: Samba de Roda was proclaimed a UNESCO Masterpiece in 2005 and inscribed on the Representative List in 2008 (file 00101); it influenced the urban samba that became a Brazilian national symbol (UNESCO).

sources.title: Samba de Roda of the Reconcavo of Bahia

notes: Ambiguous slug resolved to Samba de Roda (UNESCO file 00101) as best-supported referent, noting the capoeira-roda alternative.

name: Samba
tagline: Brazil's iconic Afro-Brazilian dance and music.
profile: Samba is the iconic Afro-Brazilian music-and-dance genre. Its rural forms (notably samba de roda, Bahia) fed the urban 'samba carioca' of early-20th-century Rio de Janeiro, which became central to Carnival and a symbol of Brazilian national identity. Samba no pe is a solo footwork dance improvised to samba music; samba-enredo drives samba-school parades. Percussion includes surdo, tamborim, pandeiro, cuica and agogo (English Wikipedia).
insight: Folklorist Oneida Alvarenga and writer Mario de Andrade documented samba's rural roots; samba de roda was the first Brazilian musical genre IPHAN registered as national heritage (English Wikipedia).
notes: Q11403 is the umbrella samba item; the dedicated dance article currently redirects within Wikipedia.

name: Samba de Gafieira
tagline: Rio's elegant ballroom partner samba, the 'Brazilian tango'.
profile: Samba de Gafieira is a Brazilian ballroom partner dance (danca de salao) that emerged in 1940s Rio de Janeiro in working-class dance halls called gafieiras. Evolving from maxixe and later absorbing Argentine tango influence, it features the passo basico, saida lateral, leg adornments and (in performance) acrobatic figures. A unified teaching syllabus was agreed by Rio teachers in 2001 (English Wikipedia; Atomic Ballroom).
insight: Distinct from international ballroom Samba, it is sometimes called the 'Brazilian tango'; danced to samba, choro, pagode and other rhythms (English Wikipedia).

sources.title: Samba de Gafieira: The Tango of Brazil

name: Samba funk
profile: Samba funk is primarily a musical subgenre fusing samba with American funk, originating in the late 1960s with pianist Dom Salvador and Grupo Abolicao (precursor to Banda Black Rio). A related ballroom dance offshoot, 'samba-funkeado,' was created by dance teacher Jimmy de Oliveira, mixing samba de gafieira with funk. It is frequently conflated with samba-rock/samba-soul.
notes: Not a clearly distinct documented dance; primarily a music subgenre with a minor 'samba-funkeado' dance offshoot. Wikidata/Wikipedia for a distinct dance not verified; left blank. Flagged.

name: World Pump Festival
profile: World Pump Festival (WPF) is a competition/event series for the arcade rhythm game Pump It Up, run by Korean manufacturer Andamiro, with Speed and Freestyle categories and national qualifiers. The first WPF was held in Korea (2005); later editions moved to Mexico, China and Indonesia.
notes: NOT A DANCE and NOT Brazilian - an esports/rhythm-game (Pump It Up) festival run by Andamiro (South Korea). Country corrected. Flagged.

name: Buchimish
era: Traditional folk; taught internationally from the 1960s
tagline: Lightning-fast Thracian line dance in 15/16.
profile: A line/chain horo from Pazardzhik county in Western Thrace, central Bulgaria, in a complex 15/16 meter counted 2+2+2+2+3+2+2. Danced in an open circle with a front belt-hold (lesa na poyas), men typically at both ends with women in the middle. Styling is flat-footed, low and small-stepped with a constant knee-flex bounce; the figure moves side to side. Accompaniment ranges from a solo gaida (bagpipe) to a full folk band with tapan (Folk Dance Federation of California; Bulgarian folk dance, Wikipedia).
insight: Dick Crum speculated the name came from a now-lost song mentioning hemlock; today Bulgarians class it simply as a fast Thracian dance, introduced to North America by Dennis Boxell at the 1967 University of the Pacific Folk Dance Camp (Folk Dance Federation of California).

sources.title: Bucimis
sources.publisher: Folk Dance Federation of California, South

notes: No dedicated English Wikipedia article (appears only as a list entry in 'Bulgarian folk dance'); no Wikidata item located. wikipedia_url/wikidata_qid left blank per rules. 15/16 meter and Pazardzhik/Thrace origin corroborated.

name: Bulgarian folk dance
era: Pre-Christian/Thracian roots; systematically collected from the 19th century
tagline: The umbrella tradition of Bulgarian chain/line dancing.
profile: The broad category of Bulgarian folk dance, overwhelmingly built on the horo (хоро) chain/line dance plus the solo/couple rachenitsa. Defined by asymmetrical 'Bulgarian rhythms' (short=2, long=3 units) producing 5/8, 7/8, 9/8, 11/16, 13/16 and 15/16 meters across seven ethnographic regions. Handholds include the V (down), W (shoulder height), shoulder hold and belt-hold (na lesa). Instruments: gaida, gadulka, kaval, tambura and tapan. Danced at weddings, holidays and village gatherings (English Wikipedia; Britannica).
insight: Ethnomusicologist Timothy Rice's fieldwork found each generic dance name is tied to a unique repeating pattern of unequal short and long beats; the national repertoire is commonly cited at ~300 chain dances (Radio Bulgaria/BNR, quoting the Taratanci Foundation).
notes: UMBRELLA CATEGORY, not a single dance - flagged.

name: Calusari
era: Attested from 17th-century notations (Ioan Caianu); pre-Christian roots
tagline: Romanian all-male Whitsun healing/fertility ritual dance.
profile: An all-male ritual dance from the Olt/Oltenia region of southern Romania, performed at Whitsun/Pentecost (Rusalii). Performers in white tunics with bell-trimmed hats and ribbons execute acrobatic stamping, heel-clicking, leaping and leg-swinging, carrying ornate sticks/a sword and a flag, accompanied by two violins and an accordion. Led by a vataf (master) who initiates an odd-numbered, oath-bound brotherhood; thought to confer protection, healing and fertility. Spread to northern Bulgaria (kalushari) via Romanian/Vlach settlers (English Wikipedia; UNESCO).
insight: Inscribed on the UNESCO Representative List in 2008 (proclaimed a Masterpiece in 2005), it became Romania's first inscribed element of spiritual culture; Mircea Eliade noted its mythico-ritual scenario has nothing in common with Christianity (UNESCO; Wikipedia).
notes: COUNTRY CORRECTED from Bulgaria to Romania - the Calus ritual is primarily Romanian (UNESCO file RL/00090, 2005/2008). It also forms part of Vlach heritage in Bulgaria and Serbia.

name: Daychovo horo
era: Originated in North Bulgaria; popularized post-WWII
tagline: Northern Bulgarian 9/8 circle dance with a calling leader.
profile: A Bulgarian folk dance in nine-beat meter (9/16 or 9/8), the Daychovo-type rhythm being 2+2+2+3. Unusual in being a circle dance that nevertheless has a leader who calls figures/variations. Traditionally danced 'na prava' (straight line) by men grasping neighbors' belts in an X, or in an open mixed circle with V handhold. Originating in North Bulgaria, accompanied by gaida, gadulka, kaval, accordion and tapan (English Wikipedia; Folk Dance Federation of California).
insight: The name means 'Daycho's dance.' Per dance researcher Boris Conev (via Dick Crum), it traces to a 19th-century Pleven cattle merchant named Daycho whose downfall after 1878 inspired derisive tavern lyrics set to a lively 9/16 melody (Folk Dance Federation of California).

sources.title: Dajcovo Horo (Zizaj Nane)
sources.publisher: Folk Dance Federation of California, South

notes: Meter written as both 9/16 and 9/8; both denote the same 2+2+2+3 pattern.

name: Dunavsko Horo
tagline: Northern Bulgaria's beloved New Year's Danube line dance.
profile: A traditional chain/line dance from the Severnyashka (Northern/Danube) region, based on a simple 6-bar pravo horo in 2/4 marching time, with many variations and quick forward/back arm movements (basic pattern: five steps forward, three back). Set to the famous brass-band composition by Diko Iliev, written 18 April 1937 while he worked in the orchestra of the 36th Infantry Kozloduy Regiment at Oryahovo (Fort Collins Folk Dance; Radio Bulgaria/BNR).
insight: Per Radio Bulgaria/BNR (quoting Taratanci's Iva Todorova), it is 'the invisible spiritual thread that connects millions of Bulgarians' and, alongside Pravo Horo, a well-known chain-dance anthem; it is played every New Year's Eve after the national anthem (Fort Collins Folk Dance; BNR).

sources.title: 'Dunavsko Horo' chain dance: the invisible thread

notes: No dedicated English Wikipedia article (Bulgarian 'Дунавско хоро' exists); no English-linked Wikidata item confirmed. Fields left blank per rules. Distinguish the traditional dance from Diko Iliev's specific 1937 composition.

name: Graovsko Horo
tagline: Fast Shop-region men's horo in 2/4.
profile: A horo from the Graovo area (around Pernik) of the Shopluk region in west-central Bulgaria, performed in 2/4 time with the sharp, small, energetic Shop styling - light, agile, knee-flexed steps that look as if dancers barely touch the ground. Danced in short lines with belt or crossed-hand hold, often to gaida and tapan, punctuated by vigorous shouts. Its step pattern is shared with Kjustendilska Rachenitsa (the same pattern in 7/8) (Society of Folk Dance Historians; Tour4Fun).
insight: Graovsko unites many archetypal Shop steps and is among the region's signature dances, performed by the Philip Kutev National Folklore Ensemble (Society of Folk Dance Historians).
notes: No dedicated English Wikipedia article or Wikidata item located. Closely related to Kjustendilska Rachenitsa, differing mainly in meter (2/4 vs 7/8).

name: Kopanitsa
tagline: Lightning 11/16 line dance of western Bulgaria.
profile: A family of lively line dances from western Bulgaria (the Shop-Graovo region, also Thrace/Sredna Gora) in 11/16 meter, rhythm 2+2+3+2+2. Dancers form a curved line facing in, holding hands down or belts; many are 'called' dances where the leader on the right end signals pattern changes. Often very fast, with musicians speeding up to challenge the dancers. The name derives from kopam ('to dig/hoe') (English Wikipedia; Folkdance Footnotes).
insight: In northern Bulgaria the same 11/16 family is called gankino (after the girl's name Ganka); in western Thrace it is often krivo ('crooked'); related forms exist in Macedonia and Serbia (Kopacka) (English Wikipedia).
notes: Wikidata Q3816446 verified (subclass of Bulgarian horo, time signature 11/16).

name: Nestinarstvo
era: Probable Thracian fire-cult roots; first documented 1862
tagline: Strandzha barefoot fire-dancing rite for Sts Constantine & Helena.
profile: A barefoot firewalking ritual with ecstatic dance, the climax of the Panagyr on the feast of Saints Constantine and Helena (3-4 June, Orthodox calendar) in the village of Bulgari in the Strandzha region of south-east Bulgaria. After a morning procession with sacred icons to a holy spring (with drum and bagpipe), the nestinari enter a trance and tread a ring of glowing embers carrying the icons, led by the sacred drum, to ensure the community's health and fertility (UNESCO; English Wikipedia).
insight: Inscribed on the UNESCO Representative List in 2009 (decision 4.COM 13.05; file RL/00191); it survives authentically chiefly in Bulgari, with related Greek 'Anastenaria' carried by refugees from Eastern Thrace after the 1912-13 Balkan Wars (UNESCO).

sources.title: Nestinarstvo, messages from the past: the Panagyr of Saints Constantine and Helena in Bulgari

notes: UNESCO file RL/00191 (2009). Living religious rite - treated respectfully, not as spectacle. English Wikipedia covers it under 'Anastenaria' (no standalone 'Nestinarstvo' article). QID nuance: Q29083 = nestinarstvo; Q16149104 = Anastenaria (carries the UNESCO ICH ID).

name: Paydushko Horo
tagline: The pan-Balkan 'limping' dance in 5/8.
profile: A Balkan line dance in 5-beat meter divided 2+3 (quick-slow), particularly associated with Bulgaria and North Macedonia. Dancers face left holding hands; traditionally a men's dance, now often mixed. The basic footwork pairs 'step-hop' and 'step-step' patterns producing a characteristic limping gait. In southern Bulgaria, Macedonia and Greece the quick-slow is sometimes reversed to slow-quick (3+2) (English Wikipedia; Folkdance Footnotes).
insight: The name comes from Turkish paytak ('a person with uneven legs'); the dance is among the strongest candidates for a genuinely pan-Balkan village dance, appearing as Pajdusko, Payduska, Baidouska, Paidusca and Pajtushka across the region (English Wikipedia).
notes: Wikidata Q648545 verified (enwiki, mkwiki, trwiki sitelinks). Pan-Balkan spread noted.

name: Pravo Horo
tagline: Bulgaria's most basic 'straight' national line dance.
profile: The 'straight/right' horo, a simple village line dance in 2/4 or 6/8 (counted 3+3) with a three-measure pattern (quick-quick-slow-slow). Dancers form concentric curving lines facing in, using a hand hold or belt hold (na lesa); the basic step dives toward the center and travels counter-clockwise. Regional styles vary - smooth in Thrace, sharp with lifted knees in the Shop region, earthy in Dobrudzha (where it is called Opas). Considered the national dance of Bulgaria (English Wikipedia; Eliznik).
insight: In Greek Thrace the same dance is called Zonaradiko; it is a staple of weddings and feast days and can continue for hours, the in-and-out motion becoming hypnotic (English Wikipedia).
notes: Wikidata Q2108049 verified (noted as the same as Zonaradiko); disambiguated from other 'pravo' forms.

name: Rachenitsa
tagline: Iconic Bulgarian 7/8 dance, solo or couple, with handkerchief.
profile: The iconic Bulgarian dance in 7/8 or 7/16 meter, rhythm 2+2+3 (quick-quick-slow). Performed many ways - solo, in couples, trios, segregated, or in a horo line - most famously as a competitive solo/couple display in which a man and woman try to outdance each other, often waving a handkerchief (the name derives from raka, 'hand'). Widespread across all ethnographic regions, especially Thrace; accompanied by gaida, gadulka, kaval and tapan (Bulgarian folk dance, Wikipedia; Yves Moreau).
insight: French scientist Ami Boue described the handkerchief-holding couple dance in the early 19th century; choreographer Boris Tsonev called it 'the crown of all other folk dances' (Folk Dance Federation of California).

sources.title: Folk Dances of Bulgaria (Yves Moreau)
sources.publisher: Folk Dance Federation of California, South

notes: There is NO English Wikipedia article 'Ruchenitsa' (covered only as a section of 'Bulgarian folk dance'); wikipedia_url left blank. Wikidata Q1132484 ('racenica') verified for the dance, disambiguated from the 1894 Mrkvicka painting (Q38407848) and Rachenitsa Nunatak.

name: Sborenka
tagline: Dobrudzhan gathering dance in 2/4.
profile: A circle/line dance from the Dobrudzha region of northeastern Bulgaria, performed in 2/4 time. The name translates as 'together / a gathering.' Dobrudzhan style is moderate-tempo, danced with the body leaning slightly back, chest forward, knees bent, and prominent shoulder and arm movements that mimic agricultural work (sowing, harvesting, kneading bread) - Dobrudzha being 'the granary of Bulgaria.' Typically a mixed circle or semicircle; men also dance it as a distinct men's dance (Svet Dimitrov; Magic of Art).
insight: It is one of the signature dances of Dobrudzha, listed alongside Raka, Opas, Tropanka and the Dobrudzhanski ruchenik in regional repertoires (Svet Dimitrov).

sources.title: Bulgarian Folk Dances: The Ultimate Guide

notes: Well attested in regional folklore literature/recordings but lacks dedicated English Wikipedia/Wikidata coverage. Fields left blank per rules.

name: Cueca
era: Late 18th century to present; form consolidated by late 19th century
tagline: Chile's national handkerchief courtship dance.
profile: A couples courtship dance in which partners wave handkerchiefs and trace circular figures, turns and half-turns, evoking the courtship of a rooster and hen (Britannica; Wikipedia). Danced to guitar, accordion, guitarron, harp, tambourine and handclaps, in alternating 6/8-3/4 with hemiola; structured in cuarteta, seguidilla and remate. Central to the September Fiestas Patrias; widely held to descend from the Peruvian zamacueca. Declared Chile's national dance by Decree No. 23 (Diario Oficial, 6 November 1979) (Marca Chile; Wikipedia).
insight: The Pinochet dictatorship's official promotion of cueca from 1979 reshaped its imagery; in protest the cueca sola debuted on 8 March 1978 in Santiago by the folk ensemble of the Association of Families of the Detained-Disappeared (AFDD), per Chile's Museo de la Memoria y los Derechos Humanos.
notes: Origin disputed (zamacueca-derived, Afro-Andalusian, Mapuche theories); zamacueca lineage most accepted. Also danced in Bolivia, Peru, Argentina; the Mexican 'chilena' is a descendant.

name: Bambuco
tagline: Andean Colombia's courtly string-led national air.
profile: A couples courtship dance-song of the Colombian Andes (Huila, Tolima, Cundinamarca), in 6/8-3/4 sesquialtera, performed by string ensembles of tiple, bandola and guitar (Britannica; Colombia Country Brand). Dancers perform gentle, waltz-like flirtation using a handkerchief (raboegallo), with brief delicate contact. Celebrated at the Festival Folclorico y Reinado Nacional del Bambuco in Neiva (Britannica).
insight: Long described as Colombia's 'national air,' the bambuco genre has no standalone national-heritage statute; Ley 1026 of 2006 instead declared the Neiva festival cultural heritage of the nation (Ley 1026 de 2006, SUIN-Juriscol).
notes: National heritage attaches to the Neiva festival (Ley 1026/2006), not the genre itself.

name: Bullerengue
tagline: Afro-Colombian women-led sung drum dance.
profile: An Afro-Colombian baile cantado (sung dance) of the Caribbean coast (Bolivar, Cordoba, Sucre, Antioquia; San Basilio de Palenque area), led by elder women cantadoras in a call-and-response circle answered by chorus and handclaps (World Music Central; Universidad de Antioquia). Entirely percussive: tambor alegre (hembra), tambor llamador (macho), maracas; women dance in wide polleron skirts. Born in palenques (Maroon communities) as a vehicle of memory and resistance.
insight: George List made the oldest known field recording of bullerengue in Evitar, Bolivar, in 1964; the dance was declared Patrimonio Cultural Inmaterial de la Nacion by Ley 2520 of 4 August 2025, alongside mapale and son de negro (El Tiempo; Congreso de Colombia).
notes: Colombian national intangible heritage (Ley 2520/2025), not UNESCO. Shared Afro-diasporic tradition with Darien, Panama.

name: Cumbia
tagline: Colombia's Afro-Indigenous candle courtship dance.
profile: Folk dance-music of Colombia's Caribbean coast (Momposina region/Magdalena delta) blending African drums, Indigenous flutes and Spanish elements (Wikipedia; Colombia Country Brand). Danced in a circle around the musicians; the woman holds lit candles in her right hand and her skirt in the left, the man circles holding a sombrero vueltiao, partners not touching. Traditional ensemble: tambora, tambor alegre, llamador, gaita hembra/macho, flauta de millo, guache, maracas.
insight: 'Cumbia tradicional del Caribe colombiano' was approved by Colombia's Consejo Nacional de Patrimonio Cultural in 2022 and formalized by Resolucion DM No. 0321 de 2024 - an administrative LRPCI inscription, not a law of Congress (Radio Nacional; Semana).
notes: Colombian origin; many distinct national variants (Mexican, Peruvian, Argentine, Panamanian). Colombian national heritage (CNPC 2022), not UNESCO.

name: Mapale
tagline: Fast, frenetic Afro-Colombian drum dance.
profile: A fast, energetic Afro-Colombian dance of the Caribbean coast (Magdalena River banks, Cartagena, San Basilio de Palenque), named after the Cathorops mapale fish whose out-of-water movements inspired the steps (Wikipedia; Sounds and Colours). Driven by tambor alegre and tambora with handclaps and singing; emphasizes rapid hip and torso isolation and athletic improvisation. Originally a fishermen's night dance, later more sensual in character.
insight: Declared Patrimonio Cultural Inmaterial de la Nacion via Ley 2520 of 2025, together with bullerengue and son de negro (Infobae; Congreso de Colombia).
notes: African (largely Angolan) roots consistently cited. Colombian national heritage (Ley 2520/2025), not UNESCO.

name: Peruvian cumbia
tagline: Peru's psychedelic electric-guitar cumbia (chicha).
profile: A Peruvian subgenre of cumbia that became popular in coastal cities, mainly Lima, in the 1960s, fusing Colombian cumbia with Andean huayno, Cuban percussion and psychedelic/surf rock; built on electric guitars, bass, organ/synthesizer and pentatonic Andean harmonies (Wikipedia). 'Chicha' is named after a corn-based liquor; pioneers include Los Destellos, Los Mirlos and Juaneco y Su Combo.
insight: This entry was mislabeled as Colombian in the source catalog; Peruvian cumbia is a distinct Peruvian genre derived from - but not the same as - Colombian cumbia (Wikidata Q7171184).

sources.title: Chicha, the electric pulse of cumbia

notes: COUNTRY CORRECTED from Colombia to Peru - Peruvian cumbia (chicha) is a Peruvian genre derived from Colombian cumbia.

name: Porro
tagline: Caribbean brass-band dance music of the savannas.
profile: A festive couples dance-music of Colombia's Caribbean savannas (Cordoba, Sucre, Bolivar), in 2/2 'compas partido,' derived from traditional gaita ensembles meeting European-style wind/military bands (Wikipedia ES; ColombiaOne). Two folk types: porro palitiao (Sinu River/San Pelayo) and porro tapao/sabanero (El Carmen de Bolivar). Played by 'pelayera' brass bands; popularized in orchestral form by Lucho Bermudez and Pacho Galan.
insight: Ley 2259 de 2022 (19 July 2022), Art. 1: 'Reconozcase al Porro como manifestacion del Patrimonio Cultural Inmaterial y el Festival del Porro de San Pelayo como una accion de salvaguardia' (SUIN-Juriscol).

sources.title: Porro: The Heartbeat of Colombia's Caribbean Coast

notes: Colombian national heritage (Ley 2259/2022, dated 19 July 2022), not UNESCO. Often described as cumbia-derived.

name: Sanjuanero
tagline: Huila's festive bambuco courtship dance.
profile: A festive Colombian bambuco of Huila/Tolima tied to the San Pedro/San Juan festivities; the music was composed in 1936 by Anselmo Duran Plazas with lyrics by Sofia Gaitan Yanguas, first recorded in the 1950s by the duo Garzon y Collazos (Wikipedia). The traditional choreography, created by folklorist Ines Garcia de Duran in 1960, has three basic steps (caminado, bambuqueado, contradanza) and eight figures depicting courtship. Anthem of the Festival Folclorico y Reinado Nacional del Bambuco in Neiva.
insight: Though derived from bambuco with joropo influence, the Sanjuanero Huilense is widely considered Colombia's most representative folkloric dance and is the obligatory piece performed by contestants of the Reinado Nacional del Bambuco (Wikipedia; Gobernacion del Huila).
notes: Dedicated English Wikipedia article exists.

name: Currulao
tagline: Afro-Pacific marimba courtship dance.
profile: An Afro-descendant marimba dance-music of Colombia's South Pacific coast (Choco, Valle del Cauca, Cauca, Narino) and Ecuador's Esmeraldas, in 6/8 with interlocking polyrhythms and call-and-response vocals (Smithsonian Folklife; Music of Colombia, Wikipedia). The ensemble centers on the marimba de chonta, cununo drums, bombo and guasa shaker; a courtship dance with handkerchief play and ritual links to arrullos and alabaos.
insight: Currulao is integral to UNESCO's 'Marimba music, traditional chants and dances from the Colombia South Pacific region and Esmeraldas Province of Ecuador' (file 01099): inscribed via Decision 5.COM 6.8 (Nairobi, 2010) and extended on 2 December 2015 in Windhoek to include Ecuador (UNESCO ICH).

sources.title: Marimba music, traditional chants and dances (file 01099)

notes: No dedicated English Wikipedia article (covered within 'Music of Colombia'); wikipedia_url left blank. Wikidata Q5794983 resolves specifically to currulao. UNESCO file 01099 (2010 Colombia, 2015 extended to Ecuador).

name: Moreska
tagline: Korcula's Moorish-vs-Christian sword-battle dance-drama.
profile: A traditional sword dance from the town of Korcula on the Adriatic island of the same name (southern Dalmatia). Two groups of male dancers (Moreskanti) - historically Moors vs Christians, later the White King vs the Black King (Moro) - stage a mock battle in seven sword-clashing figures (kolapa) over a captured maiden, the Bula. Accompanied by brass-band music with a score reconstructed by local composer Krsto Odak. Performed on St. Theodore's Day (29 July) and weekly in summer; moreskanti are documented in a Korcula town journal of 1666 (English Wikipedia; Korcula Tourist Board).
insight: Korcula is the only place where the Moreska survives with each soldier wielding two real swords, the dance having vanished elsewhere in the Mediterranean by the 19th century (English Wikipedia).
notes: Protected as Croatian national intangible heritage; not on the UNESCO list.

name: Nijemo Kolo
tagline: Silent circle dance of the Dalmatian hinterland.
profile: A closed-circle couple dance from the Dalmatian hinterland (Dalmatinska zagora) of southern Croatia, performed essentially without musical accompaniment - the rhythm set by the dancers' steps. Male dancers lead female partners in vigorous, spontaneous, seemingly rule-free steps that publicly test the woman's skill; the lead is the kolovoda. It may be framed by ojkanje, rera or ganga singing or the diple (twin-reed shepherd's flute). Danced at fairs, weddings, carnivals and saint's-day celebrations (English Wikipedia; UNESCO).
insight: Inscribed on the UNESCO Representative List in 2011 (file 00359); its silence may have originated as a way for peasants to dance despite prohibitions, the rhythmic thumping of shoes itself a form of defiance (UNESCO).

sources.title: Nijemo Kolo, silent circle dance of the Dalmatian hinterland

notes: UNESCO Representative List 2011, file 00359.

name: Abakua
tagline: Afro-Cuban men's secret society with masked ireme dancers.
profile: Abakua is an all-male Afro-Cuban initiatory religious fraternity founded in 1836 in Regla, derived from the Ekpe ('leopard') societies of the Efik/Efut peoples of the Cross River basin (Calabar). Concentrated in Havana, Matanzas and Cardenas, organized into lodges (potencias). The masked ireme (naango, 'diablito') dancer represents an ancestral spirit, performing sharp, angular, staccato movements echoing the ekue/bonko drums; ceremonies (plantes) involve drumming, dancing and chanting in a secret Abakua language. Public appearances occur on the Day of the Three Kings (6 January) (English Wikipedia; The Appendix).
insight: Abakua is considered the only African-origin male initiatory society reconstituted in the Americas, and its rhythms profoundly influenced Cuban popular music, especially the rumba columbia (English Wikipedia).

sources.title: El Nanigo: A Spirit Dancer of Afro-Cuba

notes: Primarily a fraternal/religious society; sacred chants/ceremony kept secret by members - profile limited to publicly documented aspects.

name: Bolero
tagline: Cuba's slow romantic song-and-dance genre.
profile: The Cuban bolero is a slow, romantic vocal/song genre (also danced) that originated in Santiago de Cuba in the late 19th century - distinct from the older Spanish bolero in 3/4. 'Tristezas' by Jose 'Pepe' Sanchez (composed 1883) is regarded as the first bolero. It grew from the trova tradition of itinerant guitar-playing trovadores; in Cuba it is usually written in 2/4 with a son-related rhythm (bolero-son). It spread via the Trio Matamoros and Mexico's Trio Los Panchos across Latin America (English Wikipedia; UNESCO).
insight: The bolero was inscribed on the UNESCO Representative List in 2023 (file 01990) as a binational Cuba-Mexico element; UNESCO describes it as an indispensable part of the Latin American sentimental song (UNESCO).

sources.title: Bolero: identity, emotion and poetry turned into song

notes: Cuban bolero (music-genre item Q15830404) - distinct from Ravel's Bolero and the Spanish dance. UNESCO file 01990 (2023), binational Cuba-Mexico.

name: Casino
tagline: Cuban-style salsa, basis of Rueda de Casino.
profile: Casino (known since the 1970s as Cuban salsa / salsa cubana) is a partner dance developed in Havana in the early-to-mid 1950s, named after the casinos deportivos dance halls. A circular (rather than slotted) partner dance, it fuses Son Cubano footwork with figures from Cuban mambo, cha-cha-cha, rumba guaguanco and North American jive, traditionally danced contratiempo. Dancers improvise references to Afro-Cuban folklore (Orisha gestures, rumba). It is the basis of Rueda de Casino, a round dance with a caller and frequent partner changes (English Wikipedia).
insight: Casino's spontaneous integration of Afro-Cuban dance vocabulary (rumba, Orisha gestures) within a partner dance distinguishes it sharply from the slotted, linear North American salsa styles (English Wikipedia).
notes: English Wikipedia article 'Casino (dance)' is now titled 'Cuban salsa'; QID Q1744659 confirmed.

name: Cha Cha Cha
tagline: Playful Cuban dance born from the danzon-mambo.
profile: Cha-cha-cha is a Cuban dance and music genre introduced by composer/violinist Enrique Jorrin of the charanga Orquesta America in the early 1950s, developed from the danzon-mambo. Jorrin simplified the syncopation to make the music easier to dance; dancers at Havana's Silver Star Club improvised a triple step whose shuffling sound ('cha-cha-cha') named the genre. The first recordings, 'La enganadora' and 'Silver Star,' were recorded in March 1953 on Panart. The footwork resembles steps in Afro-Cuban Santeria dances. Played by charanga ensembles (English Wikipedia).
insight: After the success of 'La enganadora,' cha-cha-cha eclipsed both mambo and danzon as the most popular dance style in Cuba, and by 1955 the craze had spread internationally; it became one of the five International Latin ballroom dances (English Wikipedia).
notes: Separate items for the dance (Q208370) and music (Q16986699). Created by Enrique Jorrin c.1953.

name: Conga line
tagline: Carnival processional line dance from Cuba.
profile: The conga line is a novelty line dance derived from the Cuban carnival conga (comparsa) of Santiago de Cuba, where neighborhood comparsas march in serpentine processions (arrollando) descended from Afro-Cuban Congolese cabildos. Dancers form a line, each holding the waist of the person ahead, with three shuffle steps and a kick on the fourth beat, to a staccato conga-drum rhythm (plus the corneta china in Santiago). It became a US craze in the 1930s-1950s, popularized by Desi Arnaz and Xavier Cugat (English Wikipedia; Harvard ReVista).
insight: The conga was one of the earliest successful 20th-century Cuban musical exports, helping prepare international audiences for son, mambo and salsa - even as it was banned at times in Cuba as a dangerous mass gathering (English Wikipedia; Harvard ReVista).
notes: era field filled (was missing). Distinct from the 'Conga' disambiguation Q342357.

name: Cuban Rumba
tagline: Afro-Cuban secular drum-dance-song complex.
profile: Cuban rumba is a secular Afro-Cuban genre of music, dance and song that originated in urban Havana and Matanzas in the late 19th century, rooted in African (Abakua, Yuka/Congo) and Spanish-derived (coros de clave) traditions. The rumba complex comprises three forms: yambu (slow, couple), guaguanco (couple, featuring the vacunao pelvic thrust), and columbia (fast, solo, male, acrobatic, linked to Abakua). Performed with tumbadoras, lead quinto, claves, palitos/cata and call-and-response vocals (English Wikipedia; UNESCO).
insight: 'Rumba in Cuba' was inscribed on the UNESCO Representative List in 2016 (file 01185); UNESCO notes it historically acted as an expression of self-esteem and resistance (UNESCO).
notes: DUPLICATE relationship with slug 'rumba' (same Q388475). Distinct from Congolese rumba and ballroom rumba. UNESCO file 01185 (2016).

name: Danza (Cuba)
tagline: 19th-century Cuban contradanza-derived ballroom dance.
profile: The Cuban danza was an early-19th-century creole ballroom dance and music form - a derivative of the contradanza (contradanza criolla / danza criolla / habanera). The oldest known Cuban danza, 'El Sungambelo,' dates to 1813. Unlike the figure-based contradanza, the danza introduced the closed couple-hold (influenced by the waltz), with slower, more relaxed movements and the characteristic habanera rhythm. It is a direct ancestor of the danzon (1879, Miguel Failde) and thus of mambo and cha-cha-cha (English Wikipedia: Contradanza; Peter Manuel, CUNY).
insight: Per musicologist Argeliers Leon, the danza was musically near-identical to the contradanza but danced differently, marking the Cuban transition from communal sequence dances to independent couple dances (English Wikipedia: Contradanza).

sources.title: Cuba: From Contradanza to Danzon (Peter L. Manuel)

notes: No dedicated English Wikipedia article for the CUBAN danza (distinct from the Puerto-Rican 'Danza' Q2109360). Documented under 'Contradanza' (Q1133940). wikipedia_url/wikidata_qid left blank; era filled.

name: Guapacha
tagline: Cuban rhythm/style related to the cha-cha-cha.
profile: Guapacha (also 'wa-pa-cha') is a Cuban rhythm and social-dance style, a trendy variant of the guaracha/cha-cha-cha that peaked in the early 1960s. The Cuba Music Week genre database describes it as a trendy Cuban social dance based on the guaracha; on a Tito Rodriguez LP it was promoted as 'nothing more than the Cha Cha with clapping accompaniment.' In ballroom technique, 'guapacha timing' denotes a characteristic syncopation (delaying a step) applied within cha-cha-cha figures (Cuba Music Week; Wikibooks).
insight: Guapacha survives less as an independent genre than as 'guapacha timing,' a syncopated rhythmic device used by cha-cha-cha dancers; it is also associated with the singer Amado Borcela, nicknamed 'Guapacha' (Wikibooks).

sources.title: International Latin Technique / Cha Cha Cha

notes: English Wikipedia 'Guapacha' is a disambiguation page only; no Wikidata QID for the rhythm. Fields left blank per rules.

name: Guaracha
tagline: Fast, satirical Cuban song-dance genre.
profile: The guaracha is a Cuban genre of rapid tempo with comic, picaresque or satirical lyrics, documented since the late 18th/early 19th century. Played in musical theatres and working-class dance salons, it became integral to bufo comic theatre by the mid-19th century and was a favourite in Havana's brothels around 1900. Accompanied by guitar and tres (later by conjuntos and big bands), it uses soloist-coro call-and-response, combinations of 6/8 with 2/4, and slang-filled topical lyrics. It survives in trova, conjunto and salsa repertoires (English Wikipedia; Smithsonian).
insight: During the 19th-century independence struggle, the bufo theatre's guarachas served as a political weapon, lampooning Spanish authorities and supporting Cuban independence (English Wikipedia).
notes: Distinct from the modern Colombian electronic 'guaracha' (aleteo).

name: Mambo
tagline: Cuban dance/music from the danzon-mambo, popularized by Perez Prado.
profile: Mambo is a Cuban dance and music genre. The music traces to the danzon de nuevo ritmo (danzon-mambo) created in the late 1930s by Orestes Lopez and Israel 'Cachao' Lopez in Antonio Arcano's charanga; the term derives from their 1938 danzon 'Mambo.' The original Cuban/Mexican ballroom dance (faster, less rigid than danzon) replaced rhumba as the fashionable US Latin dance; Damaso Perez Prado popularized it internationally from Mexico in the 1950s. A later New York breaking-step form became 'mambo on 2' (closely tied to salsa) (English Wikipedia).
insight: The original Cuban mambo dance had no basic breaking steps - Cuban dancers described it as 'feeling the music' - and US dance teachers standardized it for the commercial ballroom market (English Wikipedia).
notes: Separate dance (Q333002) and music (Q1646666) items. The Lopez brothers/Cachao are credited as originators; Perez Prado popularized.

name: Rumba
tagline: The Cuban rumba (same as cuban-rumba; not ballroom rumba).
profile: This slug refers to the Cuban rumba - the secular Afro-Cuban drum/dance/song complex (yambu, guaguanco, columbia) of Havana and Matanzas. It must be distinguished from (a) the ballroom 'rumba'/'rhumba,' which actually derives from the Cuban bolero-son and is a separate International Latin dance, and (b) Congolese rumba. The plain English Wikipedia 'Rumba' page (Q6446572) is an umbrella overview of the many styles called rumba (English Wikipedia: Cuban rumba; Bolero).
insight: The ballroom 'rumba' is a misnomer dating to the early 1930s, when Cuban music (e.g. 'The Peanut Vendor,' actually a son-pregon) was marketed abroad under the single catch-all label 'rumba' (English Wikipedia: Bolero).
notes: DUPLICATE of slug 'cuban-rumba' (same Q388475). Distinct from ballroom rumba and Congolese rumba. UNESCO file 01185 (2016).

name: Son Cubano
tagline: Foundational Cuban son, ancestor of salsa.
profile: Son cubano is a genre of music and dance originating in the highlands of eastern Cuba (Oriente - Sierra Maestra, Guantanamo, Baracoa, Santiago) in the late 19th century, a syncretic blend of Spanish (vocal style, guitar/tres, lyrical structure) and African Bantu (percussion, call-and-response) elements. Precursors include changui, nengon and kiriba. The classic 1920s Havana sextet standardized the format: tres, guitar, bongo, claves, maracas and double bass, later adding a trumpet (septeto). Danced by a couple, contratiempo (English Wikipedia; MasterClass).
insight: Son is widely called 'the father of salsa'; by the 1960s in New York it fused with other Latin styles to become salsa, and within Cuba it evolved into songo and timba (English Wikipedia).
notes: Declared national intangible cultural heritage of Cuba (2012); national Day of Son observed 8 May. UNESCO Representative List, inscribed 10 December 2025 (20.COM, New Delhi), ref 02299. Sweep 5 patch.

name: Tumba Francesa
tagline: Afro-Haitian-Cuban dance-drumming of Oriente.
