profile: Tumba francesa ('French drum') is an Afro-Haitian-Cuban genre of dance, song and drumming brought to eastern Cuba (Oriente) by French colonists and their enslaved Haitians fleeing the Haitian Revolution. It fuses the kinetic vocabulary of French court ballroom dances with African drumming. Two main dance styles survive: the mason (a light parody of French ballroom dance) and the yuba (improvised, to rousing drum rhythms). Instruments include the cata idiophone, three single-headed tumbas, the tamborita and the chacha/maruga, with a compose leading call-and-response with female tumberas (English Wikipedia; UNESCO).
insight: Inscribed on the UNESCO Representative List in 2008 (file 00052), having originally been proclaimed a Masterpiece of the Oral and Intangible Heritage of Humanity in 2003 (UNESCO).
aliases: tumba francesa La Caridad de Oriente
notes: QID Q976802 is the genre item (distinct from the society item Q5551651). UNESCO file 00052 (Masterpiece 2003, RL 2008).

name: Yemaya dance
tagline: Orisha dance honoring Yemaya, goddess of the sea.
profile: The Yemaya dance is the Afro-Cuban orisha dance for Yemaya (Yemoja), the Yoruba/Lucumi orisha of the sea and motherhood (syncretized with Our Lady of Regla; color blue), within Santeria/Regla de Ocha ritual and its staged folkloric form. It is performed to bata-drum toques (Yemaya's flowing 6/8) with akpwon/coro call-and-response in Lucumi; the dancer's arms and skirt mimic crashing waves and ocean swells in sweeping, undulating, circular motions, sometimes imitating swimming or diving. Staged by ensembles such as the Conjunto Folklorico Nacional de Cuba (Hakai Magazine; Dancing the Orishas).
insight: As scholars note, while Yemaya is honored through dance within Santeria ceremony, Yemaya is not a dance in itself - it is an orisha, and the dance is one component of orisha worship and possession ritual (Hakai Magazine).

sources.title: The Goddess Who Moves Like the Waves
Dancing the Orishas: From Havana to Arcata

notes: No dedicated English Wikipedia entity for 'Yemaya dance'; covered under Santeria/orisha dance and the deity article 'Yemoja' (deity QID Q1477162). Fields left blank; sacred content treated respectfully.

name: Yuka
tagline: Cuban-Congo (Bantu) secular drum-dance, ancestor of rumba.
profile: Yuka is a secular Afro-Cuban tradition of drumming, singing and dancing developed in western Cuba (Matanzas, Pinar del Rio) by enslaved Kongo (Bantu) people during colonial times. The Bantu word yuka means 'to beat' and also names the three drums used (caja, mula, and the smallest). The secular couple dance is a stylized courtship contest - the man pursues, the woman avoids - featuring the vacunao pelvic thrust and movements imitating roosters and hens. Yuka predates rumba and gave rise to the yambu style; it survives in Kongo communities of Pinar del Rio (English Wikipedia).
insight: Yuka (with Abakua) is one of the principal African roots of Cuban rumba, and its drums are considered direct ancestors of the conga/tumbadora (English Wikipedia).
notes: Documented since the 1940s by Harold Courlander and Maria Teresa Linares.

name: Haiti
tagline: Likely mislabel - Haiti is a country, not a dance.
profile: This row is almost certainly a mis-scrape. 'Haiti' is a sovereign country, not a dance form, and does not belong as a dance entry under Cuba. It may have been intended to point to one of the Haitian-derived Cuban traditions of the Oriente region, most plausibly tumba francesa (a secular Afro-Haitian dance-song-drumming genre brought by Saint-Domingue refugees after the 1790s Haitian Revolution, concentrated in Santiago de Cuba and Guantanamo), or related forms such as tahona or gaga/vodu-cubano. No dance is simply called 'Haiti.'
insight: The most likely intended Haitian-Cuban tradition (tumba francesa) is itself UNESCO-inscribed (Masterpiece 2003 / Representative List 2008, file 00052), suggesting the catalog row should be re-pointed rather than deleted (UNESCO; Wikipedia).
notes: NOT A DANCE - 'Haiti' is a country. Likely mis-scrape. Recommend re-pointing to tumba francesa/tahona/gaga if a Haitian-Cuban tradition was intended. Do not invent a dance.

name: Juego de mani
era: Clearly by the 19th c. on Cuban sugar plantations; Kongo-Angola roots
tagline: Afro-Cuban combat dance, a Cuban cousin of capoeira.
profile: Juego de mani (also mani, baile de mani, or bambosa) is a combined martial art and dance developed by enslaved Africans in Cuba, with roots in Kongo-Angola (Central African) culture. Historically popular in Matanzas and Las Villas, it featured circling, competitive male dancing in a ring; practitioners are maniseros. The rhythm is set by drummers, using the music of Palo Monte. Techniques include low kicks, foot sweeps, punches, elbow and palm strikes; sticks and machetes were sometimes used. It influenced non-combative social dances such as rumba columbia (English Wikipedia; Capoeira History, citing Fernando Ortiz 1951).
insight: Scholars including Matthias Rohrig Assuncao note mani shares form and meaning with capoeira, supporting the view that African-derived combat games in the Americas shared common Kongo-Angola features (English Wikipedia).

sources.title: El Juego de Mani: Preserving the Hidden Warrior Art of Afro-Cuban Resistance

notes: 'Mani' means 'war' in an African language, not the Spanish for 'peanut.' Music tied to Palo Monte.

name: Sonderhoning
era: Oldest tunes from the 1700s; continuous ~300-year tradition
tagline: Maritime couple dance from Fano island.
profile: Sonderhoning is a traditional couple dance and melody type from the village of Sonderho on the island of Fano off Denmark's west coast. The dance is a pols (a three-step turning couple dance in a circle) and is notable because the dance is in 3/4 while the melodies are most often in 2/4. It has a walking/promenade part (gangtrin) and a turning part, danced with a special hold; music is traditionally played on the fiddle, the repertoire carried for five to six generations by the Sonnichsen and Brinch families. It is the social centerpiece of Sonderho festivities, danced in traditional Fano costume (Danish Wikipedia; Fonden Gamle Sonderho; Univ. of Toronto folk-dance archive).
insight: Per Fonden Gamle Sonderho, Sonderho is the only community in Denmark with a living music-and-dance tradition traceable ~300 years that never fell out of use; the foundation applied in 2018 for UNESCO listing, but it is not inscribed.

sources.title: The Sonderho music and dance tradition

notes: No dedicated English Wikipedia article (Danish article exists); wikipedia_url left blank. QID Q12338430 needs final confirmation. Applied for UNESCO 2018; not inscribed.

name: Bachata
era: 20th century; first single recorded 30 May 1962 (Jose Manuel Calderon)
tagline: Romantic Dominican guitar music and sensual partner dance.
profile: Bachata is a Dominican music-and-dance tradition that emerged in the 20th century from a fusion of rhythmic bolero with Afro-Antillean genres (Cuban son, cha-cha-cha, merengue). The dance is a couple's dance with sensual hip movement and a simple eight-step structure (side, side, tap/'pop'), in open or closed position. The typical group has five instruments: requinto (lead guitar), segunda (rhythm guitar), bass, bongos and guira. Lyrics express love and nostalgia; once stigmatized as 'musica de amargue' before globalizing via Juan Luis Guerra, Aventura and Romeo Santos (UNESCO; English Wikipedia).
insight: UNESCO inscribed 'Music and dance of Dominican Bachata' on the Representative List in 2019 (file 01514, decision 14.COM 10.b.10); whereas the merengue bid took 12 years, the bachata bid was expedited in two (Listin Diario via DR1.com, 2019; UNESCO).

sources.title: Music and dance of Dominican Bachata

notes: Two English articles: 'Bachata (dance)' (Q12414569, carries UNESCO RL/01514) and 'Bachata (music)' (Q40461). UNESCO file 01514 (2019) verified.

name: Gaga
era: 20th c. in DR (derived from Haitian Rara via sugar-labor migration)
tagline: Afro-Dominican Holy Week procession, the local form of Haitian Rara.
profile: Gaga (Haitian Rara, creolized to gaga in the DR) is an Afro-Dominican religious procession of music and dance performed during Semana Santa (Holy Week) in the sugar bateyes, especially the east/southeast (San Pedro de Macoris, La Romana) and border provinces. It blends Vodu Dominicano (Las 21 Divisiones), folk Catholicism and African elements. Bands parade led by a 'dueno,' with male dancers fronting, then female dancers, then musicians; processions stop at crossroads for rituals. Instruments include futubas/vaksin (bamboo trumpets), improvised tin trumpets, palos (drums), guiras, maracas, whistles and whips (Smithsonian Folkways; Univ. of Maryland dissertation).
insight: Ethnomusicologist June Rosenberg characterized Gaga as distinctively Dominican rather than Haitian even though it derives from Haitian Rara, reflecting how the batey context produced an independent Creole identity (Univ. of Maryland dissertation, Hernandez-Sang).

sources.title: Caribbean Revels: Haitian Rara and Dominican Gaga
Dominican Gaga from Outside and Within (dissertation)

notes: Living spiritual practice - treat respectfully. No confirmed dedicated English Wikipedia; 'Rara' (Q3419744) is the related Haitian article. wikipedia_url/qid left blank pending verification of a distinct Dominican Gaga QID.

name: Merengue
era: Mid-19th century to present; national symbol under Trujillo
tagline: The national music and partner dance of the Dominican Republic.
profile: Merengue is the national music and dance of the Dominican Republic, danced in pairs in a 2/4 rhythm with a steady marching/side-to-side basic step and characteristic hip motion from weight changes. The core conjunto tipico features the diatonic accordion, the tambora (two-headed drum) and the guira (metal scraper) - symbolizing European, African and Taino cultures. Flirtatious gestures accompany the dance; it pervades daily life from celebrations to political campaigning. On 26 November 2005 the DR president decreed a National Merengue Day (UNESCO; English Wikipedia).
insight: UNESCO inscribed 'Music and dance of the merengue in the Dominican Republic' in 2016 (file 01162), noting it attracts people of different social classes and promotes coexistence among communities (UNESCO).

sources.title: Music and dance of the merengue in the Dominican Republic

notes: Q282131 carries UNESCO ICH 01162 and links the English 'Merengue music' article. Distinct from 'Merengue (dance)' (Q4413211) and Venezuelan merengue (Q17079948). UNESCO file 01162 (2016).

name: Merengue tipico
era: Oldest style of merengue; dates to the 1840s-1850s
tagline: The oldest, accordion-led Cibao style of merengue.
profile: Merengue tipico (also merengue cibaeno, colloquially perico ripiao) is the oldest surviving style of merengue, originating in the rural Cibao around Santiago in the mid-19th century. Early ensembles used strings, but two-row diatonic button accordions replaced them when Germans arrived in the 1880s via the tobacco trade, joined by guira, tambora and (historically) the marimba box bass, later electric bass, saxophone and congas. It is fast (2/4), with rhythms including merengue derecho and pambiche, and a climactic improvisational jaleo/mambo section - the 'country music' of the DR (English Wikipedia; Smithsonian Folkways).
insight: Per ethnomusicologist Sydney Hutchinson and Smithsonian Folkways, a strong transnational merengue tipico scene has connected Santiago and New York City since the 1960s, reshaping the tradition while keeping it alive in the diaspora.

sources.title: Merengue Tipico from the Dominican Republic

notes: Covered under the broader 2016 merengue inscription but not separately inscribed. Navarrete believed to be the exact place of origin; perfected by Nico Lora (1880-1971).

name: Palo (Baile de palo)
era: Colonial roots (16th c.); continuous folk-religious practice
tagline: Afro-Dominican sacred drum-and-dance for saints and misterios.
profile: Palo (also atabales; baile de palos as the dance) is Afro-Dominican sacred drum-and-dance music with roots in the Kongo region of Central-West Africa, played at religious ceremonies coinciding with saints' days (velaciones), funerals and special occasions. Drums called palos are hollowed logs with cowhide heads: a master drum (palo mayor) with two or three slimmer alcahuetes, plus guira. Palos are tied to Dominican folk Catholicism and the cofradias, with a pantheon of misterios shared with Vodu Dominicano (Las 21 Divisiones). The baile de palos is a couple dance of 'respect,' traditionally danced unembraced; spirit possession may occur (English Wikipedia; Mediatheque Caraibe/Lameca).
insight: Ethnomusicologists Josue Santana and Edis Sanchez note the palos drums are considered sacred and are even 'baptized' before use - underscoring that this is a religious, not merely recreational, tradition.

sources.title: Palos Drumming of the Dominican Republic

notes: Distinguish carefully from Cuban Palo (Palo Monte/Mayombe, a Kongo religion). English 'Palo music' (Q24204889) is the Dominican atabales tradition. The related Congos of Villa Mella are separately UNESCO-inscribed. Treat respectfully.

name: Pripri
tagline: Afro-Dominican accordion-and-balsie dance of the south/east.
profile: Pri-pri (pripri) is an Afro-Dominican music-and-dance genre built on three instruments: the one-row diatonic accordion, the guira, and the balsie (a single-headed drum whose player alters pitch with foot pressure). Danced in couples, it is markedly distinct from Cibao merengue: its rhythm is often in 12/8 rather than 4/4. In Villa Mella it is connected to the festivities of the Holy Spirit and the funerary rites of the Cofradia del Espiritu Santo, as well as secular parties. Some researchers group carabine, mangulina and guarapo within a broader 'pri-pri complex' (Ojala.do; Hoy/Museo del Hombre Dominicano).
insight: Musician-researcher Tone Vicioso calls pri-pri 'una joya' and notes Marta Ellen Davis's finding that Trujillo rejected pri-pri as too lascivious, championing merengue instead as political propaganda - explaining why pri-pri remained marginal.

sources.title: El pri-pri: patrimonio ritmico y musical dominicano
Museo del Hombre reconoce al creador del baile Pri Pri

notes: Likely no dedicated English Wikipedia - fields left blank. Distinguish the pri-pri ensemble from the baile de pri-pri / merengue de palo echao.

name: Sarandunga
era: Colonial-era cofradia roots; annual feast of St John the Baptist (late June)
tagline: Bani's Afro-Dominican drum-dance devotion to St. John the Baptist.
profile: The Sarandunga is a three-part complex of Afro-Dominican music and dance performed by the cofradia (brotherhood) of San Juan Bautista in and near Bani for the saint's late-June feast. The ensemble is unusual: three small double-headed laced-goatskin drums plus one guira, mostly in lively 6/8 - unlike the long-drum palos of the wider region. Subgenres include capitana, jacana and bomba rhythms, plus the morano (sung in quatrains, played in procession and at the altar). The festival climaxes before dawn with a procession of banner, saint and drums to the Bani River, where St. John's red banner blesses the water. The dance follows the baile de palos pattern but the male role is far more virtuosic, twirling a red handkerchief (Bloomsbury Music and Sound; Lameca).
insight: Per the Lameca ethnographic study, the morano appears to represent a syncretism between the ancient Canary Island drum dance (tajaraste) and Central-African influences, and the community preserves Sarandunga without help from folklorists or outside cultural groups.

sources.title: Case study of an Afro-Dominican cofradia: the brotherhood of St. John the Baptist of Bani and its sarandunga

notes: Per oral history the saint's statue and tradition trace to Port-au-Prince, Haiti. No dedicated English Wikipedia - fields left blank.

name: Bomba del Chota
era: Colonial origins (late 16th-c. African slave labor); consolidated 19th-20th c.
tagline: Afro-Ecuadorian drum, song and dance of the Chota Valley.
profile: Bomba del Chota is the Afro-Ecuadorian music-and-dance tradition of the Chota-Mira valley on the Imbabura/Carchi border, in communities such as Carpuela, El Juncal, Chalguayacu and La Concepcion (English Wikipedia). The word 'bomba' names the goatskin drum, the genre, and the dance. Couples dance with marked hip movement; women are known for dancing while balancing a bottle on the head (the 'baile de la botella'). Music is built on quatrain coplas with guitar, guiro, requinto and the bomba drum, and a related ensemble, the banda mocha, uses improvised instruments such as dried gourd horns and leaves (Spanish Wikipedia; All Around This World).
insight: Distinct from Puerto Rican bomba, the Ecuadorian bomba was certified part of Ecuador's Intangible Cultural Heritage by the INPC and Ministry of Culture on 20 October 2020 as 'La Bomba, musica, danza y saberes ancestrales' - the 18th manifestation certified within a national inventory of 8,327 (INPC).

sources.title: 'La Bomba' es parte del Patrimonio Cultural Inmaterial del Ecuador

notes: English Wikipedia article is titled 'Bomba (Ecuador)'. Disambiguated from Puerto Rican bomba. National heritage (INPC, 20 Oct 2020). Coordinates approximate Chota Valley core communities.

name: Sanjuanito
era: Pre-Hispanic roots asserted; popularized from early 20th c. (origins contested)
tagline: Festive Andean 2/4 dance-genre, an Ecuadorian national music.
profile: Sanjuanito is an Indigenous and mestizo festive dance and music genre of the Ecuadorian Sierra, originating in Imbabura Province and associated with (though not exclusive to) the Otavalo people (English Wikipedia). Written in 2/4 with pentatonic melodies in minor mode, it is tied to the Fiestas de San Juan (24 June), coinciding with Inti Raymi. Indigenous versions feature dancers circling central musicians playing two flutes and a bombo; mestizo and Pan-Andean versions add guitar, rondador, bandolin, violin, accordion and quena (EcuadorUniversitario; La Hora).
insight: Considered an Ecuadorian national rhythm, it should not be confused with the Colombian 'sanjuanero,' a distinct bambuco song from Huila (Q7418595). Ecuador's FUNPAE has petitioned the Ministry of Culture and INPC for national heritage status (English Wikipedia; La Hora).
notes: Disambiguated from Colombian sanjuanero (Q7418595). Coordinates for Otavalo. Origins contested (Moreno: autochthonous Imbabura; d'Harcourt: huayno-derived). No confirmed national declaration as of mid-2026 (FUNPAE petition pending).

name: Yumbada
era: Pre-Hispanic origins; present Cotocollao ritual revived ~20 years ago
tagline: Indigenous ritual dance-drama of forest 'yumbos' near Quito.
profile: The Yumbada is an Indigenous (Kichwa) and mestizo ritual dance-drama performed in neighborhoods of Quito, most prominently Cotocollao and La Magdalena, each June around Corpus Christi and the solstice (Casa de la Cultura Ecuatoriana). Dancers personify 'yumbos,' shaman figures associated with the Amazonian/montane forest, led by a 'mamaco' or pingullero who plays a small drum and a three-note pingullo flute. The ritual climax is the 'Yumbo Huanuchiy' (Killing of the Yumbo), a dramatized murder and resurrection linked to healing, fertility and the mythic alliance of the Quitu and Cara peoples (Frank Salomon, IFEA; El Telegrafo). Dancers carry black chonta-palm lances and wear long wigs, ribbons, plumes and masks; it is treated as a sacred rite rather than folkloric spectacle.
insight: Anthropologist Frank Salomon documented the Yumbada as a ritual drama; four yumbadas coexist today in Quito's Zona La Delicia (Rumicucho, Pomasqui, Tanlahua, Cotocollao), and the Cotocollao community pursued, then withdrew from, formal national heritage declaration, fearing folklorization (Salomon, IFEA; Quito Informa).

sources.title: La 'Yumbada': un drama ritual quichua en Quito (Frank Salomon)
sources.publisher: Institut francais d'etudes andines / OpenEdition Books

sources.title: La Yumbada de Cotocollao, una danza de origen prehispanico

notes: Treated respectfully as a living sacred ritual. NO dedicated English Wikipedia article and no Yumbada-specific Wikidata QID (distinct from the Yumbo people/genre) - both flagged. Coordinates for Cotocollao, northern Quito. Primary source: Frank Salomon, 'Killing the Yumbo' (1981).

name: Estonian folk dance
era: Ancient circle/chain dances; systematized as 'rahvatants' early 20th c.; Dance Celebration from 1934
tagline: Estonia's umbrella folk-dance tradition, crowned by the Dance Celebration.
profile: Rahvatants is the broad umbrella of Estonian folk dance, encompassing ancient circle and chain dances, mimicry dances, set-dances, and couple dances such as the labajalavalss (flat-foot waltz, danced without rising onto the toes), kaerajaan, and tuljak (English Wikipedia; Kapper). Generally calm in character yet noted for a wide diversity of basic steps, it is typically accompanied by accordion, fiddle and other folk instruments. It is socially central to Estonian identity and concentrated in the Estonian Dance Celebration (Tantsupidu), first held in 1934 as the Dance and Gymnastic Festival within the first Estonian Games, now staged with the Song Celebration; UNESCO notes the tradition culminates in large-scale festivals every fifth year (English Wikipedia; UNESCO).
insight: Anna Raudkats's 1926 publication 'Eesti rahvatantsud' entrenched the idea that folk dances were old-fashioned village dances; the three most generally known - labajalavalss, kaerajaan and tuljak - are repeatedly cited as the canonical examples (Kapper, Folklore 54).

sources.title: Estonian Folk Dance: Terms and Concepts in Theory and Practice

notes: UMBRELLA CATEGORY - flagged. UNESCO link is INDIRECT: the Estonian Song and Dance Celebration is part of the inscribed 'Baltic song and dance celebrations' (RL 2008/proclaimed 2003); the inscription covers the CELEBRATIONS, not Estonian folk dance generally. English Wikipedia article carries multiple-issues tags - broad claims treated cautiously.

name: Kaerajaan
era: Origin tied to an 1889 mocking song from Laaniste/Ahja; documented as a dance by Anna Raudkats in the 1910s-1920s
tagline: Estonia's best-known folk dance, born from a teasing song.
profile: Kaerajaan (Kaera-Jaan, 'Oats-Jaan / John of Oats') is a lively couple/mixer folk dance from southeastern Estonia, linked musically and structurally to the European quadrille (Univ. of Tartu; Kapper). Its name comes from a teasing song about Jaan Matson, a blacksmith's apprentice at Ahja manor known as a ladies' man; the lyric begins 'Kaera-Jaan, Kaera-Jaan...' (Tartu 2024; Tallinn University). Typically accompanied by accordion and sung melody, it features prancing steps and partner figures and is among the most widely performed dances at Estonian celebrations and abroad; a popular stage arrangement was choreographed by Mait Agu.
insight: Per Tartu 2024 (European Capital of Culture), the Laaniste blacksmith Jaan had no idea he inspired Estonia's best-known folk dance, still popular 135 years later; once thought purely original, kaerajaan was later recognized as linked to the international quadrille tradition (Tartu 2024; Kapper, Folklore 54).
notes: No dedicated English Wikipedia (Estonian, Voro, Latvian articles exist); wikipedia_url left blank. Performed within the UNESCO-inscribed celebrations but not individually inscribed.

name: Tuljak
era: Music by Miina Harma 1897; dance by Anna Raudkats; at song celebrations from 1928, every Dance Celebration since 1934
tagline: Estonia's celebratory wedding dance and Dance Celebration finale.
profile: Tuljak is a popular Estonian couple dance of wedding-celebration character, telling the courting and wedding story of Tonn and Manni (All Around This World; Estonian Wikipedia). The choral song 'Tuljak' was composed by Miina Harma in 1897 as an arrangement of several folk melodies, with lyrics by Karl Ferdinand Karlson; Anna Raudkats created the accompanying dance, which draws on the Viru waltz and older dances (Estonian Music Information Centre; Miina Harma Gumnaasium). The traditional dance-tale of courtship was traced to Slavonic dances; the choreographed Tuljak has been the grand finale of every Estonian Dance Celebration, accompanied by choir and orchestra.
insight: 'Tuljak' has been played at every dance and song celebration since 1934 and is so emblematic that Estonians compare it to the anthem-like 'Mu isamaa on minu arm' (ERR).
notes: CORRECTION: music is by Miina Harma (1897), NOT Cyrillus Kreek. No dedicated English Wikipedia; wikipedia_url left blank. QID Q57303018 represents the Raudkats choreographed dance 'based on Tuljak.' Finale of the UNESCO-inscribed Dance Celebration but not separately inscribed.

name: Katrilli
era: Quadrille forms reached Finland late 18th c.; widespread early/mid-19th c.; Karelian/Skolt forms from Russia late 19th c.
tagline: Finland's quadrille - a square or two-line figure set-dance.
profile: Katrilli is the Finnish quadrille, a contra/set dance performed in a square (four couples) or two opposing lines, derived from the French cotillion/contredanse and the broader quadrille family (Finnish Wikipedia; Kansantanssin Ystavat). Its turns (vuorot) combine standard figures - circles, vis-a-vis (vastuu), place-changes, pass-throughs (lapikaynti) and karkelo. Two waves reached Finland: a Swedish-derived 'ruotsinkatrilli' across most of the country and a Russian-derived form in Finnish/Russian Karelia and among the Skolt Sami. Steps range from walking and half-run to polka and waltz; Karelian katrillis often feature a male improvisation section (ripaska). Music is led by fiddle, accordion and harmonium.
insight: Among Finnish dances recorded by collectors, katrillis are one of the most common types; since the 1980s the improvised 'huutokatrilli' (called/shouted quadrille) has become a late-night highlight at the Kaustinen Folk Music Festival (Finnish Wikipedia; kaustinen.net).

sources.title: Katrilli
sources.publisher: Suomalaisen Kansantanssin Ystavat (Friends of Finnish Folk Dance)

notes: No dedicated English Wikipedia (Finnish article exists). The generic 'quadrille' item Q394084 carries the Finnish label 'katrilli' but represents the international quadrille - left blank to avoid mismatch.

name: Purpuri
era: Arrived from the east early 19th c. (Oulu 1809, Helsinki 1820s); wedding centerpiece into the 1950s-60s; revived from early 1980s
tagline: Finland's potpourri wedding suite of sequential figure dances.
profile: Purpuri ('potpourri') is a suite of figure dances performed one after another, historically the principal ceremonial and social wedding dance - especially in Swedish-speaking Ostrobothnia (Kokkola, Kronoby, Nedervetil, Terjarv), western Uusimaa, Aboland and around Kaustinen (Wiki for Living Heritage; Finnish Music Quarterly). At three-day weddings, the 'rospurpuri' opened with a long polonaise ('rosa') danced by bridesmaids and groomsmen, paired by the bridal couple; later purpuris were public. It is accompanied by fiddle and by horn ensembles (e.g. Kronoby Hornkapell) when danced outdoors; the constituent dances draw on the quadrille, minuet, polska, polka and waltz.
insight: The purpuri was so central that the seminal Kaustinen ensemble formed in 1946 and later led by Konsta Jylha (1910-1984) was named 'Purppuripelimannit'; tradition-bearers' later adoption of national costume for staged purpuri performances entwined the dance with folk-costume display (Wiki for Living Heritage; FMQ).

sources.title: The 'purpuri' dance in the Kokkola area
sources.publisher: Wiki for Living Heritage (Finnish Heritage Agency)

notes: No dedicated English Wikipedia; QID Q118288522 verified. 'Suomalainen purpuri' is on Finland's national Wiki-inventory of Living Heritage, NOT the UNESCO list.

name: Skolt Sami quadrille
era: Adopted from Russia/Karelia in the later 19th c.; very popular until WWII; danced to ~1950s; revitalized from the 1970s
tagline: The Skolt Sami's emblematic set dance of Russian-Karelian origin.
profile: The Skolt Sami quadrille (kolttakatrilli) is a square/set dance of the Skolt Sami, an Orthodox Christian Sami group originally from the Petsamo region (Suonikyla) and resettled after WWII around Sevettijarvi in Inari, NE Finland (Hoppu; kolttasaamelaiset.fi). Sharing much with Karelian and Northern Russian dance culture, it reached the Skolts in the latter 19th century and was historically danced at weddings and festivities to the accordion, harmonica or singing (Hoppu; Yle). Its figures resemble the Karelian katrilli - circles, vis-a-vis, place-changes - and may include a male improvisation showing skill (ripaska). After the evacuation, the community scattered along the Sevettijarvi road, the accordions were left behind in Petsamo, and the dance nearly fell into oblivion before its revival.
insight: Petri Hoppu writes that the quadrille is the best known of the Skolts' dances and, perhaps more than any other, has become emblematic of their ethnic identity; from the 1970s the Skolts began to actively revitalize their dances, establishing a quadrille performing group in Sevettijarvi as part of a Sami cultural renaissance (Hoppu, 'Rewriting Skolt Sami Dance History').

sources.title: Rewriting Skolt Sami Dance History (Petri Hoppu)
sources.publisher: Congress on Research in Dance Conference Proceedings (Cambridge University Press)

notes: Treated respectfully as living Indigenous heritage. NO dedicated English Wikipedia and no dedicated Wikidata item (only generic 'quadrille' Q394084 and Skolt people/language items) - both flagged. The Skolt leu'dd sung tradition is separate from this dance.

name: French Cancan
tagline: High-kicking Parisian music-hall dance of the Moulin Rouge.
profile: The can-can (cancan) is a high-energy music-hall dance that emerged in working-class Parisian dance halls; per Britannica it was popular in Parisian dance halls in the 1830s and appeared in variety shows and revues from the 1840s, in lively 2/4 time danced at first to quadrille or galop music. It evolved from the final figure of the quadrille and the galop. Signature moves are the high kick (battement), rond de jambe, port d'armes, cartwheel and grand ecart (jump splits), with vigorous skirt-and-petticoat work. Choreographer Pierre Sandrini formalized the staged 'French Cancan' at the Moulin Rouge in the 1920s and his Bal Tabarin from 1928 (Wikipedia; Britannica).
insight: The tune most associated with it is Offenbach's 'Infernal Galop' (1858, from 'Orphee aux Enfers'); per Wikipedia it was never officially banned, despite the legend (Wikipedia).
notes: 'French Cancan' is the staged chorus-line form; the working-class origin is the chahut.

name: Farandole
era: Name attested from the 18th c.; practiced earlier
tagline: Open-chain community dance of Provence led by a leader.
profile: The farandole is an open-chain community dance of Provence in 6/8 time, in which dancers join hands (or link by handkerchief/ribbon) in a long line led by a leader who waves a flag and guides the chain through spirals, serpentines and arches (Wikipedia; Britannica). It is accompanied by the galoubet (a three-holed pipe) and the tambourin (a long Provencal tabor), both played by a single tambourinaire. Danced at major Provencal feasts (Corpus Christi; Tarascon's Tarasque festival) and embedded in the Provencal Christmas creche tradition. It resembles the gavotte, jig and tarantella; the Revolutionary carmagnole is a derivative.
insight: Bizet used a Provencal farandole tune in the finale of his 'L'Arlesienne' Suite No. 2 (arr. Guiraud, 1879), helping popularize the form in classical music (Encyclopedia.com / Concise Oxford Dictionary of Music).
notes: Wikidata locates it in the County of Nice; the tradition is pan-Provencal (and Catalan).

name: Bourree
era: First attested as a popular dance 1665 (Clermont-Ferrand); Baroque court form mid-17th-18th c.
tagline: Lively French folk dance of Auvergne; also a Baroque suite movement.
profile: The bourree is a French dance originating in Auvergne in the Massif Central, sometimes called the 'French clog dance,' first mentioned as a popular dance in 1665 at Clermont-Ferrand (Wikipedia; Britannica). Folk forms divide into the triple-time bourree (Montagnarde - Puy-de-Dome, Haute-Loire, Cantal, Limousin) and the duple-time line bourree (Berry, Bourbonnais, Nivernais). The step glides along the ground with stamped accents and little or no physical contact between partners - connection is through the eyes - danced in couples, quadrettes, lines or circles. Traditionally accompanied by the cabrette (Auvergnat bagpipe), vielle a roue (hurdy-gurdy), and later accordion and fiddle. As a Baroque form, Bach, Handel and Chopin wrote bourrees, giving its name to the ballet step pas de bourree (Wikipedia FR; Britannica).
insight: The folk dance and the Baroque/court form share a name and duple-time character, but no early dance notation survives to prove a direct line between the peasant dance and the courtly concert dance (Wikipedia).
notes: Dance-vs-music-form: Wikidata Q19721283 is the dance item (covering both senses).

name: Gavotte
era: Attested 16th c.; court favorite under Louis XIV; Breton folk form attested late 18th c.
tagline: French dance - both a Breton folk dance and a Baroque court dance.
profile: The gavotte is a French dance in 4/4 or 2/2 of moderate tempo, taking its name from the Gavot people of the Pays de Gap (Dauphine), per one tradition; another holds the name is generic, most likely originating in Lower Brittany (Wikipedia). As a Renaissance/Baroque dance it derived from the double branle, became popular at the court of Louis XIV (Lully, Rameau, Bach), and sits among the optional galanteries of the suite, typically with a half-bar upbeat. The Breton gavotte (gavotte bretonne / dans tro) is a major family of chain and round dances of Lower Brittany with many terroir variants - gavotte de l'Aven, gavotte des montagnes, dans fisel, gavotte glazik - danced in fest-noz to biniou-and-bombarde or kan-ha-diskan singing (Wikipedia; Wikipedia FR).
insight: French scholarship notes the Breton 'gavotte' label is a misnomer applied by outsiders: the Breton dans tro probably descends from 16th-c. trihoris rather than the French Renaissance branles, so the courtly gavotte and the Breton dance are largely unrelated traditions sharing a borrowed name (Wikipedia FR, Dans tro).
notes: Q215742 covers both senses; the Breton folk gavotte is practiced within the UNESCO-listed fest-noz, and is sometimes catalogued separately under dans tro.

name: Musette (bal-musette)
era: From the 1880s in Paris; peak to 1945; decline ~1960; revival since
tagline: Parisian accordion dance-hall scene (valse-musette, java).
profile: Bal-musette is a style of French popular music and dance that became popular in Paris from the 1880s, centered on the accordion and the working-class dance halls of the Bastille (rue de Lappe) and the guinguettes (Wikipedia). Auvergnat immigrants first danced the bourree to the cabrette bagpipe (locally 'musette') and hurdy-gurdy; Italian immigrant accordionists later transformed the scene, the accordion displacing the bagpipe. Its repertoire of close-embrace 'musette-form' dances includes the valse-musette (with the toupie variation), tango-musette, paso-musette, polka, mazurka and the java. Performers include Antoine Bouscatel, Emile Vacher, Charles Peguri and Gus Viseur; the 1930s saw cross-pollination with Django Reinhardt's gypsy jazz.
insight: More a scene/style than a single dance - by 1945 it was the most popular style of dance in France before declining sharply around 1960 (Wikipedia).
notes: Maps to 'Bal-musette' (style/scene), Q7770873. Wikidata Q11786764 ('musette') is the separate music form.

name: Java
era: Developed early 20th c.; peak in bal-musette c.1910-1960
tagline: Fast, close Parisian bal-musette waltz of the early 20th century.
profile: The java is a dance developed in France in the early 20th century, probably evolving from the valse (waltz), mainly performed in bal-musette halls between roughly 1910 and 1960 (Wikipedia). It arose from demand for an easier, faster, more sensual waltz that did not require a large hall - couples dance very close, taking small advancing steps in 3/4 time. Its provocative close hold led some 'respectable' bals-musette to ban it. It is played on the musette accordion; the name's origin is uncertain (one folk etymology links it to the Auvergnat pronunciation of 'Ca va?'). A java features in a cafe scene in Jean Vigo's film 'L'Atalante' (1934).
insight: The java is the one wholly original dance to emerge from the bal-musette scene rather than an adaptation of an existing ballroom dance (Wikipedia, Bal-musette).
notes: DOES have a dedicated English Wikipedia article (contrary to the catalog's 'may lack').

name: An Dro
era: Traditional; revived from the 1950s in fest-noz
tagline: Breton round/chain dance from the Vannetais, pinky-linked.
profile: An dro (Breton 'the turn') is a Breton folk dance in 4/4, a form of circle/chain dance originating in the pays Vannetais of southern Lower Brittany (Wikipedia). Dancers form a long open line (or couples in cortege, then called kas a-barh), joined by hooking their little fingers and swinging the arms; the step takes a short move right and a longer move left so the chain drifts steadily leftward, the leader at the left end drawing spirals. An dro is considered an 'arm dance' (vs. the 'feet dances' gavotte/hanter-dro). It is sung (call-and-response) or played on biniou/bombarde, accordion and other fest-noz instruments. An dro tunes are central to the Breton repertoire from Alan Stivell and Tri Yann to the rock band Matmatah.
insight: One of the most widely known Breton dances outside Brittany, performed within the UNESCO-inscribed fest-noz tradition (Wikipedia; UNESCO).
notes: Practiced within the UNESCO-inscribed Fest-noz (RL 2012, file 00707).

name: Hanter Dro
era: Traditional (declined mid-19th c.; revived in modern fest-noz)
tagline: Breton Vannetais chain dance, the 'half-turn,' paired with an dro.
profile: Hanter-dro (Breton, literally 'half-turn') is a Breton chain dance from the pays Vannetais (Wikipedia FR; folk sources). Unlike the 'arm dances' an dro and the ridees, it is held firmly and is a 'feet dance,' like the gavotte; dancers stand in a chain (traditionally alternating men and women) holding hands with arms held still, body turned slightly left. It derives from Renaissance branles and had become moribund by the mid-19th century, progressively supplanted by the larides that derived from it. Combined with an dro it produces the dans tricot (dans trikot); the laride de Kervignac is a particular Hanter-dro form of the Lorient/Hennebont area. It is danced in fest-noz to sung kan-ha-diskan or instrumental accompaniment.
insight: Often paired/alternated with an dro within the same suite (dans tricot), it is recommended as a beginner-friendly fest-noz dance because of its simple held step (Wikipedia FR; fest-noz guides).
notes: No dedicated English Wikipedia article -> wikipedia_url blank + needs_eyes_on true; Wikidata Q847080 resolves (no enwiki sitelink). Practiced within the UNESCO-inscribed Fest-noz (RL 2012, file 00707).

name: Gigue
era: 17th c. (imported into France from the British/Irish jig); Baroque suite movement
tagline: Lively Baroque suite-closing dance; the French form of the jig.
profile: The gigue (Italian giga) is a lively Baroque dance originating from the British/Irish jig, imported into France in the mid-17th century, where it usually appears as the final movement of the instrumental suite (Wikipedia; Britannica). It is usually in 3/8 or a compound metre (6/8, 6/4, 9/8, 12/8), often in fugal/contrapuntal texture with accents on the third beats. The French gigue was light and ornamented; the Italian giga faster and more virtuosic. The gigue was probably never a court dance but was danced by nobility on social occasions; in early French theatre, performances customarily ended with a gigue. Bach's suites and Pachelbel's 'Canon and Gigue' are well-known examples.
insight: This is primarily a musical form / stylized concert dance rather than a living folk dance - Britannica notes true jigs were quick and wild solo dances while gigues were danced by couples in formal ballet style (Britannica).
notes: Dance-vs-music-form: predominantly a MUSIC form/Baroque suite movement; British-Isles origin, French-adopted. Country 'France' reflects its Baroque-suite role, not its origin. Flagged.

name: Rigodon
era: 17th-c. folk dance; court dance under Louis XIV; Baroque form 17th-18th c.
tagline: Lively duple-time couple dance of Provence/Dauphine; also Baroque form.
profile: The rigaudon (rigodon; anglicized rigadoon) is a French dance in lively duple metre, musically similar to the bourree but rhythmically simpler with regular (often eight-bar) phrases (Wikipedia). It originated as a sprightly 17th-century folk couple dance associated with the southern provinces of Vivarais, Languedoc, Dauphine and Provence, and became a court dance under Louis XIV, remaining fashionable in French and English courts through the 18th century before yielding to the minuet. The traditional couple choreography uses brisk hopping/skipping steps (pas de rigaudon), facing partners with raised arms and foot-stamps. In Provence it was played by the galoubet-and-tambourin; in Dauphine it persisted as a fiddle tradition and crossed to Quebec folklore. Campra, Rameau and Ravel ('Le Tombeau de Couperin') used it (Wikipedia; Wikipedia FR).
insight: Like the bourree and gavotte, it spans a living southern-French folk tradition and a stylized Baroque suite movement; the Wikidata item explicitly flags itself 'different from' a separate genre/form-term entity (Wikidata).
notes: Dance-vs-music-form: Q383845 is the dance/Baroque-movement item; flagged 'different from' Q125134202 (a separate genre/form-term entity, not independently opened).

name: Irish dance
era: Traditional; competitive stepdance codified late 19th-20th c.; globalized 1994 (Riverdance)
tagline: Umbrella of Irish stepdance, ceili and set dancing.
profile: Irish dance is a group of traditional dance forms originating in Ireland, broadly divided into social dances (ceili and set dancing) and performance/solo dances (stepdance) (Wikipedia). Modern competitive stepdance is characterized by a rigid upper body and intricate, fast footwork in soft shoes (ghillies) or hard shoes; other solo forms include sean-nos (relaxed, close-to-the-floor, improvised) and festival dance. Ceili dancing is performed by groups of 2-16 to reels, jigs and hornpipes; the 30 standardized ceili dances appear in CLRG's official handbook 'Ar Rinci Ceili.' Set dances derive from French quadrilles for four couples. Accompaniment is Irish traditional music - fiddle, bodhran, concertina, flute, whistle. The form was globally popularized by Riverdance (1994).
insight: Irish group dancing was particularly influenced by the French quadrille in the late 18th century - a genuine France connection, but the tradition is unambiguously Irish, so the catalog's France label is a mislabel (Wikipedia).
notes: COUNTRY CORRECTED from France to Ireland (FR->IE). The only genuine France link is the late-18th-c. quadrille influence on set dancing.

name: Breton dance
era: Traditional (some forms medieval); revived from the late 1950s
tagline: Umbrella of traditional Breton folk dances of the fest-noz.
profile: Breton dance is the umbrella of traditional dance forms from Brittany, the Celtic region of France, mostly social group dances in chains and circles (Wikipedia). Jean-Michel Guilcher identified five 'mother-forms' of Lower Brittany - the gavotte (dans tro), the en dro/an dro, the dans Treger, the dans Leon and the dans plinn - from which hundreds of terroir variants developed. Dances are performed at fest-noz (night festival) and fest-deiz (day festival), accompanied by kan-ha-diskan call-and-response singing or by sonneur duos and instruments such as the biniou (bagpipe), bombarde, accordion, clarinet (treujenn-gaol) and, via bagadou, the Scottish bagpipe. Revival came with the Celtic Circles and the modern fest-noz, credited especially to Loeiz Ropars from the 1950s.
insight: The fest-noz - 'festive gathering based on the collective practice of traditional dances of Brittany' - was inscribed on UNESCO's Representative List in 2012 (decision 7.COM 11.13; file 00707). UNESCO notes about a thousand fest-noz take place every year (UNESCO).

sources.title: Fest-Noz, festive gathering based on the collective practice of traditional dances of Brittany

notes: UMBRELLA entry - flagged. Fest-noz UNESCO file 00707 verified (RL 2012).

name: Ori Tahiti
tagline: The umbrella of all traditional Tahitian dance.
profile: Ori Tahiti ('Tahitian dance') is the umbrella term for the dance traditions of Tahiti and the Society Islands (Bora Bora, Moorea, Raiatea, Tahaa, Huahine). It unites five forms - oteea, aparima, hivinau, paoa and the recitative pataautau - characterized by a divided body (legs mark the percussion rhythm; arms/torso narrate a cultural theme), Tahitian-language song, percussion (toere, pahu) and plant-fiber costumes. Troupes (pupu ori) perform at the Heiva i Tahiti festival - founded in 1881 as 'Tiurai' and renamed Heiva in 1985 - functioning as social cohesion and identity transmission (culture.gouv.fr; Tahiti Tourisme).
insight: Banned under the 1819 Pomare Code, the dance was fully revived in 1956 when Madeleine Moua, a Papeete school principal, founded the troupe 'Heiva Tahiti'; the practice was added to France's national intangible-heritage inventory in 2017 (culture.gouv.fr).

sources.title: Le 'Ori, pratique artistique, sociale et culturelle de Tahiti et des iles de la Societe (inventory fiche)

notes: UMBRELLA entry. No dedicated English Wikipedia article (French article + Wikidata Q56871038 exist); wikipedia_url left blank. On France's national PCI inventory (2017) but NOT yet UNESCO-listed.

name: Otea
tagline: Fast hip-shaking Tahitian group dance to drums.
profile: The oteea is a percussion-driven group dance with rapid hip motion, danced in several rows. Once a male warrior dance, it now has male (oteea tane), female (oteea vahine) and mixed (oteea amui) forms. Dancers execute figures (tamau, varu, otamu, ami, faarapu) while gesturing daily-life, nature or warfare themes. Accompaniment is drums only - the toere slit-log drum, the sharkskin pahu, and the smaller faatete - with no singing. Costumes use long plant-fiber (more) skirts with tassels and elaborate headpieces (English Wikipedia; Air Tahiti Nui).
insight: Considered Tahiti's most spectacular dance; its modern choreography was standardized when Madeleine Moua founded the Heiva troupe in 1956 (English Wikipedia).

sources.title: Tahitian Dance ('Ori Tahiti): The Cultural Treasure of French Polynesia

notes: Part of ori Tahiti; covered by France's 2017 PCI inscription of the umbrella practice.

name: Aparima
tagline: Graceful Tahitian story-telling hand dance.
profile: The aparima (apa 'movement/mimic' + rima 'hands') is a gesture dance from Tahiti and the Cook Islands in which the hands narrate a story, close in spirit to hula and the Tongan tauolunga. Two types exist: aparima vava (mute pantomime, often kneeling, accompanied by pahu and toere) and aparima himene (sung, with stringed instruments such as guitar/ukulele). Costuming is a pareu or cloth dress; gestures are descriptive, symbolic or ornamental (English Wikipedia; Tahiti dance online).
insight: Unlike the oteea, the aparima is soft and narrative - the only Tahitian form always meant to pantomime an actual story (Tahiti dance online).
notes: QID Q2858132 verified via Wikipedia pageprops/DBpedia cross-check (high confidence).

name: Hivinau
era: Old but post-contact (name likely from English 'Heave now!')
tagline: Tahitian circular call-and-response dance.
profile: The hivinau is danced in two concentric circles (one of men, one of women) turning around a central orchestra and group leader. A male soloist calls a short verse and the chorus answers in heightened speech ('Hiri, haa, haa'); when dancers meet they turn and perform knee or hip shakes. Themes typically concern fishing and the sea. It often opens or closes a festival or transitions between dances and is considered the least technically demanding ori Tahiti form (English Wikipedia; Society of Folk Dance Historians).
insight: Though old, it is 'not traditional' in origin - the name probably derives from the European sailors' cry 'Heave now!' at the anchor capstan (English Wikipedia).
notes: Often performed together with the paoa.

name: Paoa
tagline: Seated Tahitian circle dance with a soloist couple.
profile: The paoa is a dance in which performers kneel or sit in a circle, sing and tap their thighs to a repetitive scanning refrain (patautau), while a selected boy and girl dance inside the circle. It is traditionally linked to tapa (barkcloth) making - women beat bark in rhythm and sang to break the monotony. Themes are usually hunting or fishing; being chosen for the central solo is an honor signifying a teacher's recognition (English Wikipedia; The Tahiti Traveler).
insight: The seated, thigh-slapping format derives from the communal rhythm of beating bark to make tapa cloth (The Tahiti Traveler).
notes: Commonly danced with the hivinau.

name: Schuhplattler
era: Possible medieval roots; modern regulated form 19th c.
tagline: Bavarian/Alpine men's shoe-slapping courtship dance.
profile: The Schuhplattler is a folk dance of the Eastern Alps (Upper Bavaria, Tyrol, Salzburg) in which men stomp, clap and strike the soles of their shoes (Schuhe), thighs and knees with flat (platt) hands. It is often a couple dance: men perform leaps and slapping sequences to the Landler portion of the music in 3/4 time (originally zither and guitar, later accordion), while women in dirndls twirl; a boys-only form (reiner Burschenplattler) also exists. The tradition is preserved by Trachtenvereine (costume clubs) in Bavaria, Austria and German-immigrant communities worldwide (English Wikipedia; Society of Folk Dance Historians).
insight: Often said to mimic the courtship display of the Auerhahn (wood grouse); the modern form was popularized after locals performed it for King Maximilian II of Bavaria on 15 July 1858, and the first Schuhplattler society formed at Miesbach in 1861 (English Wikipedia).
notes: More than 150 basic regional Schuhplattlers. Shared with Austrian Tyrol; catalog assigns it to Germany (Bavaria).

name: Landler
era: Rose to popularity late 18th c.; direct ancestor of the waltz
tagline: Alpine couple turning dance, ancestor of the waltz.
profile: The Landler is a European folk dance in 3/4 time. Couples turn under each other's arms with complex hand-holds, dance back to back, and rotate in closed hold to moderate triple-time music (Styrian harmonica, fiddle, zither). It originated in the rural Alps - Wikidata records its country as Switzerland, Germany and Austria - with its name from the 'Landl' district of Upper Austria. It strongly influenced the waltz and appears in works by Mozart, Beethoven and the Strauss family (English Wikipedia; Britannica).
insight: It is more Austrian/Alpine than specifically German - a shared Austria/Germany/Switzerland dance and the direct rustic predecessor of the Viennese waltz (Britannica; English Wikipedia).
notes: COUNTRY nuance: catalog listed Germany, but the Landler is a SHARED Alpine dance - Wikidata country = Switzerland, Germany, Austria; primary origin Austrian (Upper Austria 'Landl'). Country set to Austria with note. The Innviertler Landler is on Austria's national intangible-heritage inventory.

name: Zwiefacher
era: Term first documented 1780; older melodies predate the modern bar line
tagline: Bavarian couple dance with changing meter.
profile: The Zwiefacher is a southern German couple turning dance with quick tempo and changing meter, alternating between 3/4 (waltz) and 2/4 (Dreher/pivot) - and occasionally polka - beats, in regular or irregular patterns. Couples turn rapidly in a closed (often barrel) hold. It is danced mainly in Lower Bavaria, the Hallertau and the Upper Palatinate, and is also known in the Black Forest, Austria, Alsace, the Czech Republic and Sudetenland. Accompaniment is accordion, fiddle and brass; lyrics served as mnemonics. Researcher Felix Hoerburger catalogued 112 tunes carrying 474 different names (English Wikipedia).
insight: The name (loosely 'twofold'/'double') may refer to the two alternating meters or to the couple being tightly wound together; the dance was added to Germany's Nationwide Inventory of Intangible Cultural Heritage in 2016 (English Wikipedia).
notes: On the German Nationwide Inventory of Intangible Cultural Heritage (2016); NOT a UNESCO listing.

name: Discofox
tagline: Popular German social partner dance to disco/Schlager.
profile: Discofox is a social partner dance that evolved in Europe in the mid-1970s as a rediscovery of the closed dance hold within the otherwise solo-dominated disco scene, roughly contemporaneous with the American hustle. The beginner's basic step is 'step-step-tap' (advanced dancers split-beat a four-step basic over three beats of music). It is danced to 2/4 or 4/4 music - 1970s disco, Eurodisco, Italo disco, pop, techno and Schlager - and is hugely popular at parties, weddings and dance schools in Germany, Austria and Switzerland (English Wikipedia; Danceflavors).
insight: Its spread was greatly boosted by the 1977 film 'Saturday Night Fever'; it remains a staple of German Schlager party culture (English Wikipedia).
notes: Modern vernacular social dance; not UNESCO. Shared Wikidata item (Q1228530) links German Discofox and the Hustle concept.

name: Sirtaki
tagline: The 1964 'Zorba's Dance' - Greece's famous invented dance.
profile: A 20th-century choreographed dance created for the 1964 film 'Zorba the Greek.' Mikis Theodorakis's 'Zorba's Dance' adapted a traditional Chania syrtos tune by composer Giorgis Koutsourelis; the film choreography is attributed to Giorgos Provias. It is NOT a traditional folk dance but an amalgam of slow hasapiko and faster hasaposerviko, with syrtos (dragging) and pidikhtos (hopping) elements. Danced in line or circle, hands on neighbours' shoulders; meter 4/4 accelerating, often shifting to 2/4 in the fast part, beginning slow then becoming vigorous with hops and leaps. Bouzouki-led music; performed at tavernas, weddings and festivals (English Wikipedia; Society of Folk Dance Historians).
insight: Despite global fame as 'Greek,' sirtaki is an invented tradition created for cinema; Anthony Quinn had a broken foot during filming, and the dragging-sliding steps replaced the planned hopping steps because he could not perform the dances taught to him (English Wikipedia).
notes: DISTINCT from syrtos. A 1964 cinematic invention, not a traditional folk dance. Accompanying music has its own article ('Zorba's Dance').

name: Syrtos
tagline: One of the oldest, most widespread Greek chain dances.
profile: A collective name for a family of Greek folk dances; open circle/curving line, dancers link hands facing right, led by a leader who improvises and may break away. Among the most ancient Greek dances, described by Lucian (c. AD 125-190). Mainland versions (Peloponnese, Thessaly, Macedonia, Thrace) use 7/8; Crete, the Aegean, Asia Minor and Constantinople use 4/4 (slow-quick-quick). Traditionally segregated lines of men and women, the leader holding the second dancer by a handkerchief; played on regional instruments (Cretan lyra/violin, laouto, clarinet). Central to weddings and religious festivals (English Wikipedia; Britannica).
insight: Syrtos and kalamatianos share identical steps but differ in meter (4/4 vs 7/8); the name derives from syro, 'to drag/lead' (English Wikipedia; Britannica).
notes: NOT the same as sirtaki. Numerous regional variants (Chaniotikos, Politiko, Botaitikos).

name: Kalamatianos
era: Roots in antiquity; modern name from the town of Kalamata
tagline: Greece's national 7/8 line dance in 3-2-2.
profile: One of Greece's best-known dances, widely regarded as the national dance; danced in a chain/open circle, counterclockwise, dancers holding hands. Beat 7/8 subdivided 3+2+2 (slow-quick-quick); 12 steps over 4 bars (10 forward counterclockwise, 2 back). The lead dancer often holds the second by a handkerchief to perform elaborate steps and acrobatics. Inclusive (men, women, children); performed at weddings, festivals and diaspora gatherings, accompanied by clarinet, violin, laouto, drums and often bouzouki (English Wikipedia; Costa Navarino).
insight: Steps are identical to the syrtos but the meter differs (kalamatianos 7/8, syrtos 4/4); the kalamatianos is on Greece's National Inventory of Intangible Cultural Heritage (a national, not UNESCO, recognition).

sources.title: Kalamatianos: A Dance that Unites Greece

notes: Disambiguate the dance (Q1721946) from the music-genre article 'Kalamatiano' (Q6350364). On Greece's national ICH inventory, not UNESCO.

name: Hasapiko
era: Medieval (Byzantine-era origin as a butchers' battle mime)
tagline: The 'butcher's dance' of Constantinople; basis of sirtaki.
profile: A Greek folk dance from Constantinople, originating in the Middle Ages as a sword/battle mime by the Greek butchers' guild, adopted from the Byzantine military; historically called makellarikos horos. The slow version (hasapikos varys, 'heavy') is in 4/4; the fast version (grigoro hasapiko or hasaposerviko) in 2/4. Danced in a short line/open circle, hands on neighbours' shoulders, with synchronized steps; accompanied by bouzouki and rebetiko-era ensembles. Sirtaki is a modern choreographed version of it (English Wikipedia; Dance Museum).
insight: The hasapiko is the structural basis of the 1964 sirtaki; its fast, Balkan-influenced form is the hasaposerviko (English Wikipedia, citing Leontis, 'Culture and Customs of Greece').
notes: Danced to rebetiko (UNESCO 2017 MUSIC inscription) but the dance itself is not inscribed.

name: Zeibekiko
era: Late 17th-c. origin (Zeybek militia); popularized on the Greek mainland after 1922
tagline: Solo improvisational rebetiko dance in 9/8.
profile: A solo, improvisational Greek folk dance related to the Turkish zeybek dance, named after the Zeybeks (irregular Aegean militia of the Ottoman Empire). First seen in the late 17th c. in Constantinople and Smyrna; originally a dance for two armed men, it became a solitary improvised dance. Rhythmic pattern 9/4 or 9/8. After the 1922 population exchange it spread on the mainland via laiko/rebetiko. Traditionally male and introspective (the 'eagle dance'), danced solo within a circle of seated, clapping companions; now also danced by women. Accompanied by bouzouki and baglamas (English Wikipedia; GTP Headlines).
insight: The zeibekiko was added to Greece's National Inventory of Intangible Cultural Heritage in March 2022 alongside the bouzouki, tzouras and baglamas; it is danced to rebetiko, the music genre UNESCO inscribed in 2017 (GTP Headlines, 24 Mar 2022).

sources.title: Greece Adds 'Zeibekiko' Dance and 'Bouzouki' to Intangible Cultural Heritage List

notes: National (Greek) ICH inscription, NOT UNESCO. The 2017 UNESCO inscription is rebetiko, the music.

name: Tsifteteli
era: Ottoman-era origin; entered the Greek mainland after the 1922 population exchange
tagline: The Greek 'belly dance' in 4/4, of Anatolian origin.
profile: A rhythm and dance of Anatolia and the Balkans, the Greek counterpart of Turkish ciftetelli (Turkish 'double-stringed,' from a violin technique). In Greece it functions as the Greek 'belly dance': a solo improvisational form with hip undulations, shoulder shimmies and sinuous arm movements, typically in 4/4 (also cited as 2/4). Performed informally at weddings, glentia and festivals, mostly initiated by women; also professionally in tavernas and bouzouki clubs. Accompanied by violin, bouzouki and percussion; common in rebetiko/laiko (English Wikipedia; Folkdance Footnotes).
insight: Greek tsifteteli (one 'l') and Turkish ciftetelli (double 'l') share Ottoman roots but are documented as separate Wikipedia/Wikidata entities; Greeks often use 'tsifteteli' as a synonym for oriental dance generally.
notes: Distinct article from Turkish ciftetelli. Danced to rebetiko but the dance itself is not UNESCO-inscribed.

name: Pentozali
era: 18th c. (music credited to Cretan violinist Stefanos Triantafillakis, c. 1770-1771)
tagline: Fast Cretan war dance of high leaps and improvisation.
profile: The trademark folk dance of Crete; a vigorous war dance with high jumping movements and improvisation. Name from pente ('five') + zalos ('step/jump') - 'five-steps,' the dance having ten steps; also punned with zali ('dizziness'). Dancers form an incomplete counterclockwise circle, arms on shoulders; the lead dancer improvises acrobatic leaps while the second holds his hand as a stable base. Starts moderate and accelerates. Instrumental music: the melody on Cretan lyra (or violin) with percussive laouto accompaniment; the lyra player directs the dance's flow (English Wikipedia; Music of Crete).
insight: Tradition links pentozali to Cretan uprisings against the Ottomans - said to symbolize the 'fifth attempt' to liberate Crete; the music is credited to Stefanos Triantafillakis around 1770-1771 (English Wikipedia).
notes: Often paired with the slow Siganos.

name: Sousta
era: Ancient roots (linked to the Pyrrhic war-dance tradition); name from Venetian 'sousta' (carriage spring)
tagline: Cretan/Dodecanese couples courtship dance with hopping steps.
profile: A Greek folk dance performed at weddings as courtship between partners; prominent in Crete, the Dodecanese and the broader Aegean, and described as second only to the syrtos in commonness. A three-step dance with a 'hopping' bounce - two jump-steps forward, one back - with crossed-over linked hands symbolizing unity. Carries symbolism of romance and marriage; historically a socialisation rite among village youth. Music typically on Cretan or Pontian lyra (or violin), laouto, and mandolin/askomandoura. Named from the Venetian word for a carriage spring, reflecting its bouncy motion (English Wikipedia; IJESSR).
insight: The sousta exists in many officially documented island variants (Crete, Rhodes, Halki, Leros, Kalymnos, Karpathos, Kos, Tilos, Samos, Symi); on Rhodes alone scholars record distinct village forms in a peer-reviewed IJESSR study.

sources.title: The Various Forms of the Dance 'Sousta' on the Island of Rhodes
sources.publisher: International Journal of Education and Social Science Research (IJESSR)

notes: Cretan and Cypriot soustas are regional variants of one dance family; a single Wikipedia article covers it. Also danced in Cyprus (CY).

name: Ikariotikos
era: Traditional island dance; popularized nationally via a 1975 song by Giorgos Konitopoulos
tagline: The fast island chain dance of Ikaria.
profile: A traditional chain folk dance and accompanying song from the island of Ikaria in the NE Aegean. Danced in an open circle/line, men and women holding hands (older form, crossed palms) or by the shoulders. Structured in parts: slow walking steps (akin to Sta Tria), then steps resembling the Issios of Kalymnos, then fast steps with full leg-and-body mobility; duple 2/4 rhythm. Historically sung, then accompanied by Cretan/island lyra, the Ikarian tsampouna (bagpipe), and violin with laouto/mandolin; today also guitar, bouzouki and accordion. Danced at weddings, baptisms and religious festivals (English Wikipedia; ResearchGate ethnographic study).
insight: Some specialists hold that the original Ikariotikos was slow and the fast popular version is effectively a Ballos; its modern fame owes much to Giorgos Konitopoulos's song 'My love in Ikaria,' notably performed by Yiannis Parios (English Wikipedia).

sources.title: Ethnographic Research on the Music and Dance Tradition of Ikarian Island

notes: Has a dedicated (short) English Wikipedia article.

name: Tsamiko
era: Klephtic/armatoloi tradition; iconic of the Greek War of Independence (1821-1830)
tagline: Mainland 3/4 men's line dance of the klephts.
profile: A popular mainland Greek folk dance in 3/4 meter, also called Kleftikos for its association with the klephts who fought in the War of Independence. Slow, strict tempo emphasizing 'attitude, style and grace' over steps; dancers link hands bent 90 degrees at the elbows in a line/open circle. The lead dancer performs high acrobatic leaps (scissor-like kicks) supported by the second dancer. Danced almost exclusively by men, sometimes in the fustanella; most popular in Central Greece (Roumeli), the Peloponnese, Thessaly and Epirus. Played by zournas and daouli, or clarinet, violin and laouto (English Wikipedia; Domna Samiou Association).
insight: The name means 'dance of the Chams' (from Tsamouria/Chameria), though musicologist Samuel Baud-Bovy noted the Greek tsamiko was not actually danced among Cham Albanians; it is performed at national commemorations of the 1821 revolution.

sources.title: Tsamikos
sources.publisher: Domna Samiou Greek Folk Music Association

notes: Step counts vary by region (8/10/12/14/16 steps); some sources cite 6/8 alongside 3/4.

name: Hasaposerviko
tagline: The fast 2/4 'Serbian butcher's dance' form of hasapiko.
profile: The fast version of the hasapiko, in 2/4 meter, also called grigoro hasapiko ('fast hasapiko'); the name references Serbian and other Balkan influences. A line/open-circle dance with hands on shoulders, easier and quicker than the slow hasapiko, with changing steps - an expanded, transformed motif of the hasapiko. Accompanied by bouzouki-led ensembles and common in rebetiko/laiko; it forms the fast climactic section of the sirtaki sequence and of many taverna dance sets (English Wikipedia: Hasapiko; Dance Museum).
insight: Hasaposerviko is treated within the Hasapiko article on English Wikipedia rather than as a standalone topic; sources describe it as the fast (2/4) hasapiko expanded with Balkan/Eastern-European traits (Leontis, 'Culture and Customs of Greece'; Dance Museum).
notes: No dedicated English Wikipedia/Wikidata - those fields blank + needs_eyes_on true. The 'Hasaposerviko' URL resolves to Hasapiko content (merge/redirect).

name: Pontic dances
era: Ancient-to-Byzantine roots; carried by Pontic Greeks, especially after the 1923 displacement
tagline: Umbrella of Black Sea Pontic Greek dances (tik, kotsari, serra).
profile: An umbrella tradition of dances of the Pontic Greeks of the Black Sea region (Pontus). Best-known are the tik (the most renowned, 'straight' posture), omal (slow), kotsari/kochari (danced on the heels, shared with Armenians), seranitsa, and the serra/pyrrichios (a dynamic men's war dance descended from the ancient Pyrrhichios), plus maheria (knife dance), dipat, podaraki, atsiapat and letsina. Mostly closed-circle or line formations with hands linked or on shoulders, with sharp, tremulous movements; asymmetrical meters (e.g. 5/8, 7/16, 9/8) at high tempo. Accompanied chiefly by the Pontic lyra (kemenche), with daouli, touloum and zurna (English Wikipedia; Society of Folk Dance Historians).
insight: Pontic dances are characterized as 'sharp,' distinct from the smoother island styles; the serra (pyrrichios) retains war-dance qualities and is documented from ancient descriptions of the Pyrrhic dance (English Wikipedia).
notes: UMBRELLA entry. The Serra has its own dedicated article and item (Q3564398). Pontic lyra = kemenche.

name: Gwoka
era: Originated 17th c. (slavery); rehabilitated post-1946 and from the 1960s-70s; UNESCO 2014
tagline: Guadeloupe's emblematic Afro-Creole drum, song and dance tradition.
profile: Gwoka is the umbrella Afro-Guadeloupean tradition combining responsorial singing in Guadeloupean Creole, rhythms played on the ka drums, and improvisational dance. It is generally played with three drums - two lower-pitched 'boula' carrying the central ostinato and one higher 'make'/marqueur (soloist) that dialogues with the dancer - accompanied by chacha (gourd rattle) and tibwa (sticks). Built on seven base rhythms, it is performed in the lawonn (circle) at the lewoz night gathering, where dancers enter one at a time to face the drums while the public claps and sings the chorus. It emerged on sugar, coffee and banana plantations and became a symbol of resistance and Guadeloupean identity (UNESCO; Repriz/ZoukJam).
insight: Gwoka was inscribed on UNESCO's Representative List on 26 November 2014; ethnomusicologist Jerome Camal notes that because Guadeloupe is a French overseas department without a UN seat, the petition had to be sponsored by the French state, and the campaign was led from 2004 by Felix Cotellon of the centre Repriz (Camal, Intl Journal of Heritage Studies, 2016; UNESCO).

sources.title: Putting the drum in conundrum: Guadeloupean gwoka, intangible cultural heritage and postnationalism
sources.publisher: International Journal of Heritage Studies (Jerome Camal)

notes: UMBRELLA tradition. UNESCO file No. 00991 ('Gwoka, music, song, dance and cultural practice representative of Guadeloupean identity'), inscribed 26 Nov 2014 (9.COM). Proposed slug pending worklist reconciliation. Guadeloupe = French overseas region; sovereign state France/FR.

name: Lewoz
tagline: Warrior gwoka rhythm and the night drum gathering.
profile: Lewoz is one of the seven base gwoka rhythms - a combative/warrior rhythm in two-time, historically said to have set the rhythm for attacks on plantations and to function as an incantation dance, marked by a strong dialogue between dancer and make. The word also names the traditional night gathering (the lewoz / sware lewoz), held traditionally on Friday or Saturday until dawn, where participants form the lawonn circle around the ka drums, chacha and singers; dancers enter one by one to face the drums while the crowd claps and sings the response (Wikipedia; BNF France Ameriques).
insight: Lewoz is considered the reference rhythm and the preferred space of expression of gwoka; the English Wikipedia entity describes the lewoz event/performance tradition (rural musical performances of Martinique and Guadeloupe, as opposed to gwo ka moderne) rather than narrowly the rhythm.

sources.title: Le Gwoka | Patrimoines Partages - France Ameriques

notes: Constituent of Gwoka inscription 00991 (not separately inscribed). Wikidata Q6537469/en:Lewoz is framed as the lewoz gathering/performance, not strictly the rhythm - SCOPE-MISMATCH flagged for reconciliation. Proposed slug pending worklist reconciliation.

name: Kaladja
tagline: Slow gwoka rhythm of sorrow, hardship and love.
profile: Kaladja is one of the seven gwoka base rhythms, typically slow, expressing suffering, sadness, mourning, reflection and love; it is used at funeral wakes (veillees) and can be danced as a couple dance. Like the rest of the repertoire it is played on the ka drums (two boula plus make) with chacha and responsorial Creole song, danced within the lawonn at lewoz gatherings. Dancers perform slower, mournful gestures echoing the grief and hardship of enslaved communities (Wikipedia: Gwo ka; ZoukJam summarizing Repriz).
insight: Kaladja's slow, somber character makes it the gwoka rhythm most associated with laments and funeral wakes, contrasting with the festive toumblak and mennde.
notes: Constituent of Gwoka inscription 00991 (not separately inscribed). NO dedicated English Wikipedia article or Wikidata item; documented within the Gwo ka article - do NOT substitute the general Gwo ka URL. Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Toumblak
tagline: Joyful gwoka rhythm of love, fertility and celebration.
profile: Toumblak is among the best-known of the seven gwoka rhythms, a lively, festive rhythm associated with love, fertility, joy and the earth, generating sensual, undulating hip movements and suggestive postures. A faster, accelerated variant is called toumblak chire. Played on the ka drums (two boula plus make soloist) with chacha and responsorial Creole song, it is danced in the lawonn circle during lewoz gatherings and is often compared to African fertility dances such as the kalenda (Wikipedia: Gwo ka; Comite du Tourisme des Iles de Guadeloupe).
insight: Toumblak is frequently described as the most famous and widespread gwoka rhythm, embodying celebration and courtship amid the hardship of enslaved life.

sources.title: The 7 rhythms of Gwo ka
sources.publisher: Comite du Tourisme des Iles de Guadeloupe

notes: Constituent of Gwoka inscription 00991 (not separately inscribed). NO dedicated English Wikipedia article or Wikidata item; documented within the Gwo ka article. Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Padjanbel
tagline: Gwoka work rhythm tied to sugarcane cutting.
profile: Padjanbel (also padjabel, granjanbel, gwadjanbel) is one of the seven gwoka base rhythms, a work rhythm in 3/4 whose jerky movements recall the gestures of enslaved plantation workers, especially the cutting of sugarcane; it is also described as a women's rhythm symbolizing grace, pride and liberation, linking earth and sky. Played on the ka drums (two boula plus make) with chacha and responsorial Creole song, danced in the lawonn at lewoz gatherings (Mulaogwada; Wikipedia: Gwo ka).
insight: Padjanbel is the gwoka rhythm most explicitly tied to sugarcane-cutting labor, though some traditions reinterpret it as a warrior dance.
notes: Constituent of Gwoka inscription 00991 (not separately inscribed). NO dedicated English Wikipedia article or Wikidata item; documented within the Gwo ka article. Spelling varies widely. Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Graj
tagline: Gwoka work rhythm miming manioc/cassava preparation.
profile: Graj (grage) is one of the seven gwoka base rhythms, a work rhythm linked to agricultural labor whose movements evoke the phases of grating manioc into cassava flour and field harvesting; it is generally slower, with the reprise marked, and is sometimes danced by women as a seductive dance. Played on the ka drums (two boula plus make) with chacha and responsorial Creole song, danced in the lawonn circle during lewoz gatherings (Wikipedia: Gwo ka; Loka Guadeloupe).
insight: Graj's choreography directly imitates the grating of manioc (grage manioc), making it one of the clearest examples of gwoka as embodied memory of plantation labor.
notes: Constituent of Gwoka inscription 00991 (not separately inscribed). NO dedicated English Wikipedia article or Wikidata item; documented within the Gwo ka article. Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Woule
tagline: Swaying waltz-like 'rolling' gwoka rhythm.
profile: Woule (roule) is one of the seven gwoka base rhythms and the only one in triple time (3/4), a swaying, airy waltz-like 'rolling' rhythm sometimes danced with a scarf (foulard); it is often described as a 'Creole waltz' used to charm and mimic/satirize the European masters, and is also associated with repetitive work like graj. Played on the ka drums (two boula plus make) with chacha and responsorial Creole song, danced in the lawonn at lewoz gatherings (Wikipedia: Gwo ka; Ziloka).
insight: Woule's waltz-like triple meter is widely cited as evidence of European ballroom influence creolized into the gwoka repertoire.
notes: Constituent of Gwoka inscription 00991 (not separately inscribed). NO dedicated English Wikipedia article or Wikidata item; documented within the Gwo ka article. Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Mennde
era: Said to be the last of the seven rhythms to arrive (post-1848 abolition); living tradition
tagline: Fast carnival gwoka rhythm of Congo/African origin.
profile: Mennde (mende, minde) is one of the seven gwoka base rhythms, a fast, energetic carnival rhythm symbolizing collective celebration and escape (evasion); it incites a marching/parading pace and underlies the polyrhythms of Guadeloupean Carnival. It is said to be the last rhythm to arrive on the island, brought by contracted 'Congos' after the 1848 abolition, and shows strong direct African influence. Played on the ka drums (two boula plus make) with chacha and responsorial Creole song, danced in the lawonn during lewoz gatherings and in carnival processions (Wikipedia: Gwo ka; Mediatheque Caraibe/Lameca).
insight: Mennde is the gwoka rhythm most embedded in Carnival; its African/Congo origin story marks it as the most recent addition to the seven-rhythm canon.

sources.title: Le gwosiwo, une musique carnavalesque du Sud Basse-Terre (Guadeloupe)
sources.publisher: Mediatheque Caraibe (Lameca), Conseil departemental de la Guadeloupe

notes: Constituent of Gwoka inscription 00991 (not separately inscribed). NO dedicated English Wikipedia article or Wikidata item; documented within the Gwo ka article. Congo/African-origin claim is oral-traditional. Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Biguine
tagline: Early-20th-century Antillean clarinet-and-trombone dance-music.
profile: Biguine is a rhythmic dance and music style of the French Antilles, originating in Saint-Pierre, Martinique in the 19th century and shared with Guadeloupe. It fuses West African bele drumming with 19th-century French ballroom dance steps; the orchestrated biguine is defined by the interplay of clarinet and trombone, while the drum biguine (bidgin bele) uses the bele drum and tibwa sticks with call-and-response nasal singing. Danced as a close couple dance with a roll of the hips. It reached Paris after the 1931 Colonial Exhibition via stars like Alexandre Stellio and Sam Castandet (Wikipedia; Smithsonian Folkways).
insight: Biguine is widely cited as one of the ancestors of the jazz developed by Creoles of New Orleans. Cole Porter composed 'Begin the Beguine' in 1935; it gained fame chiefly via Artie Shaw's 1938 swing arrangement, a best-selling record (Wikipedia).

sources.title: Au Bal Antillais: Creole Biguines from Martinique - Early Recordings of Caribbean Dance Music

notes: SHARED Guadeloupe/Martinique tradition; primary origin Saint-Pierre, Martinique (pre-1902). Distinct from the anglicized 'Beguine (dance)' (Q17080629) popularized by Cole Porter. Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Quadrille (Guadeloupean)
era: Introduced 18th c. (European colonists); creolized 19th c.; revived 1980s
tagline: Antillean Creole contredanse-derived figure dance with a caller.
profile: The kwadril (Creole quadrille) is a folk figure dance of the French Lesser Antilles derived from the European quadrille/contredanse, performed in Guadeloupe in sets of figures by four couples in a square, directed by a commandeur/konmande (caller) who announces the figures. Guadeloupean sets (balakadri/balkadri) combine proper quadrilles with creolized 19th-century couple dances - biguines, mazouks (mazurkas) and valses creoles. Instrumentation includes accordion, violin, guitar, tanbou dibas, chacha, malakach, triangle, tibwa and the syak (one-metre bamboo rasp). It was creolized by enslaved and freed Africans, who added African rhythms to the European ballroom forms (Wikipedia; CTIG).
insight: Antilleans regard the quadrille not as a colonial relic but as a 'new creation' adopted and transformed by people of African descent into a shared symbol of Creole identity across Guadeloupe, Martinique, Dominica and St. Lucia.

sources.title: Quadrille
sources.publisher: Comite du Tourisme des Iles de Guadeloupe
