notes: The Kwadril entity (Q3412607) is pan-Lesser-Antillean (Guadeloupe, Martinique, Dominica, St. Lucia), not Guadeloupe-exclusive; French 'Quadrille creole' is the closest dedicated francophone entity. Distinct from European quadrille and Finnish katrilli. Proposed slug 'kwadril-gwadloup' pending reconciliation.

name: Yanvalou
era: Colonial Saint-Domingue origins (pre-1804), Fon-Mahi heritage; living tradition
tagline: Undulating Rada supplication dance honoring the serpent lwa Danbala.
profile: A sacred Rada-rite dance and rhythm of Haitian Vodou, performed widely to open ceremonies. The dancer bends forward, hands on bent knees, and creates fluid undulating circular movements through the spine, chest and solar plexus, evoking a serpent and ocean waves. It honors Danbala Wedo (the serpent lwa of creation) and his consort Ayida-Wedo, and is also danced for Agwe, Ezili and sometimes the Gede. Variants include yanvalou debout (upright), yanvalou zepol (shoulders), and yanvalou dos bas (crouching). Accompanied by the three Rada drums (manman, segon, boula) and the ogan bell; named to mean supplication in Fon (SAGE Encyclopedia of African Religion; University of Florida).
insight: Scholars Yvonne Daniel and Lois Wilcken describe yanvalou as embodied prayer whose serpentine undulation imitates Danbala; it is not for entertainment but a communicative tool between the physical and spiritual realms (SAGE Encyclopedia of African Religion).
notes: Wikipedia + Wikidata both verified about this exact dance. Sacred Vodou Rada dance. Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Banda
era: Living Vodou tradition; Fon/Ghedevi (Gede nation) heritage
tagline: Boisterous Gede dance mocking death with erotic pelvic movement.
profile: The signature dance and drum rhythm of the Gede (Guede) family of death-and-fertility lwa, led by Bawon Samdi, Gede Nibo and Maman Brigitte. Danced especially at Fet Gede (Nov 1-2) in cemeteries of Port-au-Prince, Jacmel and beyond, it features risque hip gyrations and pelvic thrusting that parody death and assert life/procreation. Performed on the Petwo (goatskin, hand-played) drums in 4/4 meter using the asymmetrical kata pattern; a slower lead-in form is called yanvalou Gede or maskawon. Possessed dancers wear black, white and purple, smoke cigars and use clairin infused with hot peppers (Gede [Haitian Vodou] Wikipedia; Lameca).
insight: Lois Wilcken and Leslie Desmangles note the banda's mockery is also political - before the lwa, social rank means nothing, so Gede's outrageous eroticism in banda can voice peasant disappointment with those in power.

sources.title: Vodou Music in Haiti - The Guinea Room

notes: DISTINCT from the Baga/Nalu 'Banda mask' of Guinea (a masquerade headdress) - different tradition. No dedicated EN Wikipedia/Wikidata for the Haitian dance; documented within 'Gede (Haitian Vodou)' and 'Dance in Haiti'. Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Kongo (Haiti)
era: Late-colonial Congo-Coast heritage (pre-1791 influx); living tradition
tagline: Seductive Central-African hip-rotation dance of the Kongo nation.
profile: The dance/rhythm of the Kongo nanchon, of Central African (Kongo/Angola) heritage, within the Kongo-Petwo branch of Vodou. The basic kongo fran step combines a gentle rotation of the hips, a toe-heel foot movement and (for women) a swirling of the skirt; varieties include kongo fran and kongo payet. Performed on goatskin Petwo/Kongo drums played by hand. Kongo lwa (e.g. Kongo Zando, Rwa Wangol, Simbi) are considered gracious and fond of song and dance; uniquely, kongo's gently seductive hip movement made it popular in secular as well as sacred settings, and it is cited as an influence on Haitian meringue and other Caribbean genres (Lameca; Lakou Brooklyn).
insight: Ethnomusicologist Gerdes Fleurant's framework notes the kongo rhythm favors a binary 4/4 form (unlike most ternary Petwo rhythms) and spawned the various merengues in Haiti, calypso in Trinidad, reggae in Jamaica and zouk in Martinique.

sources.title: Vodou Music in Haiti - The Kongo/Petwo Room
Rhythms Without End - Haitian Vodou Drum Music

notes: Lakou Soukrie (Gonaives area) preserves Kongo heritage. No dedicated EN Wikipedia/Wikidata. Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Ibo (Haiti)
tagline: Proud Igbo-nation dance miming the breaking of slavery's chains.
profile: The dance/rhythm of the Ibo nanchon, honoring lwa of Igbo (southeastern Nigeria) heritage such as Ibo Lele and Gran Ibo. Its movements express pride, defiance and resentment of bondage, with gestures symbolically breaking the chains of enslavement; the Ibo are remembered for choosing death over slavery. Danced in 4/4 with the asymmetrical kata pattern, the rhythm can be played on Rada drums although the Ibo are often grouped with the Petwo branch. The Ibo lwa are deemed proud, demanding and difficult to satisfy, presiding over the kanari (clay soul-pots) (Lakou Brooklyn; Haitian Studies Association).
insight: Jean-Leon Destine, founder of Haiti's national folklore troupe, taught that the Ibo reflects resentment against the tyranny of the slave masters - one of the canonical interpretations institutionalized in Haitian folkloric staging.

sources.title: Rhythms Without End - Haitian Vodou Drum Music

notes: Artibonite region strongly associated. DISTINGUISH from 'Ibo loa' (Q10299691), the spirit article, NOT the dance. No dedicated dance EN Wikipedia/Wikidata. Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Nago (Haiti)
tagline: Martial warrior dance for the Ogou (Yoruba) fighting spirits.
profile: A warrior dance/rhythm of the Nago nanchon (Yoruba/Anago heritage), performed within Rada ritual to summon the Ogou (Ogun) family of warrior-blacksmith lwa, associated with iron, fire and military power. Movements are virile and martial - strong footwork, marching, muscular thrusts and machete-battle gestures channeling the warrior's fire. A faster variant, Nago Zepol ('Nago shoulders'), speeds up the drumming. Played on the Rada drums; the Nago repertoire salutes Yoruba spirits with a sequence such as nago gran kou, nago cho and mazoun (Nago dance Wikipedia; Universite Laval).
insight: A Universite Laval study notes the Nago dance carries deep historic weight: it was danced at the Bois Caiman ceremony evoking Ogou that helped ignite the Haitian Revolution (1791-1804).

sources.title: De la construction de l'haitianite a partir de la danse traditionnelle

notes: Lakou Badjo (Gonaives) preserves Nago/Yoruba roots. Wikipedia + Wikidata verified. Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Mayi
tagline: Fast Rada three-step dance with agricultural movements.
profile: A Rada-rite dance/rhythm named for the Mahi people of Dahomey (present-day Benin). It is a fast-paced, three-step pattern dance with agricultural influences, suggesting farming movements, and is linked to communal-labor (konbit) traditions and offering rituals. Along with yanvalou and zepol it forms the core Rada trilogy welcoming the Fon spirits; in a Rada/Nago song-set the slow dances yield to a fast-tempo close such as mayi or zepol. Played on the three Rada drums (manman, segon, boula) with the ogan (University of Florida; Tabanka Caribbean Dance Index).
insight: In the Caribbean Dance lexicon compiled from Yvonne Daniel and colleagues, mayi is defined as a Rada dance named after the Mahis of West Africa that uses a kind of backward pedalling foot movement.

sources.title: The Role of Dance in Haitian Vodou

notes: Proposed slug 'mahi-haiti' with display name 'Mayi'. No dedicated EN Wikipedia/Wikidata. Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Zepol
tagline: Fast Rada shoulder dance that heats the ceremony.
profile: A Rada-rite dance/rhythm named from the French 'les epaules' (shoulders), characterized by fast, back-and-forth pushing/rolling of the shoulders. It typically follows yanvalou, bringing the Rada sequence to a heated, climactic conclusion, and (per Katherine Dunham) the Rada-Dahomey service may open with a 'danza zepaules' that purifies and prepares for the lwa's arrival. Said to be structurally linked to the fast agbekor/agbadza dances of the Ewe. Played on the three Rada drums (manman, segon, boula) with the ogan (Jacob's Pillow; University of Florida).
insight: Dunham observed that the rapid, shuddering shoulder movements of Les Zepaules induce a kind of self-hypnotism, and the accompanying rapid breathing helps generate the excitement that facilitates possession.

sources.title: The Role of Dance in Haitian Vodou

notes: en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Zepol exists but is a Minnesota trade-data company (Q8069337) - do NOT use. No dedicated dance article/QID. Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Haitian Meringue
era: Mid-19th century (national couple dance); traced to colonial contredanse
tagline: Haiti's national couple dance and string-based salon music.
profile: The mereng (meringue), Haiti's national dance and music, a Creole couples dance that emerged by the mid-19th century from the creolized contredanse with Afro-Caribbean influence. Also called meringue lente or meringue de salon, it is string-based (guitar, banjo, piano, horn section) - unlike the accordion-driven Dominican merengue - with a slower, nostalgic feel built on the five-note quintolet (cinquillo) rhythm and a gentle rolling of the hips (gouyad). Danced socially across Haiti by both elite and popular audiences (Meringue Wikipedia; Association for Cultural Equity).
insight: Gage Averill and Michael Largey trace a Kongo influence in the meringue's structuring five-beat figure, borrowed from the kata time-line of the Vodou kongo rhythm - linking the salon national dance to its African ceremonial roots.

sources.title: Haitian Merengue-in-Motion
sources.publisher: Association for Cultural Equity (Alan Lomax Archive)

notes: DISTINCT from Dominican merengue (separate articles). Haitian meringue Q3333303 verified. Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Rara
era: Colonial roots (Saint-Domingue Easter processions); living tradition
tagline: Lenten street procession of vaksin trumpets, song and dance.
profile: A festival/procession music and street-dance tradition performed during Lent, building from Ash Wednesday to Easter weekend, strongest in the rural areas around Port-au-Prince and famously in Leogane. Bands walk and dance for miles led by a baton-twirling major, with female dancers, queens (ren) and Vodou leaders, stopping at crossroads, cemeteries and ancestral sites. The music centers on cylindrical bamboo trumpets (vaksin/banbou) playing interlocking hocketed patterns, plus recycled-metal kone/klewon, drums, graj scrapers and bells; portable Petwo-family goatskin drums replace temple drums. Called 'Vodou taken on the road,' it is also a vehicle for political and social commentary (Smithsonian; Rara music Wikipedia).
insight: Elizabeth McAlister, in 'Rara! Vodou, Power, and Performance in Haiti and Its Diaspora' (UC Press, 2002), argues that Rara consists of an outer secular layer of Carnival 'play' surrounding a protected, secret inner layer of religious 'work'; most Rara bands have a patron lwa.
notes: DISTINCT from Dominican 'Gaga'. Leogane coordinates used as the tradition's best-known center. Verified. Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Kontredans
era: Late 18th century (colonial Saint-Domingue); now largely folkloric/heritage
tagline: Creolized French figure dance, ancestor of the meringue.
profile: The Haitian contredanse, a European-derived figure/couple dance creolized in Saint-Domingue from the late 18th century, evolved from the English country dance via France. African influences - drums, antiphonal/poetic song, and creolized movement - transformed the rigid European set dance; the quintolet/cinquillo five-note figure became central and animated the final section, feeding directly into the meringue. A group figure dance (also called kwaze le 8 / 'croisez les huit') with violin, guitar, flute and percussion, it spread across the Caribbean and is now preserved mainly in folkloric troupes (Kontradans Wikipedia; ResearchGate/Largey).
insight: Michael Largey treats the kontredans/meringue lineage as a classificatory problem, since it shares so many traits with Cuban danza/danzon and Puerto Rican danza that a uniquely Haitian origin is hard to isolate.

sources.title: Capturing kontredans: the transnational exposure of a Haitian dance form

notes: Wikidata Q17077948 is the Haitian kontradans (distinct from generic 'contra dance' Q747339). Verified. Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Punta
era: Traditional Garifuna dance; modern social form dated to the 1950s; roots in St. Vincent Garifuna culture pre-1797
tagline: The Garifuna couple dance famed for rapid hip movement.
profile: Punta is the Afro-Indigenous Garifuna couple dance practiced along Honduras's Caribbean coast (Tela, La Ceiba, Trujillo, Roatan) and across the Garifuna diaspora in Belize, Guatemala and Nicaragua. A male-female pair dances inside a circle of clapping, singing spectators; movement focuses on rapid shaking of the hips and buttocks while the upper torso stays still, often in a courtship/competition format. Music uses the primero (lead/tenor) and segunda (bass) drums, sisira (gourd maracas), occasional turtle/conch shells, and call-and-response singing in the Garifuna language, in duple meter. It is danced at celebrations, holidays and at wakes (beluria / ninth-night) (Britannica; Louisiana Folklife Program; Smithsonian Folkways).
insight: Punta is described by Encyclopaedia Britannica as a social dance of joy and festivity, as well as an emblem of cultural survival; anthropologists Nancie Gonzales and Virginia Kearns documented its central role in Garifuna ninth-night wakes.

sources.title: Traditional Music of the Garifuna (Black Carib) of Belize

notes: UNESCO qualifier: Punta is part of 'Language, dance and music of the Garifuna' (ICH file 00001), proclaimed Masterpiece 18 May 2001, inscribed 2008 (3.COM). Associated countries: Belize, Guatemala, Honduras, Nicaragua; Belize was the original proponent. The inscription covers Garifuna culture broadly, not punta as a standalone element. Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Punta Rock
era: Late 1970s-1980 origin (Dangriga, Belize); popularized in Honduras by the 1990s
tagline: Electrified modern evolution of Garifuna punta, pioneered in Belize.
profile: Punta rock is the modern, electrified, commercial dance-music evolution of traditional punta. It keeps the core Garifuna drum dialogue (primero/segunda), turtle-shell idiophones and call-and-response vocals while adding electric guitar, bass, drum kit, keyboards and a faster tempo. Sung mostly in Garifuna and Belizean Kriol (less often Spanish/English), it absorbed Caribbean pop styles (soca, calypso, reggae). In Honduras it became popular by the 1990s and absorbed salsa/cumbia influences. It animates parties, festivals and diaspora gatherings (Punta rock Wikipedia; Britannica; UWI Global Campus).
insight: Punta rock is largely credited to Pen Cayetano and his Turtle Shell Band in Dangriga, Belize; in Honduras it broke through internationally with Banda Blanca's 'Sopa de Caracol,' which peaked at No. 1 on the Billboard Hot Latin Tracks chart on March 16, 1991 and sold an estimated 1.5 million copies.

sources.title: Pen Cayetano: The Beginning of Punta Rock
sources.publisher: University of the West Indies Global Campus

notes: BELIZE ATTRIBUTION CONFIRMED: origin is Dangriga, Belize via Pen Cayetano / Turtle Shell Band (c.1978-1980); coordinates point to Dangriga. Not itself UNESCO-inscribed. Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Sique
era: Popular late 19th c.; collected by National Folklore Office from 1955
tagline: Honduras's mestizo 'national' folk rhythm and dance.
profile: The sique (also xique, quixique) is a mestizo/Creole folk dance of southern and southeastern Honduras (Choluteca region; village of Cacautare, municipality of Pespire). It is a ternary rhythm in 3/4 with the accent on the first beat (distinguishing it from the waltz), a syncretism of European salon music adapted by mestizo musicians on transculturated instruments - strings (guitar, mandolin), accordion, caramba, violin and later marimba and wind band. Its onomatopoeic name derives from the sound of the caite (sandal) rubbing the ground while dancing. It is danced at rural festivals and patron-saint celebrations (Smithsonian Folkways; Hondurasfolklore.com).
insight: Folklorist Rafael Manzanares Aguilar (1974) recorded that the dance takes its name from the onomatopoeic sound of the caite/sandal rubbing the ground; it is regarded as the national rhythm representative of Honduran mestizo folklore.
notes: No dedicated English Wikipedia or Wikidata. Coordinates: Pespire/Cacautare, Choluteca. Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: El Barreno
era: Traditional Creole/mestizo folk song-dance; authenticated by National Folklore Office (mid-late 20th c.)
tagline: Beloved Honduran Creole courtship folk song-dance.
profile: El Barreno is a Creole/mestizo folk dance and song of western Honduras (cited from Colorado, Santa Rosa de Copan, department of Copan), used in the manner of a waltz in folk-dance choreographies. It is a collective couples dance with a playful courtship theme, the man pursuing/courting the woman. Music is carried by guitar and marimba-based mestizo ensembles. It is among the most widely sung folk pieces in Honduran civic and school events and is performed at festivals and patriotic celebrations (Hondurasfolklore.com; RedHonduras.com; El Heraldo).
insight: Honduras's El Heraldo notes El Barreno is among the autochthonous legends of the towns performed by the Cuadro Nacional de Danza; its authorship is unknown and it is sometimes also claimed as Salvadoran.

sources.title: El Barreno - Honduras Folklore Perfil de Danza

notes: Competing Salvadoran attribution noted. No dedicated English Wikipedia or Wikidata. Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Mascaro (Wanaragua)
era: Afro-Indigenous Garifuna masked dance; St. Vincent origin c.1773 (oral tradition); living tradition
tagline: The Garifuna masked warrior dance (Wanaragua / John Canoe).
profile: El mascaro / baile de mascaro is the Spanish name for the Garifuna Wanaragua (also Maladi, Yancunu; 'John Canoe'/Jankunu in English) - an Afro-Indigenous masked warrior dance of the Caribbean coast (Trujillo, Cusuna and other Garifuna towns). Danced primarily by men wearing wire pink-faced 'European' masks, elaborate feathered headdresses (wababa) and women's dress with ribbons, with cowrie/shell rattles at the knees. Performed during Christmas and New Year (and patron-saint) festivities, with strong knee and open-arm movements; uniquely the dancer leads and the primero/segunda drummers follow in call-and-response (WilderUtopia; Constellations academic journal).
insight: The dance recalls Garifuna warriors who dressed as women to deceive British troops in St. Vincent; OFRANEH and diaspora artists describe the flashier and more aggressive Honduran Wanaragua style, accompanied by two drums and knee shells.

sources.title: Garifuna People: Masked Dance for the New Year (Wanaragua)
Wanaragua: Resistance, Recognition and Resilience Among the Garinagu

notes: CLASSIFICATION CORRECTION: 'el mascaro' is the Spanish name for the Garifuna (Afro-Indigenous) Wanaragua / John Canoe dance, NOT a mestizo folk dance. The separate mestizo masked dance most associated with Comayagua is the 'Baile de los Diablitos' (Moros y Cristianos / San Sebastian, first staged 1644). UNESCO qualifier: constituent of ICH file 00001 (Garifuna culture inscription), not separately inscribed. Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Csardas
era: Developed early 19th century (1830s) from 18th-century verbunkos roots
tagline: Hungary's national couple dance: slow lassu into fast friss.
profile: The csardas is the national couple dance of Hungary, danced across the Hungarian language area (Hungary, Transylvania, Slovakia, Vojvodina). It is performed by couples forming one large or several smaller circles, with improvised regional figures. Its defining structure alternates a slow lassu section and a fast friss in 2/4 or 4/4 time; movements include the two-step csardaslepcs, the lippentOS knee-bend half-turn, partner whirling and playful separation. Music is provided by a string band of violins, violas and double bass, often Romani-led. It is danced at weddings, community events and tanchaz revival gatherings (UNESCO; Britannica).
insight: The name derives from csarda (roadside tavern); promoted by the middle nobility from the 1830s as a national dance asserting Hungarian autonomy against Habsburg domination, it evolved out of the verbunkos genre (UNESCO ICH; Britannica).
notes: UNESCO RL file 01892, inscribed 5 December 2024 at 19.COM (Asuncion, Paraguay). On Hungary's National List of ICH since 2019. Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Verbunkos
tagline: 18th-century Hungarian military recruitment dance and music.
profile: Verbunkos is an 18th-century Hungarian men's dance and music genre that originated as a military recruitment (German Werbung) display in the Habsburg-ruled Kingdom of Hungary. A group of hussars performed it, the leading sergeant opening with slow movements and the youngest soldiers concluding with energetic jumps. It exists in solo and circle (korverbunk) forms, rich in improvisation, with lively athletic footwork, spinning and boot-slapping. Musically it pairs a slow lassu (dotted rhythm) with a fast friss (virtuosic running notes), often using the Hungarian/Gypsy minor scale with augmented seconds; ensembles feature lead violin (primas), cimbalom, viola/kontra, bass and clarinet. It traditionally opened the dance cycle (tancrend) (Wikipedia; RomArchive).
insight: The name comes from German Werbung (recruitment); the genre was the basis of the 19th-century Hungarian national style and the direct precursor of the csardas. The Romani composer Janos Bihari (1764-1827) is its most celebrated exponent.
notes: NOT UNESCO-inscribed. Distinct from csardas. Do NOT confuse with the UNESCO-listed Czech/Slovak Slovacko Verbunk (Q40265, file 00147, 2008). Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Karikazo
tagline: Hungarian women's a cappella sung circle dance.
profile: The karikazo is a Hungarian women's circle/chain dance, most popular in Transdanubia and the southern and northern regions of Hungary. Women hold each other (often a front basket hold) and step a few simple motifs - walking, rocking, csardas steps and turning - while singing folk songs in unison; there is no instrumental accompaniment. The song matters more than the steps, with innumerable lyric stanzas. It was performed during breaks in dance events and during Lent, when instrumental dancing was prohibited. Closely connected to medieval European circle dances; in some areas it is replaced by the korcsardas (Wikipedia; Society of Folk Dance Historians).
insight: Hungarian dance scholar Gyorgy Martin noted that peasants did not consider stepping a round dance to be dancing in the strict sense, reflecting its status as song-with-movement (Folkdance Footnotes, quoting Martin; Wikipedia).
notes: CORRECTION: karikazo is NOT individually UNESCO-inscribed. The 2011 UNESCO inscription was file 00515 'Tanchaz method' (Register of Good Safeguarding Practices, 6.COM) - a transmission model, not the dance itself. Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Legényes
era: Old stratum (medieval roots; documented forms 20th century)
tagline: Transylvanian Hungarian men's virtuoso solo dance.
profile: The legEnyes (Romanian: fecioreasca) is a men's solo dance of the ethnic-Hungarian population of Transylvania, especially the Kalotaszeg, Szilagysag and MezOseg regions around Cluj. Danced freestyle one man at a time in front of the band, it consists of points (pont) - dance phrases of typically 4-8, each of four 4-count parts, concluded with closing formulae and tied to eight-measure musical periods. Rich in jumping, heel-clicking, stamping and boot-slapping. Women participate by standing in lines and shouting/singing verses. Accompaniment is a fiddle-led string band. It is regarded as the most virtuoso men's dance in the Carpathian Basin (Wikipedia; Magyar Neprajzi Lexikon).
insight: The most celebrated legEnyes dancer was Istvan Matyas 'Mundruc' of Magyarvista in Kalotaszeg; good dancers know and combine around 30-50 different points (Wikipedia; Transylvania Now).
notes: Has its own English Wikipedia article (verified). Catalogued under Hungary as cultural origin though the home region (Kalotaszeg/Cluj area) is in present-day Romania. Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Ugros
tagline: Oldest Hungarian dance type, an athletic jumping dance.
profile: Ugros (jumping) is one of the oldest strata of Hungarian folk dance, found throughout Hungary, especially Transdanubia. It is an athletic jumping dance in 2/4 meter with an off-beat accent (comparable to polkas), performed solo, in couples (e.g. Dunantuli Ugros) or in marching forms. Movements are characterised by leaping, running and heel-clicking. Origins lie in medieval weapon dances; the family includes related herdsmen's dances and forms such as kanasztanc, haromugrOs and cinege. Accompaniment ranges from bagpipe and zither to full string/Romani bands (Wikipedia; Smithsonian Folklife Festival).
insight: Gyorgy Martin classified ugros as a foundational old-style type from which the virtuoso Transylvanian lads' dances (legEnyes) developed; it persists where the newer verbunk did not take hold (Wikipedia; Society of Folk Dance Historians).
notes: Wikidata Q7877922 has only a label (no description string) but the enwiki sitelink confirms it is the correct dance. Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Sean-nos dance
era: Traditional (old style); the term first applied to the dance in 1976
tagline: Relaxed, improvised, low-to-the-floor Irish solo dance.
profile: Sean-nos (old style) dance is an older, casual, improvised solo Irish dance tradition, strongly associated with the Connacht/Connemara Gaeltacht. It is characterised by low-to-the-ground footwork, improvised steps, free and loose movement of the arms, and an emphasis on a batter that sounds out the accented beat. It is danced in small spaces - traditionally a door off its hinges, a table top, a barrel or a stool - and follows the music closely. It contrasts with competitive stepdance (rigid torso, stylised costumes). Music is typically fiddle and accordion. It is performed solo at ceilidhs, sessions and competitions such as Oireachtas na Samhna (Wikipedia; University of Galway).
insight: Distinct from competitive stepdance, sean-nos is considered to have influenced modern tap dance; it survives most strongly in the Connemara Gaeltacht, where the term was applied to the dance form in 1976 (Wikipedia; Brendan Breathnach, Folk Music and Dances of Ireland).
notes: Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Ceili dance
era: Codified during the Gaelic revival, early 20th century
tagline: Irish group social dances for 2 to 16 dancers.
profile: Ceili dances are participatory group social dances of Ireland performed by groups of two to sixteen people, to reels, jigs and hornpipes played on fiddle, bodhran, concertina, accordion, flute or whistle. Based on heys, round dances, long dances and quadrilles, they were revived during the Gaelic revival and codified by the Irish Dancing Commission (An Coimisiun le Rinced Gaelacha). Footwork is relatively simple; emphasis is on figures and formations - long lines, circles and progressive movement. Examples include The Walls of Limerick, The Siege of Ennis and The Waves of Tory (Wikipedia; CLRG).
insight: An Coimisiun le Rinci Gaelacha (founded 1927) published its first handbook of ten ceili dances in 1939; the 30 standardised 'book' dances now appear in Ar Rinci Ceili, which replaced Ar Rinci Foirne on 1 September 2015 (CLRG; Wikipedia).
notes: needs_eyes_on: no standalone EN Wikipedia 'Ceili dance' article; documented within the broader 'Ceilidh' article (which also covers Scottish). Wikidata Q1150334 represents the broader Irish/Scottish ceilidh gathering - consider whether a narrower Irish-specific item is preferred. Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Irish set dance
era: 19th century (from early-1800s quadrilles); revived from the 1970s
tagline: Irish quadrille-derived social dance for four couples.
profile: Irish set dancing (country set dancing) consists of dances derived from continental quadrilles and contredanses, adapted to Irish reels, jigs, polkas, hornpipes and slides with sean-nos-style stepping. It is danced in square sets of four couples (eight people); both the eight dancers and the dance are called a set. A set is a sequence of several figures, each with repeated parts. Sets are named for regional origins - e.g. the Clare Set, North Kerry Set, Corofin Plain Set, South Galway Set, Connemara Reel Set and Clare Lancers Set. Unlike modern square dancing it generally requires no caller, as the figure sequence is fixed (Wikipedia; Irish Traditional Music Archive).
insight: Quadrilles reached Ireland in the early 1800s - local Limerick tradition credits soldiers returning after 1815 from the Napoleonic wars - and were localised into set-dances, which declined in the early 20th century and were revived from the 1970s (Wikipedia; ITMA).

sources.title: Music for the Quadrille in Ireland, 1800s

notes: Wikipedia article is titled 'Irish set dancing'; Wikidata Q19902510 is the precise item distinct from general 'set dance' (Q1425009). Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Irish stepdance
tagline: Competitive solo Irish dance: rigid torso, fast footwork.
profile: Irish stepdance is a competitive and performance solo dance characterised by a stiff, rigid upper body and fast, precise footwork, performed solo or in groups. It uses two shoe types: hard shoes (fibreglass tips/heels, tap-like) and soft shoes (ghillies/reel shoes). Dances divide into hard-shoe (hornpipe, treble jig, treble reel) and soft-shoe (reel, slip jig, light jig, single jig) styles. It was codified from the Munster style by An Coimisiun le Rinci Gaelacha (CLRG, founded 1927; first met 1930) and is governed competitively by CLRG, IDTANA and others; competitions are feiseanna (Wikipedia; IDTANA).
insight: Riverdance premiered as the 7-minute interval act of the 1994 Eurovision Song Contest on 30 April 1994 at the Point Theatre, Dublin (score by Bill Whelan, dancers Jean Butler and Michael Flatley), electrifying 300 million TV viewers; the full stage show opened 9 February 1995 (Wikipedia).

sources.title: Glossary
sources.publisher: Irish Dance Teachers Association of North America (IDTANA)

notes: Slug 'step-dance-ireland' does NOT collide with the umbrella 'irish-dance' slug. Q1865501 is the specific competitive solo subform. Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Tarantella
tagline: Umbrella of lively southern Italian folk dances in 6/8.
profile: Tarantella is an umbrella term for a cluster of fast folk dances of southern Italy (Calabria, Campania, Sicily, Apulia), typically in 6/8 time and accompanied by tambourines, mandolins, guitars and accordion. Regional forms include the Neapolitan courtship tarantella, Calabrian sonu a ballu, Campanian tammurriata and Salentine pizzica. It is linked to tarantism, a phenomenon in which the bite of the wolf spider was believed curable through frenzied dancing. Today it appears at weddings, festivals and as a couple/circle courtship dance (Britannica; Italian American Museum of LA).
insight: Italian ethnographer Ernesto de Martino documented in the 1950s how tarantism and tarantella performances often correlated with women experiencing personal and domestic crises, theorizing the dance as an emotional and communal outlet.
notes: UMBRELLA entry. Not UNESCO-inscribed; a 2019 nomination attempt did not result in inscription.

name: Pizzica
era: Name first attested 1797; tarantism-linked tradition from the 14th century; modern revival from the 1990s
tagline: Salentine (Puglia) form of tarantella tied to taranta healing.
profile: Pizzica is the folk dance of the Salento peninsula in Apulia, driven by the hypnotic tamburello (tambourine), violin, chitarra battente and organetto. It exists as a courtship couple dance (pizzica-pizzica), a group dance, and the combative pizzica scherma (sword/fencing dance). Historically it accompanied the taranta healing ritual for the tarantata. Since 1998 it is celebrated at the Notte della Taranta festival in Melpignano. Later spreading across Apulia, Calabria and eastern Basilicata (Wikipedia; Italian Traditions).
insight: The word pizzica first appears in a 1797 source describing an evening dance held by Taranto nobility in honor of King Ferdinand IV of Bourbon, predating its 19th-century codification within tarantism healing practices.

sources.title: The Pizzica, an Italian popular dance

notes: Distinct from the general tarantella. Not UNESCO-inscribed.

name: Saltarello
era: Documented from the late 14th century; living folk form today
tagline: The hopping/jumping dance of central Italy.
profile: Saltarello is a lively triple-meter dance named for its leaping step (Italian saltare, to jump). First recorded in a late-14th-century Tuscan manuscript (British Library Add MS 29987), it survived as a folk dance in central-southern Italy - Lazio (Rome Testaccio, Carnival), Abruzzo, Molise, Marche and Romagna. It is danced by couples to zampogna bagpipe, organetto and tamburello, accelerating to test endurance (Wikipedia; Britannica).
insight: After witnessing the Roman Carnival of 1831, Felix Mendelssohn incorporated the saltarello into the finale of his Symphony No. 4, the Italian Symphony; the fourth movement is marked Saltarello: Presto in A minor in 6/8 (Wikipedia).
notes: Also has historical forms in Spain and Portugal. Not UNESCO-inscribed.

name: Furlana
tagline: Friulian/Venetian courtship couple dance in 6/8.
profile: The furlana is a fast couple dance in compound duple (6/8) time from Friuli-Venezia Giulia, strongly associated with Venice. A courtship dance in which partners repeatedly approach and flee, circling with rapid footwork; it may have Slavonic roots. It existed as folk dance, ballroom dance and stage dance, and was introduced to France by Andre Campra (1697, 1699). Baroque composers including Couperin, J.S. Bach and Ravel wrote forlanes (Wikipedia; Oxford International Encyclopedia of Dance).
insight: Casanova recorded in his Memoires (Vol. 1, Paris 1880, p. 420) dancing the furlana in Constantinople in 1744: after six in a row I was burning hot and out of breath, for there is no national dance more violent.
notes: Not UNESCO-inscribed.

name: Monferrina
era: Popular in Piedmont from the 18th century; vogue in England/France c.1800-1810s
tagline: Piedmontese couple dance in 6/8 from Monferrato.
profile: Monferrina is a lively 6/8 folk dance named after Montferrat (Monferrato) in Piedmont, spreading across northern Italy and into Switzerland. It opens with two circular promenades arm-in-arm using a lively march step, then partners join both hands for a cross-step with bent knees, with bows and mimed teasing. It became an English country dance c.1800 (as monfrina, monfreda, manfredina) and inspired composed sets such as Clementi's 12 Monferrine, Op. 49 (Wikipedia; Traditional Tune Archive).
insight: The dance gave Italian the idiom fare la manfrina (to make a manfrina = a tediously long-winded speech), derived from the dialect name for the Monferrina.
notes: Not UNESCO-inscribed.

name: Ballo Sardo
era: Prehistoric roots (Ozieri culture c.3200-2700 BCE depictions); documented from 1805; living tradition
tagline: Traditional Sardinian circle/chain dance.
profile: Ballu sardu (also ballu tundu, round dance) is the umbrella of traditional Sardinian folk dances, performed in a closed or open circle with dancers holding hands (a manu tenta); the upper body stays rigid while the legs execute rhythmic steps, leaps and tremulous jolts. Accompanied by the launeddas (triple-pipe reed instrument), organittu accordion, and/or polyphonic cantu a tenore. The introduction is in 2/4 and the dance in 6/8. It features at weddings, harvest celebrations and saints day festivals, mainly in central Sardinia (Barbagia, Baronia, Marghine) (Wikipedia; Music of Sardinia Wikipedia).
insight: Strict hand-holding etiquette historically governed the circle: married or engaged couples could clasp palms with fingers entwined, but a man could not do so with a young girl or another man's wife.
notes: UMBRELLA entry. UNESCO false for the DANCE. The accompanying Canto a tenore singing WAS UNESCO-inscribed (2008, ICH ref. No. 00165) but that is the singing not the dance.

name: Jonkonnu
era: Documented from 1774 (Edward Long); slavery era to present; revived via 1951 Gleaner competition
tagline: Jamaican masked Christmas street masquerade and procession dance.
profile: Jonkonnu is one of Jamaica's oldest performance traditions: a masked street masquerade and procession danced at Christmas (Christmas Day, Boxing Day, New Year's Day), of West African (John Canoe) origin blended with European mumming. Bands of costumed characters - King and Queen, Cow Head, Horse Head, Pitchy Patchy, Belly Woman, Devil, Actor Boy - dance jigs, polkas, one drop and marching steps, accompanied by bamboo fife, bass and rattling (kettle) drums, shakas and graters (Wikipedia; Jamaica Information Service).
insight: The John Canoe Riots of 1841, when the Mayor of Kingston banned the Jonkonnu parade, became the most serious civil disorder in the years immediately after Emancipation, forcing the Mayor to take refuge on a ship in the harbour.
notes: Wikidata Q1612069 (Junkanoo) is a combined Bahamas/Jamaica item; flag for eyes-on review for a Jamaica-specific split. Related to but distinct from the Garifuna Wanaragua. Not UNESCO-inscribed.

name: Kumina
era: Mid-to-late 19th century (post-emancipation, c.1840s-1860s Kongo arrivals); living tradition
tagline: Afro-Jamaican BaKongo-derived ritual dance, drumming and religion.
profile: Kumina is an Afro-Jamaican religion, dance and drumming tradition derived from BaKongo (West Central African) people brought as enslaved and indentured labourers in the post-emancipation era. Centered in eastern Jamaica (St. Thomas, plus Portland, St. Mary, St. Catherine and Kingston), ceremonies feature dancing in a counterclockwise circle around two drums - the kbandu (bandu) and the playin cyas - beaten with palms and heel pressure, with shakas, graters and catta sticks. Songs use Kikongo (country) or Jamaican Creole (bailo). The near-erect dance posture inches the feet along the ground with hip propulsion; spirit possession is called myal (Wikipedia; ACIJ/JMB).
insight: Linguistic analysis confirms Kumina's direct BaKongo roots: Bantu-language specialist Hazel Carter identified 47 of 48 Kumina song words as Congo words, for example ngoma (drum) and Ndzaambi amphuungu (God).

sources.title: ACIJ/JMB Fact Sheet - Kumina
sources.publisher: African-Caribbean Institute of Jamaica / Jamaica Memory Bank

notes: Sacred/ritual tradition - treat respectfully. Kumina drumming heavily influenced Rastafari Nyahbinghi and Jamaican popular music. Not UNESCO-inscribed.

name: Mento
tagline: Rural Jamaican folk music and couple dance predating ska/reggae.
profile: Mento is Jamaica's foundational rural folk music and dance, a Creole fusion of African rhythms and European folk forms (including the quadrille) that predates and shaped ska and reggae. Acoustic bands use guitar, banjo, hand drums and the rhumba box (a large box-mbira bass). The mento dance is an informal couple dance with bent-knee footwork, hip swaying and pelvic circling to a 3-3-2 rhythmic pattern, performed at festivals, nine-nights, house and tea parties. Often confused with Trinidadian calypso, with which it was conflated commercially in the 1950s (Wikipedia; Jamaica Information Service).
insight: Mento was Jamaica's first commercially recorded music; many calypso hits popularized abroad by Harry Belafonte - such as Day-O (Banana Boat Song) - were in fact remakes of classic Jamaican mento tunes.
notes: Not UNESCO-inscribed.

name: Dancehall
tagline: Jamaican popular dance culture tied to dancehall music.
profile: Dancehall is Jamaica's popular dance culture born in the late-1970s inner-city Kingston sound-system scene (Trench Town, Rose Town, Denham Town). Dancers continuously invent named steps - Bogle, Willie Bounce, Butterfly, Gully Creeper, Dutty Wine, Nuh Linga - performed at street dances and stage shows to dancehall (bashment/ragga) music. The movement vocabulary draws on Kumina, Nyahbinghi and Jonkonnu roots and has exerted major global influence, feeding into hip-hop and pop choreography (Wikipedia; Jamaica Observer).
insight: Gerald Bogle Levy (1964-2005) of Kingston's Black Roses Crew, called by Beenie Man the greatest dancer of all time, created more named dancehall moves than any other figure, including the signature Bogle dance.
notes: Dance QID left blank (needs_eyes_on). Wikidata Q474027 = dancehall MUSIC genre, NOT the dance. No Wikidata item exists for dancehall dance culture as an umbrella. Bogle dance Q4937657 is the closest dance-specific anchor. Not UNESCO-inscribed.

name: Nyahbinghi
era: 1940s-1950s codification (first island-wide grounation 1958); living sacred tradition
tagline: Rastafari ceremonial drumming, chant and dance (grounation).
profile: Nyahbinghi is the sacred ceremonial drumming, chanting and dance tradition of the Rastafari movement, performed at gatherings called grounations (groundations) on holy days such as Haile Selassie I's birthday (July 23). A three-drum ensemble - bass (thunder), funde and repeater (akete/kete) - sets a slow, heartbeat-like 4/4 pulse over call-and-response psalmody, with shakers/shaka; participants chant, pray and dance through the night. Rooted in Afro-Jamaican Burru and Kumina drumming, the style was codified by Count Ossie in the Wareika Hills and became the rhythmic basis of ska, rocksteady and roots reggae (Wikipedia; Rastafari RE:ONLINE).
insight: Count Ossie's hand drums first appeared on the 1959/60 ska recording Oh Carolina by the Folkes Brothers (produced by Prince Buster), introducing Nyahbinghi's ceremonial pulse to popular Jamaican music.
notes: Sacred tradition - treat respectfully. Do NOT use Q3346560 (the legendary East African woman/Rastafari concept). Q17000682 is the correct item for the Nyabinghi rhythm/dance article. Not UNESCO-inscribed.

name: Latvian folk dance
era: Traditional; roots in pre-Christian Baltic tribal culture; stage-formalized from the 1930s, dance festival component added in 1948
tagline: Latvia's circle and couple dances of rural life.
profile: Latvian folk dance (tautas dejas) encompasses circle, couple, and line dances rooted in the agrarian and communal life of the Baltic tribes. Movements emphasize intricate footwork, stiffened hips/upper body, geometric floor patterns, partner-switching, stomps and claps, turns and twirls. Music uses kokle (Baltic psaltery), bagpipe, and fiddle; many dances accompany dainas (folk songs). Performed at weddings, social gatherings, and festivals such as Janis, and showcased every five years at the Riga Song and Dance Festival (Latvian Song and Dance Festival Wikipedia; UNESCO ICH).
insight: The Song and Dance Celebration tradition was proclaimed a UNESCO Masterpiece in 2003 and inscribed on the Representative List in 2008 (file 00087, jointly with Estonia and Lithuania). Per UNESCO ICH file 00087, these grand events assemble as many as 40,000 singers and dancers.
notes: No dedicated EN Wikipedia article (Wikidata Q27556076 has only lvwiki sitelink). UNESCO inscription is for the Baltic Song and Dance Celebrations festival tradition (file 00087), not 'Latvian folk dance' as a standalone element. Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Ligo
era: Ancient pre-Christian solstice tradition; documented since the 16th century
tagline: Latvian midsummer celebration of songs, fires, and dance.
profile: Ligo/Janis is the Latvian summer solstice (St. John's) celebration on the night of 23-24 June. It is a cultural celebration that INCLUDES dance, not a dance in itself. Festivities feature ligo songs (dainas with the ligo refrain), bonfires, oak-leaf and flower wreaths, Janu cheese and beer, and country dancing (zalumballe) including circle dances around fires. Held in the countryside, it celebrates fertility, nature, and renewal (Janis Wikipedia; Latvian Culture Canon).
insight: Ligo is grammatically a verb/interjection, not a noun; linguist Emilis Melngailis popularized Ligosvektki in 1900 but later regretted it as displacing the older name Janis.

sources.title: Midsummer's Eve (Janis or Ligo svetki) in Latvia

notes: FLAG: this is a cultural celebration/holiday that includes dance and song, not a dance itself. No separate UNESCO inscription. Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Sutartines
era: Ancient; polyphony predating Lithuania's Christianization; most examples recorded 19th-20th c.
tagline: Lithuanian polyphonic multipart songs with circle dances.
profile: Sutartines (from sutarti, to be in concordance) are archaic Lithuanian polyphonic multipart songs, primarily a SINGING tradition performed by women in northeastern Lithuania (Auktaitija). Songs have simple melodies with two to five pitches, comprising a meaningful main text and a refrain that may include nonce words; there are almost forty different styles. They include accompanying dance: uncomplicated, moderate movements such as walking in a circle or star while linking arms and stamping feet. Men perform instrumental versions on skudutiai (panpipes), kankles (zither), horns, and wooden trumpets (UNESCO ICH; Lithuanian folk music Wikipedia).
insight: Inscribed on the UNESCO Representative List in 2010 (5.COM, file 00433). The nomination file was drafted by ethnomusicologist Dr. Daiva Vyciniene of the Lithuanian Folk Culture Centre.
notes: FLAG: primarily a MUSIC/SONG tradition (vocal polyphony) that also involves dance. UNESCO file 00433, inscribed 2010 (5.COM). No standalone EN Wikipedia article. Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Lithuanian folk dance
era: Traditional; stage-formalized from the National Awakening (first staged dance Suktinis, St. Petersburg 1903); first festival Kaunas 1937
tagline: Lithuania's symmetrical ring and couple folk dances.
profile: Lithuanian folk dance comprises sokiai (ordinary dances) and rateliai (ring dances), noted for symmetry of music, movement, and pattern. Steps are light and mostly footwork (over 30 steps including polka, waltz, and running steps); turns are gentle. Pairs embrace polka/waltz-style or link arms. Forms include suktinis (twirler, the finale dance), blezdingele (swallow dance), sadute (bride's dance), kepurine (hat dance), and the imported polka and waltz. Accompanied by kankles, fiddle, basetle, lamzdeliai, and later accordion (Lithuanian National Culture Centre; Baltic Song and Dance Celebrations UNESCO).
insight: The first staged Lithuanian folk dance, Suktinis, was performed in St. Petersburg in 1903; the tradition is covered by the UNESCO-inscribed Baltic Song and Dance Celebrations (proclaimed 2003, inscribed 2008, file 00087).
notes: No dedicated EN Wikipedia article (Wikidata Q25497437 has only ltwiki sitelink). UNESCO inscription is via Baltic Song and Dance Celebrations (file 00087), not a standalone element. Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Ghana (Malta)
era: Traditional folk tradition; documented from 1838 (George Percy Badger); first studio recordings 1931
tagline: Maltese improvisational folk singing duels with guitar.
profile: Ghana (pronounced ana) is the traditional folk SINGING tradition of Malta, not primarily a dance. It uses a tight, strained vocal style and includes spirtu pront (an improvised rhyming song-duel), ghana tal-fatt (narrative ballads), and la Bormliza (a high-pitched style historically sung by women). Per UNESCO ICH file 01681, the most popular form is the quick-wit ghana, an improvised duel between one or two pairs of singers, focusing on rhymes, convincing argumentation and witty repartee. Accompanying instruments historically included guitar, rabbaba (friction drum), zaqq (bagpipe), and tanbur (frame drum). Performed in wine bars, village feasts, and open-air celebrations (Ghana Wikipedia; UNESCO ICH).
insight: L-Ghana was inscribed on the UNESCO Representative List in 2021 (16.COM, file 01681), which calls it vital to the transmission and development of the unique Semitic Maltese language; UNESCO inscription confirmed, contrary to initial expectation.
notes: FLAG: primarily a SINGING tradition, not a dance. UNESCO file 01681, inscribed 2021 (16.COM). Distinct from West African Ghana. Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Maltese folk dance
era: Documented from the 18th-19th c.; parata linked to commemoration of the 1565 Great Siege
tagline: Malta's traditional dances: parata sword dance and il-Maltija.
profile: Maltese folk dance encompasses the parata (an ancient sword dance opening Carnival, commemorating the 1565 Maltese/Knights victory over the Ottomans, danced by two concentric rings of Christians and Turks striking wooden swords to a bass drum, violin, and bagpipe) and il-Maltija, the 18th-century country dance (kuntradanza) regarded as the Maltese national dance. Also cited is the gnien (garden) dance. Performed at Carnival, weddings, and folklore festivals; instruments include zaqq (bagpipe) and tambur (Maltese folklore Wikipedia; Smithsonian Folkways).
insight: Il-Maltija emerged as a symbol of Maltese nationalism by the mid-19th century, with documentation tied to the Governor's Palace and high-society balls; its peasant-versus-aristocratic origins remain debated.
notes: UMBRELLA entry. No dedicated EN Wikipedia article and no Wikidata item found. Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Bele
era: Colonial slavery-era origins (Martinique under French rule since 1635); revived in the 1980s
tagline: Martinique's Afro-Caribbean drum-song-dance of resistance.
profile: The Martinican bele (bel air) is an Afro-Caribbean ritual and social drum-song-dance tradition rooted in the music of enslaved Africans on sugar plantations. A sware bele is organized around the tambour bele (a goatskin drum the player straddles), the ti-bwa (two sticks struck on the drum's back), a lead singer (lavwa / voix douvan), a chorus (reponde / voix deyye), and dancers. The drummer (tanbouyye) dialogues with the lead dancer. Styles include bele pitche, gran bele, belya, bele li Sid, and kalinda. Themes of courtship, fertility, labor, and liberation (Bele Wikipedia; Martinique Tourisme; The Conversation).
insight: Per Dr. Camee Maddox-Wingfield (The Conversation, 2022), the 1848 abolition in Martinique was sparked by the arrest of Romain, an enslaved man who refused to comply with his master's ban on beating drums; sware bele ceremonies on Emancipation Day (May 22) preserve this history.

sources.title: The Martinican bele dance - a celebration of land, spirit and liberation

notes: Distinct from St. Lucia bele and from Guadeloupean gwoka. Not UNESCO-inscribed (only Martinique ICH element is the Martinique yole, 2020). Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Mazurka Creole
era: Mazurka arrived Martinique late 1800s/early 20th c.; Creolized form popularized 1920s
tagline: Martinique's syncopated Creolized mazurka couple dance.
profile: The mazurka creole (mazouk) is the Antillean/Martinican Creolized version of the European (Polish) mazurka, a partner dance and music style of the Saint-Pierre repertoire. It comprises two figures - le pique (fast) and la nuit (slower) - and differs rhythmically from the European mazurka, felt in 6/8 rather than 3/4. It is a fluid couple dance with swaying, suspension, and turns, played by orchestras of violin, clarinet, trombone, and banjo and generally sung in Creole. Mazouk is the etymological root of the word zouk (AZ Martinique; Afrisson).
insight: Alexandre Stellio popularized the mazurka creole in the 1920s by adding a new figure, la nuit; the Creole form is felt in 6/8 whereas the metropolitan mazurka is in 3/4, making it a genuinely distinct dance tradition.
notes: No dedicated EN Wikipedia article or Wikidata item. Distinct from the European mazurka. Mazouk is the etymological root of zouk. Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Biguine (Martinique)
era: Originated Saint-Pierre, Martinique, 19th century; Paris popularity in the 1930s
tagline: Martinican dance-music fusing bele with French ballroom steps.
profile: Biguine originated in Saint-Pierre, Martinique in the 19th century, fusing West African traditional music genres (bele) with 19th-century French ballroom dance steps. It comprises drum biguine (bidgin bele: bele drum + tibwa, call-and-response, nasal vocals) and orchestrated biguine (clarinet, trombone, banjo). Danced by couples, with the woman in a doudou dress. It achieved fame in 1930s Paris via Alexandre Stellio and Sam Castandet (Wikipedia).
insight: Biguine is one shared Martinique/Guadeloupe tradition (Martinican in origin, Saint-Pierre); it is the same tradition already catalogued under slug 'biguine' (Guadeloupe entry).
notes: DUPLICATE of catalog entry slug 'biguine' (Guadeloupe). Biguine is one shared Martinique/Guadeloupe tradition originating in Saint-Pierre, Martinique. Kept per standing rule (keep all rows, flag duplicates). Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Jarabe Tapatio
tagline: Mexico's national courtship dance, the Jalisco hat dance.
profile: Originating as a courtship dance in Guadalajara, Jalisco in the 19th century, the Jarabe Tapatio is Mexico's national dance. A couple pantomimes courtship: the woman (in china poblana dress) first rejects, then accepts the man (dressed as a charro), who tosses his sombrero to the floor for her to dance around. Movements feature flirtatious zapateado footwork, hops, slides and kicks. The standard melody was composed by Jesus Gonzalez Rubio; music is performed by mariachi or string ensembles. It is performed at civic celebrations and taught in schools nationwide (Wikipedia; Britannica).
insight: It became internationally famous after Russian ballerina Anna Pavlova premiered it en pointe in her piece Fantasia mexicana at the Teatro Arbeu on March 18, 1919. It was officially proclaimed Mexico's national dance in 1924.
notes: English Wikipedia article titled 'Mexican hat dance' (not 'Jarabe Tapatio'). Not UNESCO-inscribed. Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Son Jarocho
tagline: Veracruz folk music and zapateado dance of the fandango.
profile: A regional folk music-and-dance tradition of the Sotavento region of southern Veracruz (extending into Oaxaca and Tabasco). It blends indigenous, Spanish (fandango) and West African elements, in sesquialtera 6/8 meter. Its core social setting is the fandango, a community party held around a wooden platform (tarima) on which dancers perform percussive zapateado. Instruments include the jarana jarocha, requinto jarocho, arpa jarocha, leona and tarima. The most widely known son is La Bamba (Wikipedia; Smithsonian Folkways).
insight: A late-1970s movimiento jaranero revival, led by groups such as Mono Blanco and centered on the Tlacotalpan jaranero encounter, restored the tradition and spread it to Mexico City and the United States.

sources.title: Music of Mexico Vol. 3: La Huasteca

notes: Not UNESCO-inscribed. Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Danza de los Viejitos
tagline: Michoacan Purepecha 'Dance of the Little Old Men'.
profile: A traditional Purepecha folk dance from Michoacan, especially the Lake Patzcuaro village of Jaracuaro. Young men wear wooden masks of smiling old men, white manta clothing, colorful sarapes, ribboned hats and carry canes, exaggeratedly mimicking the frailty of old age before bursting into vigorous zapateado. Originally a ritual (T'arche Uarakua) honoring the old sun/fire god Tata Jurhiata for good harvests, danced by four petamunis (community elders). Accompanied by string instruments (violin, guitar, vihuela) and pirekua songs (Wikipedia; CID/Secretaria de Cultura).
insight: The comic spasms and stooped walk give way to energetic stamping that, according to Purepecha tradition, symbolizes the enduring strength of the culture; the dance is performed at Christmas, Candlemas and patron-saint feasts.
aliases: Dance of the Little Old Men

sources.title: Danza de los Viejitos. Registros en Jaracuaro

notes: Dance itself not inscribed; the related Purepecha songs were UNESCO-inscribed in 2010 as 'Pirekua, traditional song of the P'urhepecha' (RL/00398). Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Huapango
tagline: Huasteca music-and-dance with zapateado on a wooden tarima.
profile: A family of Mexican music-and-dance styles from the Huasteca region (Hidalgo, San Luis Potosi, Veracruz, Tamaulipas, Queretaro, Puebla). The name derives from Nahuatl cuauhpanco, 'on top of the wood,' referring to the tarima platform on which dancers perform zapateado. The classic huapango huasteco is played by a trio huasteco - violin, jarana huasteca and guitarra quinta huapanguera - with falsetto singing and a complex sesquialtera rhythm mixing duple and triple meter. Variants include huapango arribeno, huapango norteno and huapango de mariachi (Wikipedia; Smithsonian Folkways).
insight: Huapango overlaps heavily with the term son huasteco - historically huapangos were songs with fixed lyrics while sones huastecos were vehicles for improvised verse.

sources.title: La Huasteca: Huapangos y Sones Huastecos

notes: Significant overlap with son huasteco. Not UNESCO-inscribed. Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Concheros
tagline: Syncretic Mexican ritual dance fusing pre-Columbian and Catholic elements.
profile: A syncretic ritual dance of central Mexico (rooted in Queretaro/the Bajio and performed at sites around Mexico City) combining pre-Hispanic and Catholic elements. Dancers wear elaborate feathered headdresses and indigenous regalia, dance to drums and to the concha - a lute made from an armadillo shell. Groups are organized into hierarchical mesas with captains, performing at pilgrimage sites and on feast days while orienting to the four cardinal directions. The dance adapted the old mitote to Catholicism to preserve indigenous rite (Wikipedia; Britannica).
insight: Originally organized to preserve pre-Hispanic ritual after the Conquest, the Concheros ironically became defenders of the Catholic Church during the 1920s Cristero conflict; since the later 20th century a Mexica/Azteca offshoot has sought to strip away European/Catholic elements.
notes: Not UNESCO-inscribed; distinct from but related to Voladores. Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Voladores
tagline: Totonac 'Flying Men' pole ceremony, UNESCO-inscribed.
profile: An ancient Mesoamerican fertility ritual most associated with the Totonac people of Papantla, Veracruz. Four men climb a tall wooden pole (18-40 metres), tie themselves with ropes and launch off, spinning and descending head-down to mimic flight; a fifth man, the caporal, stays atop the platform playing a flute and drum dedicated to the sun and the four cardinal directions. The four fliers traditionally circle 13 times each, totaling 52 - the years of the Aztec calendar round (UNESCO; Wikipedia).
insight: UNESCO inscribed the 'Ritual ceremony of the Voladores' on the Representative List in 2009 (4.COM, RL/00175), describing it as a fertility dance performed by several ethnic groups in Mexico and Central America, especially the Totonac people in the eastern state of Veracruz.
notes: UNESCO Representative List, inscribed 2009 (4.COM), official title 'Ritual ceremony of the Voladores', ref. RL/00175. Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Teskoto
tagline: Solemn western-Macedonian men's dance, the 'hard one'.
profile: A slow, solemn men's line dance from the Mijak ethnographic region of western Macedonia (the villages of Galichnik, Lazaropole, Gari and the Reka region). Its name means 'the hard one.' The dance begins with a slow, non-metric section requiring precise lifts, steps and leaps, with the leader improvising. It represents the hardship of villagers leaving to work abroad (pecalba) and the suffering of the region. Traditionally accompanied by zurla and tapan (drum), historically played by Romani musicians from Debar (Wikipedia; Macedonian Heritage).
insight: Teskoto has been the signature dance of the national Tanec ensemble since its 1949 founding and inspired Blaze Koneski's 1948 poem 'Teskoto'; it is the centerpiece of the annual Galichnik Wedding Festival.
notes: Not UNESCO-inscribed. The related Macedonian dance Kopachka was inscribed in 2014 (9.COM, RL/00995). Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Oro (Macedonia)
tagline: Macedonian open circle/chain dance, the generic folk-dance form.
profile: The oro is the Macedonian open circle or chain dance and the generic term for Macedonian folk dances. Dancers hold hands or shoulders and move in a circle or arc, often beginning in a semicircle. Rhythms are frequently complex and asymmetrical (Balkan meters reaching up to 22/16). It is danced at weddings, christenings, name-days and religious and national holidays, accompanied by gajda (bagpipe), zurla, tapan, kaval and chalgija ensembles. Many regional named forms exist (Teskoto, Kopachka, Pravoto, Starotikvesko, Adana, Vlechenoto) (Wikipedia Hora/Oro; Folkdance Footnotes).
insight: The word oro derives from Greek khoros ('dance'), shared across the Balkans (Bulgarian horo, Romanian hora); the Macedonian oro circle dance is distinct from the Roma/Balkan cocek and from the Montenegrin paired Oro (which derives from 'orao,' eagle).
notes: English 'Oro (dance)' redirects to 'Hora (dance)'; distinct Macedonian-oro Wikidata item Q12908299 exists but has no English article. Intentionally distinct from the catalog's existing 'cocek' entry. Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Halling
tagline: Norwegian solo men's acrobatic competition dance.
profile: A vigorous Norwegian folk dance (bygdedans) traditionally performed solo by young men at weddings and parties, named for the Hallingdal valley and also associated with Valdres. Danced in fast 6/8 or 2/4 (95-106 bpm), it is an acrobatic, competitive display of strength and agility with moves such as the nakkespretten (neck jump), kruking, hodestift and the climactic kast/hallingkast - kicking a hat held high (230-280 cm) on a stick. It is accompanied by fiddle or Hardanger fiddle (Wikipedia; Britannica).
insight: Edvard Grieg composed halling pieces in his Lyric Pieces, and the Frikar Dance Company performed halling in Norway's winning 2009 Eurovision entry 'Fairytale' with Alexander Rybak.
notes: Not individually UNESCO-inscribed. Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Springar
tagline: Norwegian turning couple dance in asymmetric triple time.
profile: A Norwegian regional couple's turning dance (bygdedans) in an uneven 3/4 rhythm, traditionally danced by a man and a woman, with the man given more room to improvise vigorous figures and footwork. Regional variants (Telespringar, Valdresspringar, Vossaspringar, Hallingspringar) differ in their asymmetric beat patterns - e.g. Telemark's long-medium-short versus Valdres's short-longer-long. It is played chiefly on the Hardanger fiddle (hardingfele), often in three parts: figuring, free dance (lausdans) and closed-hold turning (samdans) (Wikipedia; HFAA).
insight: Motion-capture ethnochoreology studies confirm telespringar's systematic long-medium-short beat-duration pattern in both the fiddler's foot-stamping and the dancers' body motion, distinguishing it from an even triple meter.
notes: Not individually inscribed. The broader Setesdal music-and-dance tradition was UNESCO-inscribed in 2019 (14.COM, RL/01432). Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Gangar
tagline: Norwegian walking dance in duple time, partner of springar.
profile: A Norwegian folk dance (bygdedans) in duple time (2/4 or 6/8) - a 'walking dance' distinguished from the springar mainly by meter and tempo. It survives chiefly in Telemark and Setesdal, where it is regarded as one of the most stylish Norwegian dances; in western Norway its tunes were largely taken over by the rull. It forms part of the Norwegian bygdedans family alongside springar, halling and pols, and is typically accompanied by the Hardanger fiddle (Wikipedia Bygdedans; Society of Folk Dance Historians).
insight: In Setesdal the melodies are named after the gangar dance and the stev songs performed between dances; UNESCO's 2019 Setesdal inscription notes that 'the melodies are named after the gangar dance and are mostly dance tunes.'
notes: No standalone English Wikipedia article (URL blank, needs_eyes_on). Wikidata Q4585173 verified. The broader Setesdal tradition (RL/01432, 2019) includes gangar but it is not separately inscribed. Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Polka Paraguaya
era: 19th century; reportedly first danced in Asuncion on November 27, 1858
tagline: Paraguay's lively creole couple dance with harp and guitar.
profile: A folk music and couple dance from Paraguay, distinct from the European polka because it juxtaposes ternary and binary rhythms (felt in 6/8 with a sesquialtera/hemiola interplay between 6/8 and 3/4). Danced in pairs with linked arms, partners briefly unlinking to perform figures (enlaces, cadenas, toreo). Accompanied by the Paraguayan harp and guitars, sometimes violin or accordion. Variants include polca syryry, polca popo, polca jekutu, polca valseada and the dance-oriented galopa. Performed at community events and festivals, often in traditional dress (Wikipedia; Music of Paraguay Wikipedia).
insight: It is considered the best-known genre of Paraguayan folk music, identified by its polyrhythm - a triple-meter bass against a compound-duple melody.
notes: Not UNESCO-inscribed. Recognized within Paraguay as national cultural heritage. Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Guarania
tagline: Slow, lyrical Paraguayan genre created by Jose Asuncion Flores in 1925.
profile: A musical genre created in Paraguay by Jose Asuncion Flores in 1925; the first guarania, Jejui, premiered at the Hotel Cosmos in Asuncion. Characterized by slow, melancholic rhythms and minor keys, expressing yearning, grief and national sentiment. Uses guitar, harp (UNESCO notes guitar and double bass) and the same dual-meter device as the polka but at a slower tempo. Lyrics often in Guarani. More urban than rural in appeal. Per UNESCO ICH file 02128, the Guarania is characterized by syncopated rhythms and a slow tempo that is inspired by the Paraguayan polka (UNESCO; Wikipedia).
insight: Per UNESCO ICH file 02128, the Guarania 'is a collective expression of Paraguayan values perceived as a symbol of resilience and Paraguayan values'; it was inscribed on the UNESCO Representative List on December 3, 2024 (19.COM, Asuncion).
notes: FLAG: primarily a MUSIC/SONG genre with a danceable component. UNESCO Representative List, inscribed December 3, 2024 (19.COM), file 02128. Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Marinera
era: Formalized and named 1879; roots in colonial-era zamacueca
tagline: Peru's national handkerchief courtship couple dance.
profile: An elegant partner dance using handkerchiefs as props, re-enacting a courtship. Derived from the zamacueca with Spanish, African, Andean (and Moorish/Romani) influences; renamed Marinera in 1879 in honor of the Peruvian navy. Danced in 6/8; couples never touch. Three main regional variants: Marinera Limena (Lima, salon style, guitar and cajon), Marinera Nortena (Trujillo, woman dances barefoot, brass band) and Marinera Serrana (highlands, slower, minor tone). The National Marinera Festival is held in Trujillo each January (Wikipedia; Smithsonian Folklife Festival).
insight: Marinera was declared Patrimonio Cultural de la Nacion on January 30, 1986 by Peru's Instituto Nacional de Cultura via Resolucion Suprema No. 022-86-ED - the first such declaration granted by the Peruvian State.
notes: Peruvian national heritage tradition (1986) but NOT UNESCO-inscribed. Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Festejo
era: 19th-century roots; modern form consolidated mid-20th century
tagline: Joyful, vigorous Afro-Peruvian coastal dance driven by the cajon.
profile: A festive Afro-Peruvian music and dance form from Peru's central coast (Lima, Canete, Chincha, Ica). High-energy and improvisational, with full-body movements, small jumps, quick turns and hip motion mirrored in the arms. Percussion-driven: cajon, cajita, quijada (jawbone), plus guitar; typically in 6/8 or 12/8. Danced in pairs, groups or solo at social and cultural occasions. Modern choreography was standardized from 1949 onward and revived mid-century by figures such as Jose Durand, Nicomedes Santa Cruz and Victoria Santa Cruz (Wikipedia; Kennedy Center).
insight: Once nearly lost - by the early 1900s the original choreography had almost completely disappeared (per musicologist William Tompkins) - festejo was reconstructed during a mid-20th-century Afro-Peruvian cultural revival.
notes: Not UNESCO-inscribed. Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Huayno
region: Andes (Peru, Bolivia, northwest Argentina, northern Chile)
era: Pre-Columbian roots; modern form took shape in colonial Peru
tagline: The major Andean couple dance of the Quechua and Aymara.
profile: A genre of Andean music and dance, especially common in Peru, western Bolivia, northwest Argentina and northern Chile, popular among indigenous peoples, especially the Quechua. Music in 2/4 with a stressed first beat followed by two short beats; pentatonic, rhythmic melodies. Instruments include quena, harp, siku, charango, violin, guitar, mandolin and accordion. The dance begins with the man offering his right arm (or placing a handkerchief on the woman's shoulder) as an invitation, followed by energetic foot-stamping (Wikipedia; Britannica).
insight: Britannica notes it antedates the Spanish conquest and may once have been an Inca funeral dance, though today it is purely festive.
notes: Multinational Andean genre. Not UNESCO-inscribed. Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Polonaise
era: Roots in 16th-17th century chodzony; courtly form 17th-18th century
tagline: Poland's stately processional national dance in triple time.
profile: A stately Polish processional couple dance in 3/4 time, the most dignified of Poland's five national dances. Originating from the folk 'walking dance' (chodzony) at village weddings, it became a courtly dance opening royal balls. Couples promenade arm-in-arm with gliding steps, bending the knee slightly on the third step, forming bridges and figures led by the first pair. Today it opens major events and is the obligatory first dance at the studniowka (high-school prom). Per UNESCO, the dance 'symbolizes cooperation, reconciliation and equality' (UNESCO ICH; Wikipedia; Britannica).
insight: Per UNESCO ICH file 01982, the polonaise is a joyful Polish group dance that can be enacted by a few to even several hundreds of pairs marching in procession. It was once banned by Russia during the partitions and became a symbol of Polish identity and survival.
notes: UNESCO Representative List, inscribed December 5, 2023 (18.COM, Kasane, Botswana), file 01982. Added to Poland's national ICH list in 2015. The only one of Poland's five national dances individually inscribed. Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Mazurka
era: Known from the 16th century; popularized internationally in the 19th century
tagline: Lively Polish triple-time couple dance with off-beat accents.
profile: A Polish musical form and folk dance in triple metre (3/4 or 3/8) at a lively tempo, with strong accents unsystematically placed on the second or third beat. Originating in the Mazovia plains around Warsaw (home of the Mazurs), it is danced by a circle of couples with foot-stamping and heel-clicking. The folk family comprises the mazur, oberek and kujawiak. It spread across European salons in the 19th century, especially through Chopin; the Polish national anthem itself is a mazurka ('Mazurek Dabrowskiego') (Wikipedia; Polish Music Center USC).
insight: Chopin wrote at least 59 piano mazurkas (1825-1849), preserving the dance's repetition and rhythmic patterns while blending mazur, kujawiak and oberek elements into a new concert genre.
notes: Distinct from the Martinique mazurka creole (already in catalog as slug mazurka-creole). One of Poland's five national dances; not individually UNESCO-inscribed (only the polonaise is). Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Krakowiak
era: 16th-17th century origins; name first appeared in print in 1816
tagline: Fast, syncopated couple dance from the Krakow region.
profile: A fast, syncopated Polish folk dance in duple (2/4) time from the Krakow region of Lesser Poland (Malopolska), also known as the Cracovienne. Set for several couples and directed by a leading male dancer who sings and indicates the steps; characteristic steps include the galop, holubiec (heel-clicking jump) and krzesany (sliding stamp), with group figures such as the Star. The folk outfit (with the rogatywka peaked hat and peacock feathers) became Poland's national costume. Chopin wrote a concert Rondo a la Krakowiak (Op. 14, 1828) (Wikipedia; Polish Music Center USC).
insight: In the 19th century the krakowiak became a Parisian and Viennese ballroom favorite (the Cracovienne) and, alongside the polonaise and mazurka, a Romantic-era symbol of solidarity with partitioned Poland.
notes: Slug retained exactly as provided (krakowiakk - double k). One of Poland's five national dances; NOT individually UNESCO-inscribed. Near-duplicate Wikidata item Q24788818 exists and is tagged 'different from' Q648283; Q648283 is the correct dance item. Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Fado
tagline: Portugal's mournful urban song genre - a music, not dance, tradition.
profile: A Portuguese music genre traceable to 1820s Lisbon, performed by a solo singer (male or female) accompanied by the guitarra portuguesa (a pear-shaped 12-string cittern unique to Portugal) and an acoustic guitar. Characterized by mournful melodies and the sentiment of saudade (longing). Two main schools: Lisbon fado and the more erudite Coimbra fado (sung by male university students in academic dress). Per UNESCO ICH file 00563, Fado represents a Portuguese multicultural synthesis of Afro-Brazilian sung dances, local traditional genres of song and dance, musical traditions from rural areas, and the cosmopolitan urban song patterns of the early 19th century (Wikipedia; UNESCO).
insight: Fado is fundamentally a singing tradition with no significant choreographic dance component; it was inscribed by UNESCO in 2011 (file 00563, 6.COM, Bali) as an urban popular SONG of Portugal.

sources.title: Fado, urban popular song of Portugal

notes: FLAG: Fado is primarily a SONG genre, not a dance. UNESCO Representative List, inscribed 2011 (6.COM, Bali), file 00563 - as a MUSIC/SONG tradition. Fado itself has no significant dance form. Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Vira
era: Traditional folk dance (19th century and earlier)
tagline: Lively Minho couple dance in fast triple time.
profile: A traditional Portuguese couple dance, most popular in the Minho region of northern Portugal but performed nationwide. It has a three-step rhythm similar to a waltz but faster, with couples dancing front-to-front without holding hands; another form has matched pairs in a large circle revolving counter-clockwise while snapping fingers, dancers taking turns to step into the center. Accompanied by instruments such as the concertina, guitarra portuguesa and percussion; dancers wear colorful Minho regional costumes. The name refers to the characteristic 'turning' (virar) of the bodies (Wikipedia; Portugal.com).
insight: Often called the queen of Minho dances, the vira was exported across Portugal during the Estado Novo's folklore standardization via the ranchos folcloricos, so even distant towns such as Nazare adopted it.
notes: Not UNESCO-inscribed. Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Corridinho
tagline: Fast Algarve couple dance in duple time.
profile: A fast Portuguese folk dance from the Algarve region (mainly the Faro area), danced by couples who form a circle - girls on the inside, boys on the outside - and rotate side by side. The name derives from correr ('to run'). It comprises three parts: o corrido (the run), o rodado (the rotation, a fast-paced waltz-like spin) and a escovinha. Originally accompanied by small flutes/fifes (pifaro) and harmonicas, later dominated by the accordion. It bears resemblance to the polka and mazurka and is performed by ranchos folcloricos at Algarve festivals (Wikipedia; Portugal.com).
insight: The corridinho spread through former Portuguese colonies - surviving in Goa, Daman and Diu, Macau and as the Kristang branyo in Malaysia, which in turn influenced the Malay joget.
notes: Not UNESCO-inscribed. Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Bomba
tagline: Afro-Puerto Rican drum-and-dance dialogue between dancer and lead drummer.
profile: Bomba is Puerto Rico's oldest musical tradition, developed by enslaved West Africans on coastal sugar plantations in towns such as Loiza, Mayaguez, Ponce and San Juan. It unfolds as a call-and-response conversation in which the dancer leads and the lead drummer (subidor/primo) follows every movement. Performers form a circle (batey) with barriles (rum-barrel drums covered in goatskin), maracas, a cua, and singers. Core rhythms include sica, yuba (6/8) and holande. Women dance with long ruffled skirts, flicking the fabric to challenge the drum (Wikipedia; Britannica).
insight: Per Britannica, in the 21st century the dance served as a rallying point in Black Lives Matter protests, appearing at vigils for George Floyd, Breonna Taylor and Tony McDade. Bad Bunny featured bomba dancers and drummers prominently in 2025-2026 performances.
notes: Distinct from 'bomba-del-chota' (Ecuador). Not UNESCO-inscribed (Puerto Rico is a US territory, not an independent UNESCO State Party). Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Plena
tagline: The 'sung newspaper' of Puerto Rico driven by panderetas.
profile: Plena is an Afro-Puerto Rican song-and-dance genre that originated around 1900 in the working-class barrio of San Anton in Ponce, evolving from bomba. Known as el periodico cantado (the sung newspaper), it spread news, gossip and social commentary through call-and-response vocals. It is driven by panderetas (hand frame drums) in three sizes - seguidor, segundo/punteador and requinto - plus guiro, and sometimes cuatro and accordion. The rhythm is in 2/4 and lyrics are typically octosyllabic and assonant (Wikipedia; Smithsonian Folkways).
insight: Plena gained mass reach via radio and records by the 1930s and saw a major revival in the 1990s (Plena Libre, Viento de Agua) and again in 2025 around Bad Bunny's album featuring plena groups such as Los Pleneros de la Cresta.
notes: Primarily a music genre with an associated couple/social dance. Not UNESCO-inscribed. Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Danza (Puerto Rico)
tagline: Elegant 19th-century Puerto Rican salon couple dance.
profile: The Puerto Rican danza is an elegant ballroom couple dance and musical genre that emerged in the mid-19th century, centered on Ponce, blending the European contradanza and habanera with Afro-Caribbean syncopation.
