A danza typically opens with a paseo (an elegant introductory walk in which couples promenade), followed by more animated sections. Music is instrumental and orchestral (strings and piano), in binary meter. Key composers include Manuel Gregorio Tavarez ('father of Puerto Rican danza') and his pupil Juan Morel Campos, the most prolific danza composer (Wikipedia; Tabanka Caribbean Dance Index).
insight: Puerto Rico's anthem 'La Borinqueña' is a danza, and the genre is celebrated each mid-May at the Fiesta Nacional de la Danza in Ponce, sponsored by law since 1972 on Juan Morel Campos's birthday.
notes: Distinct from the existing Cuban 'danza' catalog entry. Wikipedia article at 'Danza' (Puerto Rico-specific). Not UNESCO-inscribed. Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Hora
tagline: Romania's national circle dance of community and unity.
profile: The hora is a traditional Romanian and Moldovan circle dance in which dancers hold hands and the closed circle spins, usually counterclockwise, following a sequence of three steps forward and one step back. The Hora Mare (Great Hora) is danced at weddings, festivals and on holidays and functions as a metaphor for the community. Accompaniment includes cymbalom, accordion, violin, viola, double bass, saxophone, trumpet or pan pipes. It is closely related to the Serbian kolo and Bulgarian horo (Wikipedia; Britannica).
insight: Hora Unirii, composed for the 1859 union of Wallachia and Moldavia, made the hora a patriotic symbol of Romanian national unity.
notes: Distinct from the Balkan cocek entry. The Romanian Calusari is separately UNESCO-inscribed (see slug calusari). Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Calus
tagline: Romanian all-male acrobatic ritual healing dance.
profile: The Calus (Calusari) is an all-male ritual dance society from the Olt region of Oltenia in southern Romania, performed mainly on Whit Sunday (Pentecost). Dancers wearing colorful hats, embroidered shirts and bell-adorned trousers perform complex dances combining stamping, heel-clicking, leaping and leg-swinging, accompanied by two violins and an accordion. Led by a vataf (master) who inherits magic charms (descantece), the group was believed to hold magical healing powers and went house to house promising health and prosperity (UNESCO; Wikipedia).
insight: Per UNESCO ICH, inscribed in 2008 (3.COM) on the Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity (originally proclaimed in 2005); it became the first monument of spiritual culture in Romania to be UNESCO-inscribed.
notes: DUPLICATE of catalog entry slug 'calusari'. Both entries share Wikidata Q369261 and the same Wikipedia article. UNESCO RL/00090, inscribed 2008 (3.COM). Kept per standing rule (keep all rows, flag duplicates). Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Doina
tagline: Lyrical improvised Romanian solo chant of longing (dor).
profile: The doina is a lyrical, solemn, improvised Romanian song form, performed solo with or without instrumental accompaniment (straight flute, bagpipes, violin, pan-pipe, cymbalom). It uses free parlando-rubato rhythm and rich ornamentation to express dor (longing), melancholy, love, loneliness and grief. Until 1900 it was the only musical genre in many regions of Romania. It is regarded as the essence of Romanian folklore and historically influenced traditional dances, with regional variants (hore lunga in Maramures, doina cu noduri in Oltenia) (UNESCO; Wikipedia).
insight: Per UNESCO ICH, 'Although some fifteen people have been identified as representatives of the various types of doina, an environment conducive to performance and transmission must be restored' - a safeguarding concern at nomination.
notes: FLAG: primarily a MUSIC/SONG genre, not a dance per se; flagged accordingly. UNESCO RL/00192, inscribed 2009 (4.COM). Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Highland dance
tagline: Competitive solo Scottish dances danced to bagpipes.
profile: Highland dance is a competitive, technical solo dance form developed in the Scottish Highlands in the 19th-20th centuries, created from the Gaelic folk dance repertoire but formalized with the conventions of ballet. Performed to Highland bagpipe music in ghillies, the core competition dances are the Sword Dance (Gille Chaluim), Highland Fling, Seann Triubhas and Reel of Tulloch. Judged on timing, technique and artistic interpretation, it is recognized as a sport by the Sport Council of Scotland and is now danced overwhelmingly by women (Wikipedia; Britannica).
insight: Until 1986 only four standard dances remained in competition, narrowed over decades to ease judging, causing many older Highland dances to be lost. The Royal Scottish Official Board of Highland Dancing (RSOBHD), formed in 1950, is the world governing body.
notes: Not UNESCO-inscribed. Distinct from Scottish country dance (social, set-based). Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Scottish country dance
tagline: Social set dance in reels, jigs and strathspeys.
profile: Scottish country dance (SCD) is the distinctively Scottish form of social country dance in which groups of couples trace progressive patterns in sets (usually three or four couples, in longwise, square or triangular formation). Dances are categorized as reels, jigs and strathspeys by music type. Footwork includes skip-change of step, pas de basque and the strathspey travelling step. It became Scotland's national ballroom form, popularized after the 1745 Jacobite rising. There are more than 15,000 documented Scottish country dances (Wikipedia; RSCDS).
insight: The Royal Scottish Country Dance Society was formed at a Glasgow meeting on 26 November 1923 (co-founded by Ysobel Stewart and Jean Milligan) and granted Royal status in 1951.
notes: Not UNESCO-inscribed. Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Kolo
era: Ancient origins; oldest versions recorded second half of 19th century
tagline: South Slavic collective circle dance, symbol of Serbian identity.
profile: Kolo is a South Slavic collective folk dance in which dancers interlink in a chain - holding hands or waists in V or W formation - usually forming a circle, with almost no movement above the waist. Found under this name in Serbia, Bosnia and Herzegovina, and Croatia, it is danced at weddings, festivals and religious ceremonies. Basic steps are simple while experts add syncopated ornaments. Music is generally fast-paced, traditionally on frula and gajde, today often accordion or trumpet. Famous Serbian kola include Moravac, Uzicko and Cacak (UNESCO; Wikipedia; Britannica).
insight: Kolo has an important integrative social function, fostering collective identity at local, regional and national levels; inscribed for Serbia on 9 December 2017 (12.COM, UNESCO Representative List).
notes: UNESCO RL/01270, inscribed 2017 (12.COM, Serbia). Shared tradition across Bosnia and Herzegovina, Croatia and Serbia; inscribed for Serbia. Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Cacak
era: Documented from second half of 19th century
tagline: Fast central-western Serbian circle dance in 2/4.
profile: Cacak (Cacak kolo) is a fast, energetic Serbian circle dance, typically a 10-measure dance in 2/4 meter, documented from the second half of the 19th century as a dance from south-eastern Serbia and widely popular in western and central Serbia. It is danced in a circle or broken-circle lines with regional variants including Crnotravski, Banjski, Niski, Medevacki and Silovacki, and characteristically follows a 3-2-1 step pattern. It is one of three dances listed on the national list of Intangible Heritage of the Republic of Serbia (Wikipedia: Serbian dances; Society of Folk Dance Historians).
insight: Per the English Wikipedia 'Serbian dances' article, Cacak is one of three dances listed on the national list of Intangible Heritage of the Republic of Serbia.
notes: No dedicated English Wikipedia article or Wikidata QID; covered only within 'Serbian dances'. As a Serbian kolo variant falls under the broader 2017 Kolo UNESCO inscription but is not separately inscribed. Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Uzicko Kolo
era: Older dance tradition; signature melody mid-20th century
tagline: Fast, fiery western Serbian circle dance from Uzice.
profile: Uzicko kolo is one of Serbia's most famous and widespread fast circle dances, named after the town of Uzice in western Serbia. It is fast and energetic, often accompanied by clapping and stomping, and is among the most popular kola at Serbian weddings and celebrations - usually danced first because most people know it. Its basic pattern uses roughly eight counts to the right starting on the right foot, then eight to the left, sharing its core step with Moravac and Zikino kolo (Wikipedia: Serbian dances; Folkdance Footnotes).
insight: Folk-dance documentation (Folkdance Footnotes) describes Uzicko as essentially the same widespread dance as Moravac, distinguished mainly by its faster, more recent melody.
notes: No dedicated English Wikipedia article or Wikidata QID; covered only within 'Serbian dances'. Subsumed under the 2017 Kolo UNESCO inscription as a Serbian kolo variant, not separately inscribed. Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Flamenco
tagline: Andalusian art of song, guitar, dance and handclaps.
profile: Flamenco is an Andalusian art form fusing cante (song), toque (guitar), baile (dance) and jaleo/palmas (handclapping and shouts), rooted in the Gitano (Roma) communities of southern Spain with historical presence in Murcia and Extremadura. Baile uses complex footwork (zapateado), arm movements (braceo) and emotional intensity; instruments include guitar, castanets, cajon and hand-clapping. Performed at festivals, penas (clubs) and private gatherings (UNESCO ICH; Wikipedia; Britannica).
insight: UNESCO inscribed flamenco on the Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity in 2010, calling it 'the badge of identity of numerous communities and groups, in particular the Gitano (Roma) ethnic community.'
notes: UNESCO Representative List, inscribed 16 November 2010 (5.COM, decision 5.COM 6.39), file RL/00363. Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Jota
tagline: Fast triple-time Spanish folk dance with regional variants.
profile: The jota is a genre of music and dance found throughout Spain, most strongly associated with Aragon but with distinct forms in Navarre, Valencia, Castile, Galicia and beyond. It is usually in 3/4 (sometimes described as 6/8), danced by couples with arms raised, castanets, heel-toe steps and small jumps. Accompaniment varies by region: a rondalla of guitar, bandurria and lute in Aragon/Castile; bagpipes and drums in Galicia. Performed at festivals such as the Fiestas del Pilar (Wikipedia; Britannica).
insight: Britannica describes the jota as a courtship dance traditional in northern Spain, particularly Aragon, in which couples click castanets as they execute lively, bouncing steps.
notes: Not on the UNESCO Representative List; the Aragonese jota is part of Aragon's regional cultural heritage. Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Sardana
tagline: Catalan circle dance and symbol of Catalan identity.
profile: The sardana is a Catalan circle dance in which men and women join hands alternately facing inward, performing meticulously counted short (curts) and long (llargs) steps. Originating in the Emporda region, it was modernized by Josep Maria 'Pep' Ventura (1817-1875) who introduced the tenora in the 1850s and fixed the cobla as a standardized ensemble of 11 musicians playing 12 instruments. A social, all-ages street dance performed at festivals and Sunday gatherings. In 2010 the Government of Catalonia added it to the Catàleg del Patrimoni Festiu de Catalunya (Wikipedia; Britannica).
insight: The Spanish parliament urged UNESCO promotion of the sardana in 2015, but there is no Representative List inscription; it sits on Catalonia's national festive heritage catalog.
notes: On Catalonia's national festive heritage catalog (2010); NOT on the UNESCO Representative List. Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Sevillanas
era: Derived from seguidilla; flamenco influence 19th century
tagline: Andalusian couple folk dance of Seville's Feria de Abril.
profile: Sevillanas are a folk music and dance form of Seville and its region, derived from the Castilian seguidilla and later influenced by flamenco. Danced by couples of all ages, the choreography has four parts (paseillos, pasadas, careos, remate), each divided into coplas. Rhythm is generally 6/8 (sometimes read as 3/4), accompanied by guitar, castanets, clapping and tambourine. Most prominent at the Feria de Abril and the El Rocio pilgrimage (Wikipedia; Seville tourism).
insight: Though often mistaken for flamenco by visitors, sevillanas are a more standardized social festival dance; learners typically start with sevillanas before flamenco.
notes: Not UNESCO-inscribed. Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Paso Doble
tagline: Fast march-like Spanish dance evoking a bullfight.
profile: The pasodoble is a fast Spanish dance and music in binary 2/4 time, derived from a Spanish military march and associated with bullfighting, where it accompanies the matadors' entrance (paseo) and passes (faena). In the partner dance the leader represents the matador and the follower the cape. It became one of the five International Latin ballroom dances, with sharp, proud, theatrical movements. Its signature tune is 'Espana cani' (Gypsy Spain), a pasodoble composed in 1923 by Pascual Marquina Narro (Wikipedia; WikiDanceSport).
insight: Spain has pasodoble scores dating to 1780, predating the music's 19th-century association with bullfights; ballroom technique was later codified in Paris and London.
notes: Not UNESCO-inscribed. Spanish/French origin debated. Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Kawina
tagline: Afro-Surinamese Creole drum-driven social music and dance.
profile: Kawina is an Afro-Surinamese (Creole) music and dance genre that arose in the last decades of the 19th century from older forms (banya, laku, tuka), related to West African music. It uses call-and-response singing led by a cantor, plus kawina drums, kwakwa-bangi, sakka, conga and cuatro; the music is in four-quarter time with an emphasized fourth beat. Kawina dances are performed in an anticlockwise circle; sub-styles include prisir (entertainment) kawina and winti (religious) kawina, tied to the Winti spiritual tradition (Wikipedia; World Music Central).
insight: Until the early 20th century kawina was the dominant Afro-Surinamese music style before being overtaken by kaseko, which derives much of its rhythm and lyrics from kawina.
notes: NOT UNESCO-inscribed (CORRECTION: Suriname ratified the 2003 ICH Convention on 5 Sept 2017 and has zero inscribed elements; claimed 2022/2023 inscription is false). QID Q2249365 is the music genre; verify against sibling QID for the kawina drum/instrument before publication. Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Polska (Sweden)
tagline: Swedish triple-time couple turning dance, oldest Swedish folk dance.
profile: The polska is a Scandinavian (chiefly Swedish) folk dance in 3/4 time, usually for couples who grasp each other and pivot one full revolution per measure, often alternating a promenade with a clockwise couple turn. Regional styles (semiquaver/sixteenth-note, quaver/eighth-note, and triplet polska) vary across Dalarna, Halsingland and the Norwegian-border provinces. Played mainly on fiddle, with nyckelharpa and accordion. Danced at dansbanor (dance-floor evenings) and folk events (Wikipedia; Society of Folk Dance Historians).
insight: Per folk-dance scholar Gordon Tracie, 'probably seventy-five percent of all the dance tunes played by Swedish folk musicians are in Polska rhythm'; the hambo is a 19th-century offshoot of the gammal polska.
notes: Not UNESCO-inscribed. Distinct from the 2/4 Polish polka. Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Hambo
tagline: Swedish couple turning dance in 3/4, offshoot of the polska.
profile: The hambo (full name hambopolska) is a Swedish couple turning dance in 3/4 time with a strong accent on the first beat, a tempo of roughly 100-120 bpm, and a fixed eight-measure pattern alternating dalsteg (open steps) with the clockwise polska turn. Part of the gammaldans tradition, it derives from the polska family and is danced socially in Sweden and among Swedish diaspora and folk-dance communities abroad; the Halsingehambo competition features a slow, elegant svikt (dip-and-lift) style (Wikipedia; Society of Folk Dance Historians).
insight: Folk-dance scholar Gordon Tracie referred to the hambo as the national dance of Sweden; it is the most popular surviving form of the centuries-old polska.
notes: Not UNESCO-inscribed. Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Calypso
tagline: Trinidadian kaiso: social commentary through song and music.
profile: Calypso is a music genre that originated in Trinidad and Tobago among Afro-Trinidadians in the early-to-mid 19th century, with roots in West African kaiso and French Antillean traditions. Characterized by rhythmic, harmonic vocals originally in French Creole, later English, led by a chantuelle/calypsonian, it functions as social and political commentary and satire. Associated with Carnival, calypso tents, extempo competitions and the Calypso Monarch title; instruments include guitar, cuatro, maracas, brass and steelpans (Wikipedia; Britannica; NALIS).
insight: Calypso allowed the masses to challenge the actions of the unelected colonial Governor and Legislative Council, prompting censorship; it is distinct from Cuban son.
notes: Not UNESCO-inscribed (T&T has zero inscribed ICH elements). Primarily a music tradition with associated Carnival movement. Distinct from son-cubano. Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Steel pan / Steelband
tagline: Trinidad's national instrument and steelband tradition.
profile: The steelpan is a tuned percussion instrument developed in Trinidad and Tobago around 1939 from oil drums, with notes hammered into a concave playing surface; it evolved from tamboo-bamboo and earlier Carnival percussion. Steelbands (pan) range from pan-round-de-neck to large wheeled conventional bands of up to 120 players, governed by Pan Trinbago. Primarily a MUSIC/instrument tradition, central to Carnival and the Panorama competition, rather than a dance per se (Wikipedia; NALIS; T&T Intellectual Property Office).
insight: The steelpan was legally declared T&T's National Musical Instrument in 2024 (National Musical Instrument Act, passed July 2024) and received a national Geographical Indication on 9 August 2024; the UN proclaimed 11 August as World Steelpan Day from 2023 (UNGA resolution A/RES/77/316).
notes: FLAG: primarily a MUSIC/INSTRUMENT tradition, not a dance. NOT UNESCO ICH-inscribed (T&T has zero inscribed ICH elements; the 'UNESCO 2012' claim circulating online is false). Real recognitions: UN World Steelpan Day (2023) and national GI (2024). Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Limbo
tagline: Caribbean dance passing under a lowering bar bending backwards.
profile: Limbo is a Trinidad and Tobago dance contest in which dancers pass under a horizontal bar while bending backwards, balancing on feet akimbo with knees extended, without touching the bar or the floor; the bar is progressively lowered. Originating at wakes in Tobago/Trinidad as a funerary ritual linked to the cycle of life and death, it moves to Afro-Caribbean drum patterns. Popularized internationally by Julia Edwards ('First Lady of Limbo'), who added human bars and fire at the 1959 opening of Queen's Hall (Wikipedia; National Dance Association of T&T).
insight: Limbo is considered the unofficial national dance of Trinidad and Tobago and was revived for entertainment in the 1940s-50s before spreading across the Caribbean tourism circuit.
notes: Not UNESCO-inscribed. Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Hopak
era: Originated 16th century; modern stage form developed early-mid 20th century
tagline: Ukraine's national dance; an acrobatic Cossack men's dance.
profile: The Hopak originated among the Zaporozhian Cossacks in the lands around the Dnieper River in central Ukraine, beginning as an all-male celebratory dance performed after battle. Movements feature high leaps, squatting kicks (prysiadka), spins and acrobatic improvisation by men, with women performing quick steps, sways and turns. Music is in fast 2/4 time, traditionally played on violins, bandura, bagpipes, cimbaloms and sopilka. It evolved from a social dance into couples, solo and mixed-group forms, and is the culminating showpiece of most Ukrainian dance ensembles (Wikipedia; Britannica).
insight: Often called the national dance of Ukraine, the modern three-part staged Hopak was developed by ethnochoreologist Vasyl Verkhovynets in the interwar period; a martial-art form (Combat Hopak) was revived from 1985.
notes: Not UNESCO-inscribed. Not on Ukraine's national ICH register (Zaporizhzhia oblast not yet represented). Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Kozachok
era: Emerged 16th-17th century; popular in European courts 17th-18th century
tagline: Lively Ukrainian Cossack couple dance in 2/4 time.
profile: The Kozachok is a traditional Ukrainian folk dance that originated with the Cossacks and traces to the Vertep itinerant puppet theatre. It is a fast, linear couple dance in 2/4 time, typically in a constantly increasing tempo and improvisatory character; in Ukraine usually in a minor key. The woman leads and the man follows, imitating her figures, while she signals movement changes by hand-clapping. Basic moves include bihunok, dorizhka, vykhyliasnyk, virovochka and (for men only) prysiadka. It is smoother and more lyrical than the Hopak (Wikipedia; Internet Encyclopedia of Ukraine).
insight: Kozachok became fashionable in European court music in the 17th century; choreographer Vasyl Avramenko standardized a regional courtship variant, the Kozachok Podilskyi, for one to four couples.
notes: Not UNESCO-inscribed. Wikidata lists it as indigenous to Ukrainians, Russians and Belarusians. Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Arkan
era: Traditional/pre-modern Hutsul ritual dance; documented by 19th-century ethnographers
tagline: Hutsul men's circle warriors' dance from the Carpathians.
profile: Arkan is a men's circle dance of the Ukrainian Hutsul people from Hutsulshchyna in the southwestern Carpathian Mountains. Traditionally performed by men around a burning bonfire with arms upon one another's shoulders, it uses a repeated root step (side-step, cross-behind, side-step, hopped bent-knee front kick) in 2/4 rhythm, building in momentum. It was historically tied to a rite of passage by which a young Hutsul man earned the right to dance, carry a shepherd's bartka (axe) and a wide belt. The melody is played on sopilka, trembita and tsymbaly (Wikipedia; Internet Encyclopedia of Ukraine).
insight: The word arkan derives from a Turkic word for rope/lasso (via Romanian), and the dance was an element of male initiation among the Hutsuls; it is inscribed on Ukraine's National Inventory of Intangible Cultural Heritage (2020).
notes: Not UNESCO-inscribed. Inscribed on Ukraine's National Inventory of ICH in 2020 (inventory no. 019). Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Morris dance
tagline: English folk dance with bells, sticks and handkerchiefs.
profile: Morris dance is a form of English folk dance based on rhythmic stepping and choreographed figures performed by a group (a side), usually wearing bell-pads on the shins and accompanied by costumed musicians. Dancers may wield sticks, swords or handkerchiefs. Regional styles include Cotswold (handkerchiefs and sticks), North West (processional, in clogs), Border (vigorous, with disguise), Molly (Fenland Plough Monday tradition) and sword traditions (longsword, rapper). Music is provided live on fiddle, melodeon or accordion (Wikipedia; Britannica; Morris Federation).
insight: Its earliest surviving mention dates to 1448, recording a payment to Morris dancers by the Goldsmiths' Company in London; the modern tradition was revived from near-extinction by folklorists including Cecil Sharp and Mary Neal around 1899-1911.

sources.title: Morris dance | English Folk, Performance, Rituals

notes: Not UNESCO-inscribed. Not on a UK national ICH list. Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Maypole dance
era: Medieval origins; ribbon-plaiting form from the 19th century
tagline: Spring ribbon dance around a decorated pole.
profile: The maypole dance is a ceremonial folk dance performed around a tall pole garlanded with greenery, flowers and ribbons, woven into complex patterns by the dancers. Traditionally performed on May Day (May 1), it is associated with spring fertility rites across Europe. In the revived English form, pairs of dancers each hold a ribbon and weave in and out around the pole, plaiting the ribbons down and then reversing to unwind them. Earlier maypoles had no ribbons and featured circle dances around a flower-decked pole. The plaited-ribbon maypole was largely a 19th-century innovation (Wikipedia; Britannica).
insight: The first documented ribboned maypole appeared on stage at the Victoria Theatre (the Old Vic) in London in 1836; it was standardized as a school custom by John Ruskin in 1881 at Whitelands College in Chelsea.
notes: Not UNESCO-inscribed. Wikidata Q76574 is for Maypole (the pole/festival custom); no separate Maypole dance item exists. Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: English country dance
era: Recorded from 1651 (Playford); declined by ~1830; revived early 20th century
tagline: English set/figure dance; ancestor of contredanse and contra.
profile: English country dance (ECD) is a social dance for groups of couples performing a predefined sequence of figures fitted to a fixed length of music, in longways sets, circles or squares. Documented from John Playford's The English Dancing Master (1651), which listed over a hundred tunes each with its figures. Dancers interact with partners and others, usually progressing so each dances with everyone in the set; a caller commonly teaches and prompts figures. Music is typically 32-bar jigs and reels played on fiddle and other acoustic instruments (Wikipedia; Britannica; Early Dance Circle).
insight: ECD spread to the court of Louis XIV as the contredanse and was the progenitor of the New England contra dance, the quadrille and square dance; it was revived from near-extinction by Cecil Sharp and the English Folk Dance Society in the early 20th century.

sources.title: Country dance | British, Folk & Social Dance

notes: needs_eyes_on: 'English country dance' redirects to 'Country dance' article (Q4763331); no standalone enwiki article or distinct QID for ECD. Not UNESCO-inscribed. Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Square dance
era: Roots in 17th-century European dance; American form 19th-20th century
tagline: American four-couple dance led by a caller.
profile: The square dance is performed by four couples arranged in a square, executing geometric figures cued by a caller. It descends from English country dances and the French quadrille and cotillion, brought by European colonists, with the distinctive call format developed in America. Two main North American types exist: traditional square dance and modern western square dance (standardized by Callerlab). Traditional accompaniment includes fiddle, banjo, guitar and accordion. Square dancing was the national folk dance of the United States for 1982 and 1983 under Senate Joint Resolution 59 (Wikipedia; US House of Representatives; Congress.gov).
insight: 31 states have designated square dance as their official state dance; the 1982 national designation was time-limited and expired in 1983 - no permanent national designation has passed.

sources.title: Can I Have this Dance?
sources.publisher: US House of Representatives History, Art & Archives

notes: Not UNESCO-inscribed. The 1982 national designation was temporary (1982-83 only). Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Lindy Hop
era: Born 1928, Harlem; swing-era peak late 1930s-early 1940s; revived from the 1980s
tagline: Original African-American Harlem swing dance.
profile: The Lindy Hop is an American swing dance born in the African-American communities of Harlem, New York City, around 1928. It fuses jazz, tap, breakaway and Charleston, combining solo and partnered dancing with the improvisation of African-American vernacular dance and the eight-count structure of European partner dances. Its defining move is the swingout, alternating open and closed positions. Danced to big-band swing jazz, it was home to the Savoy Ballroom and spread through troupes such as Whitey's Lindy Hoppers and films like Hellzapoppin (1941) (Wikipedia; Kennedy Center).
insight: The dance is associated with the Savoy Ballroom in Harlem and with George 'Shorty' Snowden; its name is popularly linked to Charles Lindbergh's 1927 transatlantic flight. It was revived internationally beginning in the 1980s.
notes: Not UNESCO-inscribed. Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Tap dance
era: Fusion from 17th-19th centuries; emerged as tap c. 1920s
tagline: Percussive American dance in metal-tapped shoes.
profile: Tap dance is a percussive American dance form created from the fusion of West and Central African rhythmic step dances (such as the juba/gioube) with Irish jig, Scottish and English clog and hornpipe traditions, blended by enslaved Africans and immigrant laborers in urban settings. Dancers sound out audible rhythms by striking the floor with heel-and-toe metal taps. Styles include rhythm (jazz) tap and Broadway tap. Tap developed through minstrel shows, vaudeville and jazz clubs, with metal taps becoming common around the 1920s (Wikipedia; Britannica; Library of Congress).
insight: William Henry Lane ('Master Juba,' c. 1825-1852) is regarded as a foundational figure who fused African rhythms with Irish step technique; the form influenced and co-evolved with jazz music throughout the early-to-mid 20th century.

sources.title: Tap dance | Origin, History, Styles, & Facts

notes: Not UNESCO-inscribed. Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Hula
era: Pre-contact (pre-1778) origins; auana form from the 19th century; Hawaiian Renaissance revival from the 1970s
tagline: Sacred Hawaiian dance expressing chant and song.
profile: Hula is a Native Hawaiian dance form that dramatizes or portrays the words of an accompanying chant (oli) or song (mele) through fluid hand gestures, footwork and hip movements. It is performed sitting (noho) or standing (luna). Hula kahiko is the ancient style performed before Western contact, accompanied by chant and traditional instruments such as the ipu (gourd drum), pahu (drum), split bamboo and seed-filled gourd rattles. Hula auana is the modern style that evolved under Western influence, accompanied by melodic song with ukulele, guitar and bass. Hula is a living, sacred Indigenous cultural practice of Native Hawaiians - not entertainment - tied to genealogy, spirituality and the goddess Laka/Pele (Wikipedia; Britannica).
insight: Hula was suppressed by missionaries (Queen Kaakumanu's 1830 proclamation forbade public hula), but was revived under King David Kalakaua (reigned 1874-1891) and again during the 1970s Hawaiian Renaissance and at the Merrie Monarch Festival.
notes: Not UNESCO-inscribed. Sacred Native Hawaiian tradition - treated with full cultural respect. Distinction between kahiko (ancient) and auana (modern) noted. Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Joropo
era: Originated ~18th century; national dance from 1882; UNESCO-inscribed 2025
tagline: Venezuela's national dance; a fast, swirling Llanos couple dance.
profile: The joropo (also musica llanera) is Venezuela's national dance and music, a fast partner dance that originated in the Llanos (plains) and has Indigenous, African and European roots. A man and woman hold each other at a certain distance, starting in a waltz position, then follow specific steps with the woman swirling and the man stomping his feet (zapateo) in rapid succession. It combines music, poetry, singing and dance, accompanied by the harp, cuatro, maracas, violin and accordion; songs tell of love, nature, humour and daily life. Regional variants include tuyero, oriental and llanero (UNESCO; Wikipedia; France 24).
insight: Joropo became Venezuela's national dance and music in 1882, and the well-known song 'Alma Llanera' - a joropo - is considered the country's unofficial national anthem. UNESCO inscribed it in December 2025 for Venezuela only.
notes: UNESCO Representative List, file 02092, inscribed 9 December 2025 (20.COM, New Delhi). VENEZUELA ONLY - not a joint Venezuela-Colombia inscription. Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Caporales
era: Created 1969 by the Estrada Pacheco brothers; declared national heritage via Ley 137/2011
tagline: Bolivian mestizo dance of the overseer, acrobatic and Afro-influenced.
profile: Caporales was created in 1969 by Jorge and Ernesto Estrada Pacheco in La Paz, drawing on the Afro-Bolivian Saya dance of the Yungas. It portrays the caporal (overseer of enslaved people) and the Afro-Bolivian women he supervised, with acrobatic men's leaps, high kicks and bell-adorned boots, and sensuous women's choreography in short pollera skirts. Performed at Carnaval de Oruro and the Entrada Universitaria, accompanied by brass bands in 2/4 time (Wikipedia; Bolivia national law Ley 137/2011).
insight: Despite its 1969 choreographic origin, Caporales is now one of the most popular Bolivian dances, inspiring competing legal claims with Peru over its origin (Ley 137/2011 declares it Bolivian cultural heritage).
notes: Created 1969; declared Bolivian national heritage via Ley 137/2011. Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Diablada
era: Colonial-era origins; performed at Carnaval de Oruro (UNESCO 2001/2008, file 00003)
tagline: Spectacular devil masquerade dance of Oruro Carnival.
profile: The Diablada is a masked group dance in which performers wear elaborate devil costumes representing the archangel Michael defeating the seven deadly sins. It originated in the silver mines of Potosi and Oruro, where Indigenous miners merged pre-Columbian worship of the Supay (earth spirit) with Catholic iconography. Performed at Carnaval de Oruro during the procession to the Sanctuary of the Virgin of Socavon, the Diablada features pairs of male devils and a dominant she-devil (China Supay), with dramatic combat choreography and brass band accompaniment. The Oruro Carnival was UNESCO-proclaimed in 2001 and inscribed on the Representative List in 2008 (file 00003) (Wikipedia; UNESCO).
insight: The Diablada migrated from Bolivia to Chile, Peru and other Andean countries, generating competing national claims; it is one of the most internationally recognized Andean dances.
notes: UNESCO qualifier: Diablada is a constituent dance of the Carnaval de Oruro (UNESCO file 00003, proclaimed 2001, inscribed 2008). The carnival is the inscribed element, not the Diablada as a standalone. Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Morenada
era: Colonial origins (18th-19th c.); developed from enslaved African workers' traditions
tagline: Bolivian masked processional dance of the enslaved Afro-miners.
profile: The Morenada is one of Bolivia's most popular dances, performed at Carnaval de Oruro and other festivals. Dancers wear elaborate, heavy costumes including large masks of African/dark-skinned faces, enormous wheels (flecos), and mechanical masks that roll their eyes and stick out tongues. The dance represents the enslaved African workers forced to labor in Bolivia's silver mines, mimicking exhaustion and suffering with slow, shuffling steps to brass band music. It has spread throughout the Andes and Andean diaspora communities (Wikipedia; Carnaval de Oruro UNESCO file 00003).
insight: The Morenada is now one of the largest and most spectacular dances at Carnaval de Oruro, with some fraternities numbering in the hundreds; its heavy costumes can weigh over 30 kg.
notes: UNESCO qualifier: constituent dance of Carnaval de Oruro (file 00003). Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Kullawada
era: Pre-Columbian origins (Aymara weavers); modern form documented 19th-20th c.
tagline: Bolivian Aymara weavers' dance performed at Oruro Carnival.
profile: The Kullawada is a traditional Bolivian dance representing the Aymara weavers (kullawas) of the Bolivian altiplano. Dancers carry distaffs and spindles, mimicking the gestures of weaving; costumes represent the traditional dress of Aymara weavers with elaborate textiles. Historically a dance of the indigenous artisan class of Oruro and the surrounding altiplano, it is performed at Carnaval de Oruro and various Aymara festivals. Accompanied by brass bands in festive 2/4 or 6/8 meter (Wikipedia; Carnaval de Oruro sources).
insight: The Kullawada is one of the oldest dances at Carnaval de Oruro and represents the indigenous Aymara artisan tradition that predates Spanish colonization, though its current carnival form developed in the colonial era.
notes: UNESCO qualifier: constituent dance of Carnaval de Oruro (file 00003). Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Saya
era: African origins; developed in the Yungas region post-16th century
tagline: Afro-Bolivian Yungas drum-and-dance tradition.
profile: Saya is the Afro-Bolivian music and dance tradition of the Yungas region (Los Yungas and Sud Yungas provinces of La Paz department), practiced by descendants of enslaved Africans brought to work in Bolivia's silver mines. It features call-and-response singing, large barrel drums (caja), and a distinctive hip-swinging dance. Saya is both a cultural identity marker for Afro-Bolivian communities and a living tradition performed at community celebrations, festivals and political mobilizations. Distinguished from the Caporales dance that was inspired by it (Wikipedia; UNESCO Afro-Bolivian cultural sources).
insight: Saya gained national visibility when Afro-Bolivian leaders used it in political demonstrations in La Paz in the 1990s to demand constitutional recognition; it is distinct from the commercially popular Caporales, which derived from it.
aliases: Canto y baile de la Saya
notes: Distinct from Caporales which derived from it. Not UNESCO-inscribed as a standalone element. Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Tinku
region: Northern Potosi, Bolivia (Aymara and Quechua communities)
era: Pre-Columbian origins; modern dance form preserves ritual combat tradition
tagline: Bolivian ritual combat dance from the northern Potosi highlands.
profile: Tinku (meaning 'encounter' or 'meeting' in Quechua/Aymara) is both a ritual and a dance from the northern Potosi department of Bolivia, practiced by Aymara and Quechua communities. Originally a ritual combat (peleas) at festivals between communities from different moieties (upper/lower halves of the community), offering blood to Pachamama as a fertility offering; the dance form stylizes these combat movements with stamping, punching gestures, high-altitude footwork and colorful costumes with pointed leather helmets. Performed at festivals such as Cruz de Mayo and at Carnaval de Oruro (Wikipedia; MoMA/anthropological sources).
insight: Tinku embodies the Andean concept of complementary opposition (yanantin): the controlled ritual combat between upper and lower communities was believed to ensure good harvests by offering blood to Pachamama, the earth mother.

sources.title: Tinku: Ritual Combat in the Andes

notes: Not UNESCO-inscribed as a standalone. Proposed slug pending reconciliation.

name: Diablos Danzantes de Yare
tagline: Masked red devils dance in penance on Corpus Christi.
profile: A Catholic Corpus Christi ritual dance from San Francisco de Yare, Miranda, in which men in red costumes and grotesque horned masks dance backward in penance until they submit to the Blessed Sacrament, symbolizing the triumph of good over evil. The brotherhood is the oldest in the Americas, dating to the 18th century. It is part of the UNESCO-inscribed 'Dancing Devils of Corpus Christi of Venezuela,' covering 11 brotherhoods recognized on the Representative List on 6 December 2012 (file 00639) (UNESCO; Wikipedia).
insight: The tradition is part of the UNESCO-inscribed 'Dancing Devils of Corpus Christi of Venezuela,' inscribed 6 December 2012 at the 7th session of the Intergovernmental Committee (7.COM), file 00639.
notes: UNESCO file 00639, inscribed 6 December 2012 (7.COM). Distinct from joropo. UNESCO element covers 11 brotherhoods; Yare is the most famous. Discovery-phase addition.

name: Chamame
tagline: Close-embrace accordion-driven folk dance of Corrientes.
profile: A folk music-and-dance genre of northeastern Argentina (especially Corrientes province), with Guarani cultural roots blending Spanish, African and Jesuit-mission influences. Couples dance in a close chest-to-chest embrace (abrazo) without set choreography, in 2/4 time. Accompanied by accordion, bandoneon, guitar and the spontaneous sapukay (joyful cry). The dance-music is deeply tied to the Corrientes landscape - rivers, lagoons and the subtropical Iberá wetlands (UNESCO; Wikipedia).
insight: Inscribed on the UNESCO Representative List on 16 December 2020 at the 15th session (file 01600); it was Argentina's third UNESCO ICH inscription, after tango (2009) and filete porteno (2015).
notes: UNESCO RL file 01600, inscribed 16 December 2020 (15.COM). Argentina's third UNESCO ICH inscription. Discovery-phase addition.

name: Cuarteto
tagline: Upbeat Cordoba dance music with a tunga-tunga beat.
profile: A popular dance-music genre born in Cordoba in 1943, blending criollo styles with Italian and Spanish immigrant music. Named for its original four-piece bands (piano, violin, accordion, bass), it is danced in fast circles (la vuelta de cuarteto) to the characteristic 'tunga-tunga' bass rhythm. The genre democratized dance halls and became a symbol of working-class Cordoba culture; it evolved to add larger ensembles and modern instruments while retaining its distinctive beat (UNESCO; Wikipedia).
insight: Inscribed 9 December 2025 at the 20th session (20.COM) of the Intergovernmental Committee in New Delhi (file 02243), as 'Cuarteto: music, dance and lyrics in the city of Cordoba, Argentina' - Argentina's fourth UNESCO ICH expression. The foundational 'tunga-tunga' left-hand bassline is credited to pianist Leonor Marzano (1943).

sources.title: Cuarteto: music, dance and lyrics in the city of Cordoba, Argentina

notes: UNESCO RL file 02243, inscribed 9 December 2025 (20.COM, New Delhi). Argentina's fourth UNESCO ICH inscription. Discovery-phase addition.

name: Bumba-meu-boi
tagline: Maranhao festival dance-drama centered on a resurrected ox.
profile: A ritual festival complex from Maranhao combining music, dance, theatre and play around the figure of an ox that dies and is brought back to life. Tied to the June festivals (festas juninas) honoring saints, it includes distinct regional styles called sotaques (Boi de Matraca, Boi de Orquestra, Boi da Baixada, Boi de Pindaré, Boi Costeiro), each with specific costumes, instruments, rhythms and poetic forms. The cultural complex involves hundreds of communities and thousands of performers (UNESCO; IPHAN).
insight: The Cultural Complex of Bumba-meu-boi from Maranhao was inscribed on the UNESCO Representative List on 11 December 2019 at the 14th session (14.COM), file 01510 - NOT 2024 as sometimes incorrectly reported.

sources.title: Cultural Complex of Bumba-meu-boi from Maranhao

notes: UNESCO RL file 01510, inscribed 11 December 2019 (14.COM). Discovery-phase addition.

name: Zamacueca
tagline: Colonial courtship dance, ancestor of the Chilean cueca.
profile: A colonial-era song-and-dance form originating in the Viceroyalty of Peru with Spanish, Andean and African roots. A handkerchief-waving courtship dance, it spread south and evolved into the Chilean cueca, while also seeding the Peruvian marinera. Danced by a couple who never touch, waving handkerchiefs to simulate a courtship, in 6/8 or 3/4 time with guitar, cajita and singing (Wikipedia; Cueca Wikipedia).
insight: The word 'cueca' is short for 'zamacueca,' and scholars consider it the direct predecessor of Chile's national dance; it is also the ancestor of Argentina's cueca and Peru's marinera.
notes: Shared Peru/Chile heritage; catalogued under Chile per worklist. Discovery-phase addition.

name: Tonada
tagline: Melodic Chilean song form derived from Spanish settler music.
profile: An important Chilean traditional song form arising from music brought by Spanish settlers, distinguished from the cueca by an intermediate melodic section and a more prominent melody. Unlike the cueca, the tonada is primarily sung rather than danced, though it anchors central-Chilean folk repertoire at festivals and fiestas patrias. Performed with guitar, guitarron chileno and sometimes accordion by groups such as Los Huasos Quincheros and researcher-performers Violeta Parra and Margot Loyola (Wikipedia; Smithsonian Folkways).
insight: Violeta Parra collected and recorded hundreds of tonadas from rural central Chile in the 1950s-60s, crediting the form as the richest vein of Chilean traditional music.
notes: Primarily a song form; included as song-dance complex. English Wikipedia article is the broad Hispanic 'Tonada'. Discovery-phase addition.

name: Cachimbo
tagline: Festive northern-Chilean courtship dance with handkerchiefs.
profile: A folk dance of northern Chile (Tarapaca and Antofagasta regions), danced in pairs with handkerchiefs. Like the cueca it stages a courtship pursuit led by the man, with turns matched to the rhythm and gliding steps. Accompanied by zamponas, siku, guitar, violin and other instruments; a northern counterpart to the cueca, danced especially in valleys and altiplano communities of Tarapaca (Wikipedia ES; Wellnessbeam).
insight: The cachimbo is considered the northern Chilean equivalent of the cueca, sharing the handkerchief-waving courtship structure but with Andean-influenced instruments and rhythms from the atacameno/aymara traditions.

sources.title: Explore 16 Folk Dances of Chile by Region

notes: No English Wikipedia article; Spanish article and Wikidata QID Q5738644 verified. Discovery-phase addition.

name: Sirilla
tagline: Lively Chiloe couple dance of Spanish origin.
profile: A lively dance from the Chiloe Archipelago of southern Chile, descended from the Spanish seguidilla. Performed by two couples facing each other in a square formation, it features intricate footwork, partner changes, turns and handkerchief play. One of the signature dances of Chiloe's strongly Spanish-influenced southern culture, distinct from the Andean-influenced dances of the north (Wikipedia ES; Wellnessbeam).
insight: The sirilla is one of the oldest surviving dances in southern Chile and is closely linked to the Chiloe archipelago's distinct creole (chilote) culture.

sources.title: Explore 16 Folk Dances of Chile by Region

notes: No confirmed Wikidata QID; verify before publishing. No English Wikipedia article. Discovery-phase addition.

name: Danzon
tagline: Elegant ballroom couple dance of Cuban origin, beloved in Veracruz.
profile: An elegant, slow partner dance of Cuban origin (Matanzas, 1879) that entered Mexico through Yucatan and Veracruz, where it became deeply rooted. Couples dance with refined, structured footwork; today plazas in Veracruz, Mexico City and Oaxaca host regular danzon gatherings (bailes de danzon). Popular with 25 of Mexico's 32 states; more than 200 registered dance troupes (Wikipedia; Library of Congress).
insight: Danzon is popular in 25 of Mexico's 32 states, with more than 200 registered dance troupes and 20 orchestras, per the National Center for Research and Promotion of Danzon.

sources.title: Dancing the Danzon: Hispanic Heritage Month

notes: Shared Cuba/Mexico heritage; catalogued under Mexico per worklist gap. Discovery-phase addition. | UNESCO RL/00563, inscribed 2010 (5.COM).

name: Danza de la Pluma
tagline: Oaxacan feather-crown dance-drama of the Spanish conquest.
profile: A dramatic dance-theatre form from the Central Valleys of Oaxaca, performed by dancers in towering feathered crowns who enact the Spanish conquest and the fall of Moctezuma II. Accompanied by wind bands, it is the culminating act of the traditional Guelaguetza festival and is performed in towns such as Cuilapam de Guerrero, Zaachila and Teotitlan del Valle over multiple days (Wikipedia ES; Smithsonian).
insight: Considered the supreme dance of Oaxaca, the Danza de la Pluma stages the entire Aztec-Spanish encounter in a multi-day outdoor pageant; it is one of the most visually spectacular dances in all of Mexico.

sources.title: Una Sinfonia de Plumas Oaxaquena
sources.publisher: Smithsonian Center for Folklife and Cultural Heritage

notes: No English Wikipedia article; Spanish article + Wikidata QID Q5799167 verified. Discovery-phase addition.

name: Vogue
tagline: Stylized Harlem ballroom dance of model-like poses.
profile: A highly stylized dance that grew out of the Black and Latino LGBTQ ballroom scene of Harlem from the 1960s, taking its name and angular poses from fashion-magazine modeling. Styles include Old Way, New Way and Vogue Fem; it reached global fame via Madonna's 1990 song 'Vogue' and the 1990 documentary Paris Is Burning. Performed within competitive 'houses' - chosen families led by mothers and fathers - at balls where categories are judged (Wikipedia; Smithsonian NMAAHC).
insight: Willi Ninja is called the 'Godfather of Voguing'; the style's angular geometry is traced to fashion-model poses in Vogue magazine as reinterpreted by Harlem's queer Black and Latino communities from the 1960s.

sources.title: A Brief History of Voguing
sources.publisher: National Museum of African American History and Culture (Smithsonian)

notes: Not UNESCO-inscribed. Discovery-phase addition.

name: Ciranda
tagline: Pernambuco circle dance of ever-growing rings.
profile: A circle dance and music form from Pernambuco, especially Ilha de Itamaraça, in which dancers join hands in a large ring on beaches and squares and move to a slow, repeated rhythm marked by the surdo or zabumba. The circle grows as more people join; there is no limit to participants. Three main steps: the onda (wave), sacudidinho (little shake) and machucadinho (little crush). Originating among fishing communities, it is popular at festivals and public celebrations throughout Brazil's northeastern coast (Wikipedia PT; Society of Folk Dance Historians).
insight: The ciranda is associated with singer-songwriter Lia de Itamaracu (1944-2017), the 'Queen of Ciranda,' who performed continuously for decades and remains the genre's defining figure.
notes: No standalone English Wikipedia article; Portuguese article + Wikidata Q504476 verified. Discovery-phase addition.

name: Xaxado
tagline: Sertao warrior dance of the cangaceiros of Pernambuco.
profile: A folk dance originating in the Pajeú and Moxoto regions of the Pernambuco sertao in the 1920s, associated with the cangaceiro bandits of Lampiao. Danced with lateral, foot-dragging steps in a line or circle, it was originally a male dance celebrating battle victories; its name evokes the sound of sandals (alpercatas) dragging on dry ground. Popularized nationally by Luiz Gonzaga, who incorporated it into his baiao repertoire (Wikipedia; Bahia.ws).
insight: It was popularized nationally by Luiz Gonzaga, who incorporated it into his baiao repertoire, keeping alive the memory of sertao resistance; the xaxado has experienced a revival through forró festivals and northeastern pride movements.
notes: Discovery-phase addition.

name: Baiao
tagline: Northeastern Brazilian couple dance led by accordion and zabumba.
profile: A music-and-dance genre from northeastern Brazil built on a syncopated 2/4 rhythm, performed by the forro trio of accordion (sanfona), zabumba and triangle. Danced in pairs with diagonal side-steps, it carries indigenous and caipira influences and addresses sertao life, drought and migration. 'Asa Branca' by Luiz Gonzaga (1947) is its defining song (Wikipedia; Bahia.ws).
insight: Luiz Gonzaga, the 'King of Baiao,' and his song 'Asa Branca' (1947) made the genre a landmark of Brazilian culture; the baiao remains a foundational rhythm influencing forro, xote and axe.
notes: Distinct from forro (already catalogued) as a specific rhythm/dance. Discovery-phase addition.

name: Kaseko
tagline: Afro-Surinamese dance music fusing brass, drums and call-response.
profile: The dance and entertainment music of the Surinamese Creoles, fusing Western march music, jazz, calypso and Afro-Surinamese winti and kawina traditions. Rhythmically complex, it features the skratji bass drum, snare, and a wind section of saxophone, trumpet and trombone over call-and-response vocals. Kaseko emerged from 1930s street music known as Bigi Poku ('big music') and became dominant in Suriname by the mid-20th century (World Music Central; Music of Suriname Wikipedia).
insight: The term likely derives from the French 'casser le corps' ('break the body'); kaseko displaced kawina as the dominant Afro-Surinamese style while incorporating its rhythmic vocabulary.
notes: Discovery-phase addition.

name: Aleke
tagline: Modern Maroon drum-and-dance genre of eastern Suriname.
profile: A modern Maroon music-and-dance genre that integrates traditional Aukan (Ndjuka) drumming with a faster, more danceable rhythm and electric instrumentation. Developed from the 1950s by speeding up traditional Aukan music to make it more danceable, it has become one of Suriname's most popular music forms, played on kawina-style drums plus keyboards, guitar and bass (Music of Suriname Wikipedia; Suriname African Heritage).
insight: The name references Alexander, a city worker and musician who, in the 1950s, sped up traditional Aukan music to make it more danceable; aleke is now a major export of Surinamese Maroon culture.
notes: No standalone English Wikipedia article; covered in Music of Suriname. Wikidata Q22142599 verified. Discovery-phase addition.

name: Muneira
tagline: Galician bagpipe dance in lively 6/8 time.
profile: A traditional dance and musical genre of Galicia and parts of Asturias, performed in a lively 6/8 tempo to the gaita (Galician bagpipe), bombos and tambourines. Often danced in pairs or circles with arms raised, it reflects the region's Celtic heritage and is sometimes called the 'miller's dance' (muino = mill). Gaita virtuoso Carlos Nunez is among its best-known performers (Wikipedia; Superprof).
insight: Roots World describes the muneira as the Galician equivalent of a jig; it is central to the Galician folk revival (renacemento) that has made Galicia's Celtic music internationally recognized since the 1970s.

sources.title: Your Guide to the Traditional Spanish Dance Styles

notes: Discovery-phase addition.

name: Fandango
tagline: Lively Spanish couple courtship dance with castanets.
profile: A fast couple dance that became Spain's most famous dance before flamenco, traditionally accompanied by guitar, hand-clapping and castanets. Emerging in the 18th century (likely in Andalusia), it spread to Extremadura, Asturias, Valencia, the Basque Country and beyond, with shorter variants called fandanguillos. In Andalusia it merged with flamenco to give rise to the 'fandangos flamencos' (Wikipedia; Sensational Spain).
insight: In Andalusia the fandango merged with flamenco to give rise to one of the most elemental flamenco palos ('fandangos flamencos'), directly connecting the folk and art-music traditions.
notes: Pan-Spanish/Portuguese form; catalogued under Spain per worklist gap. Distinct from already-catalogued Iberian dances. Discovery-phase addition.

name: Highland Fling
tagline: Vigorous Scottish solo dance performed on the spot.
profile: One of the core competitive Highland dances, a vigorous solo dance performed on the spot to a strathspey (or occasionally a reel), traditionally said to celebrate victory or imitate a stag displaying on a hillside. It features intricate footwork, shedding movements and sharply pointed feet, danced in full Highland costume to bagpipe music. Along with the Sword Dance and Seann Triubhas, it forms the core of competitive Highland dancing (Wikipedia; RSOBHD).
insight: One theory holds the Highland Fling is a Foursome Reel reduced to its solo show steps; another links it to Gaelic harvest celebrations; its modern competitive form was standardized by the Royal Scottish Official Board of Highland Dancing (RSOBHD), formed 1950.
notes: Discovery-phase addition.

name: Sebucan
tagline: Venezuelan ribbon dance braiding a maypole of colors.
profile: A ribbon or maypole dance ('baile de las cintas' / 'palo de cintas') in which dancers each hold the end of a colored ribbon and weave and unweave them around a central pole. In eastern Venezuela and Guayana it is called sebucan, named after the indigenous woven tube (sebucan) used to press cassava. The braided pattern formed by the ribbons resembles the indigenous sebucan used to extract liquid from grated bitter cassava (Wikipedia ES; Hablemos de Culturas).
insight: The braided pattern formed by the ribbons resembles the indigenous sebucan used to extract liquid from grated bitter yuca amarga; the dance thus embeds indigenous technological knowledge in celebratory form.
notes: No confirmed Wikidata QID; no English Wikipedia article. Verify before publishing. Member of the broader maypole/ribbon dance family. Discovery-phase addition.

name: Tambor (Venezuela)
tagline: Afro-Venezuelan drum dance of the central coast.
profile: An Afro-Venezuelan music-and-dance form from the central coast, especially the Barlovento region of Miranda state, driven by large mina and curbata drums. It peaks during the Fiesta de San Juan (June) and the Festival de San Pedro (June 29) and blends Indigenous, African and European elements; tambores commonly emerge at any Venezuelan celebration late at night. Rooted in African heritage and devotion to saints such as San Juan Bautista and San Benito de Palermo (Wikipedia; MLB.com cultural piece).
insight: Joropo and tambor are the two dances most identified with Venezuelan culture; tambor is rooted in African heritage, particularly of the Barlovento communities descended from enslaved Africans brought to cacao plantations.
notes: Discovery-phase addition.

name: Champeta
tagline: Afro-Colombian dance music born in Cartagena's barrios.
profile: An Afro-Colombian music-and-dance genre that emerged on the Caribbean coast around Cartagena and the San Basilio de Palenque area, blending African genres (soukous, highlife, coupé-décalé) with local Caribbean rhythms. Danced with sensual, energetic hip and body movements, it grew through massive sound-system (pico) culture and street parties (fiestas de pico). Despite long stigmatization as 'music of the poor,' champeta gained mainstream acceptance in the 2000s-2010s (Wikipedia; Urban Champeta Wikipedia).
insight: Champeta is a defining cultural expression of Cartagena's Afro-descendant communities and was long stigmatized by elites as lowbrow; its rehabilitation parallels broader recognition of Afro-Colombian cultural contributions.
notes: Caribbean-coast genre; distinct from Pacific-coast currulao (already in catalog). Discovery-phase addition.

name: Les Lanciers
era: Introduced 1860; current form set before WWI; living tradition
tagline: Denmark's beloved five-figure quadrille, danced at school proms.
profile: Les Lanciers (The Lancers) is a square dance for four couples, a variant of the quadrille composed of five figures, each performed four times so every couple leads. Introduced to Denmark from England in 1860, it became the rage of Copenhagen high society and acquired a uniquely Danish feature - the fast 'gentlemen's mill.' While the dance vanished elsewhere around 1920, Denmark is now the only country where it survives as a living social tradition, taught in most high schools and performed at school galas and proms (Wikipedia; ScienceNordic).
insight: University of Copenhagen associate professor Inger Damsholt notes Danes are the only people in the world who still dance Lanciers regularly, 'without any of us being aware that we are dealing with something particularly Danish.'
notes: Denmark's de-facto national quadrille; QID verified to the dance. Discovery sweep 2.

name: Dansk Polka
tagline: Smooth, moderate-tempo Danish form of the polka.
profile: The Danish polka (dansk polka) is the Danish regional form of the pan-European polka, a duple-meter couple dance. Characterised as a smooth, moderate-tempo polka, contrasting with the bouncy and faster polkett of Sweden and polkka of Finland. The polka entered the Danish popular repertoire around 1850 alongside the mazurka, and was preserved by folk-dance societies from 1901 onward, becoming part of the standard gammeldans repertoire of waltz, mazurka, schottis, polka and hopsa (Scandinavian Dance Terms; Danish traditional music Wikipedia).
insight: Gordon Tracie's Scandinavian dance lexicon defines dansk polka as a smooth, moderate tempo polka of Danish type, as contrasted with the bouncy and usually faster polkett of Sweden and polkka of Finland.

sources.title: Scandinavian Dance Terms
sources.publisher: Folk Dance Federation of California South

notes: No standalone English Wikipedia article or Wikidata QID for the Danish variant specifically. Flag for human review. Discovery sweep 2.

name: Kolomyika
tagline: Fast Hutsul circle dance and rhymed song from the Carpathians.
profile: The kolomyika is a Hutsul (Ukrainian) genre combining a fast-paced 2/4 folk dance with comedic rhymed couplets. Named after the Carpathian town of Kolomyia in eastern Galicia, it is a mimetic circular dance with many variants, historically popular among Ukrainians and Poles and known as kalamajka in northeastern Slovakia. It remains a living wedding and festivity dance in western Ukraine. The Ukrainian national anthem was written in kolomyika verse, and Bela Bartok believed the Hungarian swineherd dance derived from it (Wikipedia; Internet Encyclopedia of Ukraine).
insight: Thousands of kolomyika texts have been transcribed; Volodymyr Hnatiuk's Kolomyiky (3 vols, 1905-07) contains 8,622 texts, and Bela Bartok believed the Hungarian swineherd dance derived from the Ukrainian kolomyika.
notes: QID verified as combined music+dance genre. Discovery sweep 2.

name: Hutsulka
tagline: Lively Hutsul couple dance of the Ukrainian Carpathians.
profile: The hutsulka is a fast, energetic couple dance of the Hutsul highlanders of the Ukrainian Carpathians (Hutsulshchyna), rhythmically related to the kolomyika and kozachok but faster and of later origin. It is among the most popular living Hutsul dances, performed at weddings and gatherings with rapid footwork, spins and four characteristic figures (tropata, hayduka, holubtsi, perekruchuvannya), accompanied by troista muzyka ensembles featuring fiddle, tsymbaly, sopilka and drum (Wikipedia: Ukrainian dance; Internet Encyclopedia of Ukraine).
insight: According to the Internet Encyclopedia of Ukraine, the hutsulka is the most popular Hutsul folk dance; around Rakhiv it appears in the related local form trybushanka.
notes: CONFLICT: en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hutsulka may redirect to Kolomyika article; Wikidata Q8273152 has its own item ('Ukrainian folk dance', inception 1850) with ukwiki sitelink. Flag for human confirmation. Do not confuse with song 'Hutsulka Ksenya'. Discovery sweep 2.

name: Viennese Waltz
era: Late 18th century-present; socially established at the Congress of Vienna (1814-15)
tagline: Fast rotary 3/4 ballroom waltz, the heart of Vienna's ball culture.
profile: The Viennese waltz (Wiener Walzer) is the original and fastest form of the waltz, a rotary couple dance in 3/4 time danced at about 180 beats (58-60 measures) per minute, performed in closed hold with natural (right) and reverse (left) turns. Emerging from Austrian/Bavarian turning dances in the late 18th century, it gained social acceptance at the Congress of Vienna (1814-15) and was raised to global popularity by the Strauss dynasty. It is central to Vienna's ball season, opened with the proclamation 'Alles Walzer,' and to New Year rituals via Johann Strauss II's 'Blue Danube' (Wikipedia; Austrian Commission for UNESCO).
insight: In November 2017 the Austrian Commission for UNESCO added 'The Viennese Waltz - Played, Danced, and Sung' to Austria's NATIONAL Inventory of Intangible Cultural Heritage; it has NEVER been inscribed on UNESCO's international Representative List and no joint Austria-Germany-Czech inscription exists.

sources.title: The Viennese Waltz - Played, Danced, and Sung

notes: CRITICAL: unesco:false. The 'joint Austria-Germany-Czech 2023 inscription' claim is unsupported; only a 2017 Austrian national-inventory listing exists. Discovery sweep 2.

name: Lindjo
region: Dubrovnik Littoral and Konavle, southern Dalmatia
era: Name dates to late 19th century; older as kolo/poskocica
tagline: Lively Dubrovnik-area paired wheel dance led by the lijerica.
profile: Lindjo is the traditional paired wheel dance (kolo/poskocica) of the Dubrovnik Littoral and Konavle in southern Dalmatia, Croatia, accompanied by the three-stringed lijerica. A caller (vikac) directs the figures and pairings while the lijerica player stomps the rhythm. The name derives from the late-19th-century nickname of celebrated lijerica player Nikola Lale of Petraca (1843-1907). It is inscribed on Croatia's national Register of Cultural Properties and kept alive by the Lindjo Folklore Ensemble (founded 1964/65) (Wikipedia; ResearchGate ethnography).
insight: A group of 270 dancers performed lindjo on Dubrovnik's Stradun in October 2013, an event entered into the Guinness Book of World Records as the largest performance of this intangible cultural heritage element.

sources.title: Intangible Culture as Heritage: The Lindjo - Kolo Dance, from the Dubrovnik Littoral

notes: Croatian national heritage register, not UNESCO. Complements existing moreska and nijemo-kolo entries. Discovery sweep 2.

name: Sarba
region: Romania and Moldova (especially sub-Carpathian south and Moldavia)
tagline: Fast Romanian line/circle dance with driving triplet rhythm.
profile: The sarba is a lively Romanian folk dance, normally played in a 2/4 melody with triplet groupings giving a 6/8 feel. It can be danced in circle, line or couple formations, alternating travelling and resting phrases with improvisation, the end dancers acting as leaders. The name means 'Serbian [dance],' though no equivalent exists in the current Serbian repertoire. Widespread across Romania and Moldova - especially sub-Carpathian and Moldavian regions - it is nearly indispensable at weddings and survives in Klezmer music (Wikipedia; Eliznik ethnographic pages).
insight: Ethnochoreology classifies the common sarba as a three-measure structured social dance; Ciuleandra is a specific sarba type from north Oltenia and Muntenia, particularly associated with demonological legends.
notes: Complements existing hora and calus entries. Discovery sweep 2.

name: Reinlender
tagline: Norwegian schottische, played with distinctive syncopation.
profile: The reinlender is the Norwegian form of the schottische, a duple-meter couple turning dance in the gammeldans (old-time) repertoire alongside vals, polka and mazurka. Its name suggests origin in the Rhine region (Rheinlander). The Norwegian reinlender is characteristically played very legato with a decided syncopation; the basic step combines side-steps with a turning sequence, and it remains common at Norwegian social dances and folk gatherings (Schottische Wikipedia; Scandinavian Dance Terms).
insight: Gordon Tracie's Scandinavian dance lexicon describes the reinlender as the Norwegian form of the Schottische, characteristically played very legato, with a distinctive syncopation - contrasting with the staccato Swedish schottis.

sources.title: Scandinavian Dance Terms
sources.publisher: Folk Dance Federation of California South

notes: No dedicated English Wikipedia article; covered under general 'Schottische'. Standalone Norwegian/German articles only. Discovery sweep 2.

name: Jenkka
tagline: Fast Finnish schottische, staccato and quick-tempo.
profile: The jenkka is the Finnish form of the schottische, a fast partner dance in 2/4 or 4/4 time at about 140 beats per minute. Arising in the mid-19th century and danced in rural areas well into the 1960s, it uses a polka-like run (left-right-left-hop, right-left-right-hop) followed by a chain of pivot turns in closed position. Generally faster than the Norwegian reinlender and Swedish schottis, and characteristically staccato. The 1960s line-dance craze letkajenkka (letkis) was derived from it (Wikipedia; Finnish Living Heritage Wiki).
insight: Tracie's lexicon notes that in some Finnish districts the schottische is instead called sotiisi (from the Swedish schottis) and is played somewhat slower and less staccato than the jenkka.

sources.title: Folk partner dancing and dance music

notes: Complements existing katrilli, purpuri and skolt-sami-quadrille entries. Discovery sweep 2.

name: Schottis
era: Early 19th century-present (modern tempo fixed late 19th century)
tagline: Swedish schottische, the standard Scandinavian form.
profile: The schottis is the Swedish form of the schottische, the standard Scandinavian schottische and a staple of the gammaldans repertoire. A duple-meter couple dance arriving in Sweden in the early 1800s - considerably slower then - and taking its modern tempo and style in the late 19th century. The older legato regional form, gammal schottis, is distinguished by svikt (springiness) and is danced without the flamboyant hop of the common schottische (Schottische Wikipedia; Scandinavian Dance Terms).
insight: Tracie's lexicon defines schottis as the 'common garden variety Scandinavian schottische, essentially a Swedish form,' while gammal schottis is a legato, often minor-mode variant marked by its characteristic springiness.

sources.title: Historical Notes on the Swedish Gammal Schottis
sources.publisher: Folk Dance Federation of California South

notes: No standalone English 'Schottis' article; uses general Schottische QID Q329385. Flag for possible merge with general schottische entry. Discovery sweep 2.

name: Horo
tagline: Bulgaria's chain/circle dance family in asymmetric meters.
profile: Horo is the umbrella term for Bulgaria's chain and circle dances, in which dancers hold hands or belts in a line or circle, often in asymmetric meters (e.g., 5/16, 7/16, 11/16). Each region has its own style - pravo, shopsko, paydushko, dunavsko, kopanitsa - so distinctive that knowledgeable observers can identify a group's home region from how they dance the pravo. The word horo simply means 'dance' and is appended to specific dance names (Bulgarian folk dance Wikipedia; Hora dance Wikipedia).
insight: The well-known tune 'Eleno Mome' exists in three notated meters (7, 13 and 12 beats), illustrating how Bulgarian horo rhythms resist exact rational subdivision and are distinct from Western metered dance.
notes: UMBRELLA entry to anchor named Bulgarian horo types. Catalog already has specific Bulgarian horos (rachenitsa, kopanitsa, paydushko-horo, etc.). Discovery sweep 2.

name: Breaking (B-boying)
tagline: Athletic Bronx street dance, the foundational hip-hop dance.
profile: Breaking (also breakdancing or b-boying/b-girling) is an athletic street dance developed by African American and Puerto Rican youth in the Bronx, New York City, in the early 1970s. One of the four pillars of hip-hop culture, it comprises four core movement types - toprock, downrock/footwork, power moves and freezes - set to the drum breaks of funk, soul and hip-hop records looped by DJs (a concept credited to DJ Kool Herc). It spread globally and became a competitive battle culture; it debuted as an Olympic sport at Paris 2024 (Wikipedia; Britannica).
insight: Breaking was contested as an Olympic medal sport for the first time at Paris 2024 (Place de la Concorde, 9-10 August); it is NOT inscribed on any UNESCO intangible cultural heritage list.
notes: Explicitly unesco:false; Olympic sport (Paris 2024), NOT UNESCO ICH. Discovery sweep 2.

name: Blues dance
tagline: African-American vernacular partner dances danced to blues music.
profile: Blues dance is a family of African-American vernacular partner dances created to accompany blues music, rooted in sub-Saharan African dance traditions carried by enslaved people and creolized in the American South. Emerging in juke joints and later urban rent parties and ballrooms, it is marked by a grounded, athletic stance, weight on the balls of the feet, bent knees, close partner connection and a blues aesthetic of rhythmic body movement and improvisation. Idioms split broadly into Jukin' (juke-joint) and Ballroomin' styles, with named forms such as the Slow Drag, Struttin', Funky Butt and Savoy Walk (Wikipedia; Spurlock Museum).
insight: W.C. Handy documented around 1905 in Cleveland, Mississippi how a three-man string band's haunting tune sent 'the dancers wild,' an early account of blues-idiom dancing; the form is closely related to and influenced the Lindy Hop and swing dances.

sources.title: Blues Dancing and Its African American Roots

notes: Discovery sweep 2.

name: Kujawiak
era: 19th century (name first attested 1827; adopted as national dance late 19th century)
tagline: The slow, lyrical Polish couple dance from Kuyavia.
profile: The kujawiak is a Polish folk dance from the Kuyavia (Kujawy) region of central Poland and one of the five national dances of Poland, alongside the krakowiak, mazur, oberek, and polonaise. Set in triple meter, it is the most lyrical and melancholic of the group, danced by couples who rotate smoothly around a circle in a flowing, rubato style. It belongs to the mazurka rhythm family and was historically performed in a suite with the faster oberek. A Kuyavia folk band typically featured one or two violins, a bass, and sometimes a clarinet and small drum (Wikipedia; Polish Music Center USC).
insight: Per Wikipedia, the kujawiak is characterized by its rubato tempo and calm, lyrical nature - contrasting with the faster mazur and oberek - and was historically performed in a medley sequence with the oberek.
notes: Completes Poland's five national dances in the catalog (with polonaise, mazurka, krakowiakk already present). Of Poland's five national dances, only the polonaise is UNESCO-inscribed (2023, 18.COM). Discovery sweep 3.

name: Oberek
era: Term 'obertas' first attested 1679; national-dance status 19th century
tagline: The fastest Polish national dance, full of spins and lifts.
profile: The oberek (also obertas or ober) is a lively Polish couple dance in fast triple meter and the fastest of Poland's five national dances. Its name derives from the Polish obracac sie ('to spin'), reflecting its core dynamic of rapid rotation. Originating in the villages of Mazovia (Mazowsze), it features quick steps, constant turns, stamps, and acrobatic lifts, and is traditionally accompanied by a violin-led village band (kapela). It belongs to the mazurka rhythm family with the slower kujawiak and moderate mazur (Wikipedia; Polish Music Center USC).
insight: Wikipedia notes the oberek is performed at a much quicker pace than the Polish waltz, and the Polish-American oberek became the second-most popular dance in Polish-American music after the polka.
notes: Completes Poland's five national dances. Of Poland's national dances, only the polonaise is UNESCO-inscribed (2023). Discovery sweep 3.

name: Rapper Sword
region: Northumberland and County Durham, North East England
tagline: Fast Northumbrian sword dance of the mining villages.
profile: Rapper sword (or short sword dance) is a hilt-and-point sword dance unique to Northumberland and County Durham in North East England, emerging from the pit villages of Tyneside and Wearside where miners first performed it. Five dancers are linked in an unbroken chain by flexible two-handled steel 'rapper' swords, weaving intricate figures, locks, and a final star at high speed, accompanied by traditional folk music and percussive stepping. Supporting characters Tommy and Betty announce the dance and engage the audience (Wikipedia; Rapper Online).
insight: The tradition nearly died out by the early 20th century but was revived after Cecil Sharp published notations in The Sword Dances of Northern England; a further revival was initiated in 1949 by students at Kings College in Newcastle upon Tyne. The modern dance is performed at around 140-160 beats per minute.

sources.title: The Rapper Sword Dance - Origins and History

notes: Distinct from the Scottish Highland sword dance already in the catalog. English (Northumbrian) tradition. Discovery sweep 3.

name: Salsa
region: New York City (roots in Cuba and Puerto Rico)
era: Mid-20th century (term popularized in New York, 1960s-1970s)
tagline: Afro-Caribbean partner dance crystallized in New York.
profile: Salsa is a popular Latin partner dance danced to salsa music, developed from a lineage of Afro-Cuban and Caribbean social dances - especially son cubano, danzon, mambo, cha-cha-cha, and rumba. The term was popularized in New York in the 1960s-70s by Johnny Pacheco, Fania Records, and the Fania All-Stars as an umbrella label for an Afro-Caribbean dance music with roots in Cuba and mid-century New York. Distinct regional styles include New York ('on 2'), Los Angeles, Cuban (casino), and Colombian (Cali) salsa (Wikipedia; US National Park Service).
insight: Eddie Torres - dubbed the Mambo King by Tito Puente - is credited with formalizing the New York 'on 2' timing and created the first official Mambo (salsa) syllabus in 1972; salsa's tempo ranges roughly 150-250 bpm, with most social dancing between 160 and 220 bpm.
notes: Salsa/Salsa de Cali is NOT on the UNESCO international list (Colombia's Resolution 0367/2024 is national-list only). Country = United States as the city where the term/dance was crystallized, with Cuban and Puerto Rican roots. Discovery sweep 3.

name: Polka
era: Early 19th century (origin story c. 1830; Prague ballrooms 1835; Paris ballrooms c. 1843)
tagline: Lively Bohemian couple dance in 2/4 time.
profile: The polka is a dance style and genre of dance music in 2/4 time originating in 19th-century Bohemia, now part of the Czech Republic. Characterized by three quick steps and a hop danced by rotating couples, it swept European ballrooms in the 1840s ('polkamania') and spread across the Americas with Central and Eastern European immigrants, generating regional styles from polka nortena in Mexico to Polish-American polka. The name is commonly linked to the Czech word pulka ('half-step') (Wikipedia; Britannica).
insight: Encyclopaedia Britannica describes the polka as a lively courtship dance of Bohemian folk origin characterized by three quick steps and a hop danced to music in 2/4 time; Czech cultural historian Cenek Zibrt traced its half-tempo and half-jump step to the Czech word pulka.
notes: Assigned to Czech Republic/Bohemia as origin country. Resolves the gap where polka is referenced throughout the catalog without its own entry. Discovery sweep 3.

name: Waltz
era: 18th century (fashionable in Vienna c. 1780s; reached England 1812-1813)
tagline: The foundational closed-position triple-time couple dance.
