profile: The waltz (from German Walzer, 'to roll or revolve') is a ballroom and folk dance in triple (3/4) time, performed primarily in closed position, originating from German-speaking regions of Europe in the 18th century. Evolving from the landler and allemande ('German Dance'), it was the first widely popular dance performed in a closed hold, shocking polite society before becoming the model for many later ballroom dances. The modern slow/standard (English) waltz, danced around 84-90 bpm, became the predominant ballroom form in the 20th century (Wikipedia; Britannica).
insight: Per Wikipedia, the waltz especially its closed position became the example for the creation of many other ballroom dances, and diarist Thomas Raikes recorded that no event ever produced so great a sensation in English society as the introduction of the waltz in 1813.
notes: Distinct from existing viennese-waltz (AT) and laendler (AT) entries. Country = Germany to reflect the German Walzer origin and differentiate from the Austrian Viennese variant. Discovery sweep 3.

name: Schottische
era: Mid-19th century (appeared across Europe c. 1850; in Bavaria as Rheinlander by 1844)
tagline: German couple dance, a slower polka relative.
profile: The schottische is a partnered country dance, considered a kind of slower polka, that spread across Victorian-era ballrooms as part of the mid-19th-century Central European dance craze. In Germany, Austria, Switzerland and Sweden the polka-like folk variant is known as the Rheinlander (or Bavarian polka), danced to tunes in 2/4 time. Despite its German name meaning 'Scottish,' it has no direct Scottish origin, having formed in German-speaking and Bohemian Central Europe. National variants include the Norwegian reinlender, Finnish jenkka, Swedish schottis, and Spanish chotis (Wikipedia; WikiDanceSport).
insight: German dancing master Friedrich Zorn recorded that the schottische appeared across Europe in 1850 'which had, as early as 1844, been executed in Bavaria under the name Rheinlander,' and in the Rhenish countries was known as the Bavarian Polka.
notes: Rheinlander has no standalone English Wikipedia article; catalogued as Schottische (Q329385) with Rheinlander as primary alias. Complements existing reinlender (NO, needs_eyes_on), jenkka (FI), schottis (SE) entries. Discovery sweep 3.

name: Vikivaki
tagline: Iceland's medieval Nordic ring and ballad dance.
profile: Vikivaki is an old Nordic ring dance practiced in Iceland from the Middle Ages to the present, danced in a circle with participants holding hands or each other's shoulders, stepping two steps left and one right with stamping accents, often on the eighth step. The word denotes the dance, the ballad-songs (vikivakakvedi) sung to accompany it, and the festive gatherings where it is performed. It is the best known of the Icelandic sagnadansar (story dances) and is widely described as the national dance of Iceland (Iceland Review; Folk Dance Federation of California).
insight: Banned and nearly lost under Danish rule, the tradition was revived in the 20th century from a repertoire compiled by Helgi Valtynsson; as Iceland Review notes, the collection includes around 30 dances, only five of which are Icelandic folk songs - it also includes Schubert songs and Norwegian folk songs.
notes: No English Wikipedia article; only Icelandic article (is.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vikivaki). Iceland has no UNESCO ICH inscriptions. Discovery sweep 4.

name: Landler (Swiss form)
era: Swiss form crystallized in early 1900s Zurich
tagline: Switzerland's national folk dance and music in 3/4 time.
profile: The Landler is a triple-time couple folk dance prevalent in Switzerland, southern Germany and Austria. The Swiss form developed its own distinctive style and instrumentation (Schwyzerorgelei accordion, clarinet, double bass) and became, in its Zurich-centered heyday of the 1930s-40s, the undisputed number-one dance and entertainment music in the country, contributing strongly to Swiss national identity. Dancers perform turning and stamping movements in pairs, often in traditional costume (SUISA blog; Smithsonian Folkways).
insight: Per SUISA blog, so-called Landler kings like Stocker Sepp, Kasi Geisser and Jost Ribary shaped the Zurich sound which later went down in history as Swiss national music; in its 1930s-40s heyday Landler had achieved national fame as the undisputed number one music for dance and entertainment.

sources.title: The Landler originated in the city of Zurich

notes: Distinct from the Austrian Landler entry already in catalog; shares the English Wikipedia article. Switzerland ratified the 2003 ICH Convention on 16 July 2008; the Landler is on Switzerland's national List of Living Traditions but NOT UNESCO-inscribed internationally. Discovery sweep 4.

name: Klompendansen
era: Traditional (roots claimed to 14th century); standardized clog-dance form 20th century
tagline: Dutch wooden-clog folk dance.
profile: Klompendansen is the traditional Dutch clog dance, also called Boerendansen (farmer-dancing). Dancers wear wooden shoes (klompen) and create rhythm by tapping toes and heels on a wooden floor, accompanied by accordion and barrel organ. Dancing clogs are made lighter than everyday clogs, with ash-wood soles and a lower-cut top. It is performed in traditional regional Dutch costume and remains a recognizable emblem of Dutch folk culture (Wikipedia; DanceUs).
insight: Per Wikipedia, in 2006 nearly 500 teenagers attempted the Guinness Book of World Records bid for the largest number of clog dancers, held in The Hague; they danced the ballet version of the Dutch clog dance rather than the folk version.
notes: Wikidata Q25497462 may conflate Dutch and Lithuanian dances; verify. Netherlands ratified the 2003 Convention in 2012 but Klompendansen is not UNESCO-inscribed. Discovery sweep 4.

name: Welsh stepdance (Clocsio)
era: Traditional, unbroken tradition; revived mid-20th century
tagline: Athletic Welsh clog dance with candle and broom tricks.
profile: The Welsh stepdance (Dawns stepio) or Welsh clog dance (Clocsio) is a traditional percussive dance in wooden clogs, performed historically by slate-quarry workers and farmers who competed to display stamina and athleticism. It is stylistically distinct from English clogging and includes signature tricks such as extinguishing a candle flame with the clog soles, toby stepping (a squatting Cossack-like kick), straddle jumps and leaping over brooms. It is an integral part of the eisteddfod competition tradition (Wikipedia; Wales.com).
insight: Welsh clog dancing is described as an unbroken tradition (not a revival), kept alive partly by the traveller community and by figures such as Hywel Wood of Parc near Bala.
notes: The UK is not a State Party to the 2003 ICH Convention. Discovery sweep 4.

name: Furiant
tagline: Fast, fiery Bohemian dance with shifting accents.
profile: The Furiant is a Bohemian folk dance characterized by rapid tempo and irregular changing rhythms, hemiolas and syncopation. Usually written in 3/4 with off-beat accents that divide the opening measures into duple units, it is danced in pairs and became extremely popular in 19th-century Bohemia. Czech composers including Smetana (The Bartered Bride) and Dvorak (Slavonic Dances) incorporated it, making it, alongside the sousedska, a Czech national dance (Wikipedia; Radio Prague International).
insight: The word also denotes a stubborn, boastful person; as Ladislav Urban wrote in The Music of Bohemia (1919, Czechoslovak Arts Club of New York City): 'Besides the Polka, there is another Czech folk-dance with characteristic wild rhythm: The Furiant, which means a boasting farmer.'
notes: Most associated with Plzen in western Bohemia. Discovery sweep 4.

name: Ceska beseda
era: Mid-19th century (created during the Czech National Revival, 1860s)
tagline: Czech national ballroom folk dance in quadrille form.
profile: The Ceska beseda is a Czech national figure dance performed in quadrille formation in several parts, each woven from regional folk dances (sousedska, furiant, kuzelka, strasak and others) chosen by national-revival leaders to form a composite national dance, comparable to Mexico's Jarabe Tapatio. Originally danced in tuxedos and evening gowns to orchestral music, it later spread in folk-costume and brass-band forms, with well-known Moravian and emigrant (American Czech) variants (Society of Folk Dance Historians; Clarkson History).
insight: Beseda means 'company' or 'gathering'; the original Ceska beseda was a high-brow salon dance meant to show that Czech culture was as sophisticated as the fashionable salons of Paris.
notes: No English Wikipedia article. Wikidata Q11179556 is the dance; do not confuse with Q47005375 (a theatre building in Liberec). Discovery sweep 4.

name: Nijemo kolo (Bosnia)
region: Dinaric region of Bosnia and Herzegovina
tagline: Dinaric 'silent' circle dance without instruments.
profile: The Nijemo kolo ('silent kolo') is a closed circle dance of the Dinaric region spanning Bosnia, Croatia and Montenegro, performed without instrumental accompaniment. Dancers sing and keep time by the sound of their feet and the jingling of metal costume jewelry, usually progressing from simple steps to faster footwork. In Bosnia the regional forms are known as Starobosansko kolo and Glamoc kolo; male dancers lead female partners in energetic, seemingly spontaneous steps. It is distinct from the accordion/brass-accompanied kolo already catalogued for Serbia (Folkdance Footnotes; UNESCO: Croatian variant).
insight: Nijemo literally means 'mute'; the term 'silent' entered English because translators found no natural rendering of a dance performed deliberately without music. The Croatian Dalmatian-hinterland variant is UNESCO-inscribed (2011, RL 00359); the Bosnian form is not.
notes: The Croatian variant IS UNESCO-inscribed (2011, RL 00359, Croatia only); the Bosnian form is NOT. Distinct from existing kolo-serbia entry. Discovery sweep 4.

name: Crnogorsko Oro
era: Traditional (origin attributed to the Crmnica region)
tagline: Montenegro's eagle-imitating circle dance.
profile: Crnogorsko oro is the national folk dance of Montenegro, originating in the Dinaric region. The name derives from the dialectal 'orao' (eagle), referencing how dancers imitate an eagle's movements. Young men and women form a circle (kolo) and sing, daring one another into the center; a daring young man enters and dances eagle-style, often joined by a woman, while the crowd responds in song. The dance is performed without musical instruments and frequently ends with dancers forming a two-tier human circle/pyramid (Wikipedia; Discover Montenegro).
insight: Oro has been proclaimed a cultural good of national significance and was presented as a symbol of national tradition at Expo 2020 Dubai, finishing with a human pyramid.
notes: Discovery sweep 4.

name: Red River Jig
tagline: Metis fiddle tune and jig, the Metis national anthem.
profile: The Red River Jig (Michif: oayache mannin) is the most famous Metis dance and fiddle tune, blending First Nations footwork with Scottish, Irish and French-Canadian step-dance, reel, jig and quadrille steps. Danced in two parts - a basic jig step to a high fiddle section, then fancy footwork to a lower section - it is performed with the upper body kept straight and arms at the sides. Often called the unofficial Metis national anthem, it remains central to Metis identity across the Homeland (Wikipedia; Manitoba Metis Federation).
insight: Per the Manitoba Metis Federation, the first recorded reference to the Red River Jig was in 1860, when Mr. Macdallas played the tune for the wedding dance of a Metis couple; Father Pere Brocher, who conducted the marriage ceremony, named the tune the Red River Jig.
notes: Canada is not a State Party to the 2003 ICH Convention. Metis sacred tradition - treat with cultural respect. Discovery sweep 4.

name: Gigue quebecoise
tagline: French-Canadian percussive step dance.
profile: In Quebec, percussive step dancing is known as la gigue, brought by Irish and Scottish immigrants from the 1830s and adopted by French-speaking Canadians. The movements of the feet, ankles and legs are small and close to the ground, producing rhythmic sounds. In French-speaking Quebec the gigue is danced in 2/4 and 3/2 time and is interwoven with quadrilles, cotillions and the distinctively Quebecois square dances (sets). It is traditionally passed down within families (Dance in Quebec Wikipedia; Washington Revels).
insight: Pierre Chartrand notes that in Quebec the gigue is mostly danced to reels rather than 6/8 jigs, distinguishing it from Irish and Ontario English-Canadian step traditions.

sources.title: The Origin of the Gigue (Quebecois Step Dancing)

notes: No standalone Wikipedia article or distinct Wikidata item for gigue quebecoise specifically; covered within Dance in Quebec (Q3015656). Discovery sweep 4.

name: Kaseko (French Guiana)
tagline: Creole drum rhythm and dance of French Guiana.
profile: Kaseko is a Creole musical genre of French Guiana that also designates its drums and its dance - a fusion of African, European and American styles played with three drums and a ti-bwa. The name derives from the French 'casser le corps' ('break the body'), describing a swift dance used during the slavery era. Built on African rhythmic traditions blended with European ballroom forms, it is performed as a lively, often flirtatious couple's dance at weddings, festivals and community dances (bals konvwe) (Wikipedia; Music of French Guiana).
insight: Kaseko sits within a rich Creole repertoire that also includes the graje, lerol (a Guianese Creole quadrille), beliya, labasyou and kaladja, forming a distinct French Guianese Creole dance tradition separate from mainland French Caribbean forms.
notes: Distinct from the Surinamese kaseko (slug: kaseko) already in catalog. French Guiana is an overseas department of France; France is a State Party but Kaseko is not UNESCO-inscribed. Discovery sweep 4.

name: Kwe-kwe (Queh-queh)
tagline: Afro-Guyanese pre-wedding song-and-dance ritual.
profile: Kwe-kwe (also queh-queh, kweh-kweh, mayan) is a pre-nuptial ritual indigenous to Guyana, performed on the eve of an Afro-Guyanese wedding. Of African derivation but with no known direct African equivalent, it evolved within Guyanese Creole communities. Dancers form a circle moving anti-clockwise; rhythm is created by the pounding of feet and the human voice as the primary instrument. Led by a tutor who calls and the group responds, the ritual teaches moral behaviour, chastity and the rites of marriage through witty, sexually candid songs (Guyana Chronicle; Things Guyana).
insight: Scholars stress that although kwe-kwe has African roots, it was created in the Guyanese context to replace longer African initiation rites; Associate Professor Gillian Richards-Greaves emphasizes it should be respected as a distinctly Guyanese social institution, not merely a performance.

sources.title: Interview with Associate Professor Gillian Richards-Greaves on Kweh-Kweh

notes: No Wikipedia article and no Wikidata item. Flag for human review. Discovery sweep 4.

name: Hip-Hop Dance
region: New York City and Los Angeles
tagline: The umbrella of street dance styles born of hip-hop culture.
profile: Hip-hop dance is the umbrella of street dance styles that evolved with hip-hop culture in the United States from the early 1970s onward. It encompasses the funk styles (popping, locking, boogaloo), uprock, krump, waacking, and later social and studio new-style forms, distinguished by freestyle improvisation, crews, cyphers and competitive battles. It emerged in African American and Latino communities in New York and California. Distinct from but related to breaking (b-boying), which is only the first and most floor-oriented of many styles (Wikipedia; Britannica).
insight: Hip-hop dance is NOT synonymous with breaking; breaking is the floor-power style while the California-born funk styles (popping, locking) and New York uprock are parallel and equally foundational pillars of hip-hop vernacular movement.
notes: Umbrella entry distinct from existing breaking-bboying and voguing entries. Discovery sweep 5.

name: Irish Stepdance
tagline: Rigid upper body, rapid precise footwork in solo and team Irish dance.
profile: Irish stepdance is a performance dance rooted in traditional Irish dance, characterized by a stiff upper body and fast, precise footwork, performed solo or in teams in hard or soft shoes. Taught by travelling dance masters from the 18th century, it was codified by An Coimisiun le Rinci Gaelacha and is danced competitively worldwide at feiseanna. It was popularized globally by Riverdance from 1994 (Wikipedia; CLRG).
insight: Before the late 1890s arms were kept relaxed; per Wikipedia, sometime in that decade or the one following, a dance master had his students compete with arms held firmly down to their sides, hands in fists, in order to call more attention to the intricacy of the steps - establishing the rigid-arm style still used today.
notes: Distinct from ceili (group social) and sean-nos (improvised old style) already in catalog. Discovery sweep 5.

name: Bulerias
tagline: Fast, festive 12-beat flamenco palo that ends any flamenco gathering.
profile: Bulerias is a fast flamenco palo built on a 12-beat compas, originating among the Cale Romani of Jerez de la Frontera in the 19th century as an upbeat ending to soleares and alegrias. It is among the most popular and dramatic flamenco forms, prized for its freedom of improvisation, and traditionally closes flamenco gatherings with dancers taking turns in a semicircle. Accompanied by guitar, palmas (hand-clapping) and jaleo (shouts), the tempo is typically 240+ beats per minute (Wikipedia; Flamenco Wiki).
insight: Bulerias is the flamenco style permitting the greatest improvisation; it is also the only flamenco dance form in which male dancers are permitted to leap, making it a showcase for athleticism as well as artistry.
notes: Distinct sub-entry of the existing flamenco umbrella entry. Flamenco as a whole was UNESCO-inscribed in 2010 (RL/00363); bulerias is not separately inscribed. Discovery sweep 5.

name: Contra Dance
era: English/French origins late 17th century; American form 18th century; revived 1960s-70s
tagline: Long lines of couples progressing through called figures to live music.
profile: Contra dance is an American folk dance of long paired lines of couples, with mixed origins in English country dance, Scottish country dance and 17th-century French contredanse. A caller teaches figures before the music, and couples progress up and down the set dancing with each other couple, almost always to live fiddle-led music. It is strongest in New England and has spread across North America; its defining figures include do-si-do, swing, allemande left and chain (Wikipedia; CDSS).
insight: Because contra has no required footwork - most figures are a walking step to the beat - many newcomers find it easier to learn than other social dances; each time through lasts about 64 beats with a new neighbor couple.
notes: Distinct from existing square-dance and english-country-dance entries. Discovery sweep 5.

name: Ceilidh
era: Traditional; formalized in the 19th-century Gaelic revival
tagline: Traditional Gaelic social gathering with folk music and group dancing.
profile: A ceilidh (Scottish Gaelic ceilidh; Irish ceili) is a traditional Gaelic social gathering originating in the rural communities of Scotland and Ireland. Originally a social visit of storytelling, song, music and poetry, it now centers on participatory group dances - such as the Dashing White Sergeant and Gay Gordons - led by a caller and accompanied by a live band of fiddle, accordion, and other instruments. The modern ceilidh functions as a social and community event at weddings, festivals and village halls (Wikipedia; Visit Scotland).
insight: The word derives from the Old Irish cele ('companion'); in many modern events the dancing has displaced the older storytelling and music-recitation meanings of the gathering entirely, though traditional communities maintain the full repertoire.
notes: Cross-border Scotland/Ireland tradition; filed under Scotland (primary). Distinct from the Irish ceili-dance (if in catalog) - ceilidh is the broader social gathering tradition. Discovery sweep 5.

name: Habanera
tagline: 19th-century Cuban dance that seeded tango, danzon and Latin music.
profile: The habanera (Cuban contradanza or danza) is a 19th-century Cuban music-and-dance genre derived from the European contredanse and infused with African cross-rhythms (the tresillo). It was the first Cuban dance to gain international popularity and the progenitor of the danzon, mambo and cha-cha-cha. The distinctive habanera rhythm (dotted eighth + sixteenth + two eighths) became the foundational groove of Latin American popular music. Danced by couples with a gentle swaying hip motion (Wikipedia; Encyclopedia of Latin American Music).
insight: The habanera rhythm traveled worldwide via sailors and sheet music, shaping the Argentine tango, the Brazilian maxixe, and even Bizet's Carmen (1875); it is the rhythmic ancestor underlying much of Latin dance music.
notes: English Wikipedia 'Habanera' is a disambiguation page; dance/music article is 'Contradanza'. Earlier than and distinct from existing danzon entry. Discovery sweep 5.

name: Soca
tagline: High-energy 'soul of calypso' and its Carnival wining dance.
profile: Soca is a music-and-dance genre originating in Trinidad and Tobago in the 1970s, created by Lord Shorty (Garfield Blackman, 1941-2000) as an offshoot of calypso fusing Afro-Trinidadian and Indo-Trinidadian rhythms. His 1973 'Indrani' is regarded as the first soca recording. Its high-energy, studio-produced sound drives Carnival, where the companion dance 'wining' uses fast hip and waist movements. Sub-styles include power soca, groovy soca and chutney soca (Wikipedia; Britannica).
insight: 'Soca' is a portmanteau of 'soul' and 'calypso'; Lord Shorty originally spelled it 'sokah' to reflect its East Indian influence, but journalist Ivor Ferreira's misspelling during the 1976 Trinidad Carnival season fixed the spelling as 'soca.'
notes: Primarily music with significant Carnival dance component (wining). Not UNESCO-inscribed. Discovery sweep 5.

name: Zouk
tagline: French Antillean party music and partner dance turned global craze.
profile: Zouk is a music-and-dance movement pioneered by the band Kassav' in the French Antilles (Guadeloupe and Martinique) in the early 1980s, originally a fast, percussion-driven Carnival style ('zouk beton') synthesizing kadans, konpa and biguine. Its slower romantic form, zouk-love, became a global partner dance, and Creole-language zouk became an emblem of Antillean cultural pride. The word zouk is Creole for a nightlong dance party (Wikipedia; Britannica).
insight: Kassav's 1984 album Yelelele marks the onset of international recognition of zouk, and its hit 'Zouk-la se sel medikaman nou ni' started the zouk dance craze in Latin America and the Caribbean, later spawning the distinct Brazilian zouk partner-dance lineage.
notes: Cross-border French Antilles tradition (Guadeloupe GP / Martinique MQ); filed under Guadeloupe as primary origin. Not UNESCO-inscribed. Discovery sweep 5.

name: Bon Odori
tagline: Communal summer dance circling a yagura to honor ancestors.
profile: A folk dance performed during the Obon season to welcome and send off ancestral spirits; dancers move in a circle around a raised wooden platform (yagura) to taiko drums, shamisen, flutes and folk songs (minyo), with slow, repetitive, graceful gestures that vary by region. In 2022 Furyu-odori was inscribed on UNESCO's Representative List (Decision 17.COM 7.B.18), encompassing 41 representative ritual folk dances across 24 prefectures including various bon odori (Wikipedia; Nippon.com).
insight: In 2022 Furyu-odori was inscribed on UNESCO's Representative List encompassing 41 representative ritual folk dances across 24 prefectures, incorporating the previously inscribed Chakkirako (No. 00274).

sources.title: Japanese Ritual Dances Added to UNESCO List

notes: Furyu-odori RL/01701 (2022); 41 dances including bon odori. UNESCO RL 2022.

name: Awa Odori
tagline: Japan's largest dance festival, the exuberant Fool's Dance.
profile: A festival dance held during Obon; groups (ren) in yukata and happi advance through streets in a two-beat zomeki rhythm on shamisen, taiko, shime-daiko, kane gong and flute. Women take small graceful steps in conical amigasa hats while men dance low and energetically. The festival attracts over 1.3 million tourists every year and is the largest dance festival in Japan (Wikipedia; JNTO).
insight: Its famous chant runs: 'The dancers are fools, the watchers are fools; if both are fools, you might as well dance!' - capturing the dance's egalitarian, joyful philosophy.
notes: Not separately UNESCO-listed (Furyu-odori covers broader category).

name: Nihon Buyo
tagline: Classical Japanese theatrical dance derived from kabuki.
profile: A refined classical dance blending mai (circling) and odori (rhythmic) movements, performed in kimono to shamisen and nagauta narrative singing; movements are slow, controlled and symbolic, often using a folding fan, with named schools (iemoto). The term was coined in the early 20th century to distinguish classical stage dance from folk and Western dance forms (Wikipedia; UNESCO Kabuki).
insight: The term 'Nihon buyo' was coined in the early 20th century to distinguish classical stage dance from folk and Western dance forms; it encompasses five schools descended from Edo kabuki.
notes: Parent art Kabuki is UNESCO-inscribed (RL/00163, 2008) but Nihon buyo itself is not. QID unconfirmed.

name: Kabuki
tagline: Stylized Japanese dance-drama with elaborate makeup.
profile: A classical theatre integrating stylized dance (buyo), song and dramatic acting, with kumadori makeup, lavish costumes and dynamic mie poses, set to shamisen-led nagauta; performers (historically all male, including onnagata female-role specialists) execute highly codified movement. Founded reputedly by the female entertainer Izumo no Okuni (UNESCO; Wikipedia).
insight: Kabuki was founded in the early 1600s reputedly by the female entertainer Izumo no Okuni, but women were soon banned, giving rise to the all-male tradition and the onnagata female-role specialist.
notes: UNESCO RL 2008 (RL/00163). Theatre encompassing dance. QID unconfirmed.

name: Kumi Odori
tagline: Ryukyuan musical dance-theatre of the Okinawan court.
profile: An Okinawan musical theatre combining dance, music and spoken word, using stylized movement, historical Ryukyuan language, distinctive costume and the sanshin lute, drawing on local folk performance and mainland Japanese influences. Created in 1719 by Tamagusuku Chokun specifically to receive Chinese imperial envoys, fusing Ryukyuan dance and music with elements borrowed from Japanese Noh and kabuki (UNESCO; Wikipedia).
insight: Kumi Odori was created in 1719 by Tamagusuku Chokun specifically to receive Chinese imperial envoys, fusing Ryukyuan dance and music with elements borrowed from Japanese Noh and kabuki.
notes: UNESCO RL 2010 (RL/00405). QID unconfirmed.

name: Lion Dance
era: Recorded from the Tang dynasty and earlier
tagline: Two performers animate a lion costume for luck and festivity.
profile: Performed by two dancers - one head, one tail - mimicking a lion's movements to drums, cymbals and gongs; Northern style emphasizes acrobatic jumping, Southern style (Hok San, Fut San) emphasizes martial-arts agility and expressive head movements. Performed at Lunar New Year, weddings and openings for good fortune. On China's national ICH list (Wikipedia; Chinese Historical & Cultural Project).
insight: The lion is not native to China; a lion-dance-like performance was described as early as the third century AD and recognized at the time as a foreign import linked to Buddhism.
notes: On China's national ICH list. Practiced across East/SE Asian diaspora.

name: Dragon Dance
era: Han dynasty roots; modern form more recent
tagline: A team carries a long dragon puppet in undulating motion.
profile: A team manipulates a long flexible dragon figure on poles, simulating sinuous undulating movement as the dragon chases a pearl of wisdom, accompanied by drums, gongs and cymbals; a highlight of Chinese New Year. Per Wikipedia, there are more than 700 different dragon dances in China, and the dance once functioned as a ritual to appeal for rain as the Chinese dragon was associated with rain (Wikipedia; Dance in China Wikipedia).
insight: Per Wikipedia's Dance in China article, there are more than 700 different dragon dances in China, and the dance once functioned as a ritual to appeal for rain at a time of drought as the Chinese dragon was associated with rain.
notes: On China's national ICH list.

name: Tibetan Opera (Ache Lhamo)
region: Tibet Autonomous Region and Qinghai-Tibetan Plateau
tagline: Masked Tibetan dance-drama of song, dance and ritual.
profile: A classical secular Tibetan theatre combining folk song, dance, chant, acrobatics and religious performance; it opens with a purification/prayer ceremony, centers on a narrative voiced by a single speaker with supporting singers and dancers, and closes with a blessing. Performers wear vividly colored masks (red kings, green queens, yellow religious figures) and rely on drum and cymbal. Tradition credits its founding to the 14th-century mystic Thangtong Gyalpo (UNESCO; Wikipedia).
insight: Tradition credits its founding to the 14th-century mystic and bridge-builder Thangtong Gyalpo, who is said to have used performances to raise funds to build iron bridges across Tibet.
notes: UNESCO RL 2009 (RL/00208).

name: Peacock Dance (Dai)
tagline: Graceful Dai dance imitating the sacred peacock.
profile: Imitates the peacock - a sacred symbol of beauty and good fortune - through fluid knee, arm, wrist and finger articulation and the three-bends body posture, accompanied by elephant-foot drums, gongs and cymbals. Despite frequent tourism claims of UNESCO status, this dance is on China's National ICH list (inscribed 20 May 2006) only, with no UNESCO inscription (Wikipedia; academic sources).
insight: Despite frequent tourism claims of 'world heritage,' the Dai peacock dance is on China's National ICH list (inscribed 20 May 2006) only; it is famously associated with dancer Yang Liping.
notes: English Wikipedia article is pan-Asian umbrella. National ICH 2006 only, no UNESCO.

name: Yangge
tagline: Lively northern Chinese group folk dance for festivals.
profile: A representative northern Chinese group folk dance in which colorfully costumed dancers - often with red silk ribbons, fans or waist drums - sway and swing in lines or circles to drums, gongs and trumpets, especially in the first fifteen days after the Lunar New Year. Developed from a Song-dynasty dance called Village Music (cuntian yue) and inscribed on China's National ICH list (2006) (Wikipedia).
insight: Yangge developed from a Song-dynasty dance called Village Music (cuntian yue) and is inscribed on China's National Intangible Cultural Heritage list (2006); during the Communist era it was also used as a political mass dance.
notes: National ICH 2006. No UNESCO inscription.

name: Cham Dance
tagline: Masked Tibetan Buddhist ritual dance of monks.
profile: A masked, costumed ritual dance performed by Buddhist monks during religious festivals; dancers in elaborate masks of deities, protectors and animals move in slow, deliberate, meditative patterns to cymbals, long horns (dungchen), drums and chanting, enacting the triumph of dharma over malevolent forces. Considered a form of moving meditation and merit-making rather than entertainment (Wikipedia).
insight: Cham is considered a form of moving meditation and merit-making rather than entertainment, performed to purify and protect the community; monks may spend years learning specific roles.
notes: Sacred Buddhist ritual; respectful framing. QID unconfirmed.

name: Ganggangsullae
tagline: Women's moonlit circle dance of harvest and fertility.
profile: A seasonal harvest and fertility ritual performed mainly at Chuseok under the full moon; women join hands in a circle and sing and dance led by a lead singer with the refrain 'ganggangsullae,' accelerating from slow (gin) to fast (jajin) tempos, miming folk games between songs. By tradition it offered rural young women - otherwise forbidden to sing aloud or go out at night - a rare sanctioned break during Chuseok (UNESCO; Wikipedia).
insight: Legend links it to Admiral Yi Sun-sin's wartime deception tactic of having women circle hilltops in military dress to create the appearance of a large army during the Japanese invasions.
notes: UNESCO RL 2009 (RL/00188).

name: Talchum
era: Folk roots; many forms codified in the Joseon era
tagline: Masked dance drama satirizing social hierarchy.
profile: A mask dance drama combining dance, music and theatre; an ensemble of six to ten musicians accompanies masked performers who sing, mime and exchange dialogue, using caricatures of monks, nobles and commoners to satirize hierarchy. No formal stage is needed; the audience cheers and jeers as part of the drama. Surged in transmission among university students in the 1970s-80s as a vehicle of social criticism (UNESCO; Wikipedia).
insight: Talchum surged in transmission among university students in the 1970s-80s as a vehicle of social criticism; 'talchum' was originally a regional term for Hwanghae Province dances, now used generally.
notes: UNESCO RL 2022 (RL/01742). 13-18 regional variants.

name: Buchaechum
tagline: Group fan dance evoking flowers, waves and butterflies.
profile: A Korean fan dance performed by groups of female dancers in brightly colored hanbok, using large fans painted with pink peonies to form shifting images of flowers, birds, butterflies and waves, usually to folk song (minyo) or instrumental sanjo, drawing on shamanic, court and folk vocabulary. Per Wikipedia, created in 1954 by dancer Kim Baek-bong and first performed at Seoul City Hall (Wikipedia; Korean dance Wikipedia).
insight: Per Wikipedia, Buchaechum was created in 1954 by dancer Kim Baek-bong, who drew influences from both Korean shamanic ritual dances and traditional Joseon court and folk dances; reconstructed as a group dance for the 1968 Mexico City Olympics.
notes: Neoclassical; not UNESCO-listed.

name: Salpuri-chum
era: Shamanic origins; refined as stage solo
tagline: Solo white-scarf dance releasing sorrow and han.
profile: A solo dance, traditionally by a woman in white holding a long white silk scarf, grown out of shamanistic rituals to cleanse misfortune; choreography begins almost imperceptibly and builds until the scarf swirls and snaps, expressing the release of grief and the Korean emotion han. Korea's Important Intangible Cultural Heritage No. 97 (Korean dance Wikipedia; Daebak).
insight: Because such emotional depth must be conveyed through minimal movement, salpuri is considered one of the most technically demanding dances in the Korean repertoire; it is Korea's Important Intangible Cultural Heritage No. 97.
notes: Korea's National ICH No. 97; QID and exact Wikipedia title unconfirmed.

name: Amis Harvest Festival Dance (Ilisin)
tagline: Communal circle dance of the Amis harvest festival.
profile: During the Amis harvest festival, tribespeople in ceremonial costume dance hand-in-hand in a circle led by a singer, with movements of walking, running and jumping; singing is the medium of communication with ancestral spirits, and the festival progresses through welcoming, feasting and sending off spirits over several days. The festival is traditionally a male-focused rite in which a woman may signal courtship by placing a betel nut in a man's adofot bag (Wikipedia; DMTIP).
insight: On the last night of Ilisin, women join the dancing and a woman may place a betel nut in a man's adofot ('Valentine's bag') to signal courtship interest - one of the few opportunities for such public expression.

sources.title: Amis people
sources.publisher: Digital Museum of Taiwan Indigenous Peoples

notes: Indigenous Austronesian dance; respectful framing. QID unconfirmed.

name: Mongol Biyelgee
tagline: Seated nomadic folk dance of expressive upper-body gestures.
profile: Performed by dancers of different western Mongolian ethnic groups while half-sitting or cross-legged in the confined space of a ger; hand, shoulder, chest and head movements express daily life (milking, herding, sewing, hunting) and spiritual practices, in ethnically distinctive embroidered costumes and silver jewelry. Regarded as the forebear of Mongolian national dance (UNESCO; Gogo.mn).
insight: Biyelgee is on UNESCO's Urgent Safeguarding List (2009) because its master transmitters are mostly elderly and declining in number; it is regarded as the forebear of Mongolian national dance.

sources.title: Mongol Biyelgee, Mongolian traditional folk dance

notes: UNESCO Urgent Safeguarding List 2009 (USL/00311), NOT Representative List.

name: Khon
tagline: Masked dance-drama enacting the Ramakien epic.
profile: A masked dance-drama combining codified graceful dance, a piphat ensemble, narrators, a chorus and glittering costumes, depicting the Ramakien (Thai Ramayana) - Rama's battle with the demon king Thosakan and his monkey army; ogre and monkey characters wear masks, and demanding training begins young. First described by French envoy Simon de la Loubere in 1687 (UNESCO; Wikipedia).
insight: The earliest Western description is from French envoy Simon de la Loubere in Du Royaume de Siam (1687): 'That which they call Cone [khon] is a figure dance... The dancers are masked and armed, and represent rather a combat than a dance.'

sources.title: Khon masked dance drama in Thailand

notes: UNESCO RL 2018 (RL/01385).

name: Xoe Dance of the Tai People
region: Northwestern Vietnam (Yen Bai, Lai Chau, Son La, Dien Bien)
tagline: Communal Tai circle dance of hospitality and harmony.
profile: A Tai (Thai) ethnic dance performed at rituals, weddings, festivals and community events; in the popular circle xoe, dancers join hands and raise/open arms, leaning back with chest lifted, to gourd lutes, mouth organs, drums, gongs, cymbals and reed flutes in a 2/4 or 4/4 beat. There are some 30 distinct xoe dances; a key identity marker for Vietnam's 1.82 million Tai citizens (UNESCO; Saigoneer).
insight: UNESCO inscribed the Art of Xoe dance of the Tai people of Vietnam in 2021 (RL/01575), recognizing 30 distinct xoe dances tied to over 1.82 million Tai citizens recorded in Vietnam's 2019 Population and Housing Census.

sources.title: Art of Xoe dance of the Tai people in Viet Nam

notes: UNESCO RL 2021 (RL/01575). Wikipedia article/QID needs verification.

name: Nha Nhac (Vietnamese Court Music)
tagline: Vietnamese royal court music with accompanying court dance.
profile: The broad range of musical and dance styles performed at the Vietnamese royal court from the 15th to mid-20th centuries, especially at Hue; court dances accompanied refined orchestral music at ceremonies and receptions, embodying the cosmological and hierarchical order of the imperial court. UNESCO recognized Nha Nhac in 2003 as the most comprehensive surviving reference point for classical Vietnamese court performance (UNESCO; Wikipedia).
insight: UNESCO recognized Nha Nhac in 2003 as the most comprehensive surviving reference point for classical Vietnamese court performance; during the American War and subsequent years, the tradition was nearly lost.
notes: Primarily court MUSIC with accompanying dance. UNESCO 2003/2008 (RL/00074).

name: Royal Ballet of Cambodia
era: Linked to the Khmer court for over 1000 years
tagline: Sacred Khmer court dance of intricate hand gestures.
profile: Known for intricate codified hand and finger gestures, slow elegant movements and elaborate golden costumes, performed at coronations, weddings, funerals and Khmer holidays, voiced by a narrator or women's chorus with a pinpeat ensemble; four character types exist - Neang (woman), Neayrong (man), Yeak (giant), Sva (monkey). The ballet nearly perished under the Khmer Rouge and was revived by Princess Norodom Buppha Devi (UNESCO; Wikipedia).
insight: The ballet nearly perished under the Khmer Rouge, who eliminated most master dancers; its revival was led by Princess Norodom Buppha Devi, the first professional Apsara dancer of the modern era.
notes: UNESCO 2003 Masterpiece, RL 2008 (RL/00060).

name: Lakhon Khol
tagline: All-male Khmer masked dance-drama of the Reamker.
profile: An all-male masked dance-drama enacting episodes of the Reamker (Khmer Ramayana), with vigorous codified movements, masks and a pinpeat ensemble plus narrative recitation; it has ritual functions tied to agricultural cycles and community protection. The Lakhon Khol of Wat Svay Andet was inscribed on UNESCO's Urgent Safeguarding List in 2018 (UNESCO).
insight: The Lakhon Khol of Wat Svay Andet was inscribed on UNESCO's Urgent Safeguarding List in 2018, the same session Thailand's related Khon was inscribed on the Representative List.
notes: UNESCO Urgent Safeguarding 2018 (USL/01374), NOT Representative List. QID unconfirmed.

name: Bedhaya
tagline: Sacred slow court dance of nine female dancers.
profile: A sacred Javanese court dance performed by nine women in the kraton (palace), epitomizing refined (alus) elegance with slow, controlled, meditative movements accompanied by gamelan and choral singing; among the most revered dances of the Javanese courts with deep cosmological meaning. The most sacred form, Bedhaya Ketawang, is attributed to Sultan Agung of Mataram (c. 1643) (Wikipedia).
insight: The most sacred form, Bedhaya Ketawang, is attributed to Sultan Agung of Mataram (c. 1643) and is traditionally performed only once a year for the Surakarta susuhunan's accession anniversary.
notes: Not separately UNESCO-listed. Foundational Javanese court dance.

name: Three Genres of Traditional Balinese Dance
tagline: Sacred, semi-sacred and entertainment dances of Bali.
profile: UNESCO recognizes three genres: sacred Wali (Rejang, Sanghyang Dedari, Baris Upacara) in temple inner sanctums; semi-sacred Bebali (Topeng Sidhakarya, Gambuh, Wayang Wong); and entertainment Balih-balihan (Legong Kraton, Joged Bumbung, Barong Ket). Dancers articulate face, eyes, hands, hips and feet in tight coordination with gamelan rhythms (UNESCO; Wikipedia).
insight: Balinese children begin formal dance training as young as seven, 'taught to dance with their hands before they can walk,' reflecting how deeply the art is woven into village life and communal identity.

sources.title: Three genres of traditional dance in Bali

notes: UNESCO RL 2015 (RL/00617). CORRECTED from brief's erroneous 01577.

name: Kecak
tagline: Male chorus chant-dance retelling the Ramayana.
profile: A dramatic dance in which a chorus of 50-100 bare-chested men chant 'cak-cak-cak' in interlocking rhythms (no gamelan), surrounding dancers who enact Ramayana scenes - Rama, Sita, Hanuman; often performed at sunset. Evolved in the 1930s from the sacred Sanghyang trance ritual into a mass theatrical performance, partly shaped by collaboration with German artist Walter Spies (Wikipedia; UNESCO Three Genres).
insight: Kecak evolved in the 1930s from the sacred Sanghyang trance ritual into a mass theatrical performance, partly shaped by collaboration with German artist Walter Spies for Western audiences.
notes: Not individually UNESCO-listed; related Sanghyang is in the Wali genre under RL/00617. QID unconfirmed.

name: Legong
tagline: Refined Balinese court dance of intricate finger and eye movement.
profile: A refined classical Balinese dance traditionally performed by young girls, characterized by precise finger gestures, rapid eye movements (seledet) and synchronized steps to gamelan, with gold headdresses and silk costumes; tells Balinese folklore and falls within the Balih-balihan entertainment genre of the UNESCO-inscribed Three Genres (Wikipedia; UNESCO RL/00617).
insight: Legong dancers traditionally began rigorous training around age five and were highly esteemed, often marrying royalty or wealthy merchants, then retiring from dance after marriage.

sources.title: Three genres of traditional dance in Bali

notes: Part of UNESCO 2015 Three Genres (RL/00617, Balih-balihan). QID unconfirmed.

name: Saman Dance
tagline: Seated Gayo dance of percussive body rhythm and chant.
profile: Performed by rows of kneeling dancers who clap hands, slap chests, thighs and the ground, click fingers and sway and twist heads and bodies in tight unison; a leader (Sheikh) leads sung verses in Gayo language conveying religious, romantic or humorous guidance. The original Saman uses no instruments at all - the rhythm comes entirely from synchronized body percussion and voices (UNESCO; Wikipedia).
insight: Created to spread Islam in the land of Gayo, the original Saman uses no instruments at all - the rhythm comes entirely from the dancers' synchronized body percussion and voices, where a single mistake breaks the line.
notes: UNESCO Urgent Safeguarding List 2011 (USL/00509), NOT Representative List.

name: Tinikling
tagline: Bamboo-pole folk dance imitating the tikling bird.
profile: Two clappers tap and slide two bamboo poles on the ground and against each other in a triple-metre pattern while dancers step and hop between them barefoot, avoiding being caught; accompanied by rondalla string music (bandurria, guitar, laud), imitating the tikling bird dodging traps. Despite popular legend tying it to Spanish-colonial punishment, similar bamboo-pole dances exist across South and Southeast Asia suggesting a pre-Hispanic origin (Wikipedia; Britannica).
insight: Despite popular legend, similar bamboo-pole dances exist across South and Southeast Asia (Cheraw in India, Magunatip in Sabah), suggesting a pre-Hispanic origin; and contrary to common belief, no Philippine law actually declares a national dance.
notes: Widely called 'national dance' but not legally designated. Not UNESCO-listed.

name: Carinosa
tagline: Flirtatious Hispanic-influenced Filipino courtship dance.
profile: A folk dance of Hispanic heritage performed by a couple in flirtatious, affectionate interplay, the woman often using a fan or handkerchief in a hide-and-seek courtship pattern, danced to lilting Hispanic-influenced music; the name derives from Spanish 'carinosa' (affectionate one). Philippine cultural authorities confirm neither Carinosa nor Tinikling has legal national-dance status (Wikipedia).
insight: Carinosa is frequently cited alongside Tinikling as a candidate national dance, but Philippine cultural authorities confirm neither has legal national-dance status; the informal designation reflects colonial-era cultural nationalism.
notes: Hispanic-influenced; not UNESCO-listed.

name: Singkil
tagline: Maranao princess dance weaving through clashing bamboo poles.
profile: A Maranao dance derived from the Darangen epic, in which a royal figure steps gracefully through criss-crossing clashing bamboo poles while manipulating fans or scarves, accompanied by a kulintang gong ensemble; attendants may hold parasols, showcasing poise amid rhythmic danger. Rooted in the Darangen, the Maranao epic that UNESCO inscribed in 2008 (Wikipedia).
insight: Singkil is rooted in the Darangen, the Maranao epic that UNESCO inscribed in 2008 - though it was the epic chant, not the dance itself, that received the inscription.
notes: Indigenous Maranao; respectful framing. Related Darangen epic is UNESCO 2008. QID unconfirmed.

name: Zat Pwe
tagline: All-night Burmese dance-drama of Jataka tales.
profile: The classical Burmese dance-drama, an all-night outdoor performance blending music, dance, song, drama and comedy led by a male star (mintha) and female counterpart (minthamee); Burmese dance is marked by angular, fast, energetic movements and emphasis on pose, accompanied by the hsaing waing percussion-gong-oboe ensemble with stories from Buddhist Jataka tales. Deeply shaped in 1767 when Siamese court dancers were brought to Burma (Wikipedia; Asia Society).
insight: Burmese dance was deeply shaped in 1767, when King Hsinbyushin's forces sacked Ayutthaya and brought Siamese court dancers back to Burma, infusing forms such as the Yama Zatdaw (Burmese Ramayana).
notes: No dedicated English Wikipedia article for Zat Pwe; uses Burmese dance article. QID unconfirmed.

name: Yein
tagline: Synchronized Burmese group dance for festivals.
profile: A group dance with multiple dancers moving head, waist, feet and hands synchronously to the hsaing waing orchestra; emerged as court traditions spread to Mandalay and Yangon, performed at temple fairs, Buddhist festivals and the Thingyan water festival marking the Myanmar New Year. Yein performances often alternate large unison sequences with individual spotlight passages (Wikipedia; Burmese dance sources).
insight: Yein performances alternate large unison sequences with individual spotlight passages, blending communal harmony with showcases of personal skill - the balance of conformity and individuality central to Burmese aesthetics.
notes: No standalone article; shares Burmese dance page. QID unconfirmed.

name: Mak Yong
tagline: Ancient Malay dance-theatre of myth and healing.
profile: An ancient Malay dance-drama combining acting, vocal and instrumental music, refined gestures and elaborate costumes; most roles are played by women and stories draw on Kelantan-Patani mythology featuring royals, divinities and clowns. The orchestra centers on the rebab (three-stringed fiddle), gendang drums and tetawak gongs; performances open with an offering and can run several nights. Inscribed by UNESCO in 2005 while officially banned in its home state of Kelantan by the state government (UNESCO; Wikipedia).
insight: Mak Yong was inscribed by UNESCO in 2005 even as it was officially banned in its home state of Kelantan by the state government over its pre-Islamic animist roots; the ban was lifted only in 2019.
notes: UNESCO 2005 Masterpiece, inscribed RL 2008 (RL/00167). Also found in S. Thailand and Riau Islands.

name: Zapin
tagline: Malay-Arab dance of paired, gliding footwork.
profile: A Malay performing-art dance of Hadhrami (Yemeni) Arab origin introduced to the Malay Archipelago around the 14th century with the spread of Islam; danced in pairs with smooth gliding steps and gentle hand movements to the gambus (lute), accordion, violin, marwas and rebana drums. Originally performed at weddings and religious celebrations. Shared across the Malay world - Malaysia, Singapore, Indonesia and Brunei (Wikipedia).
insight: Zapin is shared across the Malay world - Malaysia, Singapore, Indonesia and Brunei - reflecting maritime trade and Islamic cultural exchange; regional styles differ in tempo, costume and accompanying instruments.
notes: Cross-regional; also a Singapore tradition. Not UNESCO-listed.

name: Lam Vong
tagline: Graceful Lao circle dance of national identity.
profile: The national dance of Laos: couples form concentric circles - men inside, women outside - moving counterclockwise with graceful arm and hand gestures and simple footwork in cycles of eight movements repeated to the khaen (bamboo mouth organ), drums and fiddle; dancers maintain respectful distance without physical contact, opening with a bow (nob). Inscribed on UNESCO's Representative List on 5 December 2024 (UNESCO; Wikipedia).
insight: On 5 December 2024, UNESCO inscribed Lam Vong (Fonelamvonglao) on the Representative List (RL/02099), recognizing its eight steps that reflect the Lao way of life, including unity, a love of fun, and gratitude for the land.
notes: UNESCO RL 2024 (RL/02099). English Wikipedia covered under Romvong (shared regional dance); Lam Vong-specific QID unconfirmed.

name: Bharatanatyam
era: Roots in Natya Shastra (200 BCE-200 CE); modern revival 1930s
tagline: South India's fire-element temple dance of sculptural poses.
profile: A solo classical dance from Tamil Nadu characterized by bent knees (araimandi), geometric lines, intricate footwork, expressive mudras and abhinaya. Originally a devadasi temple dance, revived for the public stage by Rukmini Devi Arundale and E. Krishna Iyer. Accompanied by Carnatic music with mridangam, nattuvangam, flute, violin and veena; dancers wear silk and ankle bells (ghungroos). Sculptural evidence and the Natyashastra place its grammar over two millennia old (Wikipedia; Britannica).
insight: Sculptural evidence and the Natyashastra place its grammar over two millennia old, yet what is performed today was substantially reconstructed in the 20th century after near-eradication by colonial-era anti-nautch campaigns.
notes: One of 8 SNA classical dances. Not individually UNESCO-listed.

name: Kathak
era: Medieval bardic roots; codified in Mughal courts (16th-19th c.)
tagline: North India's storytelling dance of spins and footwork.
profile: A classical dance evolved from kathakar storytellers, blending Hindu devotional narrative with Mughal court refinement. Marked by rapid chakkars (spins), tatkar (rhythmic footwork), and subtle abhinaya. Performed to Hindustani music with tabla, sarangi, sitar; dancers wear heavy ankle bells. Both a Hindu temple and Muslim court tradition; flourished under Wajid Ali Shah of Awadh (Wikipedia; Testbook).
insight: Kathak is uniquely syncretic among Indian classical forms, performed historically in mosque courtyards and absorbing Persian aesthetics, distinguishing it from the geometric temple dances of the south.
notes: Shared cultural form with Pakistan (classical Kathak). One of 8 SNA classical dances.

name: Kathakali
tagline: Kerala's masked story-play of gods and demons.
profile: A highly stylized dance-drama from Kerala enacting Ramayana and Mahabharata episodes. Famous for elaborate chutti (rice-paste) makeup, towering headgear and color-coded characters (green = noble, red = demonic). Performed traditionally by men, including female roles, with sophisticated facial and eye expressions and codified mudras. Accompanied by chenda and maddalam drums and vocalists (Wikipedia; Britannica).
insight: Kathakali draws on the Hasta Lakshana Deepika's 24 basic mudras which combine into roughly 470 symbols; dancers spend three to four hours applying makeup before all-night performances.
notes: One of 8 SNA classical dances. Distinct from Koodiyattam (UNESCO 2008, RL/00172).

name: Kuchipudi
tagline: Andhra's dance-drama of song, speech and footwork.
profile: A classical dance-drama from Kuchipudi village, Krishna district, combining nritta, nritya and natya with Carnatic music in Telugu. Distinctive for the tarangam where the dancer balances on the rim of a brass plate. Traditionally performed by all-male Brahmin troupes; female dancers introduced in the 20th century. Uses mridangam, veena, flute and cymbals (Wikipedia; Britannica).
insight: In 1678 the last Shia Sultan of Golkonda, Abul Hasan Tana Shah, was so impressed by a performance of Bhama Kalapam that he granted the Kuchipudi village to its Brahmin dancers, on condition they continue the dance-drama.
notes: One of 8 SNA classical dances.

name: Odissi
tagline: Odisha's lyrical temple dance of tribhangi curves.
profile: An eastern Indian classical dance often called the oldest surviving form, based on temple sculpture and the mahari/gotipua traditions. Characterized by the tribhanga (three-bend) posture and chowk square stance, fluid torso movement and over fifty mudras. Performed to a blend of Hindustani and Carnatic music with pakhawaj, flute and sitar. Reconstructed by gurus including Kelucharan Mohapatra (Wikipedia; India Ministry of Culture).
insight: Odissi was revived in the 1950s by studying surviving Gotipua and Mahari traditions alongside temple carvings at Konark and Bhubaneswar, giving the modern dance its distinctively sculpturesque quality.

sources.title: Dance
sources.publisher: Ministry of Culture, Government of India

notes: One of 8 SNA classical dances.

name: Manipuri (Jagoi)
tagline: Manipur's gentle Krishna-devotion dance of veiled grace.
profile: A classical dance from Manipur centered on Raas Leela depicting Radha-Krishna, marked by soft, rounded, flowing movements (lasya) and the absence of ankle bells. Female dancers wear the stiff, mirror-embroidered kumil skirt and a transparent veil; male pung cholom drum dances are vigorous. Rooted in pre-Vaishnavite traditions and the Gaudiya Vaishnava bhakti movement (Wikipedia; SPM IAS Academy).
insight: Unlike other classical forms, Manipuri dancers wear no ankle bells, preserving the dance's serene floating quality; Rabindranath Tagore helped revive Raas Leela after seeing it in 1919 in Sylhet.
notes: QID Q18988611 maps to Manipuri Raas Leela article; verify it is the intended classical-dance item. One of 8 SNA classical dances.

name: Mohiniyattam
tagline: Kerala's swaying solo dance of the enchantress.
profile: A graceful solo dance of Kerala in the feminine lasya style, named for Mohini, Vishnu's enchantress avatar. Features gentle swaying (lahari) body movements, glides and subtle abhinaya in white-and-gold costume. Performed to Carnatic music in Manipravalam (Malayalam-Sanskrit) with idakka, mridangam, veena and flute. Patronized by Swathi Thirunal and revived by Vallathol Narayana Menon at Kerala Kalamandalam (Wikipedia; Johns Hopkins Library).
insight: The first documented mention of Mohiniyattam appears in the 16th-century legal text Vyavaharamala as a payment to a dancer; it was banned by anti-nautch laws between 1931 and 1938.
notes: One of 8 SNA classical dances.

name: Sattriya
tagline: Assam's monastic Vaishnava dance-drama of Krishna.
profile: A classical dance created by the saint-reformer Sankardev as part of Ankiya Naat one-act plays performed in Assam's sattras (Vaishnava monasteries). Themes center on Krishna and Vishnu avatars. Traditionally performed by male monks (bhokots), now also by women. Accompanied by khol drums, cymbals and devotional song; performed before a copy of the Bhagavata Purana rather than an idol (Wikipedia; India Ministry of Culture).
insight: Sattriya is the youngest of India's classical dances, recognized by the Sangeet Natak Akademi on 15 November 2000, yet its monastic lineage is unbroken since the 16th century.

sources.title: Dance
sources.publisher: Ministry of Culture, Government of India

notes: QID unverified; needs direct wikidata.org lookup. One of 8 SNA classical dances; recognized 15 Nov 2000.

name: Chhau
tagline: Eastern India's masked martial dance of epics.
profile: A semi-classical masked dance with three styles - Seraikella (Jharkhand), Purulia (West Bengal) and Mayurbhanj (Odisha) - enacting Mahabharata, Ramayana and folk themes. Blends martial mock-combat (khel), stylized animal gaits and village-chore movements. Performed by men at night in an open akhada to mohuri and shehnai pipes and a battery of drums (dhol, dhumsa). Tied to the spring Chaitra Parva festival (UNESCO; Wikipedia).
insight: Two of the three styles use elaborate masks while Mayurbhanj uses none; UNESCO inscribed Chhau in 2010 (file 00337) as one of India's signature ritual-martial dances.
notes: UNESCO RL 2010, file 00337 (5.COM). Brief had 00376 - CORRECTED to 00337.

name: Kalbelia
tagline: Rajasthan's serpentine dance of the snake-charmer community.
profile: Folk songs and dances of Rajasthan's Kalbelia (snake-charmer) community. Women in flowing black skirts swirl in serpent-like movements, while men play the poongi (snake-charming woodwind), khanjari, dholak, been and morchang. Performed at celebrations and especially Holi; lyrics are improvised and transmit mythology orally with no written manuals (UNESCO; Wikipedia).
insight: Once professional snake handlers, the Kalbelia evoke their former occupation through serpentine dance; UNESCO (file 00340) notes the songs exist purely as oral tradition with no texts or training manuals.

sources.title: Kalbelia folk songs and dances of Rajasthan

notes: UNESCO RL 2010, file 00340 (5.COM). Brief had 00380 - CORRECTED to 00340.

name: Garba
tagline: Gujarat's circular Navaratri dance honoring the goddess.
profile: A ritualistic devotional dance performed during the nine nights of Navaratri, circling counter-clockwise around a perforated earthen lamp-pot (garbo) or an image of goddess Amba. Dancers use simple clapping steps that build from slow circles to frenzied whirling, singing in unison. Performed in homes, temple courtyards, streets and open grounds; inclusive across socio-economic and gender lines (UNESCO; UN India).
insight: Inscribed in 2023 (file 01962) as India's 15th UNESCO element; per UNESCO, Garba fosters social equality by diluting socio-economic, gender and religious structures.
notes: UNESCO RL 2023, file 01962 (18.COM 8.b.32). India's 15th inscription.

name: Bhangra
era: Harvest folk dance; community origins; staged form from 1950s
tagline: Punjab's exuberant harvest dance of leaps and dhol.
profile: An energetic Punjabi folk dance originally performed by farmers to celebrate the Vaisakhi harvest. Features vigorous kicks, leaps, shoulder and arm movements performed in groups to the beat of the dhol drum, with shouted boliyan. Modern staged bhangra (from the 1950s) added the kaharva rhythm and luddi actions; now central to weddings, festivals and global Punjabi pop (Wikipedia).
insight: The staged bhangra of today emerged in the 1950s and continually added new movements and the kaharva-driven luddi actions through the 1970s, making the popular form considerably younger than the agrarian community dance it descends from.
notes: Shared cross-border with Pakistani Punjab. Not UNESCO-listed.

name: Bihu Dance
tagline: Assam's joyful spring harvest dance of youth.
profile: A folk dance tied to the Rongali (Bohag) Bihu festival marking the Assamese New Year and spring. Performed in groups by young men and women with brisk steps, rapid hand movements and hip sways. Women wear red-toned mekhela chador; men wear dhoti and wave the gamosa. Accompanied by the dhol, pepa (buffalo-horn pipe), taal and gogona. Celebrates fertility, romance and community (Wikipedia; ZIND Academy).
insight: Bihu dance made its first urban stage appearance in Guwahati in 1962 and has since become the central emblem of Assamese cultural identity.
notes: Not UNESCO-listed.

name: Sankirtana
tagline: Manipur's ecstatic ritual singing, drumming and dancing.
profile: A ritual performing art of Manipur's Meitei Vaishnava community combining devotional song, pung (drum) and cymbal playing, and dance narrating Krishna's life. Two drummers and around ten singer-dancers perform in temple mandapas and domestic courtyards encircled by devotees. Functions at festivals and life-cycle ceremonies as a cohesive community force (UNESCO; Wikipedia).
insight: Though it centers on ritual singing and drumming, UNESCO inscribed Sankirtana in 2013 (file 00843) for its inseparable dance dimension; it is considered the 'visible manifestation of God' by Manipuri Vaishnavas.

sources.title: Sankirtana, ritual singing, drumming and dancing of Manipur

notes: UNESCO RL 2013, file 00843 (8.COM). Ritual singing/drumming WITH integral dance.

name: Mudiyettu
tagline: Kerala's ritual dance-drama of Kali slaying Darika.
profile: A community ritual dance-drama performed in Kerala's Bhagavati temples (kavus) after the summer harvest, enacting the goddess Kali's battle with the demon Darika. Begins with kalam - a large floor drawing of Kali in colored powders - and purification fasting by performers. Features heavy makeup, towering headgear and drum-and-cymbal accompaniment; each caste plays a defined role (UNESCO; Wikipedia).
insight: UNESCO (file 00345) emphasizes Mudiyettu's role in transmitting community ethics and reinforcing collective identity; it is one of only two Kerala elements on the UNESCO list, alongside Koodiyattam.

sources.title: Mudiyettu, ritual theatre and dance drama of Kerala

notes: UNESCO RL 2010, file 00345. Separate inscription from Koodiyattam (RL/00172).

name: Ghoomar
era: Bhil tribal origin; adopted by Rajput courts
tagline: Rajasthan's twirling women's dance of swirling skirts.
profile: A traditional women's folk dance of Rajasthan performed in flowing ghaghra skirts with graceful pirouettes and circular hand movements; named from ghoomna ('to twirl'). Originated among the Bhil tribe in worship of Saraswati and was adopted by Rajput royal households for weddings, festivals and auspicious occasions. Performed in groups, often veiled, with rhythmic clapping and folk song (Wikipedia).
insight: Ghoomar began as a Bhil tribal dance honoring Goddess Saraswati before being embraced by Rajasthan's aristocratic Rajput courts, becoming a marker of royal celebration.
notes: QID not verified; needs eyes-on. Not UNESCO-listed.

name: Koodiyattam
tagline: Kerala's ancient Sanskrit temple theatre with codified dance.
profile: The oldest surviving classical Sanskrit theatre of India, performed in Kerala temple theatres (kuttampalams) by Chakyar men and Nangiar women. Blends Sanskrit drama with local Kerala traditions through a highly codified language of eye expressions (netra abhinaya) and hand gestures (hasta abhinaya), supported by mizhavu drums. A dance-theatre hybrid; precursor and relative of Kathakali (UNESCO; Wikipedia).
insight: Among the first elements proclaimed a UNESCO Masterpiece (2001) and incorporated into the Representative List in 2008 (file 00172); a single act can take days to perform due to the extreme elaboration of abhinaya.
notes: UNESCO RL, file 00172 (2001 Masterpiece, 2008 RL). QID needs eyes-on verification.

name: Kandyan Dance (Udarata Natum)
tagline: Sri Lanka's classical hill-country dance of vigorous leaps.
profile: The classical dance tradition of Sri Lanka's central hills, with five forms - ves, naiyandi, uddekki, pantheru and vannam. Rooted in the Kohomba Kankariya healing ritual to the deity Kohomba, it features dynamic footwork, leaps, whirls and acrobatics. The sacred Ves headdress is central. Accompanied by the geta bera drum and cymbals; performed at the Kandy Esala Perahera (Wikipedia; ATTD Helsinki).
insight: The sacred Ves costume of 64 ornaments was propitiatory and never secular, performed only by men inside temple precincts until the late 19th century; women were trained only from the 1950s.

sources.title: The Kandyan Dances
sources.publisher: Asian Traditional Theatre & Dance (Theatre Academy Helsinki)

notes: NOT UNESCO-listed. No Sri Lankan dance is yet on the ICH list.

name: Low Country Dance (Sanni Yakuma)
tagline: Sri Lanka's masked devil-dance of demon exorcism.
profile: A masked ritual dance tradition of Sri Lanka's southern coast, rooted in exorcistic rites such as the Rata Yakuma, Devol Maduwa and Sanni Yakuma - the latter summoning 18 disease demons (sanni), each with its own mask and drumbeat, to heal the afflicted. Distinguished from Kandyan dance by its use of masks. Accompanied by the yak bera drum in night-long ceremonies (Wikipedia; Sri Lanka Tours).
insight: Each of the 18 Sanni demons represents a specific disease and has a distinct mask, dance and drum rhythm; the night-long ritual summons, bargains with and finally banishes the illness-causing spirit.

sources.title: Sri Lankan Traditional Dances
sources.publisher: Sri Lanka Tours and Travel Blog

notes: No dedicated English Wikipedia article; covered under Dance in Sri Lanka. QID blank. Not UNESCO-listed.

name: Attan
tagline: The Pashtun circular war and celebration dance.
profile: A Pashtun circular group dance with pre-Islamic roots, performed at weddings, festivals and historically before battle. Dancers form a circle around musicians, beginning slowly and accelerating with synchronized hand and leg movements and full-body spins, often with handkerchiefs or swords. Accompanied by the dhol, surnai (shehnai) and toola flute. Shared across the Afghanistan-Pakistan border; considered a national dance (Wikipedia; Dawn).
insight: A Pashtun journalist traced Attan to an ancient Greek dance dedicated to Athena, said to survive in Afghan and Pashtun regions after disappearing in Greece during the Christian era.

sources.title: The evolution and persistence of the Attan dance

notes: Shared Afghanistan/Pakistan. Not UNESCO-listed.

name: Khattak Dance
era: Martial tradition; associated with Khushal Khan Khattak (17th c.)
tagline: Pakistan's swift sword dance of the Khattak warriors.
profile: A fast martial variant of attan performed by the Khattak Pashtun tribe, traditionally as war-preparation, carrying one to three swords and a handkerchief. Up to forty men dance in circles around lanterns, performing spins, acrobatic feats and swordplay to piper, clarion and stick-beaten drums. Distinguished from other attan by its rapid uptempo; a recognized national dance of Pakistan (Wikipedia; Youlin Magazine).
insight: Khattak is reputedly the only dance performed with active swordplay; dancers may wield up to three swords at once, each weighing about 1.5 kilograms.
notes: QID Q6400963 labeled Khattak (tribe/dance) - verify before merge. Not UNESCO-listed.

name: Jhumar
tagline: Punjab's slow, swaying men's dance of grace.
profile: A slow, graceful Punjabi folk dance - its name from jhum ('swaying') - performed mainly by men in groups at weddings and harvest gatherings, especially in the Seraiki region and parts of Balochistan. Features gentle swaying, synchronized hand gestures and rhythmic footwork to the dhol, more measured than Bhangra. Men wear white turbans and loose tunics (Youlin Magazine; Wikipedia).
insight: Jhumar is often described as the soulful, slower counterpart to the energetic Bhangra, sharing the dhol but emphasizing elegant swaying over leaps.
notes: QID unverified. Shared with Indian Punjab. Not UNESCO-listed.

name: Baul
tagline: Bengal's mystic minstrel song-and-ecstatic-dance tradition.
profile: The tradition of the Bauls - mystic wandering minstrels of rural Bangladesh and West Bengal - combining devotional song with spontaneous ecstatic dance. Singers play the ektara, dotara and dubki drum; some wear ankle bells and twirl, shaking their hair while dancing in trance. An unorthodox tradition blending Hindu, Buddhist, Vaishnava and Sufi elements, centered on the body as the abode of God (UNESCO; Wikipedia).
insight: Baul is primarily a MUSIC tradition (its UNESCO listing is for songs), though ecstatic dance is integral; it deeply influenced Nobel laureate Rabindranath Tagore's compositions.
notes: UNESCO RL, file 00107 (proclaimed 2005 Masterpiece, incorporated 2008). PRIMARILY MUSIC with integral dance.

name: Gambhira
tagline: Bengal's satirical grandfather-grandson dance-dialogue.
profile: A folk dance-play of northern Bengal performed by a pair - one as nana (grandfather), one as nati (grandson) - who critique social, political and moral issues through dialogue, song and dance. The nati wears ankle bells; a chorus repeats refrains. Accompanied by harmonium, flute, drum and judi. In Malda performers wear masks; it resembles a folk play mixing prose, verse and dance (Banglapedia; Wikipedia).
insight: Gambhira functions as living social satire, with the grandfather-grandson pair lampooning contemporary political and economic grievances through their danced dialogue.
notes: QID unverified. Shared with West Bengal, India. Not UNESCO-listed.

name: Charya Nritya
era: 8th-10th c. Mahasiddha roots; 1000+ year Newar lineage
tagline: Nepal's sacred Vajrayana Buddhist meditation dance.
profile: A sacred Buddhist ritual dance of the Newar Vajracharya priests of the Kathmandu Valley, performed as esoteric meditation embodying deities such as the Five Buddhas, Manjushri, Vajrayogini and Tara. Dancers wear elaborate deity costumes and ornaments; every gesture is symbolic. Performed to charya giti devotional songs with ta (cymbals) and damaru (drum) in temple courtyards during festivals such as Samyak (Lion's Roar; Wikipedia).
insight: Introduced by Buddhist Mahasiddhas around the 8th-10th centuries, Charya was preserved as a hidden tantric tradition by Newar Vajracharya priests after its Indian records were lost.
notes: QID unverified. Sacred ritual dance - respectful framing. Not UNESCO-listed.

name: Lakhe Dance
tagline: Nepal's fierce masked demon-protector street dance.
profile: A vigorous Newar masked dance in which a performer dons a large fearsome red-faced, long-haired mask to embody the Lakhe - a demon believed to protect children. Performed in red costume with strong, wild steps through the streets, most famously the Majipa Lakhe during the Indra Jatra (Yanya Punhi) festival in September. Accompanied by loud drums; only trained male dancers perform, learning from community elders (Wikipedia; Travories).
insight: Though the Lakhe appears as a monstrous demon, the figure is believed to protect children; almost every Newar settlement maintains its own Lakhe dance troupe.
notes: QID unverified. Not UNESCO-listed.

name: Cham (Bhutan)
era: 8th c. (Padmasambhava); canonized in Bhutan by Dorje Lingpa (14th c.)
tagline: Bhutan's sacred masked monastic dance of Tantric Buddhism.
profile: A ritual masked dance of Vajrayana Buddhism performed by monks and laymen at tshechu festivals in dzongs and monasteries. Dancers in heavy masks and swirling silk costumes embody deities, protectors and animals, performing dances that purify the ground, instruct morally and proclaim religious victory. Accompanied by long horns, cymbals, drums and conch shells; honors Guru Rinpoche (Padmasambhava). (Wikipedia; HolyMtn).
insight: A form of 'danced yoga,' many Bhutanese cham are understood as 'treasures' (terma) revealed to Tantric masters in mystical visions; the Black Hat dance commemorates the 841 CE assassination of the anti-Buddhist king Langdarma.

sources.title: What is Cham, the sacred dance of Bhutan?

notes: Shared with Tibet, Mongolia, Ladakh. Generic Cham is NOT UNESCO-listed; Drametse Ngacham is (separate entry). Sacred dance - respectful framing.

name: Drametse Ngacham
tagline: Bhutan's sacred drum mask dance from Drametse.
profile: A sacred masked dance - 'mask dance of the drums from Drametse' - performed twice yearly at the Drametse festival honoring Padmasambhava, organized by the Ogyen Tegchok Namdroel Choeling Monastery. Sixteen masked male dancers in colorful costumes representing peaceful and wrathful deities perform with ten musicians playing cymbals, trumpets and drums (bang nga, lag nga, nga chen). Believed first performed by celestial heroes and heroines (UNESCO; Wikipedia).
insight: Per UNESCO, 'the dance features sixteen masked male dancers wearing colourful costumes and ten other men making up the orchestra'; proclaimed a Masterpiece in 2005 and inscribed in 2008 (file 00161) as Bhutan's sole element on the list.
aliases: Mask dance of the drums from Drametse

sources.title: Mask dance of the drums from Drametse

notes: UNESCO RL, file 00161 (2005 Masterpiece, 2008 incorporated).

name: Kara Zhorga
tagline: Kazakhstan's lively 'black pacer' horseman's dance.
profile: A Kazakh folk dance ('Black Pacer/Ambler') depicting a horseman prancing on an ambling steed, linked to Tengriism and nomadic animal symbolism. Features energetic shoulder shakes, leg stomps, whip-waving leaps and back-bends imitating horse-racing (bayga). Performed solo, in pairs or as male zhorga at weddings and celebrations to a brisk melody (Wikipedia; Central Asia Program).
insight: Nearly lost during the Soviet-era Kazakh famine and collectivization, Kara Zhorga survived among Kazakhs who fled to China and was revived to massive popularity in Kazakhstan from around 2010.

sources.title: Kazakh Dance Culture and the Origins of Kara-Zhorga

notes: DUPLICATE FLAG: shares Wikipedia article + QID Q19656079 with Kyrgyz Kara Jorgo. Not UNESCO-listed.

name: Lazgi (Khorezm Dance)
tagline: Khorezm's 'shaking' dance of the soul entering the body.
profile: A Khorezmian folk music-and-dance form whose name means 'shaking/shuddering,' built on tremoring shoulders, arms and fingers with half-bent knees. Choreography often narrates the soul entering the body, beginning with awakening hands and building to fast, complex full-body movement accompanied by kayrak (stone castanets), surnay and drums. Has heroic male and lyrical female styles; both scenic and improvised forms (UNESCO; Wikipedia).
insight: UNESCO experts link Lazgi to dance imagery on the walls of Toprak-Kala (1st-6th c. CE), suggesting a history spanning more than 1,500 years; it was inscribed in 2019 (file 01364) at the 14th Committee session in Bogota.
notes: UNESCO RL 2019, file 01364 (14.COM). QID flagged for double-check.

name: Tanovar
tagline: Uzbekistan's lyrical Fergana dance of feminine emotion.
profile: A lyrical solo dance of the Fergana Valley school of Uzbek classical dance, expressing a woman's inner feelings and longing through soft, graceful hand and wrist articulation, fluid spins and expressive face work. Among the most refined and emotive forms of Uzbek choreography, performed to classical song; closely associated with the Fergana style alongside Katta O'yin and the Andijan polka (Advantour; Central Asia Rally).
insight: Tanovar belongs to the Fergana school - one of three classical Uzbek dance schools (Khorezm, Fergana, Bukhara) that crystallized from the separate Khiva, Kokand and Bukhara khanates between the 16th and 19th centuries.
notes: No verified English Wikipedia article/QID found; needs eyes-on. Not UNESCO-listed.

name: Lapar
tagline: Uzbekistan's playful sung-dialogue wedding dance.
profile: A traditional Uzbek song-and-dance form performed at weddings and festive gatherings, often as a witty sung dialogue or duet between performers accompanied by dance, doira (frame drum) and clapping. Combines lyrical and humorous improvised verse with light, expressive movement; functions as social entertainment and courtship play at celebrations (Advantour; Central Asia Rally).
insight: Lapar blurs the line between song and dance - its essence is a playful, improvised lyrical exchange set to movement, making it as much a verbal contest as a choreographic one.
notes: No verified English Wikipedia/QID. Not UNESCO-listed.

name: Shashmaqom
tagline: Central Asia's classical six-maqam vocal-instrumental art.
profile: A classical music tradition of Mawara al-nahr (modern Uzbekistan and Tajikistan), meaning 'six maqoms,' fusing vocal and instrumental music, melody, rhythm and Sufi poetry. Performed solo or by ensemble with tanbur, dutar, gijak, frame-drums and flutes. Primarily a MUSIC genre, though historically accompanied by courtly dance in palace receptions (UNESCO; Wikipedia).
insight: Primarily MUSIC, not dance - included for cross-reference; proclaimed a UNESCO Masterpiece in 2003 and incorporated into the Representative List in 2008 (file 00061) as a joint Uzbekistan-Tajikistan element.
notes: PRIMARILY MUSIC, file 00089 (2003 Masterpiece; 2008 RL). Joint Uzbekistan/Tajikistan inscription.

name: Kara Jorgo
tagline: Kyrgyzstan's 'black stallion' national folk dance.
profile: The most famous Kyrgyz folk dance, 'Black Stallion,' reflecting nomadic life with sharp shoulder and arm movements evoking whipping a stallion and a hunter scanning for prey. Traditionally performed by men, now by all; danced at weddings, festivals and even shopping malls. The Kyrgyz variant has a 'mountain character' (sharper) versus the Kazakh steppe style (Wikipedia; RFE/RL).
