insight: Kyrgyz ethnographers date the dance to the 11th-12th centuries; the earliest known footage is from 1924, and Kyrgyzstan has submitted Kara Jorgo to the UNESCO Representative List, though it is not yet inscribed.

sources.title: 'Black Stallion' Folk Dance Revival Sweeps Kyrgyzstan

notes: DUPLICATE FLAG: shares Wikipedia article + QID with Kazakh Kara Zhorga. Submitted to UNESCO but NOT inscribed.

name: Falak
tagline: Tajikistan's soulful mountain music of the heavens.
profile: A traditional folkloric music genre of Tajikistan's mountainous regions whose name means 'sky/universe,' sung with a wide vocal range, often a cappella with one hand pressed to the ear, increasingly accompanied by dumbra, tablak and ghizhak. Themes of injustice, gratitude, love, separation and homeland. Primarily MUSIC, performed at weddings, circumcisions and Nowruz; sometimes accompanied by dance (UNESCO; Wikipedia).
insight: Primarily a MUSIC genre included for cross-reference; inscribed on the UNESCO Representative List in 2021 (file confirmed per Tajikistan's ICH page at the 16th Committee session).
notes: UNESCO RL 2021. PRIMARILY MUSIC, not dance - flagged.

name: Pamiri Dance (Rapo)
tagline: Tajikistan's graceful Pamir highland dance of flowing hands.
profile: A traditional dance style of the Pamir highlands, one of several regional Tajik dance forms alongside Bukhara, Hissar, Khatlon and Northern styles. Marked by expressive, flowing arm and hand movements and subtle wrist rotation, often performed to the rubob and daf. Includes ancient pantomime dances imitating animals and birds, and the death-bed ritual dance poiamal preserved uniquely in the Pamirs (Facts and Details; Central Asia Rally).
insight: Tajik traditional dance preserves an exceptionally archaic layer - the poiamal 'dance beside the death bed,' a ritual form surviving only in the Pamirs.
notes: No dedicated English Wikipedia/QID. Not UNESCO-listed.

name: Kushtdepdi
tagline: Turkmenistan's rite of sung well-wishes and dance.
profile: A Turkmen performing art combining improvised well-wishing poetry, song with vocal improvisation, and dance with characteristic hand gestures, footsteps and movements timed to the melody. Once a ritual invoking God, now performed at weddings, births and national celebrations. Begins with seated respected women singing moral wishes to the young; serves as an intergenerational bridge of shared spiritual values (UNESCO; Wikipedia).
insight: Inscribed in 2017 (file 01259, 12.COM 11.b.34) as Kushtdepdi aydym we tans dessury - the rite functions explicitly as a tool for conveying good wishes across generations.

sources.title: Kushtdepdi rite of singing and dancing

notes: UNESCO RL 2017, file 01259 (12.COM). CORRECTED from task brief's 01239. Combines singing AND dance.

name: Adumu
tagline: Maasai warriors' competitive vertical jumping dance.
profile: Adumu is the Maasai jumping dance, performed by young warriors (morans) who form a circle and take turns leaping vertically with a stiff, straight body, heels never touching the ground. The pitch of the accompanying chant rises with the height of each jump. Music is provided by call-and-response throat chanting rather than drums; performed at the Eunoto coming-of-age ceremony, weddings and rites of passage (Wikipedia; Google Arts & Culture).
insight: Despite its global fame as a tourist spectacle, Adumu is embedded in the multi-day Eunoto rite marking the transition of junior warriors to senior warriors; its use in tourism is a recent and contested adaptation.
notes: Shared Kenya/Tanzania Maasai tradition. Not UNESCO-listed.

name: Eskista
tagline: Ethiopian shoulder, neck and chest shaking dance.
profile: Eskista ('dancing shoulders' in Amharic) is characterized by rapid, isolated rolling and bouncing of the shoulders, with jolting chest and neck movements, performed standing with bent knees. Accompanied by traditional instruments - masenqo (one-string fiddle), krar (lyre) and kebero (drum). Performed at weddings, festivals such as Timkat, and social gatherings; the best dancers are rewarded with shilimat (money stuck to the forehead) (Wikipedia; Music In Africa).
insight: Folklore holds that the dance imitates a snake's neck movements; performer Melaku Belay, founder of Addis Ababa's Fendika Cultural Center, is nicknamed the 'King of Eskista' and 'walking earthquake.'
notes: QID verified. Not UNESCO-listed.

name: Mbende Jerusarema
region: Murewa and Uzumba-Maramba-Pfungwe districts, Mashonaland East
tagline: Zezuru Shona fertility and courtship dance.
profile: An acrobatic, sensual couples dance of the Zezuru Shona, driven by a single master drummer (mitumba), woodblock clappers, hand-clapping, yodelling and whistles - without songs or lyrics. Men crouch, jerk both arms and kick the ground with the right leg in imitation of a burrowing mole (mbende). Performed at weddings, funerals, recreational competitions and political rallies (UNESCO; Wikipedia).
insight: Christian missionaries forced the name change from 'Mbende' (mole, a fertility symbol) to 'Jerusarema'; the dance was banned in 1910 under the Witchcraft Suppression Act yet survived as a symbol of anti-colonial pride and became the ZBC news signature tune at independence.
notes: UNESCO RL/00169 (task brief had incorrect 00241). Proclaimed Masterpiece 2005, inscribed 2008. One of few pure dance African inscriptions.

name: Chopi Timbila
tagline: Chopi xylophone orchestra with accompanying dance.
profile: Timbila are tuned wooden xylophones (made from mwenje/sneezewort wood with calabash resonators sealed in beeswax) played in orchestras of five to thirty instruments. Closely connected dances are performed by two to twelve dancers in front of the orchestra; each ngodo performance includes the solemn m'zeno song with humorous, topical lyrics chronicling community events. Performed at weddings and community events (UNESCO; Chopi people Wikipedia).
insight: The scarcity of the slow-growing mwenje tree from deforestation, plus the aging of master players, threatens the tradition's survival; UNESCO (file RL/00133) notes the dances and music are inseparable.
notes: UNESCO RL/00133 (task brief had incorrect 00218). Music-primary with dance component. No dedicated English Wikipedia dance article.

name: Gnawa
tagline: Afro-Moroccan Sufi trance music and ritual dance.
profile: A body of religious songs, rhythms and trance dance performed at all-night lila/derdeba ceremonies of healing and possession. The maâlem (master) plays the three-string guembri (sintir) bass lute, accompanied by iron qraqeb castanets and the tbel drum. Participants enter trance (jedba) through seven musical suites invoking spirits (mluk), each tied to a specific color and incense. Sacred trance ritual, not entertainment (UNESCO; Wikipedia).
insight: A sacred trance ritual in which participants negotiate relationships with the mluk spirits, appeasing or strengthening bonds; practitioners are unsettled by the commodification of their rituals as 'trance dub' at tourist festivals.
notes: UNESCO RL/01170, inscribed 2019 (task brief had incorrect 01449). Music-primary with trance-dance. Sacred ritual - respectful framing required.

name: Taskiwin
era: Ancient Amazigh martial tradition; UNESCO inscribed 2017
tagline: Amazigh martial dance with decorated powder horn.
profile: A martial dance of Amazigh (Berber) tribes of the western High Atlas, named after the tiskt - a richly decorated powder horn worn on the shoulder. Involves the art of shaking the shoulders to the rhythm of tambourines and flutes, performed by groups of men in a theatrical sequence enacting episodes of warfare (anticipation, assault, withdrawal, victory). Performed at weddings, hospitality feasts (Tinnobga) and community gatherings (Anmouggar) (UNESCO; Wikipedia).
insight: On UNESCO's Urgent Safeguarding List because the dance survives in only a few villages; its decline is linked to the 1960s forced labor migration of young Amazigh men to mines in northern France.

sources.title: Taskiwin, martial dance of the western High Atlas

notes: UNESCO USL/01256, Urgent Safeguarding List 2017 (task brief had incorrect 01258).

name: Intore
era: Pre-colonial royal warrior tradition; UNESCO inscribed 2024
tagline: Rwandan warrior 'dance of heroes'.
profile: A troupe dance recreating warriors' battle formations; dancers in lines mimic combat with an invisible adversary, leaping and wielding spears and shields to the rhythm of ingoma drums and horns, supported by songs and poems of triumph. Dancers wear sisal-fiber manes, ankle bells (amayugi) and beaded ornaments (ibitako). Trained in itorero institutions. Performed at weddings, harvest festivals and state ceremonies. Sacred, elite tradition - respectful framing required (UNESCO; Wikipedia).
insight: Inscribed on UNESCO's Representative List on 3 December 2024 (RL/02129, 19.COM, Asuncion) - Rwanda's first-ever cultural-performance inscription, part of a session that also marked first inscriptions for Ghana, Somalia, Brunei and Myanmar.
notes: UNESCO RL/02129, inscribed December 2024. Also practiced historically in Burundi. Sacred/elite tradition.

name: Vimbuza healing dance
tagline: Tumbuka trance healing dance ritual.
profile: A healing dance of the ng'oma ('drums of affliction') tradition. Most patients are women who suffer from various forms of mental illness, treated for weeks or months by healers who run a temphiri (village house). Women and children form a circle, singing songs to call helping spirits while the patient enters a trance; the only men taking part beat spirit-specific drum rhythms. Combines artistic and therapeutic functions. Sacred healing ritual - respectful framing required (UNESCO).
insight: A sacred healing ritual that developed in the mid-19th century as a means of overcoming traumatic experiences of oppression; forbidden by Christian missionaries yet remains a fundamental part of indigenous healthcare.
notes: UNESCO RL/00158, proclaimed 2005, inscribed 2008. No dedicated English Wikipedia article. Sacred healing ritual.

name: Gumboot dance
tagline: Miners' Wellington-boot percussion and protest dance.
profile: A percussive dance performed in Wellington gumboots, often fitted with bells, that originated among black migrant gold miners forbidden to speak underground. Dancers slap their boots, stamp and clap to create polyrhythmic patterns; many routines parody mine bosses and encode criticism of working conditions. Now performed by professional troupes and at tourist venues like Cape Town's V&A Waterfront (Wikipedia; South African Tourism).
insight: Born as a coded communication system in noisy, silence-enforced mine tunnels during the apartheid era; the chest-smacks and boot-taps often carried coded criticism unnoticed by applauding mine managers.

sources.title: The fascinating story behind the birth of Gumboot dancing

notes: QID verified. Not UNESCO-listed.

name: Indlamu
region: KwaZulu-Natal (Zulu); also Northern Ndebele, western Zimbabwe
tagline: Zulu warrior war dance with high kicks.
profile: A traditional Zulu war dance derived from the war dances of the amabutho (warrior regiments). The signature movement is lifting one foot high over the head and bringing it down sharply onto the downbeat with a heavy stomp. Typically two dancers in warrior pelts mirror each other. Performed in full regalia - amabheshu (loin skins), head-rings, ceremonial belts, ankle rattles, shields, knobkerries and spears - accompanied by izigubhu drums (Wikipedia; Face2Face Africa).
insight: Indlamu evolved from pre-battle motivational war dances of the Anglo-Zulu war era and now appears at weddings, coming-of-age rites and royal inaugurations; it was theatricalized by the Ama-Zebra ensemble for the 2010 FIFA World Cup ceremonies.

sources.title: A look at Indlamu, the famous Zulu war dance

notes: QID verified. Not UNESCO-listed.

name: Kpanlogo
tagline: Ga urban youth recreational drum-dance.
profile: A recreational drumming and dance form created by Ga youth in Accra around independence, blending older Ga rhythms (gome, oge, kolomashie) with highlife and American rock-and-roll. Danced in a low posture with bent knees and bowed back, with sweeping arm gestures, waist twisting and quick footwork. The ensemble uses three kpanlogo drums (male/female/master voices), the nono bell and the fao gourd rattle (Wikipedia).
insight: Initially condemned as risqué - the Arts Council banned it in 1962 and police seized instruments - yet it was performed for President Kwame Nkrumah in 1965; its bell pattern is identical to the Cuban son clave.
notes: QID verified. Not UNESCO-listed.

name: Sabar
tagline: Wolof drum, dance and gathering tradition.
profile: Sabar denotes the drum, the music, the dance and the gathering. Played by griot (gewel) percussionists on goatskin-headed drums (nder, mbeung-mbeung) with one hand and a stick (galan). The dance is energetic and improvisational, performed in a circle at weddings, naming ceremonies (ngente) and parties; dancers enter the circle one at a time with explosive, high-energy steps. Forms the rhythmic basis of mbalax pop (Wikipedia; Temple University Press).
insight: Sabar drumming historically functioned as a surrogate speech system in non-tonal Wolof; the mbalax accompaniment rhythm gave its name to the Senegalese pop genre popularized by Youssou N'Dour.
notes: QID verified. Not UNESCO-listed. Wikipedia article is drum-focused; dance is integral.

name: Raqs Sharqi
era: Codified first half of the 20th century
tagline: Classical Egyptian oriental dance.
profile: The classical Egyptian solo improvisational dance ('oriental dance') developed in early 20th-century Cairo, blending folkloric raqs baladi with ballet and ballroom influences. Movements emphasize internalized, dramatic torso and hip articulations, performed to orchestras with tabla, nay, qanun, oud and violin. Codified by stars including Badia Masabni, Samia Gamal, Taheyya Kariokka and Naima Akef during the golden age of Egyptian cinema (Wikipedia).
insight: Since the 1950s Egyptian law has banned dancers from performing with an uncovered midriff or excessive skin, and bans floorwork and certain pelvic moves; the term 'belly dance' derives from the French danse du ventre coined by 19th-century Orientalists.
notes: QID verified. Not UNESCO-inscribed.

name: Agbadza
region: Volta Region (Anlo Ewe); also Togo and Benin
era: Evolved from war dance Atrikpui; recreational form ~1920s
tagline: Ewe recreational dance evolved from a war dance.
profile: An Ewe music-and-dance evolved from the old war dance Atrikpui into a recreational form, sometimes called the 'chicken dance' for its bird-like arm movements. It has five sections: banyinyi (prayer), vutsortsor (main dance), adzo, hatsatsa (song cycle) and a final vutsortsor. The ensemble includes the gankogui bell, axatse rattle, sogo, kidi and kaganu drums. Performed at the Hogbetsotso festival, funerals and weddings (Wikipedia; GhanaWeb).
insight: Open to everyone regardless of class, age or gender - unlike many restricted Ewe dances; its bell timeline is among the oldest and most widespread key patterns in sub-Saharan Africa.

sources.title: Know the origin, rich history and cultural significance of Agbadza

notes: Cross-border Ewe tradition (GH/TG/BJ). QID verified. Not UNESCO-listed.

name: Moribayassa
tagline: Malinke women's triumph dance over adversity.
profile: A rhythm and dance of the Malinke performed exclusively by a woman who has overcome a major hardship (infertility, illness, grief) after vowing to dance it if her wish is granted. Dressed in ragged, mismatched clothing with disheveled hair, she dances around the village accompanied by other women and djembe/dunun drummers, ending at a sacred mango tree where she buries the rags. Traditionally danced only once in a lifetime. Respectful framing - sacred ritual (Wikipedia; Face2Face Africa).
insight: The 'crazy' antics signify delirious joy and ritual release - descriptions of madness hold true only to observers without ethnographic context.

sources.title: Why Malinke women of Guinea perform the Moribayassa praise dance

notes: Also practiced in Mali and Mauritania. QID verified. Sacred ritual.

name: Taarab
era: Popularized 1870s under Sultan Barghash; first star 1928
tagline: Swahili coast sung-poetry music with dance.
profile: A Swahili music genre fusing African, Arab, Indian and Western elements, sung in Kiswahili poetry usually about love. Ensembles feature qanun, oud, violin, accordion, nay and tabla/darbuka. Originally a sit-and-listen form, modern taarab (especially women's mipasho) incorporates dancing, clapping and cheering at weddings and festive occasions; movement is subtle and gesture-based (Wikipedia; AramcoWorld).
insight: Sultan Barghash imported an Egyptian ensemble to his Zanzibar palace and sent Mohamed Ibrahim to Cairo to learn the qanun; female pioneer Siti binti Saad became the first East African to record commercially (Mumbai, 1928).
notes: Music-primary with dance component. QID verified. Not UNESCO-listed.

name: Zar
region: Cairo and Upper Egypt; also Sudan, Ethiopia
era: Spread to Egypt mid-19th century from Ethiopia via Sudan
tagline: Women's trance healing and spirit-possession ritual.
profile: A possession-trance dance ritual led by women (a kudya/sheikha), performed to heal individuals - mostly women - afflicted by zar spirits. Centers on rhythmic drumming (tabl, duff, tanbur), chanted invocations naming specific spirits, incense and animal sacrifice. The afflicted dance to spirit-specific rhythms to reach trance and reconcile with (rather than exorcise) the possessing spirit. Sacred, women-centered ritual - respectful framing required (Qantara.de; Project MUSE).
insight: A sacred, women-centered ritual offering an alternative spiritual space within Islam; it originated in sub-Saharan Africa and spread via the 19th-century slave trade. The Sudanese 'tambura' subgroup uses the tambura-harp.

sources.title: Zar: Spirit Possession, Music, and Healing Rituals in Egypt

notes: Cross-regional (EG/SD/ET). No verified dedicated dance Wikipedia article/QID - left blank. Sacred ritual - respectful framing.

name: Isicathamiya
tagline: Zulu a cappella choral music with soft-step dance.
profile: A secular a cappella choral genre developed by Zulu migrant workers, sung in call-and-response four-part harmony led by a tenor soloist. Accompanied by tightly choreographed, soft tip-toe dance steps and clapping - the name derives from the Zulu -cathama ('to tread softly'). All-night competitions run in halls. Popularized globally by Ladysmith Black Mambazo via Paul Simon's Graceland (1986) (Wikipedia; Britannica).
insight: The soft-stepping choreography reportedly originated so dancers would not disturb mine-camp security guards; the related 'Mbube' (lion) genre gave the world 'The Lion Sleeps Tonight.'
notes: Primarily a cappella music with integral dance component. Not UNESCO-listed as a standalone dance. QID verified.

name: Gule Wamkulu
era: 17th century Chewa Empire; UNESCO proclaimed 2005, inscribed 2008
tagline: Chewa masked ancestral-spirit 'great dance'.
profile: A ritual masked dance performed by initiates of the Nyau secret society of the Chewa. Masked, costumed dancers in straw and fibre impersonate ancestral spirits, animals and supernatural beings, mediating between the living and the dead at initiations, funerals and harvest events. Each character carries a name and moral lesson. Only initiated men perform. Sacred secret-society ritual - some ceremonies closed to outsiders (UNESCO; Britannica).
insight: Per UNESCO, Gule Wamkulu 'dates back to the great Chewa Empire of the seventeenth century' and is recognized across Malawi, Zambia and Mozambique; photography may be forbidden at certain ceremonies.
notes: UNESCO RL/00142, Masterpiece 2005, inscribed 2008. Tri-national (MW/ZM/MZ). Sacred ritual.

name: Khaleegy (Khaliji Gulf dance)
era: Pre-oil maritime era to present (codified 19th-20th c.)
tagline: Gulf women's celebratory hair-and-thobe dance of the sea.
profile: Khaleegy is a women's social dance of the Arabian Gulf (Saudi Arabia, Kuwait, Bahrain, Qatar, UAE, Oman), performed at weddings and gatherings in an ornate thobe al-nashal. Dancers glide with shuffling steps, sway their hair, and use hand gestures evoking the sea, pearl diving and marine life. It is largely improvisational, set to a hypnotic 2/4 rhythm (Wikipedia; Mellilah).
insight: An anthropologist who married into a Kuwaiti family, Melinda Smith, suggests the movements originally mimicked Gulf pearl divers' everyday actions, with rolling dress motions imitating waves.
notes: Pan-Gulf women's dance. Not UNESCO-inscribed.

name: Dabke
tagline: Stomping Levantine line dance of weddings and festivity.
profile: Dabke is the iconic folk line dance of the Levant, performed across Lebanon, Syria, Jordan, Palestine and parts of Iraq. Dancers link arms or shoulders in an arc or line, led by a lawweeh, stamping synchronized footwork to mijwiz, derbakke and sung poetry. It anchors weddings and national celebrations and is a potent symbol of communal identity and homeland (UNESCO Palestine; Wikipedia).
insight: UNESCO's MedLiHer documentation describes dabke as the dance common to the countryside of Lebanon, Syria, Jordan and Palestine, defined by dancers lined in arcs, arms entwined, stamping feet.
notes: Cross-border entry filed under Lebanon. UNESCO inscription is PALESTININAN ONLY: RL/01998, 2023 (18.COM). The brief's 'Lebanon RL/01718, 2022' is FALSE: 01718 is Arabic calligraphy. Lebanon, Syria and Jordan have NO dabke inscription.

name: Classical Persian Dance
era: Safavid-Qajar courts (16th-early 20th c.) to diaspora present
tagline: Graceful solo court dance of Persia with expressive hands.
profile: Classical Persian dance is a concert/solo improvisational form that evolved from urban Tehran court dance under the Qajar dynasty (1795-1925), emphasizing delicate hand and wrist articulation, spins and lyrical upper-body grace set to Persian classical music and poetry. After the 1979 revolution it flourished largely in the diaspora, where it has been newly codified (Wikipedia; NYAS academic paper).
insight: Per Wikipedia, Classical Persian dance is a style of concert dance which evolved from courtroom dance; the Qajar dynasty (1795-1925) had a defining influence on the form.

sources.title: Iranian classical dance as a subject for empirical research
sources.publisher: Annals of the NY Academy of Sciences (Wiley)

notes: QID not confirmed. Radif (UNESCO 2009) is MUSIC not dance - do not conflate.

name: Ardah (Najdi sword dance)
tagline: Saudi men's sword, drum and poetry war dance.
profile: Ardah (Al-Ardah Al-Najdiyah) is the Saudi national folk dance: two rows of men, often bearing swords or canes, face each other and chant antiphonal poetry to large dance drums while a flag-bearer stands between them. Originally a pre-battle morale display of Najdi tribes, it is now performed at weddings, national days and royal ceremonies (UNESCO; Wikipedia).
insight: UNESCO states Alardah combines dance, drumming and chanting poetry that signifies the start and end of notable occasions; inscribed 2015 (10.COM) as Saudi Arabia's first UNESCO ICH element.

sources.title: Alardah Alnajdiyah, dance, drumming and poetry in Saudi Arabia

notes: File 01196, 2015 (NOT 01097 as in source brief). SAUDI-ONLY inscription, NOT a Saudi/UAE joint file.

name: Samri
tagline: Seated rhythmic clapping, drumming and sung-poetry dance.
profile: Samri is a traditional Arabian Peninsula performance combining sung Nabati poetry, hand-clapping and frame-drum rhythms, typically performed by seated or kneeling rows of men swaying to the beat, common in Najd and across Kuwait, Bahrain and Saudi Arabia. In some Gulf contexts Samri/Samra also names a women's celebratory dance (Bellydance Quickies; Wikipedia).
insight: Dance scholar Kay Hardy Campbell, who learned Gulf dances at Saudi women's wedding parties, treats al-Samri of Kuwait, Bahrain and Saudi Arabia as one of the peninsula's significant folk dances performed by both men and women.
notes: No confirmed Wikipedia/QID. Distinct from UNESCO-listed Al-Mezmar (2016). Not UNESCO-inscribed.

name: Al-Ayyala
tagline: Emirati-Omani battle dance of sticks, drums and poetry.
profile: Al-Ayyala is a performing art of the UAE and north-western Oman simulating a battle scene: two rows of about twenty men face each other holding thin bamboo sticks as mock spears, moving heads and sticks to drum rhythms while chanting poetry. In the UAE, girls in traditional dress stand at the front tossing their long hair (al-na'ashat) (UNESCO; Wikipedia).
insight: UNESCO inscribed Al-Ayyala in 2014 (9.COM) as a joint UAE-Oman element, describing two facing rows of men who carry thin bamboo sticks to signify spears or swords.

sources.title: Al-Ayyala, a traditional performing art of the Sultanate of Oman and the UAE

notes: File 01012, 2014. Joint UAE+Oman inscription.

name: Bara'a (Yemeni tribal dance)
tagline: Yemeni highland men's jambiya dagger group dance.
profile: Bara'a is a Yemeni tribal group dance performed by men in a circle or arc, each wearing the curved jambiya dagger, stepping and leaping to drum rhythms as an assertion of tribal honor and solidarity; it is a centerpiece of Yemeni weddings alongside regional sung-dance forms such as al-Sana'ani of Sana'a (Bellydance Quickies; Wikipedia).
insight: Dance scholar Kay Hardy Campbell lists al-Sana'ani of Yemen among the significant Arabian Peninsula folk dances performed at celebrations, reflecting Yemen's distinct wedding-dance repertoire.
notes: QID/Wikipedia unconfirmed. Not UNESCO-inscribed.

name: Chobi (Iraqi line dance)
tagline: Energetic Iraqi line dance of weddings and festivity.
profile: Chobi (also Choubi/Hacha) is the popular Iraqi folk line dance performed at weddings and celebrations, in which dancers link in a line and execute rhythmic stepping and shoulder movements to the fast kasoor drum rhythm, often overlapping with the Iraqi dabke variant. It is a defining social dance of Iraqi celebrations (Wikipedia; folk sources).
insight: Iraqi dabke/Chobi is documented as a distinct regional form from the Levantine dabke; recordings such as Dabke Iraqiyyi (Chobi) circulate as a named Iraqi folk-dance genre.
notes: Thin sourcing - needs stronger ethnographic source. Not UNESCO-inscribed.

name: Hora (Israeli folk dance)
era: Brought to Palestine during the Second Aliyah (1904-1914); standardized 1924
tagline: Israeli circle dance of community and celebration.
profile: The Israeli Hora is a communal circle dance brought to Ottoman Palestine by Romanian Jews during the Second Aliyah and adapted from the Romanian/Balkan hora, danced with linked shoulders or hands stepping and swaying in a rotating circle. It became emblematic of kibbutz and pioneer culture and remains central to Jewish weddings and celebrations worldwide (Wikipedia).
insight: The first standardized Israeli version - Hora Agadati - was choreographed by Romanian-Jewish dancer Baruch Agadati in Tel Aviv in 1924; different from Romanian hora which is in the catalog as hora-romania.
notes: QID unconfirmed. Not UNESCO-inscribed. Distinct from Romanian and Bulgarian hora entries already in catalog.

name: Haka
tagline: Maori ceremonial posture dance of strength and identity.
profile: Haka is a Maori ceremonial group dance combining vigorous stamping, chest- and thigh-slapping, chanting, and intense facial expressions (pukana, whetero). Far more than a war dance, it welcomes guests, marks funerals, weddings and achievements, and asserts mana and identity; globally famous via the All Blacks' Ka Mate (Wikipedia; Britannica).
insight: Per Maori tradition, haka originates with Tane-rore, son of the sun god Tama-nui-te-ra and Hine-raumati; the quivering summer air is his dance, the basis of the trembling hands in all haka.
notes: NOT UNESCO-inscribed (confirmed). Cultural sensitivity: not a generic 'war dance.' Frame as Maori ceremonial tradition.

name: Meke
tagline: Fijian storytelling dance of chant, clubs and fans.
profile: Meke is the umbrella term for traditional Fijian dance-and-chant, encompassing the men's club/war dance (cibi/wesi), women's fan dance (seasea) and the seated dance (vakamalolo). Performed by the matana (dancers) with the vakatara (singers/drummers on lali and derua), it preserves legends, history and genealogy in a society without a written past (Wikipedia; Polynesian Cultural Center).
insight: Fijian tradition holds that meke were taught by divine beings in the spirit world to chosen individuals called daunivucu, who then passed the sacred dances to their communities.
notes: NOT UNESCO-inscribed. Some tourism sites falsely claim UNESCO status.

name: Siva (Samoan dance)
tagline: Graceful Samoan dance of flowing hands and storytelling.
profile: The Samoan siva is a graceful dance in which gentle, flowing hand and body movements interpret song and story, traditionally performed solo or in groups at celebrations; the male counterpart includes the vigorous fa'ataupati (slap dance) and the fire-knife siva afi. It expresses Samoan values of grace, respect and communal joy (Far and Away Adventures; Sydney Opera House).
insight: Pacific cultural educators describe Samoa's siva as 'graceful and rhythmic, reflecting the island's traditions and values,' contrasting the women's flowing movements with the men's energetic fire and slap dances.

sources.title: 6 South Pacific Islands Known for Their Cultural Dance Shows

notes: QID Q7532284 (Siva Samoa). Not UNESCO-inscribed.

name: Lakalaka
tagline: Tonga's national dance of sung poetry and gesture.
profile: Lakalaka is Tonga's national dance, blending choreography, oratory and vocal polyphony. Up to several hundred performers stand in gender-divided rows - men dancing energetically, women with graceful hand gestures - while singing sung speeches composed by a punake (poet-composer-choreographer) on themes of history, genealogy and values. It features at coronations and constitution anniversaries (UNESCO; Wikipedia).
insight: UNESCO inscribed Lakalaka in 2008 (originally proclaimed a Masterpiece in 2003), noting the punake 'is at the same time poet, composer, choreographer and performance director.'

sources.title: Lakalaka, dances and sung speeches of Tonga

notes: File 00072, proclaimed 2003, inscribed 2008 (3.COM). Confirmed.

name: Sing-sing
era: Ancestral tribal tradition to present festival form
tagline: Papua New Guinean tribal gathering of dance and song.
profile: A sing-sing is a gathering where PNG tribes display their distinct dance, song, body paint and bilas (ceremonial dress). Originally tied to ancestor/spirit ritual and war, modern sing-sings (Goroka Show, Mount Hagen, Tumbuna) bring scores of cultural groups together, each performing its own decorated dance, helping preserve hundreds of distinct traditions (Wikipedia; Goroka Show Wikipedia).
insight: The Goroka Show began in 1956 as a Kiap (patrol-officer) initiative and its first edition drew about 10,000 attendees; modern editions feature roughly 100 tribes and draw up to 50,000 people for the weekend.
notes: Festival/gathering form rather than a single dance; entry framed accordingly. Not UNESCO-inscribed.

name: Tamure (Tahitian dance)
era: 20th c. popularization of ori Tahiti to present
tagline: Fast-hipped Tahitian dance of drums and grass skirts.
profile: Tamure is the popularly known, fast hip-shaking Tahitian dance within the broader ori Tahiti tradition, performed by men and women in grass skirts (more) to rapid to'ere drum rhythms. The ote'a tells stories through choreography; tamure became the global face of Tahitian dance (Far and Away Adventures; Wikipedia).
insight: Pacific dance guides note Tahiti's repertoire centers on ori Tahiti, known for its fast hip movements, and the storytelling ote'a - the family to which tamure belongs.

sources.title: 6 South Pacific Islands Known for Their Cultural Dance Shows

notes: French Polynesia (PF). QID unconfirmed. Not UNESCO-inscribed.

name: Barynya
tagline: Fast Russian folk dance of stomping and squat-kicks.
profile: Barynya is a fast Russian folk dance alternating sung chastushki (often humorous or bawdy verse) with frenetic, largely improvised dancing built on stomping, spins, leaps and the prisyadka (squat-kicks), accompanied by balalaika and garmon. The name (landlady) comes from the refrain 'Barynya, barynya, sudarynya-barynya' (Wikipedia).
insight: Per Wikipedia, Barynya is a fast Russian folk dance originating in the Central Russian Upland, characterized by Russian squatting, rotations, jumps and spirited stomping with no fixed choreography.
notes: Not UNESCO-inscribed.

name: Khorovod
tagline: Slavic circle dance uniting chorus song and movement.
profile: Khorovod is an ancient East Slavic round dance combining a circle dance with choral singing, historically performed mostly by women to mark the turning of the seasons and life cycle. Akin to the ancient Greek chorea, it is one of the oldest Russian choreographic forms and survives in folk festivals (Wikipedia; Rusmoose).
insight: Russian folk-dance scholarship describes khorovod (Ukrainian tanok, Belarusian karagod) as a traditional Slavic art form that combines a circle dance and chorus singing, similar to the chorea of ancient Greece.
notes: QID unconfirmed. Not UNESCO-inscribed.

name: Mevlevi Sema Ceremony
tagline: Sufi whirling dervish ceremony of spiritual ascent.
profile: The Mevlevi Sema is the sacred whirling ceremony of the Mevlevi Sufi order founded by followers of Rumi in 13th-century Konya. Semazens in white robes and tall sikke hats whirl in structured selams symbolizing the soul's mystical journey toward the divine, accompanied by ayin music, ney and recitation. It is worship, not performance (UNESCO; Wikipedia).
insight: After Turkey banned Sufi lodges in 1925, clandestine transmission focused rather on music and songs than on spiritual and religious traditions, eroding some religious significance; UNESCO proclaimed the Sema a Masterpiece in 2005 and inscribed it in 2008.
notes: File 00100, 2008. SACRED tradition - frame as worship, not entertainment.

name: Zeybek
tagline: Slow, proud Aegean warrior dance of Turkey.
profile: Zeybek is a solo or group folk dance of western/Aegean Anatolia embodying the efe (zeybek) warrior-bandit ethos. Dancers extend arms eagle-like at shoulder height, take large grounded steps, and kneel to touch the ground, performed to 9/8 rhythms ranging from very slow to fast, traditionally accompanied by davul and zurna (Wikipedia; Les Arts Turcs).
insight: Turkish folk-dance documentation notes zeybek dances are formed in general of 9/8 measures, with the term zeybek (Central Anatolian 'segmen') historically meaning warrior.
notes: Not UNESCO-inscribed.

name: Horon
tagline: Fast trembling Black Sea circle dance of Turkey.
profile: Horon is the fast, sharp circle/line dance of Turkey's eastern Black Sea region, in which linked dancers vibrate their shoulders and step quickly to the kemence (and davul-zurna). Said to evoke the quivering of anchovies (hamsi), it is intense and tightly synchronized. Predominantly male tradition from the Trabzon and Rize areas (Wikipedia; Les Arts Turcs).
insight: Horon melodies are predominantly in asymmetric meters such as 5/8, 7/16 and 9/16 (KURE Encyclopedia), and dancers can hit roughly 300 steps a minute, among the fastest of Turkey's folk dances.
notes: UNESCO status unconfirmed - a secondary source reported a 2021 inscription that could NOT be confirmed at ich.unesco.org; recorded as false pending direct verification. QID verified.

name: Halay
tagline: Anatolian line-and-circle dance of communal unity.
profile: Halay is the dominant communal line/circle dance of eastern, central and southeastern Anatolia, in which dancers join hands, shoulders or little fingers and move as one to davul and zurna, led by a halaybaşı often waving a handkerchief. The word connotes union and cooperation; it is the emblematic dance of communal solidarity (Wikipedia; DanceUs).
insight: Turkish dance documentation states the word 'Halay comes from the word Alay which means many people, unity, union, cooperation,' and is performed largely in East, Southeast and Central Anatolia.
notes: Confirmed NOT UNESCO-inscribed as of 2026. QID unconfirmed.

name: Kartuli
tagline: Elegant Georgian wedding couple dance of restraint.
profile: Kartuli is the quintessential Georgian couple dance - a romantic, wedding-like pas de deux in which the man, upper body held perfectly still, circles the gliding woman without ever touching her, expressing chivalry and dignity. It is among the most technically demanding Georgian dances, governed by strict rules of sequence and conduct (Wikipedia; TbilisiTrips).
insight: Georgian dance documentation notes Kartuli is governed by very strict rules - 'the man must not touch the woman, not even with his coat' - and in 2020 was designated a Monument of Intangible Cultural Heritage of Georgia.
notes: National ICH Monument of Georgia (2020). NOT on UNESCO international lists. Georgia's UNESCO entry is polyphonic SINGING (RL/00008, 2008).

name: Khorumi
tagline: Georgian warrior dance of search, battle and victory.
profile: Khorumi is an ancient Georgian war dance from southwestern Adjara/Guria, structured in parts - scouting a campsite, reconnoitering the enemy, the attack, and victory celebration. Performed only by men (now thirty to fifty dancers), it features strong, precise linear formations to the doli drum and chiboni bagpipe in a distinctive 5/8 (3+2) meter, without clapping (Wikipedia; TbilisiTrips).
insight: Wikipedia notes that unlike most Georgian dances, 'Khorumi is traditionally accompanied by instruments, and is not accompanied by clapping,' with the doli and chiboni as key instruments.
notes: National ICH Monument of Georgia. Not on UNESCO international lists. Martial heritage - respectful framing.

name: Acharuli
tagline: Playful, colorful Adjaran flirtation dance of Georgia.
profile: Acharuli (Gandagana) is a bright, lighthearted Georgian dance from the Adjara region, depicting playful flirtation between men and women in colorful, red-dominant costumes. Unlike the formal Kartuli, the male-female relationship is informal and joyful, with women's graceful movements and men's quick, skillful steps, often featuring jumps and distinctive arm gestures (Wikipedia; TbilisiTrips).
insight: Georgian dance documentation notes Acharuli/Gandagana is also a monument in Georgia as part of its intangible cultural heritage, distinguished by its colorful costumes and playful, informal mood.
notes: National ICH Monument of Georgia. QID unconfirmed; Wikipedia link is the general Georgian dance article.

name: Samaia
tagline: Three-woman Georgian dance honoring Queen Tamar.
profile: Samaia is a graceful Georgian women's dance performed by three dancers symbolizing Queen Tamar (12th-13th c.) - as young princess, wise mother and powerful monarch - with flowing, stately arm and hand movements evoking royal dignity and the prosperity of Georgia's golden age (Iberogeorgia; TbilisiTrips).
insight: Samaia was choreographed by Iliko Sukhishvili of the Georgian National Ballet based more on a poetic interpretation of history than any pre-existing dance steps, with costumes restored from the Vardzia fresco of Queen Tamar by painter Simon Virsaladze in the 1930s.
notes: 20th-c. staged choreography (Sukhishvili, Georgian National Ballet) on medieval themes. QID/Wikipedia unconfirmed.

name: Kochari
tagline: Armenian communal group dance of unity and identity.
profile: Kochari is Armenia's most popular traditional group dance - an open, shoulder- or hand-linked line/circle dance whose stamping, knee-bending and forward-back movements mimic fighting rams. Performed by all ages and genders at holidays, weddings and celebrations to zurna and dhol, it is a powerful emblem of Armenian solidarity and continuity (UNESCO; Wikipedia).
insight: UNESCO inscribed Kochari in 2017, calling it 'one of the rare traditional dances whose chain of transmission has never been interrupted,' providing a sense of shared identity and solidarity.
notes: File 01295, 2017 (NOT 01209 as in brief). ARMENIA-ONLY Representative List inscription. Azerbaijan has a SEPARATE 'Kochari' variant within its Yalli inscription.

name: Yarkhushta
tagline: Armenian martial clap-dance of warriors before battle.
profile: Yarkhushta is an Armenian martial clap dance from the highlands of Sasun, performed by men in two facing rows who rush forward and vigorously clap palms together like clashing weapons, to loud zurna and bass drums. Traditionally danced by soldiers before combat to dispel fear and rouse battle spirit (Wikipedia; Armenia Discovery).
insight: Wikipedia records that Yarkhushta has traditionally been danced by Armenian soldiers before combat engagements, partly for ritualistic purposes and partly to cast away fear and boost battle spirit; it is attested in early medieval authors such as Movses Khorenatsi.
notes: Not UNESCO-inscribed. Martial heritage tied to Sasun - frame respectfully.

name: Shalakho
tagline: Energetic Caucasian dance of rivalry and courtship.
profile: Shalakho is a lively, widely shared South Caucasus dance - claimed as national by Armenians and also danced in Azerbaijan and Georgia - most often depicting two men competing energetically (jumping, kicking, spinning) for a woman's attention while the woman moves with slower grace, set to a fast 6/8 zurna rhythm (Mondo Internazionale; Armenia Discovery).
insight: As one survey notes, 'the Shalakho is considered by the Armenians a national dance,' widespread across Azerbaijan, Georgia and Armenia in regionally varying versions.
notes: Contested shared heritage (Armenia/Azerbaijan/Georgia) - note sensitivity. QID unconfirmed.

name: Yalli (Nakhchivan group dances)
era: Ancient to present; UNESCO Urgent Safeguarding 2018
tagline: Azerbaijani collective chain dance of Nakhchivan.
profile: Yalli is an Azerbaijani collective dance tradition from Nakhchivan performed in a circle, chain or line, incorporating games, pantomime (bird/animal imitations) and physical movements. Its many variants include Kochari and Tenzere; some are sung and mixed-gender, others men-only mock-battle dances. Long widely practiced, it has declined and needs safeguarding (UNESCO; AZERTAC).
insight: UNESCO inscribed Yalli in 2018 on the Urgent Safeguarding List, citing 'a drastic simplification of the dances, which has entailed a loss of diversity.'

sources.title: Yalli (Kochari, Tenzere), traditional group dances of Nakhchivan
Yalli - masterpiece of Azerbaijani folk dance

notes: File 01190, 2018 (NOT 01378 as in brief). URGENT SAFEGUARDING LIST (not the Representative List). Contains a 'Kochari' variant distinct from Armenia's separately-inscribed Kochari.

name: Jangi
tagline: Azerbaijani martial dance summoning courage for battle.
profile: Jangi (meaning war/fighting) is an Azerbaijani men's martial dance historically performed to summon courage and unity before combat, built on bold, vigorous movements to a stirring instrumental melody. It functions as a heroic call-to-arms counterpart within the Azerbaijani folk repertoire alongside the celebratory Yalli (Advantour; AZERTAC).
insight: Jangi is documented in Azerbaijani folk-dance tradition as a martial/heroic dance whose name denotes war, performed to rally fighters - a martial counterpart to the celebratory Yalli.

sources.title: Yalli - masterpiece of Azerbaijani folk dance

notes: Thinnest entry - sourcing is general. QID/Wikipedia unconfirmed. Not UNESCO-inscribed.

name: Zaouli
region: Bouafle and Zuenoula departments, central Cote d'Ivoire
tagline: Guro masked solo dance honoring feminine beauty.
profile: Zaouli is a popular masked music-and-dance practiced by the Guro communities of central Cote d'Ivoire. Performed by a single masked male dancer accompanied by drums and flutes, it is famous for a near-motionless upper body above blindingly fast footwork. Created in the 1950s and inspired by a girl named Djela Lou Zaouli, it unites sculpture (the mask), weaving (the costume), music and dance, and is believed to bring prosperity and unity to the community (UNESCO; Wikipedia).
insight: UNESCO inscribed Zaouli in 2017 (12.COM, RL/01255) on the Representative List; each Guro village maintains its own dancer, and the masking process is sacred and closed to the uninitiated.
notes: UNESCO RL/01255 (2017). Brief listed 01260 in error. Sacred masking; respectful framing required.

name: Gelede
tagline: Yoruba-Nago masked dance honoring mothers' power.
profile: Gelede is a masked song-and-dance ceremony of the Yoruba-Nago community spread over Benin, Nigeria and Togo. For more than a century it has paid tribute to the primordial mother Iya Nla and to the social role of women and elder 'mothers.' Performed after harvests and at major events, it features carved headdress masks, elaborate costumes, drumming and satirical sung verses in Yoruba retracing community history and myth (UNESCO; Wikipedia).
insight: Inscribed in 2008 (3.COM, RL/00002) on the Representative List (originally proclaimed a Masterpiece in 2001), Gelede is described as the only known masked society that is also governed by women.
notes: UNESCO RL/00002 (2008; proclaimed 2001). Shared Benin/Nigeria/Togo. Sacred; respectful framing required.

name: Makishi masquerade
region: Northwestern and western Zambia (Luvale, Chokwe, Luchazi, Mbunda)
tagline: Masked ancestral spirits closing the boys' mukanda initiation.
profile: The Makishi masquerade is performed at the end of the mukanda, an annual initiation ritual for boys between the ages of eight and twelve among the Vaka Chiyama Cha Mukwamayi communities of north-western and western Zambia. Each masked character embodies an ancestral spirit - the wealthy Chisaluke, the protective Mupala, or the female Pwevo - guiding initiates through their symbolic death as children and rebirth as men (UNESCO).
insight: Proclaimed a Masterpiece in 2005 and inscribed on the Representative List in 2008 (RL/00140); in former times the mukanda spanned several months, but it is today often reduced to one month to fit the school calendar.
notes: UNESCO RL/00140 (2005/2008). Brief listed 00032 in error. No standalone English Wikipedia article. Sacred initiation; respectful framing required.

name: Borborbor
tagline: Ewe recreational drum-and-dance of the Volta Region.
profile: Borborbor (also Akpese) is a recreational social dance of the Ewe people of the mid-Volta Region of Ghana and southern Togo, including Kpando, Hohoe, Ho, Kpalime and Lome. Largely female-led, dancers wear colorful garments and wave handkerchiefs while moving in a circle around drummers, with proverbial sung lyrics that often carry Christian themes. It features at festivals, funerals, weddings and durbars (Wikipedia; GhanaRemembers).
insight: Borborbor is credited to Francis Nuatro, an ex-police officer from Kpando, who blended the traditional Konkoma rhythm with brass-band and highlife elements; its roots are traced to the village of Wusuta near the Volta Lake in the late 1940s.

sources.title: Borborbor Dance: A cultural celebration of the Ewe people

notes: Not UNESCO. Distinct from Agbadza (already in catalog).

name: Agbekor
era: Pre-colonial war dance; recreational form preserved
tagline: Ancient Ewe-Fon warrior dance of polyrhythmic drumming.
profile: Agbekor is a music-and-dance of the Ewe and Fon peoples, once known as Atamga ('great oath'), now performed by communities such as Dzogadze near Akatsi in the southern Volta Region. Historically a war dance performed before and after battle, it is characterized by highly polyrhythmic percussion in which the lead drummer (atsimevu) cues dancers through slow and fast sections, with interlude 'hatsiatsia' songs in between (Wikipedia; academic sources).
insight: Today Agbekor is a cornerstone of West African drumming curricula worldwide and is performed at funerals, festivals and chieftaincy events rather than for war.
notes: Not UNESCO. Distinct from Agbadza (already in catalog).

name: Lamban
tagline: Mandinka griot dance celebrating the jeli caste.
profile: Lamban (also Djelidon) is a ceremonial praise dance of the Manding/Mandinka peoples, traditionally exclusive to the jeli (griot) hereditary musicians, especially the Kouyte lineage. Accompanied by balafon, kora, ngoni and dunun, it celebrates the griots themselves - the oral historians, praise-singers and advisors of West Africa. It is performed across Mali, Guinea, Senegal, The Gambia and neighboring countries with little variation, defying colonial borders (academic sources).
insight: Lamban is one of the few Mande pieces created by jelis for their own entertainment, traditionally played on moonlit nights when men play instruments while women sing and dance.
notes: No English Wikipedia article or confirmed QID. Multi-country Mande dance. Not UNESCO-inscribed.

name: Egungun
tagline: Yoruba ancestral masquerade where the dead return.
profile: Egungun is a Yoruba masquerade honoring ancestral spirits (Ara Orun, 'the collective dead'), practiced across Yorubaland in Nigeria, Benin and Togo. Initiated men wear layered, voluminous cloth costumes that completely conceal the body; through drumming and whirling dance they are believed to become possessed by ancestors who bless, warn and discipline the community. Costumes accumulate fabric panels across generations as a vertical archive of family history (Wikipedia; Brooklyn Museum).
insight: Movement and dance are the most important elements - as the masquerader spins, the flying panels emit a breeze said to bless spectators; women create the costumes and sing the oriki praise poems but do not wear the mask.
notes: Not UNESCO (distinct from Gelede). Sacred ancestral rite; respectful framing required.

name: Simb
tagline: Wolof false-lion street masquerade of Dakar.
profile: Simb (Simb Gainde, 'the false lion') is a Wolof street spectacle of Senegal, especially Dakar and Saint-Louis, in which men paint themselves and dress as lions and panthers, then dance, roar and chase the audience to sabar drumming. Rooted in an oral legend of a hunter possessed by a lion's spirit, the event blends mysticism, folklore, theatrical terror and dance, with performers protected by gris-gris amulets (Garland Magazine; David Ndao photography).
insight: A typical Simb features five male 'lions' and two men dancing as the lions' wives (Gainde bu Jigeen); today it is a ticketed community event especially beloved by children.

sources.title: Simb, or the rise and fall of the Fake Lion in Senegal
Simb or The Dance of the Fake Lion

notes: No English Wikipedia article. No confirmed QID. Distinct from sabar (already in catalog).

name: Pantsula
tagline: Urban township street dance of resistance and style.
profile: Pantsula is a highly energetic, syncopated, low-to-the-ground street dance that emerged in the 1950s and 1960s in the black townships of Johannesburg as a response to apartheid-era forced removals, beginning as informal street competitions among older men. It developed political overtones in the 1980s as an expression of resistance and is marked by quick darting footwork in geometric formations, a distinct dress code (Converse All Stars, Dickies, bucket hats) and kwaito music (Wikipedia; Jukebox Collective).
insight: The word, often glossed as 'to walk like a duck,' names both a dance and a whole township subculture; it evolved from Sotho mqaquamba and marabi with American jazz, hip hop and breakdance influences.
notes: Not UNESCO. Urban contemporary tradition.

name: Toyi-toyi
tagline: High-stepping protest march-dance of liberation.
profile: Toyi-toyi is a Southern African protest dance and chant - a high-kneed, foot-stomping movement punctuated by call-and-response slogans - that became iconic in the anti-apartheid struggle. It originated as a military drill in African liberation-movement training camps (traced to Algeria and ZIPRA/MK camps in Angola, Zambia and Zimbabwe) before spreading into South African townships, where activists wielded it as a weapon of intimidation and solidarity against state forces (Wikipedia; The Conversation).
insight: Paired with chants such as the ANC's 'Amandla! Awethu!' ('power to us'), toyi-toyi remains ubiquitous in service-delivery protests, union actions and university demonstrations across South Africa and Zimbabwe today.

sources.title: The incredible journey of the toyi-toyi

notes: Not UNESCO. Shared South Africa/Zimbabwe origins.

name: Muchongoyo
tagline: Ndau warrior stamping dance with stick and shield.
profile: Muchongoyo is the signature presentational dance-drumming tradition of the Ndau people of Zimbabwe's southeastern districts (and Tsonga/Shangaan who call it Muchongolo). Historically a war dance to instill discipline before battle and celebrate victory, its signature is forceful synchronized foot-stamping with dramatic stick-and-shield gestures, performed by men while women sing and clap in a 'cow-horn' formation (Wikipedia; African Studies Review).
insight: Unlike many Zimbabwean dances, Muchongoyo has no religious dimension; ethnomusicologist Tony Perman notes it can engender hope, frustration and solidarity, and post-independence it split into a localized Ndau form and a nationalized, state-promoted variant.
notes: Not UNESCO. QID recommend spot-check before publishing.

name: Xibelani
tagline: Tsonga women's hip-shaking dance with weighted skirt.
profile: The xibelani is an indigenous dance of Tsonga (Vatsonga/Shangaan) women of Limpopo and Mpumalanga provinces of South Africa, with roots traced to coastal southern Mozambique. Dancers tie on the heavy pleated tinguvu skirt, whose swirling amplifies energetic hip movements performed to xigubu drums, mbila xylophone, hand-clapping, whistles and call-and-response singing. Performed in groups of six to twelve (Wikipedia; Werksmans heritage article).
insight: The name translates roughly to 'hitting to the rhythm'; the dance is taught to Tsonga girls from an early age and is a central feature of all important Tsonga ceremonies.
notes: Not UNESCO. Shared South Africa/Mozambique Tsonga heritage.

name: Ahidus
region: Middle Atlas mountains and Jbel Saghro
tagline: Middle Atlas Amazigh collective line-and-circle dance.
profile: Ahidus is an ancient Amazigh (Berber) collective dance-and-song performed in a line or circle by both men and women, originating in Morocco's Middle Atlas mountains and Jbel Saghro. Men hold the bendir frame drum and sing improvised poems that women repeat, the group forming a closed circle that symbolizes tribal unity, with a poet leading from the center. Men wear white djellabas and turbans; women wear colorful kaftans and silver jewelry (Wikipedia; Morocco World News).
insight: Believed to be connected to harvest celebration, ahidus is a core marker of Amazigh identity south of the Atlas Mountains and is also performed as an expression of victory after conflict.
notes: Not UNESCO-inscribed. Distinct from Taskiwin (already in catalog).

name: Ahwash
region: High Atlas, Anti-Atlas and Souss regions
tagline: High Atlas Amazigh communal dance, song and poetry.
profile: Ahwash (ahouach) is a Shilha Amazigh style of collective performance - dance, singing, poetry and frame-drum percussion - from southern Morocco's High Atlas, Anti-Atlas and Souss regions. Performed in a central village square (assais/assarag) at local festivals, it typically involves two large groups, men and women on opposite sides, alternating song, dance, poetry and drumming. Performances can involve more than twenty and sometimes 150 or more participants (Wikipedia; Morocco World News).
insight: Recognized to retain pre-Islamic Berber religious components, ahwash is often performed before a saint's tomb during moussem holidays, its communal mixed-gender form standing in contrast to conservative Islamic views.
notes: Not separately UNESCO-inscribed. Distinct from Taskiwin (already in catalog).

name: Tende
tagline: Tuareg women's mortar-drum music and dance.
profile: The tende (tindi) is a drum made from a wooden grain mortar topped with stretched goatskin, and the women-led music and dance associated with it among the Tuareg of the Sahel-Sahara, especially Niger and Mali. Played almost exclusively by women, it features female choirs and a soloist in call-and-response, performed at weddings, naming ceremonies, evening gatherings and camel festivals, where trained camels circle and 'dance' to the rhythm (Wikipedia; Garland Handbook of African Music).
insight: Passed matrilineally from mother to daughter, the tende embodies Tuareg values of integrity, reserve and self-control; the related tasikisikit features women singing as men circle on camel-back.

sources.title: Tuareg Music
sources.publisher: The Garland Handbook of African Music

notes: Not UNESCO. The Wikipedia article centers on the drum/music complex, which includes the dance.

name: Dama
tagline: Dogon masked funerary dance escorting the dead.
profile: The dama is the collective funerary masked dance of the Dogon people of the Bandiagara escarpment in Mali, organized by the Awa society of initiated men. Held months or years after deaths, it culminates in a procession of masked dancers - wearing over eighty mask types such as the cross-topped kanaga, the female satimbe, stilt-walkers and animal masks - who escort the souls of the deceased from the village to the realm of the ancestors, ending the mourning period (Smarthistory; Metropolitan Museum of Art).
insight: In the kanaga dance, performers arc the mask down to touch the earth, symbolizing the creator god Amma giving life; today dama are also staged for national holidays and tourists, raising preservation concerns.
notes: The dance is not UNESCO-inscribed; the Bandiagara Cliff is a UNESCO World Heritage SITE. No standalone English Wikipedia article for the dama dance. Sacred funerary rite; respectful framing required.

name: Chakacha
tagline: Sensual Swahili women's wedding hip dance.
profile: Chakacha is a traditional Swahili ngoma music-and-dance of coastal Kenya and Tanzania, originally associated with weddings and historically performed and watched only by women. Dancers wear light clothing with a belt at the waist for ease of hip-swaying movement that resembles Middle Eastern belly dance. It shares music and Swahili lyrics with taarab and is accompanied by msondo and chapuo drums (Wikipedia; Lingua Fonica).
insight: Once an exclusively female space, chakacha was adapted into Afropop in the late 20th century by groups such as Mombasa Roots, Safari Sound Band and Them Mushrooms.
notes: Not UNESCO. Distinct from taarab (already in catalog) though musically related.

name: Beni ngoma
tagline: Competitive Swahili coast brass-band parade dance.
profile: Beni (mbeni) is an indigenized East African brass-band music and competitive dance genre of the Swahili coast, based on a military drill performed to brass-band music. Developed in the colonial era, it appropriated symbols of colonial authority - military drill, uniforms and elaborate ranked hierarchies - while Africanizing the music and movement with Swahili songs and cross-rhythms. It spread from 1890s urban Swahili communities on the Kenyan coast across Tanzania as far as Malawi (Wikipedia; Terence Ranger).
insight: The word 'beni' derives from the English 'band'; documented by historian Terence Ranger, beni societies founded subordinate branches and carried out welfare functions, and the form remained popular for at least sixty years.

sources.title: Dance and Society in Eastern Africa 1890-1970: The Beni Ngoma

notes: Not UNESCO. Multi-country Swahili coast tradition.

name: Ngoma
tagline: East African drum-dance-celebration complex.
profile: Ngoma (Bantu for drum, dance and event) is the umbrella drum-and-dance complex of East and Southern Africa, most widespread in Tanzania, where it also denotes the celebration or ritual itself. Traditional forms feature prominent hip movements and a wide variety of instruments, especially the ngoma drums; some forms use only singing and stomping. Historically it functioned as interaction between elders and youth and accompanied rites of passage, healing and work (Wikipedia; academic sources).
insight: Banned as 'unchristian' during colonization, ngoma was strongly revived after independence under President Nyerere as a pillar of Tanzanian national identity, contrasted against Western dances in his 1962 inaugural address.
notes: Not UNESCO. Umbrella tradition. Do not confuse QID with the drum instrument item.

name: Bakisimba
tagline: Baganda royal court dance of intricate footwork.
profile: Bakisimba (Baakisiimba) is a traditional Baganda dance of the Buganda Kingdom of central Uganda that originated as a royal court dance, said to derive from the movements of an intoxicated Kabaka ('abaakisimba be bakiwoomya' - 'those who planted the bananas made it sweet'). It emphasizes intricate footwork and undulating waist movements by men and women to an ensemble of drums (mbuutu, nankasa, ngalabi), gourd rattles and cow-horn trumpets, and is performed at weddings, coronations and cultural celebrations (Wikipedia; Buganda Kingdom).
insight: Bakisimba is commonly attributed to the court of Kabaka Ssuuna II and is often performed in a suite with the faster Nankasa and Muwogola dances.
notes: Not UNESCO. The 'Buganda 2014 UNESCO' claim in source brief could not be verified. QID recommend spot-check.

name: Sande (Sowei) masked dance
region: Mende communities, Sierra Leone and western Liberia
tagline: Mende women's masked initiation dance, danced by women.
profile: The Sande (Bondo) society masquerade of the Mende of Sierra Leone and western Liberia features the sowei helmet mask - one of the only known African masking traditions in which the masks are danced by women. The ndoli jowei ('Sowei who dances'), a high-ranking female official, performs in the black-finished helmet mask and raffia costume at the ceremonies concluding the initiation of girls into adulthood and at Sande funerals, modeling ideal feminine grace, virtue and wisdom (Wikipedia; Smarthistory).
insight: The sowei mask embodies a spiritual force ('medicine') and serves as the society's teacher, healer and judge; among the Mende it is regarded merely as wood until danced with its full raffia costume and attendants.
notes: Not UNESCO. No dedicated single-dance Wikipedia article (covered within Masquerade in Mende culture); QID unconfirmed. Sacred women's initiation; respectful framing required.

name: Lobi funeral balafon dance
region: Southwestern Burkina Faso (and adjacent Ghana, Cote d'Ivoire)
tagline: Lobi xylophone-led communal funeral dance.
profile: Among the Lobi of southwestern Burkina Faso, funeral celebrations center on the gyil/balafon - a buzzing pentatonic wooden xylophone - accompanied by a calabash gourd drum. The balafon functions as a speech-surrogate that allows master musicians to communicate with both the living and the dead, while communal dancing, wailing and call-and-response mark mourning and social passage. The xylophone is regarded as the central ritual instrument of Lobi funerary life (Wikipedia; Library of Congress).
insight: Lobi funeral xylophone music has been documented in field recordings held by the Library of Congress; the gyil's role as the heart of mourning ritual connects it to broader West African xylophone-dance traditions.

sources.title: Library of Congress Audio (Lobi funeral xylophone)

notes: Not UNESCO. No standalone English Wikipedia article for the dance. QID unconfirmed. Funerary rite; respectful framing required.

name: Calypso (dance)
tagline: Hip-rolling Trinidadian dance of West African and French Creole roots.
profile: Calypso dance accompanies calypso music, rooted in West African traditions and French Creole influence in Trinidad and Tobago. Emphasis is on waist and hip movement to a 2/4 beat rather than footwork, with hip-rolling and thrusting, prominent during Carnival. The storytelling and satirical lyric tradition of calypso gave rise to the faster, party-oriented soca in the 1970s (Britannica; TT Newsday).
insight: Calypso's storytelling, satirical lyric tradition gave rise to the faster, party-oriented soca in the 1970s; the two are the dominant popular-music dance forms of Carnival in Trinidad and Tobago.

sources.title: The distinct elements of calypso, soca

notes: No dedicated English Wikipedia calypso dance article confirmed; QID left blank.

name: Bomba
tagline: Afro-Puerto Rican dance where the dancer leads the drummer.
profile: Bomba developed among enslaved West Africans on Puerto Rican sugar plantations, first documented in 1797. It is defined by a call-and-response dialogue in which the lead dancer's movements (piquetes) direct the lead drummer (subidor/primo), with women traditionally marking accents with a skirt. Sixteen rhythms exist; major centers include Loiza, Ponce, Mayaguez and Guayama (Wikipedia; Puerto Rican Cultural Center).
insight: Bomba is unique in that the dancer sets the rhythm for the musician rather than the reverse, a reversal of the usual drummer-dancer hierarchy.
notes: Preserved largely by the Cepeda (Santurce) and Ayala (Loiza) families. Not UNESCO-inscribed.

name: Plena
tagline: Narrative Afro-Puerto Rican 'sung newspaper' dance.
profile: Plena developed from bomba around the start of the 20th century in southern Puerto Rico (Ponce). Driven by pandereta hand drums, its narrative lyrics comment on news and topical events, earning it the nickname 'el periodico cantado' (the sung newspaper). It is danced socially and at festivals across Puerto Rico and the diaspora (Wikipedia; Smithsonian Folkways).
insight: Plena uses different instrumentation (panderetas) and a narrative lyric form distinct from bomba's percussion-led drummer-dancer dialogue.
notes: Often paired with bomba as 'bomba y plena.' Not UNESCO-inscribed.

name: Pols
region: Northern and eastern Norway (Rost, Trondheim, Roros)
tagline: Norwegian turning couple dance of the eastern valleys.
profile: The pols is a Norwegian bygdedans couple dance in 3/4, found in the north and east of Norway, considered a fast version of the Nordic polska. It is thought to have arrived with labor immigration to the Norwegian mining industry between the 16th and 18th centuries and is especially associated with Roros (rorospols), where the samdans (partner rotation) is central. Distinct from the springar found in the south and west (Society of Folk Dance Historians; Wikipedia).
insight: Per the Society of Folk Dance Historians, 'Just before 1900, almost every community had either Springar or Pols, but no community had both,' highlighting the geographic division of Norway's two main bygdedans traditions.
notes: Distinct from Swedish polska in catalog (slug polska-sweden). Not UNESCO-inscribed.

name: Pendet
era: Traditional temple offering; secular welcome form codified 1950s
tagline: Balinese floral-offering welcome dance.
profile: Pendet is a Balinese dance originally performed as a temple offering ritual (a form of bebalihan/wali), in which young women carry bowls (bokor) of flower petals and scatter them to welcome gods and guests. In the 1950s I Wayan Rindi and Ni Ketut Reneng codified a secular four-dancer welcome version; I Wayan Beratha expanded it to five dancers in 1961 (Wikipedia; Balinese dance sources).
insight: Pendet is distinct from Legong and Kecak - it is a participatory offering/welcome dance that, unlike sacred ritual dances, can be danced by anyone by imitation.

sources.title: Three genres of traditional dance in Bali

notes: Covered under UNESCO Three genres of traditional dance in Bali (RL/00617, 2015) within the wali/bebali category. Confirm category placement before finalizing UNESCO flag.

name: Joget
tagline: Lively Portuguese-derived Malay couple dance.
profile: Joget is a fast, social Malay couple dance traceable to the 16th century in Malacca, derived from Portuguese folk dances (Branjo and Farapeira/branyo). Performed in male-female pairs with playful, teasing hand and foot movements (partners never touch), it is a staple of weddings and festivals and is also called ronggeng or, in Malacca, chakunchak (Wikipedia; Esplanade Offstage).
insight: Joget is one of the 'adopted' Malay dance genres (with European roots), distinct from zapin, an 'adopted' genre with Arab/Hadhrami origins and Islamic devotional movement vocabulary.
notes: Verified distinct from zapin per ethnomusicological sources. Not UNESCO-inscribed.

name: Lezginka
region: North Caucasus (Lezgin, Avar, Chechen, Dagestani peoples)
tagline: Fast Caucasian warrior-and-eagle dance.
profile: The Lezginka is a folk dance common throughout the North Caucasus, named after the Lezgin people, in a fast 6/8 rhythm. It can be a solo male dance (often with a dagger or sword) imitating an eagle - falling to the knees, leaping, with sharp arm and body movements - or a non-touching couple dance in which the woman dances quietly as a 'swan.' Each Caucasian group has its own variant (Wikipedia; Britannica).
insight: Aram Khachaturian incorporated a Lezginka into his four-act ballet Gayane, first performed on 9 December 1942 by the Kirov Ballet in Perm; the dance is also foundational to Georgian, Azerbaijani and Armenian folk styles.
notes: Pan-Caucasian; Russian-language collective term for many regional dances. Not UNESCO-inscribed.

name: Liwa
region: Arabian Gulf / Eastern Arabia (UAE, Oman, Bahrain, Qatar, Kuwait)
tagline: Afro-Arabian circle dance of the Gulf coast.
profile: Liwa is a Khaleeji dance of African origin performed across Eastern Arabia, chiefly within communities descended from the Swahili Coast, as well as Baloch and Sheedi communities. Men form a circle around drummers while a mizmar (double-reed) player paces the center; dancers clap and pace, with singing in Swahili and Khaliji Arabic. It reflects Indian Ocean slave-trade and migration histories (Wikipedia; Fanack).
insight: Liwa retains two African features - its underlying rhythmic structure and Swahili-language refrains - reflecting Indian Ocean slave-trade and migration histories linking East Africa to the Gulf.
notes: Distinct from Al-Ayyala (UNESCO 2014). Not UNESCO-inscribed.

name: Govend
tagline: Mixed-gender Kurdish communal circle dance.
profile: Govend is the Kurdish communal hand-holding dance (also called halparke or dilan), performed in a circle, semicircle or line led by a sercopi who often waves a handkerchief. Danced to zurna and davul, it builds from slow to fast and is famously mixed-gender - men and women together - distinguishing it from many neighboring traditions (Wikipedia; The Kurdish Project).
insight: 'Halay' is the Turkish term for the same dance category; govend is its Kurdish equivalent and a potent symbol of Kurdish identity, at times suppressed by Turkish authorities during periods of cultural restriction.
notes: QID Q25663719 maps to 'Kurdish dance' (list-class article); a separate govend concept item Q110256218 may be preferable. Related to but ethnically distinct from Turkish halay.

name: Sharo
tagline: Fulani flogging rite-of-passage with dance and chant.
profile: Sharo (Fulfulde 'to flog') is a Fulani initiation festival of northern Nigeria in which bare-chested young men, escorted by young women and accompanied by drumming, flute (sarewa) and chant, endure public flogging without showing pain to prove their readiness for marriage and manhood. It is held during the dry-season guinea-corn harvest and at Eid al-Adha, with maiden dancers, minstrels and tricksters opening the week-long event (Wikipedia; The Guardian Nigeria).
insight: Contenders are expected to sing and dance while being flogged as a display of bravery; the festival is contested and has at times been officially discouraged by Nigerian authorities.
notes: Respectful ethnographic framing - sacred male initiation rite. Dance/chant is integral but secondary to the flogging ordeal. Not UNESCO-inscribed.

name: Dhaanto
region: Horn of Africa (Somalia and Somali region of Ethiopia)
tagline: Somali camel-gaited folk dance and song.
profile: Dhaanto is a Somali folk dance and song genre native to the Horn of Africa, traditionally attributed to the nomadic Ogaden clan and revived during the early-20th-century Dervish movement to raise soldiers' spirits, often sung on horseback. Dancers form lines and circles; the rhythm and bounce are said to mirror a camel's gait (Wikipedia; TPT Originals).
insight: Modern exponents such as Aar Maanta keep dhaanto current in the Somali diaspora, where it remains a unifying cultural marker across clan boundaries.
notes: No verified UNESCO inscription confirmed despite a suggested 2024 listing. Not UNESCO-inscribed.

name: Dinka jumping dance
tagline: Dinka courtship and celebration jumping dance.
profile: Among the Dinka of South Sudan, communal dances feature men performing high vertical leaps while women dance in a circle, often holding their arms up in the shape of cattle horns - reflecting the central place of cattle in Dinka life. The jumping dances are performed at weddings, harvest festivals and courtship gatherings, synchronized to drumming and song (secondary sources; Alamy documentation).
insight: Dinka young men's leaping serves as a display of physical prowess and endurance in courtship and celebration, closely tied to the community's cattle-centered worldview.
notes: No dedicated English Wikipedia article or confirmed QID. Flagged for eyes-on and primary ethnographic verification. Not UNESCO-inscribed.

name: Ura
tagline: Cook Islands storytelling drum dance.
profile: Ura is the principal traditional dance of the Cook Islands, usually performed by a woman who tells a story through hip, leg and hand gestures, accompanied by intense drumming from at least five drummers. Components include the ura pa'u (drum dance), kaparima (action songs) and korero (legends); men perform vigorous knee-flapping in a semi-crouch. Variants include the ura piani, ura rore (stilt), ura tairiri (fan), ura korare (spear) and ura rama (torch) (Wikipedia; Enjoy Cook Islands).
insight: Showcased at the Te Mire Ura 'Dancer of the Year' competition in Rarotonga, ura is considered the defining art form of Cook Islands identity.
notes: Not UNESCO-inscribed.

name: Te Buki (Kiribati dance)
tagline: Kiribati hip dance with heavy coconut-frond skirt.
profile: Te Buki is a women's dance of Kiribati that originated in the northern Gilbert Islands (Butaritari and Makin). Dancers wear a thick, heavy coconut-frond skirt (te kakoko, up to ~10 kg) and emphasize graceful hip movement while keeping the torso still. It belongs to the broader Kiribati dance tradition marked by outstretched arms and sudden bird-like head movements evoking the frigate bird (Wikipedia; Tourism Kiribati).
insight: Smiling while dancing, common in modern hula, is considered vulgar in Kiribati dance, which prizes storytelling, skill and endurance over entertainment.
notes: QID Q5215250 maps to 'Dance in Kiribati' (parent article). Not UNESCO-inscribed.

name: Beet (Marshallese dance)
tagline: Spanish-influenced Marshallese line and stick dance.
profile: Beet (also Jepta) is the traditional dance of the Marshall Islands, influenced by Spanish folk dances; men and women side-step in parallel lines, creating a complex rhythm. A related stick dance, the Jobwa (Jebwa), originating on Ebeju in Ujae, is performed only on very special occasions and enacts canoe-building and fishing themes with drums, sticks and chant (Wikipedia; Worldly Rise).
insight: Marshallese music is strongly vocal and uses a distinctive one-headed hourglass drum; the beet shows Spanish colonial influence in its line formation.
notes: No standalone Wikipedia article or confirmed QID. Documented within 'Music of the Marshall Islands.' Not UNESCO-inscribed.

name: Cueca
tagline: Chile's national handkerchief-waving rooster-and-hen courtship dance.
profile: The cueca is the national dance of Chile, a courtship dance in which partners wave handkerchiefs while tracing circular figures with turns and flourishes, mimicking the courtship of a rooster and hen. Danced in 6/8 or 3/4 to guitar, accordion and percussion, it was officially declared Chile's national dance by decree in 1979 and is central to the September Fiestas Patrias (Wikipedia; Britannica).
insight: The Chilean government officially declared the cueca the 'national dance of Chile' through Decree No. 23 of 6 November 1979; a solo protest variant, the cueca sola, was created in 1978 by relatives of the disappeared during Pinochet's dictatorship.
notes: QID covers the pan-Andean cueca family. A separate cueca-bolivia entry could follow. Not UNESCO-inscribed.

name: Wayang Wong
tagline: Javanese-Balinese court dance-drama enacting the Ramayana and Mahabharata.
profile: Wayang wong (wayang orang, 'human wayang') is a classical Javanese and Balinese dance-drama in which costumed human dancers replace the puppets of wayang kulit, enacting episodes of the Ramayana and Mahabharata to gamelan accompaniment with highly codified, stylized movement, dialogue and song. Centered in the Yogyakarta and Surakarta courts, it is considered an epitome of Javanese aesthetic unity (Wikipedia; Asian Traditional Theatre & Dance).
insight: A literary source from AD 930 refers to the 'wayang wwang' dance-drama as a form of wayang kulit performance with human dancers replacing puppets; the form was codified in the Javanese royal courts over subsequent centuries.
notes: Wayang puppet theatre is UNESCO-inscribed; wayang wong (the human dance-drama) is a distinct element NOT separately inscribed.

name: Reog Ponorogo
era: Centuries-old; mythic roots in the Bantarangin kingdom; UNESCO USL 2024
tagline: East Javanese theatrical dance with a giant peacock-tiger mask.
profile: Reog Ponorogo is a centuries-old theatrical dance from Ponorogo Regency in East Java that fuses dance, music and mythology, narrating the story of the Bantarangin Kingdom. Its iconic feature is the Dadak Merak - a massive tiger-head mask topped with peacock feathers, weighing roughly 50 kg, which the lead dancer balances using only his teeth. Performed at weddings, festivals, circumcisions and ceremonies (UNESCO; Jakarta Globe).
insight: UNESCO inscribed Reog Ponorogo in 2024 (Decision 19.COM 7.A.2) on the Urgent Safeguarding List (file USL/01969), noting that performances are dwindling and Reog masters are increasingly hard to find; it is Indonesia's 14th ICH element.

sources.title: Reog Ponorogo inscribed on UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage List

notes: UNESCO file USL/01969 (Urgent Safeguarding List, 2024, 19.COM). Task brief had incorrect file 02102 - CORRECTED.

name: Noh
tagline: Masked classical Japanese dance-theatre performed since the 14th century.
profile: Noh is a major form of classical Japanese dance-drama performed since the 14th century, integrating masks, ornate costumes and props in a slow, codified, dance-based performance accompanied by a hayashi ensemble and chant. Emotions are conveyed through stylized gestures, and supernatural protagonists narrate tales drawn from traditional literature. Together with comic kyogen it forms nogaku (Wikipedia; UNESCO).
insight: UNESCO inscribed 'Nogaku theatre' on the Representative List in 2008 (file RL/00012), originally proclaimed a Masterpiece in 2001; Japan designated nogaku an Important Intangible Cultural Property in 1957.
notes: UNESCO file RL/00012 (Nogaku theatre). Task brief had incorrect file 00009 - CORRECTED. Distinct from existing kabuki-japan.

name: Moresca
