tagline: Renaissance mock-combat dance of Moors versus Christians.
profile: The moresca (morisca, mauresque) is a Renaissance dance of exotic character that flourished across 15th-16th century Europe, typically staging a mock battle between Moors and Christians, with elements including blackened faces, bells on costumes and men disguised as fools. It appeared both as a solo dance and a group sword-combat dance and is etymologically and choreographically linked to the English Morris dance (Wikipedia; Britannica).
insight: In the 15th century the moresca was the most frequently mentioned dance type in literature; a noted example appears in Franco Zeffirelli's 1968 film Romeo and Juliet where it is performed at the Capulets' ball.
notes: Distinct from Croatian Moreska (moreska-croatia) and from the moresche song genre.

name: Volta (La Volta)
tagline: Scandalous Renaissance leaping couple dance with close-embrace lifts.
profile: The volta (la volta, lavolta) is a vigorous late-Renaissance couple dance, a variant of the galliard in triple time, whose signature figure is a close-embrace turn in which the man lifts and spins his partner using his thigh. Originating as a Provencal/Italian folk dance, it became a court favorite - reputedly of Elizabeth I - but was denounced by moralists as lewd for its intimate hold (Wikipedia; Britannica).
insight: Britannica notes the volta became 'respectable, but never completely dignified, after Queen Elizabeth I of England danced it with the earl of Leicester'; detailed instructions were recorded by Thoinot Arbeau in his 1589 Orchesographie.
notes: Wikidata item labeled 'Lavolta.' Survives as a folk dance in Provence.

name: Eisa
era: Centuries-old Obon dance; transformed in 20th century
tagline: Okinawan drum-dance honoring ancestors at the Obon festival.
profile: Eisa is a folk drum-dance from Okinawa Island derived from the Obon dance performed by young people to honor ancestral spirits. Danced by 20-30 performers in lines or circles, it combines singing, chanting and three types of taiko drums (odaiko, shimedaiko, paarankuu) with sanshin accompaniment, often led by a flag-bearing hatagashira. Modern eisa features brightly colored costumes and turbans (Wikipedia; Government of Japan).
insight: Eisa derives from nembutsu Buddhist chant-dances; after WWII the Okinawa City all-island contest (from 1956) drove more elaborate costumes and larger drum ensembles, and the competitive element was later abolished in favor of a festival format.
notes: Distinct from mainland Japanese Bon Odori. Also performed in Hawaii and mainland Japan by Okinawan diaspora. Not UNESCO-inscribed.

name: Pandanggo
era: Hispanic colonial period onward; Pandanggo sa Ilaw codified 1930s
tagline: Philippine fandango-derived folk dance, famed for balancing oil lamps.
profile: Pandanggo is a Philippine folk dance derived from the Spanish fandango, danced in a lively 3/4 with clapping. Its most famous version, Pandanggo sa Ilaw from Lubang Island, Mindoro, has dancers balance three lit oil lamps (tinghoy) - one on the head and one on the back of each hand - simulating fireflies at dusk. Many regional variants exist, and it is the base dance of the carinosa (Wikipedia; Tagalog Lang).
insight: According to Wikipedia, any dance considered jovial and lively was called pandanggo by the early 18th century; the music for Pandanggo sa Ilaw was composed by National Artist Col. Antonio R. Buenaventura in the early 1930s.
notes: Distinct from carinosa and tinikling, both already in catalog. Not UNESCO-inscribed.

name: Quadrille (Caribbean)
tagline: Africanized Caribbean adaptation of the European square dance.
profile: The Caribbean quadrille is the Africanized descendant of the European square dance for four couples, carried to the islands by the plantocracy in the 18th-19th centuries. In Jamaica it survives in 'ballroom' and 'camp' (longways) styles, accompanied by mento bands; Francophone islands dance the kwadril. Enslaved Africans reshaped its rigid figures with looser posture, hip movement and flamboyant footwork (Wikipedia; Government of Jamaica).
insight: Per Wikipedia, a quadrille derivative in the Francophone Lesser Antilles is known as kwadril; in Jamaica quadrille is a traditional folk dance done in two styles, ballroom and camp.

sources.title: Jamaica's Heritage in Dance and Music

notes: QID is for the general quadrille; the Caribbean form lacks a separate QID. Not UNESCO-inscribed.

name: Winti (Winti Prey)
tagline: Afro-Surinamese spirit-possession dance ritual of the Winti religion.
profile: Winti is an Afro-Surinamese religion whose central rite, the Winti Prey ('Winti play'), is a communal dance ceremony in which drumming, singing and circling dances induce spirit possession by deities (winti) of the earth, water, air and forest. Rooted in Akan, Fon and Kongo traditions brought by enslaved Africans, the dance was banned by Dutch law from 1874 to 1971 and survives as living Afro-Surinamese heritage (Wikipedia; Duke University).
insight: Practicing Winti was illegal in Suriname from 1874 until 1971; per Duke University's Sacred Arts of the Black Atlantic, 'Winti Prey are dance rituals in which Winti can manifest themselves by taking possession of participants in the ritual.'
notes: BORDERLINE: Winti is primarily a religion; included for its central Winti Prey dance ritual. Distinct from kaseko-french-guiana. Flagged for editorial review. Not UNESCO-inscribed.

name: Contradanza
tagline: Cuban country dance, ancestor of danzon, mambo and cha-cha-cha.
profile: The contradanza (contradanza criolla, danza) is the Cuban adaptation of the European contredanse that took root in late-18th and 19th-century Cuba, becoming the first written Cuban music to incorporate an African rhythmic pattern. A genteel figured group dance, it is the acknowledged progenitor of the danzon, mambo and cha-cha-cha, and is closely linked to the habanera (Wikipedia).
insight: Per Wikipedia, the Cuban contradanza became the first written music based on an African rhythm pattern and gave rise to the danzon, mambo and cha-cha-cha in the 19th-20th centuries.
notes: QID unconfirmed - blank per no-fabrication rule. Distinct from existing danzon. Not UNESCO-inscribed.

name: Fandango Jarocho
tagline: Veracruz community zapateado celebration on the wooden tarima.
profile: The fandango jarocho is the communal celebration at the heart of the son jarocho tradition of the Sotavento region of Veracruz, Mexico, where the community gathers around a raised wooden platform (tarima) and dancers perform percussive zapateado footwork as a living instrument alongside jarana guitars, requinto and harp. Blending Spanish, Indigenous and West African elements, it is a participatory call-and-response festivity (Smithsonian Folkways; Wikipedia).
insight: Per Smithsonian Folkways, in the fandango the dancers' zapateado footwork on the tarima functions 'as a kind of percussion, like a drum... a vital instrument in the traditional performance of son jarocho.'
notes: QID unconfirmed for the dance/fandango specifically; son jarocho music has its own article. Not UNESCO-inscribed.

name: Moreska
era: At least mid-17th century; medieval roots
tagline: Korcula sword dance of mock battle over a captured maiden.
profile: The Moreska is a traditional sword dance from the town of Korcula on the Croatian Adriatic island of the same name, an elaborate production in which two armies of dancers engage in a choreographed mock battle over the fate of a veiled young woman (the Bula). Rooted in the Spanish Reconquista Moros y cristianos tradition, the Korcula version is unique in using two swords per dancer across seven escalating dances (Wikipedia).
insight: The Korcula Moreska dates to at least the mid-17th century and is the only modern version using two swords held by each soldier; once performed rarely on St. Theodore's feast day, it is now staged weekly for tourists.
notes: Distinct from the broader Moresca (moresca). QID unconfirmed. Not UNESCO-inscribed.

name: Danza de los Voladores
tagline: Totonac flying-pole fertility ceremony of the calendar.
profile: The Danza de los Voladores ('Dance of the Flyers') is an ancient Mesoamerican fertility ritual still performed in Mexico, especially by the Totonac of Veracruz. Four men launch themselves tied by ropes from atop a pole up to 40 metres high, descending in spiraling flight while a fifth — the caporal — plays flute and drum on the platform. Each flyer circles 13 times, totaling 52 revolutions — the Mesoamerican calendar round (Wikipedia; UNESCO).
insight: UNESCO inscribed the ritual ceremony of the Voladores in 2009 (RL/00175) as the second Mexican ICH element so recognized; English Wikipedia notes a 30-metre pole while the UNESCO file specifies 18–40 metres.
notes: UNESCO RL/00175, inscribed 2009. Second Mexican element after 2008 Festivity of the Dead.

name: Candombe
tagline: Afro-Uruguayan drum-parade dance of Montevideo.
profile: Candombe is an Afro-Uruguayan music and dance tradition rooted in the drumming of enslaved Africans brought to Montevideo. Built on three barrel drums — chico, repique and piano — it is performed in street parades called llamadas, especially in the Sur, Palermo and Cordon neighborhoods. Iconic figures include the Mama Vieja, the Gramillero and the Escobero (Wikipedia; UNESCO).
insight: Each of the three core Montevideo neighborhoods has its own distinctive 'piano' drum beat, signaling local identity within the shared tradition; UNESCO inscribed Candombe in 2009 (RL/00182, 4.COM).

sources.title: Candombe and its socio-cultural space: a community practice

notes: UNESCO RL/00182, inscribed 2009 (4.COM). Distinct from Brazilian Candomble.

name: Landler
tagline: Alpine couples dance and forerunner of the waltz.
profile: The Landler is a European folk dance in 3/4 time from the Alpine regions of Austria, Bavaria, Switzerland and Slovenia. A turning partner dance featuring hopping, stamping and intricate arm-and-hand holds, it takes its name from the Landl district of Upper Austria. As dance halls grew in the 19th century it was made faster and more elegant, strongly influencing the development of the waltz (Wikipedia; Britannica).
insight: Beethoven, Schubert, Bruckner and Mahler all wrote Landler; Mahler replaced the traditional minuet with a Landler in several of his symphonies, giving them a rustic Austrian character.
notes: Not UNESCO-inscribed. Forerunner of the waltz.

name: Galliard
tagline: Vigorous Renaissance court dance of leaps and jumps.
profile: The galliard is a form of Renaissance dance and music popular across Europe in the 16th century, originating in 15th-century Italy. It consists of choreographed patterns, typically five steps per measure (the cinque passi), characterized by athletic leaps, jumps and hops ending in a 'cadence' landing. It was commonly performed as the lively afterdance to the stately pavane (Wikipedia; Britannica).
insight: Queen Elizabeth I was a celebrated galliard dancer; a 1589 courtier's report records that 'the Queene is so well as I assure you, six or seven galliards in a morning, besides musicke and singing, is her ordinairie exercise.'
notes: Pan-European Renaissance court dance. Coordinates approximate (Florence).

name: Pavane
tagline: Stately Renaissance processional court dance.
profile: The pavane is a slow processional dance common in 16th-century Europe, almost certainly of Italian origin (its name likely derives from padovana, 'of Padua'). A dignified couple dance adapted from the basse danse, it opened ceremonial balls and displayed elegant dress, with dancers advancing and retreating in 2/2 or 4/4 time. It was customarily paired with its vigorous afterdance, the galliard (Wikipedia; Britannica).
insight: The earliest known pavane music was published in Venice by Ottaviano Petrucci in Dalza's 1508 lute tablature; the form later inspired works by Faure (Pavane Op. 50) and Ravel (Pavane pour une infante defunte).
notes: Coordinates set to Padua, probable namesake. Not UNESCO-inscribed.

name: Apsara Dance
tagline: Cambodian celestial-nymph classical dance.
profile: Robam Tep Apsara is one of the signature dances of the Royal Ballet of Cambodia, performed by women in tight-fitting traditional dress whose graceful, codified gestures narrate classical myths. Created in the 1950s under Queen Sisowath Kossamak and inspired by the apsara bas-reliefs of Angkor Wat, it became a national symbol of Cambodia and is performed at ceremonies, festivals and cultural events worldwide (Wikipedia; UNESCO).
insight: Princess Norodom Buppha Devi, Kossamak's granddaughter, became the first professional apsara dancer of the modern era, performing internationally from age five; the tradition nearly died out under the Khmer Rouge but was revived after 1979.
notes: A repertory dance of the Royal Ballet of Cambodia, inscribed UNESCO Masterpiece 2003 (RL/00060). Not separately inscribed. Verify whether royal-ballet-cambodia entry already exists to avoid double-counting.

name: Cham Dance
region: Tibet / Himalayan region (shared: Bhutan, Mongolia, India)
tagline: Tibetan Buddhist masked monastic ritual dance.
profile: The cham dance is a sacred masked and costumed dance associated with Tibetan Buddhism, performed at monasteries across Tibet, Bhutan, Sikkim, Ladakh and Mongolia. Accompanied by monks playing long horns, oboes, cymbals and drums, dancers in elaborate deity masks and brocade robes perform movements that offer moral instruction, are considered a form of meditation, and often depict incidents from the life of Padmasambhava (Wikipedia; Samye Institute).
insight: The famous Black Hat (zhva nag) dance commemorates the 9th-century assassination of the anti-Buddhist king Langdarma by the monk Lhalung Pelgyi Dorje — considered a righteous act to preserve the Dharma.
notes: Trans-Himalayan tradition. Not itself UNESCO-inscribed (Drametse Ngacham in Bhutan IS inscribed — RL/00161, already in catalog). Sacred ritual — respectful framing required.

name: Yein
tagline: Burmese synchronized group dance.
profile: Yein is a form of group dance native to Myanmar in which multiple dancers move their heads, waists, feet and hands synchronously to the beats of a traditional Burmese hsaing waing orchestra. Historically known as nat aka ('spirit dance'), it is a celebrated feature of the annual Thingyan Water Festival, typically performed by uniformly dressed female troupes (Wikipedia; Burmese dance sources).
insight: Burmese classical dance was largely codified in the 1950s by dancer Oba Thaung at the State School of Music in Mandalay, after the court tradition ended; yein is now a staple of cultural diplomacy performed for visiting heads of state.
notes: Not UNESCO-inscribed. Coordinates set to Mandalay, center of Burmese classical dance.

name: Tammurriata
region: Campania (Vesuvius area, Agro Nocerino-Sarnese, Amalfi Coast)
era: Early modern period (c. 1700s); Marian devotional roots older
tagline: Campanian frame-drum dance of the seven Madonnas.
profile: The tammurriata is a Campanian folk dance driven by the tammorra, a large jingled frame drum. A couple dance with highly symbolic arm gestures, it is closely tied to the cult of the Seven Madonnas and Marian devotional festivals, where dancers circle and pair off around drummers and call-and-response singers. Distinct from the Apulian pizzica and the generic tarantella by its percussive style and sacred Marian context (Wikipedia; Visit Naples).
insight: The tammorra drum has jingles like a tambourine but is much larger; the dances are performed at night feasts (feste) in devotion to local Marian shrines across Campania, blurring the boundary between sacred and folk celebration.
notes: Sacred Marian devotional context. Not UNESCO-inscribed. Distinct from generic tarantella and from Apulian pizzica.

name: Lesnoto
era: Traditional; named by Janković collectors c. 1939
tagline: Macedonian line dance in asymmetric 7/8 meter.
profile: Lesnoto ('the easy/light one') is a Macedonian line dance in 7/8 meter following a three-steps-right, one-step-left pattern. Danced in a curved open line or circle with hands joined or in shoulder-hold, it is among the most widespread Balkan social dances; the term was applied by Yugoslav folk-dance collectors Ljuba and Danica Janković around 1939 and spread through the mid-20th century festival movement (Wikipedia; Society of Folk Dance Historians).
insight: The naming 'lesnoto' was an editorial choice by the Janković sisters in 1939 to organize Macedonian dances; before this the dance had many local names, and the category has since been contested by ethnographers.
notes: QID not confirmed — left blank. Distinct from kolo-serbia. Not UNESCO-inscribed.

name: Pasillo
tagline: Andean salon waltz-dance of melancholy and elegance.
profile: The pasillo is a 19th-century Andean salon dance and song genre derived from the European waltz, whose name ('little step') refers to its short dance steps. It spread from Colombia during the independence era to Ecuador, where the slow, melancholic Ecuadorian pasillo became a national genre; it is danced in pairs to guitar, mandolin and other string instruments (Wikipedia).
insight: The Ecuadorian pasillo is widely regarded as the national music and dance of Ecuador, associated with longing and melancholy; Julio Jaramillo, known as 'Nuestro Juanito,' became its most celebrated 20th-century interpreter.
notes: QID not confirmed — left blank. Shared Ecuador/Colombia. Not UNESCO-inscribed.

name: Tamunangue
tagline: Afro-Venezuelan sacred dance suite honoring Saint Anthony.
profile: Tamunangue, also called Sones de Negro, is an Afro-Venezuelan religious dance-and-music suite from Lara State performed each June 13 to honor Saint Anthony of Padua. Named for the tamunango drum, it comprises a sequence of distinct sones — La Batalla, La Bella, La Juruminga, El Poco a Poco, El Yiyivamos, La Perrendenga, El Galeron and El Seis Figureao — blending indigenous, Spanish and African elements (Wikipedia; Library of Congress).
insight: The Tamunangue was declared national cultural heritage of Venezuela by decree in 2014; the suite's eight sones each have distinct rhythms, movement vocabularies and social functions within the festival.
notes: QID not confirmed — left blank. National heritage Venezuela 2014. Sacred Marian devotion. Not UNESCO-inscribed.

name: Yakshagana
region: Coastal Karnataka (Dakshina Kannada, Udupi, Uttara Kannada)
tagline: Coastal Karnataka's all-night Ramayana dance-drama.
profile: Yakshagana is a traditional dusk-to-dawn dance-drama of coastal Karnataka combining elaborate costumes, vivid makeup, percussion-led music (himmela) and danced dialogue (mummela) to enact episodes from the Ramayana, Mahabharata and Puranas. Performers in towering headgear improvise dance and spoken debate guided by a bhagavata singer-narrator, making each performance unique (Wikipedia).
insight: Unlike most classical Indian dance-dramas, Yakshagana's dialogue is entirely improvised within the performance — the bhagavata sings the story cues and the actors improvise their spoken responses in verse.
notes: Not UNESCO-inscribed. Karnataka state intangible heritage. Sacred/devotional context — respectful framing.

name: Lavani
era: Documented from 18th-19th century; Peshwa era patronage
tagline: Maharashtra's fast-tempo folk song-and-dance of expressive power.
profile: Lavani is a powerful, fast-tempo folk song-and-dance genre of Maharashtra performed by women in nine-yard nauvari sarees to the beat of the dholki drum. Known for its rhythmic vigor and expressive, often sensual storytelling, it is closely associated with Tamasha folk theatre and was patronized during the Peshwa era as a form of entertainment and social commentary (Wikipedia).
insight: Lavani blends entertainment with social critique — its lyrics have historically challenged caste norms and gender expectations, making it a tool of marginalized Marathi communities even as it was patronized by Peshwa courts.
notes: Not UNESCO-inscribed. Maharashtra state intangible heritage.

name: Therukoothu
region: Northern Tamil Nadu (and Tamil Sri Lanka)
tagline: Tamil street theatre dance-drama of Draupadi devotion.
profile: Therukoothu ('street play') is a Tamil open-air street-theatre form of northern Tamil Nadu combining song, music, dance and ritual, performed overnight at village temple festivals with heavy costumes and bold makeup. Its repertoire centers on Mahabharata episodes, especially those concerning Draupadi, and it carries strong devotional and communal functions. Ritual performance (Kattaikkuttu) is distinct from more secular forms (Wikipedia).
insight: Therukoothu functions as both entertainment and sacred ritual — the Draupadi cult performances are understood as acts of devotion in which the deity may manifest through the dancer.
notes: Not UNESCO-inscribed. Sacred Draupadi ritual context — respectful framing.

name: Karagattam
tagline: Tamil pot-balancing folk dance of the rain goddess.
profile: Karagattam (Karakattam) is an ancient Tamil folk dance in which performers balance a decorated water pot (karagam) on the head while executing acrobatic footwork. Dedicated to the rain goddess Mariamman, it exists in two forms: Aatta Karagam, danced for entertainment, and Sakthi Karagam, performed as a temple ritual offering. Often accompanied by nadaswaram and thavil (Wikipedia).
insight: The balancing of the karagam pot — decorated with flowers and topped by a figurine — while performing complex footwork and acrobatics is considered a form of yogic discipline and devotional offering to the goddess.
notes: QID not confirmed — left blank. Sacred Mariamman ritual form. Not UNESCO-inscribed.

name: Barong dance
tagline: Sacred Balinese lion-spirit dance-drama of good versus evil.
profile: The Barong dance is a sacred masked Balinese dance-drama portraying the eternal struggle between good — embodied by the lion-like protective spirit Barong — and evil, embodied by the witch Rangda. Performances often culminate in the trance-based keris (kris) dagger sequence, in which entranced dancers turn blades on themselves unharmed, reflecting deep Balinese Hindu cosmology. Performed at temple ceremonies and during odalan festivals (Wikipedia; UNESCO).
insight: The Barong trance sequence is not theatrical but genuinely ritual — dancers entering a trance state are considered to be protected from the kris blades by the Barong spirit itself; high priests conduct purification rites afterwards.

sources.title: Three genres of traditional dance in Bali

notes: Inscribed within UNESCO RL/00617 'Three genres of traditional dance in Bali' (2015). Sacred trance ritual — respectful framing required.

name: Topeng
tagline: Balinese-Javanese masked dance-drama of kings and clowns.
profile: Topeng is an Indonesian masked dance-drama — most highly developed in Bali — in which dancers wear character masks to enact stories drawn from the Panji cycles and the Hindu epics, blending dance, music, mime and comedy. The sacred Balinese Topeng Sidhakarya/Topeng Pajegan form serves ritual functions and is included among the nine dances inscribed within UNESCO's 2015 'Three genres of traditional dance in Bali' (Wikipedia; UNESCO).
insight: Topeng Pajegan is performed by a single dancer who changes masks to portray all characters — kings, warriors, priests and clowns — in a complete ritual context; the final Sidhakarya mask is believed to bless a ceremony and may not be worn outside its sacred context.

sources.title: Three genres of traditional dance in Bali

notes: Inscribed within UNESCO RL/00617 'Three genres of traditional dance in Bali' (2015). Broad Topeng form not separately listed. Sacred ritual context.

name: Nora (Menora)
region: Southern Thailand (Nakhon Si Thammarat, Phatthalung, Trang)
tagline: Southern Thai bird-princess dance-drama with acrobatic grace.
profile: Nora (Menora/Manora) is a lively, acrobatic classical dance-drama of southern Thailand over five hundred years old, featuring bird-like costumes, beaded crowns, curled metal fingernails and improvisational singing. Performed at temple fairs and community rites and based on the Jataka tale of the half-bird princess Manohara, it was inscribed on the UNESCO Representative List of Intangible Cultural Heritage in 2021 (Wikipedia; UNESCO).
insight: The ritual Nora rong khru ceremony — performed by a trained Nora master to invite spirits of past Nora teachers into a dance performance — has strict protocols and is distinct from secular staged Nora, which may be enjoyed by all.

sources.title: Nora, dance drama in southern Thailand

notes: UNESCO RL/01587, inscribed 2021 (16.COM). Thailand's second ICH inscription after Khon (2018). Partial sacred context — Nora rong khru ceremony.

name: Bugaku
tagline: Ancient masked imperial court dance of Japan.
profile: Bugaku is the ceremonial court dance of the Japanese imperial household, performed for over twelve hundred years to the orchestral music called gagaku. Marked by slow, precise, regal movements and elaborate costumes and masks, it is divided into 'dances of the left' (togaku, Chinese-derived) and 'dances of the right' (komagaku, Korean-derived), and is among the oldest continuously performed dance traditions on earth (Wikipedia; Britannica).
insight: Bugaku has been transmitted within the Imperial Household Agency by hereditary families such as the Ono and Togi for roughly a thousand years; its parent tradition gagaku was inscribed by UNESCO in 2009.
notes: Not separately UNESCO-listed; parent tradition Gagaku inscribed 2009 (RL/00136). QID Q282337 is the dance, not the Dainichido Bugaku festival.

name: Kagura
tagline: Shinto ritual dance to entertain the gods.
profile: Kagura ('god-entertainment') is a Shinto theatrical dance with roots predating Noh, traditionally performed by shrine maidens (miko) to invoke and honor kami. The term contracts kami no kura ('seat of the gods'). It divides into mikagura (imperial court rites) and satokagura (folk forms), the latter spawning regional styles tied to the agricultural calendar across most of Japan (Wikipedia; Britannica).
insight: Per the Kojiki and Nihon Shoki, kagura derives from the myth of Ame-no-Uzume's dance that lured the sun goddess Amaterasu from her cave; the regional Hayachine Kagura (Iwate) was inscribed by UNESCO in 2009.
notes: Generic kagura not inscribed; Hayachine Kagura inscribed 2009. Sacred Shinto ritual - respectful framing.

name: Gwoka
tagline: Guadeloupean drum, song and dance of resistance.
profile: Gwoka unites responsorial Creole singing, rhythms played on the ka (a goatskin barrel drum), and improvisational dance representative of Guadeloupean identity. Originating among enslaved Africans, participants and public form a circle in which dancers enter in turn to face the drums, structured around seven fundamental rhythms at open-air lewoz gatherings (UNESCO; Wikipedia).
insight: Gwoka was inscribed on UNESCO's Representative List on 26 November 2014 (file 00991, 9.COM), the culmination of a grassroots campaign led by Felix Cotellon, president of Guadeloupe's centre for traditional music and dance.

sources.title: Gwoka: music, song, dance and cultural practice

notes: UNESCO RL/00991, inscribed 26 November 2014 (9.COM). Wikipedia article at /wiki/Gwo_ka.

name: Kizomba
tagline: Slow, sensual Angolan partner dance from semba.
profile: Kizomba is a social partner dance and music genre originating in Angola in the late 1970s-early 1980s, meaning 'party' in Kimbundu. It emerged as a fusion of Angolan semba with Antillean zouk, slowing the cadence and adding a stronger bassline. Danced in close embrace, leaders guide followers across the floor with elegant footwork and smooth body movement (Wikipedia; Embassy of Angola).
insight: Eduardo Paim, called the 'father of kizomba,' consolidated the genre with his band SOS; on 18 April 2024, Angola's Ministry of Culture declared Kizomba national intangible cultural heritage, citing its 'wide dissemination at an international level, as assets of Angolan identity.'
notes: Angolan national heritage since 18 April 2024. Not UNESCO-inscribed.

name: Kuduro
tagline: High-energy Angolan electronic street dance.
profile: Kuduro is an uptempo, energetic music and dance born in Luanda in the late 1980s, built on a fast four-to-the-floor beat with carnival, soca, zouk beton, house and techno influences. Performed in street circles where dancers take turns in the center, its movements incorporate pop-and-lock, breakdance and carnival steps, sometimes emulating disability in a performance of resilience (Wikipedia; NPR).
insight: Tony Amado coined the genre name in 1996 with his track 'Amba Kuduro'; per English Wikipedia the dance 'predated the music,' and Amado got the idea after seeing Jean-Claude Van Damme dancing in the 1989 film Kickboxer.

sources.title: Kuduro, The Dance That Keeps Angola Going

notes: Not UNESCO-inscribed.

name: Semba
tagline: Ancestral Angolan partner dance, parent of kizomba.
profile: Semba is a traditional Angolan music and partner-dance genre rooted in the older rhythm massemba, its name referring to a touch of belly buttons (umbigada). It rose in 1950s Luanda through groups like Ngola Ritmos, served as a voice of anti-colonial resistance, and is the acknowledged predecessor of kizomba, kuduro and a relative of Brazilian samba (Wikipedia; UNESCO ICH Angola page).
insight: Liceu Vieira Dias, 'the father of modern Angolan music,' founded N'gola Ritmos in 1947 and laid the foundations for modern semba; UNESCO has a 2026 Representative List nomination under processing for 'Semba: music and dance in Angola.'
notes: UNESCO 2026 nomination pending (eval through March 2027). Set unesco=true if inscribed.

name: Bikutsi
tagline: Beti women's foot-stamping dance of Cameroon.
profile: Bikutsi is a music-and-dance genre of the Beti/Ewondo people around Yaounde; the name means 'beat the earth,' referring to rhythmic foot-stamping. Built on an intense 6/8 rhythm, it began as a women's circle tradition for expressing joys and grievances and grew into one of Cameroon's leading popular styles, internationally popularized by Les Tetes Brulees from 1987 (Wikipedia; Africanews).
insight: Messi Me Nkonda Martin, frontman of Los Camaroes, is called 'the father of modern bikutsi' for translating the balafon's sound onto electric guitar by threading cotton cord between the strings.
notes: Female-led tradition. Not UNESCO-inscribed.

name: Assiko
tagline: Bassa guitar-driven popular dance of Cameroon.
profile: Assiko is a lively Cameroonian dance and music genre associated with the Bassa people, a local variant of palm-wine music that became popular after independence in 1960. Driven by guitar and percussion (often a bottle struck with metal), it features fast, intricate footwork and is performed at celebrations across the coastal and southern regions (Wikipedia).
insight: Jean Bikoko and Dikoume Bernard were among the key performers who popularized assiko as a guitar-based palm-wine style in post-independence Cameroon.
notes: Not UNESCO-inscribed.

name: Zouk
tagline: French Antillean party music and partner dance.
profile: Zouk is a music movement and dance pioneered by the band Kassav' in the early 1980s in Guadeloupe and Martinique. Original 'zouk beton' was a fast carnival style synthesizing kadans, konpa and biguine; it later spawned the slower 'zouk-love.' Danced belly-to-belly with small steps emphasizing the core, it became a global social dance and seeded Brazilian zouk (Wikipedia; Britannica).
insight: Kassav's 1984 hit 'Zouk-la se sel medikaman nou ni' established zouk as a viable Caribbean genre, and the band used Guadeloupean gwoka drums on early recordings, elevating an Afro-Caribbean tradition once disparaged as crude.
notes: Q227045 is the genre/dance. Not UNESCO-inscribed.

name: Strathspey
tagline: Slow, stately Scottish dance with Scotch snaps.
profile: The strathspey is one of the three dance types of Scottish country dancing, a slow, graceful form in 4/4 time characterized by the dotted 'Scotch snap' rhythm. Named for the valley (strath) of the River Spey, it uses a slower, more tempered travelling step and is traditionally followed by a faster reel to release rhythmic tension (Wikipedia; Britannica).
insight: The first mention of 'Strathspey' as a specific dance type is the anonymous Menzies manuscript of 1749; 18th-19th century fiddler-composers such as Niel Gow, William Marshall and James Scott Skinner ('the Strathspey King') built its repertoire.
notes: Called 'highlands' in Donegal tradition. Not UNESCO-inscribed.

name: Jig
tagline: Lively Irish folk dance in compound time.
profile: The jig is a lively folk dance, second in popularity only to the reel in Irish dance, typically in 6/8 compound metre (slip jigs in 9/8). Adopted in Ireland in the mid-16th century from earlier English forms, it has competitive variants - light jig, single/hop jig, slip jig and treble (double) jig - performed in soft or hard shoes with rapid footwork and a held torso (Wikipedia; Britannica).
insight: The term derives from Old English giga ('old dance'); it spread to France as the gigue and became fashionable at the court of Louis XIV, while remaining distinct from Irish stepdance as a tune-and-dance form.
notes: Distinct from irish-step-dance (catalog entry). Not UNESCO-inscribed.

name: Hornpipe
tagline: British and Irish solo sailor's dance.
profile: The hornpipe is a class of dance forms and tunes played in Britain and Ireland from the 16th century onward. Originally in triple metre, by the 18th century it shifted to common time. A 'sailor's hornpipe' subgenre emerged in which a soloist, dressed as Jack Tar, mimes shipboard actions - rope-hauling, climbing, saluting - with folded arms as a display of strength (Wikipedia; Royal Museums Greenwich).
insight: The Royal Navy adopted the hornpipe as a compact onboard exercise; Captain Cook ordered his men to dance it to maintain health, and Handel's 'Alla Hornpipe' from Water Music (1717) is among its most famous melodies.
notes: QID is the dance, not the instrument. Not UNESCO-inscribed.

name: Branle
tagline: Renaissance French swaying chain dance.
profile: The branle (anglicized 'brawl') is a French group chain dance adopted by European aristocracy c.1450-1650, named for its side-to-side swaying (branler, 'to sway'). Couples link arms or hands in a circle or serpentine line, alternating larger steps left with smaller steps right. Arbeau's Orchesographie (1589) documents over twenty regional types (Wikipedia; Britannica).
insight: According to Arbeau, every Renaissance ball began with the same four branles (double, single, gay, Burgundian); the branle de Poitou is cited as a possible ancestor of the minuet.
notes: Mentioned by Shakespeare in Love's Labour's Lost. Historical dance form.

name: Gavotte
tagline: French folk-turned-courtly dance in duple time.
profile: The gavotte is a French dance in moderate 4/4 or 2/2 time with a characteristic half-measure upbeat, beginning as the last of a suite of branles and becoming, at the court of Louis XIV, one of the optional dances of the classical baroque suite. It is named after the Gavot people of the Pays de Gap in Dauphine (Wikipedia; Britannica).
insight: In early courtly use the gavotte involved kissing, later replaced by the presentation of flowers; Bach, Lully, Rameau and Gluck all composed famous gavottes for court and orchestral use.
notes: Has Breton folk roots. Distinct from farandole. Not UNESCO-inscribed.

name: Minuet
tagline: Stately French courtly couple dance in triple time.
profile: The minuet is an elegant couple dance in moderate triple time that dominated aristocratic European ballrooms c.1650-1750, especially in France and England. Reputedly derived from the branle de Poitou, it used small steps (pas menus), formal bows, and floor patterns shaped like a figure-8 or the letter Z. It opened formal balls, performed by one ranking couple at a time (Wikipedia; Britannica).
insight: Jean-Baptiste Lully introduced refined minuets to opera - composing no fewer than 92 - and the minuet was the only baroque dance form not to become obsolete in the classical period, surviving as the third movement of the symphony.
notes: Not UNESCO-inscribed. Historical court/baroque dance form.

name: Allemande
tagline: Renaissance processional couple dance of German origin.
profile: The allemande ('German dance') is a Renaissance and baroque dance in moderate duple metre with a characteristic double-knocking upbeat. Dancers formed a line of couples who took hands and processed the length of the room, walking three steps then balancing. It became one of the most common instrumental dance styles of baroque music and the standard opening movement of the dance suite (Wikipedia; Britannica).
insight: Though apparently German in origin, no German dance instructions survive from its era; the first records come from French dancing master Thoinot Arbeau (1589) and the British Inns of Court, and its close-embrace turns were later carried into square and contra dance.
notes: A later triple-time 'German dance' of the Mozart/Beethoven era anticipated the waltz.

name: Sarabande
tagline: Spanish dance turned slow stately baroque movement.
profile: The sarabande is a triple-metre dance first known in 16th-century Mexico and Spain as the zarabanda - a wild, erotic dance banned in Spain in 1583. It survived in fast form in Spain and Italy, but in France and Germany became slow and stately, often emphasizing the second beat, and entered the baroque suite as one of its four standard dances (Wikipedia).
insight: The sarabande was considered so lascivious it was outlawed under Philip II in 1583, yet by the baroque era it had been transformed into one of the most dignified and introspective movements of the instrumental suite.
notes: Latin American origin debated. Not UNESCO-inscribed.

name: Chaconne
tagline: Fiery Spanish dance and baroque variation form.
profile: The chaconne (Spanish chacona) began around 1600 as a lively, sensual dance-song in the Spanish colonies and Spain, performed with castanets. It crossed to Italy and France where it slowed into a noble triple-metre form built on a repeating bass/harmonic pattern, becoming a major baroque musical form for variations such as Bach's famous solo-violin Chaconne (Wikipedia).
insight: Like the sarabande, the early chacona was regarded as scandalous and associated with the New World before being refined into a stately courtly dance and variation form in 17th-century France.
notes: Often paired with passacaglia. Not UNESCO-inscribed.

name: Varsovienne
tagline: Slow mazurka-derived 19th-century ballroom dance.
profile: The varsovienne is a slow, graceful couple dance that emerged in the mid-19th century, named for Warsaw (varsovienne = 'of Warsaw'). In 3/4 time, it combines elements of the mazurka and waltz/polka, with a characteristic pause and pointing of the foot, and spread widely through European and American ballrooms, surviving today in folk-dance repertoires (Wikipedia; Britannica).
insight: The dance became internationally fashionable in the 1850s and persists in traditional Cajun, Scandinavian and American folk-dance traditions, often danced to the tune 'Put Your Little Foot.'
notes: QID Q2034187 is the dance, not the Delavigne song. Not UNESCO-inscribed.

name: Bullerengue
region: Caribbean coast of Colombia and Panama's Darien
tagline: Afro-Colombian women-led sung dance of the Caribbean coast.
profile: Bullerengue is a traditional Afro-Colombian 'baile cantado' (sung dance) from Colombia's Caribbean coast and Panama's Darien, developed in palenques (Maroon communities) and preserved largely by elder women (cantadoras). Performed in a circle with call-and-response singing, handclaps and artisan drums (tambor alegre and llamador), women dance with dignified skirt movements; sub-styles include bullerengue sentao, chalupa and fandango de lengua (Wikipedia; World Music Central).
insight: Colombia recognized bullerengue (with its sentao, chalupa and fandango de lengua rhythms), mapale, son de negro and danza del congo as national Patrimonio Cultural Inmaterial via Ley 2520, signed 4 August 2025; singer Petrona Martinez ('La Reina del Bullerengue') earned a Latin Grammy that raised the genre internationally.
notes: Colombian national heritage (Ley 2520/2025). Not UNESCO-inscribed. Also practiced in Panama's Darien.

name: Sanam
tagline: Improvised Uyghur social dance of Xinjiang.
profile: Sanam (Chinese sainaimu) is an ethnic music-and-dance widely performed among the Uyghur people of Xinjiang at weddings, festivals and parties. Danced in male-female pairs with no fixed patterns, it features characteristic neck slides and movements of the elbows, eyes and fingers, with improvisation following the music as singers and dancers remain separated; tempo typically builds from slow to a lively allegro (Wikipedia).
insight: Sanam is one of several Uyghur dance forms (alongside Dolan, Sama and Nazirkom) related to the Xinjiang Uyghur Muqam, which UNESCO inscribed on its Representative List in 2008 (RL/00109).
notes: Sanam itself not UNESCO-inscribed; parent Uyghur Muqam is (RL/00109, 2008).

name: Anyeint
tagline: Burmese solo dance and comedy variety performance.
profile: Anyeint is a traditional Burmese performance form combining a solo female dancer's graceful dance with music, song, and interludes of comedy by male clowns (lubyet). Distinct from the group yein dance, it is a variety-show entertainment historically performed at festivals and royal/village occasions, blending refined classical dance with topical humor (Wikipedia).
insight: Anyeint is verifiable on Wikipedia and Wikidata (Q4778246) but is NOT inscribed on any UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage list; it is distinct from the group yein and from the dramatic zat-pwe.
notes: Not UNESCO-inscribed. Wikipedia spelling is 'Anyeint.' Distinct from yein (group) and zat-pwe (drama).

name: Kolam
tagline: Sri Lankan masked dance-drama of social satire.
profile: Kolam is a 'low-country' masked dance-drama from Sri Lanka's southern coast, in which masked performers enact a loose plot binding stock characters - kings, officials, animals, devils - often based on a Buddhist Jataka story. It blends ritual origins tied to pregnancy/fertility with humor, social satire and elaborate hand-carved kadura-wood masks (Wikipedia; Asian Traditional Theatre & Dance).
insight: The tradition's origin legend describes a pregnant queen's craving for a masked dance; scholars suggest kolam evolved from the Sanni Yakuma healing ritual into independent folk theatre, and the dancers historically came from the southern Karawa fisher community.
notes: No dedicated English Wikipedia article or QID. Covered within 'Dance in Sri Lanka.' Not UNESCO-inscribed.

name: Samba de Roda
tagline: Afro-Brazilian circle dance that seeded urban samba.
profile: A festive Afro-Brazilian practice combining music, dance and poetry that developed in the Reconcavo of Bahia, drawing on dances of enslaved Africans and Portuguese elements. Performers form a circle (roda); women take turns in the centre, and the umbigada (belly bump) invites the next dancer. It influenced the urban samba that became a national symbol (UNESCO; Wikipedia).
insight: Migrants carried samba de roda to Rio de Janeiro, where it shaped the urban samba now emblematic of Brazil; UNESCO noted it was originally 'proclaimed a Masterpiece' in 2005 and formally inscribed in 2008.

sources.title: Samba de Roda of the Reconcavo of Bahia

notes: UNESCO RL/00101, proclaimed 2005, inscribed 2008 (3.COM). Possible Wikidata duplicate Q50923081 to reconcile on merge.

name: Theyyam
tagline: Trance ritual where dancers embody Kerala deities.
profile: A ritual dance-worship form of North Malabar in which costumed male performers embody deities, ancestors and heroes, becoming living vessels of the divine. Performances feature towering headdresses, elaborate red face-painting, drumming and trance, often lasting many hours through the night, and culminate in the performer blessing devotees. Nearly 400 different Theyyams are documented (Wikipedia; Kerala Tourism).
insight: Lower-caste performers are venerated as gods for the ritual's duration, temporarily inverting the usual social hierarchy; the tradition is sometimes called 'the dance of the gods.'

sources.title: Theyyam - A ritual art popular in north Kerala

notes: NOT UNESCO-inscribed. A widely circulated claim that Theyyam is UNESCO-listed is contradicted by the official UNESCO India list (16 elements). Sacred ritual - respectful framing required.

name: Guerewol
region: Sahel / In-Gall area, Niger (also Chad, Cameroon)
tagline: Wodaabe male beauty pageant danced for women.
profile: An annual courtship competition among the Wodaabe Fula in which ornamented young men in elaborate face paint line up to dance and sing the yaake, rolling their eyes and baring white teeth to display beauty. Marriageable young women judge and select winners. It coincides with end-of-rainy-season clan gatherings, most famously at In-Gall alongside the Cure Salee festival (Wikipedia; BBC).
insight: A rare courtship ritual reversing the usual gender script - men compete to be chosen by women; the Wodaabe consider charm, beauty and magnetism (togu) the highest male virtues.

sources.title: The Male beauty contest judged by Women

notes: Primarily Niger Wodaabe tradition; also practiced in Chad, Cameroon, Nigeria. Not UNESCO-inscribed.

name: Ciranda
tagline: Slow Pernambuco beach circle dance for all.
profile: A traditional circle dance and song form of coastal Pernambuco, performed by participants holding hands in a large roda on beaches and town squares, moving to a slow, steady pulse. A lead singer (mestre cirandeiro) trades call-and-response verses with the chorus over zabumba, ganza and other percussion. Anyone may join or leave the ever-open circle (Encyclopedia.com; TeachRock).
insight: Lia de Itamaraca, the most famous living exponent, was declared Pernambuco state heritage; the open circle embodies its democratic ethos.

sources.title: Ciranda: The Brazilian Music and Dance that Creates Community

notes: No English Wikipedia article; anchored to Portuguese Wikipedia via shared QID Q504476. Not UNESCO-inscribed.

name: Coco
tagline: Afro-Brazilian stomping dance born in the quilombos.
profile: An African-influenced song-and-dance rhythm of northern and northeastern Brazil, marked by stomping footwork (often in wooden clogs), call-and-response singing and percussion (ganza, surdo, pandeiro, triangle). Said to derive from the rhythmic breaking of coconuts by enslaved Africans of Palmares, it takes many regional names such as coco de ganza and coco de roda (Wikipedia; Encyclopedia.com).
insight: One of the few Brazilian forms framed as a dance of resistance, born in quilombo (runaway-slave) communities of Pernambuco and Alagoas.
notes: Wikipedia article framed as 'Coco (music)'; the dance is the stomping style performed to it. Not UNESCO-inscribed.

name: Lambada
tagline: Sensual 1980s couple dance, the 'forbidden dance'.
profile: A fast partner dance that took recognizable form in Porto Seguro, Bahia, descending from Para's carimbo and blending forro, merengue, maxixe and Caribbean influences. Danced with arched legs, side-to-side steps and pronounced hip motion, it became a global craze after the French-Brazilian group Kaoma's 1989 hit. Its short skirts and close contact earned it the 'forbidden dance' label (Wikipedia).
insight: Kaoma's 'Lambada' sold five million copies worldwide in 1989 alone per the New York Times; after the early-1990s fad faded, Lambada evolved into today's Brazilian Zouk.
notes: Music originated in Belem, Para; dance crystallized in Porto Seguro, Bahia. Not UNESCO-inscribed.

name: Reel
tagline: Celtic figure dance of weaving lines and setting.
profile: A lively folk dance and tune type of Scottish origin, one of the four traditional Scottish country dances alongside jig, strathspey and waltz. Dancers alternate traveling figures (weaving 'reels of three') with setting steps danced in place, to quick 2/2 or 4/4 music. Introduced to Ireland in the late 18th century, it became central to Irish step and social dance (Wikipedia; Britannica).
insight: Distinguished from the hornpipe by its even quaver flow; the earliest reference is a 1590 Scottish witchcraft trial in which accused participants danced a reel around a churchyard.
notes: Distinct from jig and strathspey (already in catalog). Not UNESCO-inscribed.

name: Tarantella
tagline: Whirling southern Italian dance tied to spider-bite cure.
profile: A family of fast southern Italian folk dances in 6/8 time accompanied by tambourines, historically linked to tarantism - a hysterical condition attributed to the bite of the Lycosa tarantula wolf spider, supposedly cured by frenzied dancing. It exists as both a curative solo and a graceful couple courtship dance, with regional variants including Campania's tammurriata and Salento's pizzica (Wikipedia; Britannica).
insight: The tarantella is the parent umbrella form whose regional offshoots (pizzica, tammurriata, saltarello) the catalog already holds separately.
notes: QID Q324617 is the dance form. Decoy items exist (Stravinsky composition; a Calabrian variant). Not UNESCO-inscribed.

name: Estampie
tagline: Earliest surviving instrumental dance of medieval Europe.
profile: A medieval dance and musical form popular in the 13th and 14th centuries, built from a succession of repeated sections (puncta) with open (apertum) and closed (clausum) endings. Among the earliest written instrumental music, it survives in French and Italian (istanpitta) manuscripts. Whether it was actually danced is debated, though the name (from estampir, 'to resound/stamp') and strong rhythm suggest a lively couple form (Wikipedia; Britannica).
insight: The earliest named estampie is tied to troubadour Raimbaut de Vaqueiras's 'Kalenda maya'; scholars dispute whether the form was danced at all, as no choreography survives.
notes: QID Q1369726 is the dance form; Q530251 is a band named Estampie. Not UNESCO-inscribed.

name: Basse danse
tagline: Stately Burgundian 'low dance' precursor to the pavane.
profile: A dignified processional court dance of the 15th and early 16th centuries, especially at the Burgundian court, in which couples glide gracefully without lifting the feet ('low' dance, versus leaping 'high' dances). A precursor of the pavane, it was often paired with a quick tourdion. Its choreography survives in luxury sources such as the gold-and-silver-ink Brussels Manuscript of Margaret of Austria (Wikipedia; Library of Congress).
insight: Documented in the earliest printed dance manual (Toulouze, c. 1496) and the Library of Congress holds French dance-instruction manuals tracing this form's Burgundian codification.

sources.title: Burgundian Dance in the Late Middle Ages

notes: Earliest record is a 1320s Occitan poem. Not UNESCO-inscribed.

name: Cotillion
region: France; spread to Britain and North America
tagline: Four-couple figure dance, ancestor of the quadrille.
profile: An 18th-century social dance for four couples in a square, a courtly version of English country dance featuring 'changes' alternating with a unique 'figure.' It was a favored ball finale for some fifty years before the quadrille eclipsed it around 1820. In the US, quadrilles were called cotillions into the 1840s; the later 'German' cotillion added games and gift-giving (Wikipedia; Britannica).
insight: Standardized by Pierre de La Cuisse's 1762 manual Le Repertoire des bals; introduced to England by 1766 and America around 1772.
notes: Direct ancestor of both the quadrille and the American square dance. Not UNESCO-inscribed.

name: Schottische
region: Bohemia; spread across Europe and the Americas
tagline: Victorian couple dance with a misleadingly Scottish name.
profile: A partnered country dance of apparent Bohemian origin, popular in Victorian ballrooms, combining a polka-like slide-close-slide-hop with a hopped pivoting turn in closed position. Despite the German name meaning 'Scottish,' it has no direct Scottish origin. It seeded countless folk variants worldwide - Norwegian reinlender, Swedish schottis, Finnish jenkka, and Brazilian xote (Wikipedia).
insight: Its Brazilian descendant the xote became a core forró rhythm of the Northeast; first appeared in dance manuals in the 1850s.
notes: No schottische entry was previously in catalog. Not UNESCO-inscribed.

name: Rigaudon
tagline: Sprightly Provencal court dance in lively duple time.
profile: A French Baroque dance in lively duple metre, rhythmically simpler than the bourree with regular eight-bar phrases. It originated as a 17th-century folk dance for couples in southern France's Vivarais, Languedoc, Dauphine and Provence, and became a fashionable court dance under Louis XIV, with hopping steps performed at French and English courts through the 18th century (Wikipedia; Britannica).
insight: Tradition credits a Marseille dance-master named Rigaud with introducing it to Paris around 1630; Rameau and Purcell both composed rigaudons for stage and keyboard.
notes: Distinct from gavotte (simpler, duple-metre, southern folk origin). Not UNESCO-inscribed.

name: Passepied
tagline: Brisk triple-time Breton branle turned court dance.
profile: A brisk French court dance originating as a Breton branle, adapted to courtly use in the 16th century and frequent in 18th-century French opera and ballet, especially pastoral scenes, and in Baroque instrumental suites. First mentioned by Noel du Fail in 1548 as common at Breton courts, it began as a fast duple-time dance before becoming a lively triple-metre form. Its name ('pass-foot') describes a characteristic crossing step (Wikipedia; Britannica).
insight: Debussy's celebrated 'Passepied' from the Suite bergamasque shows the form's continued influence three centuries after its heyday.
notes: Rabelais and Arbeau identify it as a Breton branle type. Not UNESCO-inscribed.

name: Bhavai
tagline: Rajasthani pot-balancing dance on sword and glass.
profile: A solo folk dance of Rajasthan in which veiled performers balance a stack of earthen pots or brass pitchers on the head while dancing nimbly, pirouetting and swaying with the soles of the feet perched on a glass rim, a sword edge or a brass plate. Accompanied by male singers and instruments such as pakhawaj, dholak, sarangi and harmonium, it is thought to derive from women's desert water-carrying skills (Wikipedia).
insight: Expert dancers balance up to 22 brass/metal pitchers per Wikipedia; even slight asymmetry ends the performance.
notes: Distinct from the Gujarati Bhavai folk-theatre form. Not UNESCO-inscribed.

name: Fandango
tagline: Lively Spanish courtship dance in triple time.
profile: A lively 18th-century Spanish courtship dance and song genre in triple metre, accompanied by guitars, castanets and hand-clapping, traditionally danced by couples who begin slowly and accelerate. The earliest known fandango melody appears in a 1705 guitar book. It developed numerous regional styles such as fandangos de Huelva and Malaga's verdiales, and became a flamenco palo (Wikipedia).
insight: A foundational Iberian form whose regional offshoots feed both folk repertoire and flamenco; the earliest dance description is in a 1712 letter.
notes: Spanish 'bien de interes cultural' (national heritage) — NOT UNESCO. No slug 'fandango' was previously in catalog.

name: Gar (Tibetan court dance)
tagline: Ceremonial Tibetan court dance distinct from masked cham.
profile: A ceremonial Tibetan court dance-and-music tradition formally established in the Tibetan courts in the 17th century under the Fifth Dalai Lama, performed for the Dalai Lama's court and at New Year and enthronements. Its songs (garlu) and dances (gar) were performed by a troupe of boys (gartukpa) in brocade and headgear carrying small axes, kettle drums and flutes; variants include axe, drum, sword and bare-handed dances (Phayul; Great Tibet Tour).
insight: Unlike the masked religious cham, gar is an esoteric courtly form barely known even within Tibetan communities.

sources.title: Gartuk and Garpa - classic Tibetan court songs

notes: No standalone English Wikipedia article or confirmed Wikidata QID. Genuinely distinct from cham. HOLD until a primary encyclopedic anchor is confirmed.

name: Ryukyu Buyo (Ryukyuan classical dance)
tagline: Okinawan classical court dance, distinct from Eisa.
profile: The traditional Okinawan dance tradition that originated as refined court entertainment performed for visiting Chinese imperial envoys during the Ryukyu Kingdom, now categorized into classical (koten), popular/folk (zo) and creative styles, accompanied by sanshin, koto, flute and drums. Distinct from the folk drum-dance Eisa and from the Kumi Odori dance-drama, focusing on solo and ensemble pure-dance pieces (Wikidata; National Theatre Okinawa).
insight: Designated a Japanese Important Intangible Cultural Property in 2009 but, unlike Kumi Odori, not UNESCO-inscribed; only Japanese and Indonesian Wikipedia sitelinks exist.
notes: No dedicated English Wikipedia article (Q11573693 has only Japanese/Indonesian sitelinks). Japanese Important Intangible Cultural Property 2009. Not UNESCO-inscribed. HOLD for eyes-on.

name: Viennese Waltz
era: Second half of the 18th century to present
tagline: Fast-spinning Viennese rotary waltz of imperial ballrooms.
profile: The Viennese Waltz (Wiener Walzer) is the original form of the waltz, danced at roughly 180 bpm in continuous natural and reverse turns, roughly twice the tempo of the slow ballroom waltz. Developed in Viennese and Bavarian ballrooms from the Landler, it was the dominant European ballroom dance from the early 19th century and remains one of the five International Standard competitive ballroom dances. Popularized by Josef Lanner and the Strauss dynasty (Wikipedia; Vienna.info).
insight: 'The Viennese Waltz – Played, Danced, and Sung' was inscribed on Austria's National Inventory of Intangible Cultural Heritage in 2017 (Austrian UNESCO Commission, ID 801); it is NOT on the UNESCO Representative List.
notes: Austrian national ICH inventory 2017. NOT UNESCO Representative List. Distinct from slow/English waltz (already in catalog as 'waltz').

name: Foxtrot
tagline: Smooth American ballroom dance of long gliding steps.
profile: The Foxtrot is a smooth, progressive partner dance in 4/4 time featuring long walking steps in slow-quick-quick timing, originating in 1914 when vaudeville performer Harry Fox performed trotting steps at the Jardin de Danse atop the New York Theatre. Vernon and Irene Castle popularized it; James Reese Europe led the orchestra for their 'Castle Doggy-Fox Trot.' It is one of the five International Standard ballroom dances (Wikipedia; Britannica).
insight: Per Britannica, the foxtrot's rise eclipsed most other dances; its slower modern form ('slow foxtrot') split from the fast version, which became the quickstep, in the late 1920s.
notes: Not UNESCO-inscribed. One of five WDSF/WDSF International Standard dances.

name: Quickstep
tagline: Fast, light ballroom dance of hops, runs and chassés.
profile: The Quickstep is a fast, light progressive ballroom dance in 4/4 time that evolved from the fast foxtrot and Charleston in the late 1920s and was standardized in 1927. Characterized by rapid footwork, chassés, quarter turns, hops and runs, it conveys an exuberant quality and is one of the five International Standard competitive ballroom dances (Wikipedia; Imperial Society of Teachers of Dancing).
insight: The quickstep crystallized when the fast foxtrot was separated into two distinct dances; it absorbed Charleston-influenced hops and was formally codified at the 1927 Star Championships in Britain.
notes: Not UNESCO-inscribed. Standardized 1927 in Britain.

name: Charleston
region: Charleston, South Carolina; Harlem, New York
era: Peak 1926-1927; roots in Gullah/African American communities
tagline: Jazz Age flapper dance with kicking feet and swinging arms.
profile: The Charleston is a jazz dance popularized by James P. Johnson's 1923 tune and the Broadway show Runnin' Wild, featuring rapid side-kicks, swinging arms and the characteristic toes-in, heels-out footwork. It became the signature dance of the Jazz Age and the flapper era, performed both solo and with partners. Per Britannica, it was 'performed by both White and Black patrons at Harlem's dance clubs' and its popularity peaked between mid-1926 and 1927 (Britannica; Wikipedia).
insight: The Charleston's rhythmic DNA derives from the Juba/Ring Shout traditions of African American communities in the Sea Islands of South Carolina; Josephine Baker brought it international fame.
notes: Not UNESCO-inscribed. Peak popularity 1926-1927 before Lindy Hop superseded it.

name: Lindy Hop
tagline: Harlem swing dance, ancestor of all swing dance styles.
profile: The Lindy Hop is the original swing dance born in Harlem's Savoy Ballroom in 1928, combining Charleston footwork with a new breakaway (open position), creating the eight-count swingout that defines it. Innovated by George 'Shorty' Snowden and brought to its peak by Frankie Manning and Whitey's Lindy Hoppers, it influenced jive, East Coast Swing, West Coast Swing and jitterbug. It was revived by Swedish and American dancers in the 1980s and is now danced worldwide (Wikipedia).
insight: Per Wikipedia, the name references Charles Lindbergh's 1927 transatlantic flight ('Lindy' + 'hop'); Frankie Manning, who popularized air steps, was awarded the National Heritage Fellowship in 2009 at age 94.
notes: Not UNESCO-inscribed. Savoy Ballroom, Harlem. Revived from the 1980s; global scene today.

name: Tap dance
era: Roots 17th-19th century; popular from mid-19th century to present
tagline: Percussive American dance where shoes are the instrument.
profile: Tap dance is a form of American theatrical dance in which metal-plated shoes strike the floor to create rhythm, fusing African American Juba/Ring Shout percussive traditions with Irish jig footwork. From minstrel and vaudeville stages it evolved through the work of Master Juba (William Henry Lane), Bill 'Bojangles' Robinson, the Nicholas Brothers and Savion Glover into a concert art form. US Congress designated May 25 as National Tap Dance Day (Wikipedia).
insight: Master Juba, born around 1825, was the first documented star of tap dance; he toured England in 1848-1849 and was praised by Charles Dickens as 'the greatest dancer known.'

sources.title: From Margins to Mainstream: A Brief Tap Dance History

notes: Not UNESCO-inscribed. National Tap Dance Day: May 25 (US Congress).

name: Breaking (B-boying)
tagline: Bronx hip-hop street dance of power moves and freezes.
profile: Breaking (b-boying/b-girling) is a hip-hop street dance born at block parties in the South Bronx around 1970-1973, pioneered by DJ Kool Herc who isolated and looped the percussion 'break' sections of funk and soul records. Dancers compete in battles through four elements: toprock, downrock/footwork, power moves (windmills, headspins) and freezes. Breaking debuted as an Olympic sport at the Paris 2024 Games, with inaugural golds to Japan's B-Girl Ami (Ami Yuasa) and Canada's B-Boy Phil Wizard (Wikipedia; Red Bull).
insight: DJ Kool Herc created the 'merry-go-round' technique — switching between two copies of the same record to extend the break — which gave b-boys and b-girls an endless foundation to dance to; Rock Steady Crew brought breaking from the Bronx to Manhattan and then worldwide.
notes: Not UNESCO-inscribed. Olympic debut Paris 2024; not included for LA 2028. Practitioner-preferred term is 'breaking.'

name: Butoh
tagline: Japanese avant-garde dance of darkness and transformation.
profile: Butoh (ankoku buyo, 'dance of utter darkness') is a Japanese avant-garde performance dance founded by Tatsumi Hijikata and Kazuo Ohno in 1959 as a radical rejection of Western ballet and traditional Japanese dance. Characterized by white body paint, grotesque or contorted slow-motion movement and intense attention to bodily transformation, it emerged from postwar trauma and Dadaist-Expressionist influences and became internationally performed (Wikipedia).
insight: Hijikata's 1959 performance 'Kinjiki' (Forbidden Colors) — featuring a boy and a chicken — is considered the founding work of butoh; Kazuo Ohno's 1977 solo 'Admiring La Argentina,' performed at age 71, became its most celebrated piece.
notes: Not UNESCO-inscribed. Founded 1959. Internationally performed; Sankai Juku is its most prominent company.

name: Powwow dance
region: Great Plains and beyond (US and Canada, intertribal)
era: Modern form inaugurated 1923; roots in older Plains warrior-society dances
tagline: Intertribal Indigenous North American drum-and-dance gathering.
profile: The powwow is an intertribal gathering of Native American and First Nations peoples centered on communal singing, drumming and dance. Dance styles include Men's Northern Traditional, Grass Dance, Fancy Dance and Women's Jingle, Fancy Shawl and Cloth Dress, performed competitively and socially in a circle around the drum. The modern intertribal powwow format emerged in 1923 at the Haskell Indian Nations University in Lawrence, Kansas (Wikipedia).
insight: Many dances within a powwow are sacred or restricted and not performed publicly; the catalog entry describes only the intertribal social and competition context — Indigenous communities retain authority over what is shared.
notes: NOT UNESCO-inscribed. Sacred/restricted dances not described here — respectful ethnographic framing required. Modern intertribal format from 1923 (Haskell). Shared US/Canada tradition.

name: Jongo (Caxambu)
region: Southeast Brazil (Parai­ba do Sul valley; Rio de Janeiro, Sao Paulo, Minas Gerais, Espi­rito Santo)
tagline: Afro-Brazilian Bantu drum-circle dance of the southeast.
profile: Jongo is an Afro-Brazilian circle dance and music tradition rooted in the Bantu communities of the Paraiba do Sul valley in Southeast Brazil, historically practiced on coffee plantations. Participants form a roda, trading improvised pontos (sung verses) over two interlocking drums (caxambu and candongueiro) and a puita friction drum, with couples entering to dance the umbigada (belly-touch invitation). Registered as Brazilian Cultural Heritage by IPHAN in 2005 (Wikipedia; Encyclopedia.com).
insight: Jongo is considered a precursor to urban samba carioca; its coded point-songs were used by enslaved Africans to communicate in ways incomprehensible to overseers.
notes: IPHAN Brazilian Cultural Heritage (Livro das Formas de Expressao, 15 December 2005). Not UNESCO-inscribed.

name: Maracatu
era: Roots in 17th-18th century Congolese king coronations; Carnival tradition to present
tagline: Afro-Brazilian carnival procession of the Congo royal court.
profile: Maracatu is an Afro-Brazilian carnival procession rooted in the colonial-era ceremonial coronations of Kings and Queens of Congo (Reis do Congo) in Pernambuco. Two distinct forms exist: Maracatu Nacao (de Baque Virado) — a solemn, heavy percussion procession with a royal court and the sacred calunga doll — and Maracatu Rural (de Baque Solto) — a livelier form with the iconic caboclo de lanca (lancer) figure. Inscribed in Brazil's National Inventory of Intangible Cultural Heritage (IPHAN, 2014) (Wikipedia).
insight: The calunga doll carried by the Dama do Paco in Maracatu Nacao is considered a sacred object connecting the procession to Candomble and Jurema terreiros; it is handled only by initiated members.
notes: IPHAN national heritage 2014. Not UNESCO-inscribed. Sacred context — respectful framing for calunga doll.

name: Maculele
era: Linked to colonial sugarcane plantations; revived 1943 by Mestre Popo
tagline: Afro-Brazilian stick-fighting dance of Bahia.
profile: Maculele is an Afro-Brazilian warrior dance from Santo Amaro da Purificacao in Bahia in which paired dancers strike grimas (wooden sticks about 50-60 cm) or machetes in synchronized rhythms, circling within a roda to the beat of an atabaque drum. Legend ties it to an enslaved community whose able-bodied men were away and whose women and children defended the settlement with sticks. Mestre Popo (Paulino Aluisio de Andrade) revived it in 1943 and linked it to capoeira culture (Wikipedia; CapoeiraWiki).
insight: Maculele is often performed with dried cane stalks rather than sticks at its most traditional, worn as costume by some traditions; it is performed internationally through capoeira schools since Emilia Biancardi's Viva Bahia ensemble introduced it outside Bahia in the early 1960s.
notes: Not UNESCO-inscribed. Revived 1943 by Mestre Popo. Performed worldwide via capoeira schools.

name: Carimbo
tagline: Afro-Amazonian circle dance of Para with spinning skirts.
profile: Carimbo is a traditional music and folk circle dance of the Amazon state of Para, blending West African, Indigenous Amazonian and Portuguese elements. Women in colorful wide skirts twirl and spin to the curumbo drum while male partners try to flick a handkerchief tucked in her waistband; the dance culminates in the 'Carimbo do Peru' sequence. Mestre Verequete codified its traditional form; Pinduca created a modernized urban version from the 1970s. Registered as Brazilian national intangible heritage by IPHAN on 11 September 2014 (Wikipedia).
insight: Carimbo is NOT on the UNESCO Representative List — the circulated 'RL/01472' code is a misattribution; it is IPHAN national heritage, registered in the Livro de Registro das Formas de Expressao.
notes: IPHAN national heritage 11 September 2014. NOT UNESCO RL/01472 — that code is a misattribution. Ancestor of lambada.

name: Lundu
region: Brazil; salon form spread to the Portuguese court
era: Popular in the 18th century; largely historical
tagline: Earliest African-derived dance to enter Brazilian salons.
profile: The Lundu (landu/landum) is an 18th-century Afro-Brazilian couple dance and song genre rooted in Bantu/Angolan traditions, featuring hip-swaying, an umbigada belly-touch invitation and syncopated guitar accompaniment. The first African-derived dance to enter Brazilian salon culture, it later became fashionable at the Portuguese court. It is a direct ancestor of the choro, maxixe and samba, and its syncopated 2/4 rhythm influenced virtually all subsequent Brazilian popular music (Wikipedia; College Music Symposium).
insight: Domingos Caldas Barbosa, a freed slave and poet-musician, popularized the lundu-song in Lisbon salons in the late 18th century, bringing Afro-Brazilian sensibility directly into the Enlightenment Portuguese court.

sources.title: The Lundu and Modinha of Brazil in the Nineteenth Century

notes: Not UNESCO-inscribed. Largely historical; ancestor of choro, maxixe, samba.

name: Corridinho
era: Gained popularity in the 1800s; origins likely older
tagline: Fast-spinning Algarve folk dance in polka rhythm.
profile: The Corridinho is the traditional folk dance of Portugal's Algarve region, a lively couple dance in three parts — o corrido (the run), o rodado (the spinning) and a escovinha (the broom step) — danced to accordion music at festivals throughout southern Portugal. Partners spin rapidly together in a round, creating a joyful, exuberant character distinct from the slower northern Portuguese dances like the vira. Its legacy extended to Goa and Macau under Portuguese colonial influence (Wikipedia).
insight: The Goan Dekhni and Macanese Joget both show corridinho's formal influence; the dance is often said to have been popularized by Lorenzo Alvarez Garcia, though this is disputed in ethnographic literature.
notes: Not UNESCO-inscribed. Distinct from northern Portuguese vira and chula.

name: Chula
region: Northern Portugal (Minho to Beira Alta; Alto Douro variant)
era: Referenced as far back as the 16th century (Gil Vicente)
tagline: Northern Portuguese folk dance with sung challenge verses.
profile: The chula is a traditional northern Portuguese folk song-and-dance with competitive improvised sung verse (ao desafio), accompanied by the distinctive ramaldeira guitar and the ronda minhota ensemble (clarinet, fiddle, harmonica, cavaquinho, drum and triangle). Danced by couples with foot-stomping and finger-clicking, it is closely related to the vira but has its own distinct structure. Ethnographers have linked rhythmic elements of the chula to early Brazilian samba traditions (Wikipedia).
insight: Gil Vicente's early 16th-century reference to the chula makes it one of the oldest documented Portuguese folk dances; its competitive improvised verse tradition is shared with the Azorean despique and Brazilian embolada.
notes: Wikipedia article at 'Chula (music)'; covers Portuguese and Brazilian variants. Not UNESCO-inscribed.

name: Azonto
region: Greater Accra coastal communities (Chorkor, James Town, Teshie, Nungua, Tema)
era: Emerged early 2010s; roots in traditional Ga Kpanlogo
tagline: Ghanaian mimicry dance that swept the world in 2012.
profile: Azonto is a Ghanaian dance and music genre that went viral globally in 2012, rooted in the Ga communities of Greater Accra's coast. Evolved from the traditional Kpanlogo dance (formerly called Apaa), it is performed with a planted foot and pivoting opposite foot, using expressive hand gestures that mime everyday activities — ironing, driving, calling on a phone. It spread worldwide partly through footballer Asamoah Gyan's goal celebrations and the songs of Fuse ODG and Sarkodie (Wikipedia; GhanaRemembers).
insight: Azonto is one of the few contemporary African dances with a traceable traditional ancestor — it evolved directly from Kpanlogo, a Ga drum-and-dance tradition already in the catalog.

sources.title: How the Azonto dance captured Ghana and the world

notes: Not UNESCO-inscribed. Viral globally 2012. Roots in Ga Kpanlogo tradition.

name: Dekhni (Deknni)
era: One of the earliest dated examples c. 1869; living tradition
tagline: Goan syncretic dance fusing Indian melody and Western rhythm.
profile: Dekhni (Deknni) is a semi-classical Goan folk dance blending Indian musical scales and movement with Western rhythm, performed predominantly by women. It depicts a dancer (devadasi figure) seeking a boatman to ferry her across a river, conveying longing and devotion through graceful expressive gestures. Accompanied by the ghumot drum, mandolin and harmonium, it reflects Goa's unique Hindu-Catholic-Portuguese cultural synthesis (Wikipedia; ItsGoa).
insight: The song 'Hanv Saiba Poltodi Vetam' (Carlos Eugenio Ferreira, published Paris 1895, Goa 1926) is considered the definitive Dekhni piece; the dance's earliest documented version dates to a circa-1869 Kuxttoba performance.

sources.title: Dekhni dance is one of the oldest dance forms of Goa

notes: Wikipedia article at 'Deknni'. Not UNESCO-inscribed. Syncretic Hindu-Catholic-Portuguese heritage.

name: Tauolunga
era: Institutionalized 19th century from the Samoan taualuga
tagline: Tongan solo women's dance of graceful hand gestures.
profile: The tauolunga is a solo dance for young women in Tonga using refined hand and wrist movements that interpret a hiva kakala (sweet song). Blending the Samoan taualuga and Tongan ula traditions, it was popularized and refined by Queen Salote in the 1950s with characteristic wrist flourishes and lakalaka-influenced leg transitions. It is performed at weddings, royal occasions and community celebrations throughout Tonga and the diaspora (Wikipedia).
insight: Unlike the seated lakalaka (UNESCO-inscribed), the tauolunga is a standing solo form; audience members may drape tapa cloth or money over a dancer as a sign of appreciation in a practice called ula.
notes: Distinct from lakalaka (UNESCO RL/00072, already in catalog). Not UNESCO-inscribed.

name: Otea
tagline: Tahitian hip-vibration warrior dance to split-drum beats.
profile: The otea (ote'a) is the iconic Tahitian dance characterized by rapid hip-shaking to the tōʻere slit drum, with no singing — only percussion. Originally a male warrior dance, it is now performed by men (ote'a tāne), women (ote'a vahine) or mixed groups (ote'a amui) in rows, executing set figures while gesturing themes from daily life or warfare. It is central to the Heiva i Tahiti festival and performed worldwide by Polynesian groups (Wikipedia).
insight: The otea was among the dances banned by missionaries in the 19th century for its sensuality; it was revived at the re-established Heiva festival in 1881 and remains Tahiti's most recognizable dance form.
notes: Distinct from tāmūrē. Not UNESCO-inscribed. Central to Heiva i Tahiti.

name: Atilogwu
tagline: Igbo acrobatic youth dance asking 'is this magic?'
profile: Atilogwu is a high-energy Igbo youth dance of Southeast Nigeria combining vigorous body movement with acrobatics including flips, high jumps and human pyramids. The name translates approximately as 'is this magic?' reflecting audiences' astonishment at the performers' feats. Tempo and intensity are dictated by the drum and ogene metal gong. It is performed at festivals, marriages and coronations and has been presented internationally through cultural troupes (Wikipedia).
insight: Atilogwu was popularized on the national stage through its performances at Nigerian independence celebrations in the 1960s and through the Imo State Dance Troupe which toured internationally.
notes: Not UNESCO-inscribed. Rooted in the Ezeagu area of Enugu/Anambra states.

name: Adowa
tagline: Akan antelope dance of funerals and celebrations.
profile: Adowa is the most widespread Akan social dance of Ghana, performed at funerals, festivals, engagements and royal events. Dancers use symbolic hand gestures and a white cloth, with movement said to imitate the antelope (adowa in Twi). Accompanied by the atumpan talking drums, apentemma and dawuro/atoke bells, it is primarily performed by women though men participate. Each gesture carries specific meaning within the Akan symbolic system (Wikipedia).
insight: The atumpan 'talking drum' used in adowa transmits coded speech messages; a skilled drummer can 'speak' proverbs and instructions that Akan listeners understand — making the accompaniment a form of spoken language.
notes: Not UNESCO-inscribed. Distinct from kpanlogo (already in catalog).

name: Caporales
era: First presented publicly 14 June 1969; first major festival appearance 1972
tagline: Bolivian Andean dance of the caporal with bell-laden boots.
profile: Caporales is an energetic Bolivian dance first publicly presented on 14 June 1969 by the Estrada Pacheco brothers, inspired by the 'caporal' (slave overseer) character of the Afro-Bolivian saya. Male dancers wear boots fitted with cascabeles (bells) and perform high jumps and synchronized stomps; female dancers wear short pollera skirts and heeled boots. Its first major festival appearance was at La Fiesta del Gran Poder in La Paz in 1972 (Wikipedia).
insight: Caporales is declared Cultural and Intangible Heritage of the Plurinational State of Bolivia by Supreme Decree (June 2011); it is widely danced in Peru, Chile, Argentina and the US, where ownership is contested between Bolivia and Peru.
notes: Bolivian national heritage (Supreme Decree 2011). NOT UNESCO-inscribed. Origin disputed with Peru.

name: Concheros
tagline: Aztec-Catholic syncretic dance of armadillo-shell lutes.
profile: Concheros (Danza de los Concheros) is a syncretic Mexican ritual dance performed since the early colonial period, blending pre-Hispanic feathered regalia, Indigenous choreography and Catholic devotion.
